title: Read-path performance: eliminate copies and make batching real
description: Epic. The inner parse loop is well-tuned; the layers around it copy each record's bytes roughly nine times through an unbuffered file, the batching layer detaches the GIL per record while its comments claim per-batch amortization, and the parallel wrappers hold the GIL during parallel work. Children cover PERF-1 through PERF-10 in Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md. Absorbs bd-8zv5 (closed in favor of this epic). Gates bd-dra6: do not publish pymarc-comparison numbers before the first three children and the release-profile bead land.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Relationship to bd-l9zh (benchmarking infrastructure coherence epic): bd-l9zh fixes the ruler, this epic moves the needle. Land bd-l9zh's coherence work early (or at least bd-oayg.8's baseline fix) so improvements here are measured against trustworthy baselines; bd-dra6 publishes only after both.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Measurement protocol for all children (agreed 2026-06-11): (1) CodSpeed file-path benchmarks (pytest-benchmark reading a real on-disk file via MARCReader(path) at 1k/10k, plus a criterion from_path bench in the cargo codspeed build list) land with bd-0pg6.1 and act as permanent CI regression sensors. CodSpeed's simulated instruction counts track copy-elimination wins well but undercount syscall/kernel-side effects, so they are sensors, not payoff numbers. (2) Each child PR records a local wall-clock A/B in its PR description: main vs branch, release build (.cargo/check.sh --release), on-disk fixtures at 10k and 100k, >=5 runs, median records/sec, same quiet macOS machine. Numbers stay in PR descriptions only until bd-dra6 publishes a citable run (this epic gates bd-dra6). (3) Mechanistic claims get unit-level proof where possible (e.g. a CountingReader test for bd-0pg6.1) rather than OS-level tracing.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Measurement insight from bd-0pg6.2/.3 (2026-06-12): wall-clock is neutral within noise for copy elimination at BOTH 250B and 4KB record sizes on Apple M4 — its memory bandwidth makes per-record copies nearly free; the read path's wall-clock is dominated by parse + Python-boundary costs. Consequences for remaining children: bd-0pg6.6 (real per-batch GIL detach) is likely the largest wall-clock win; bd-0pg6.4 (lazy current_chunk) deletes the last per-record copy but expect wall-clock-neutral too; the copy-elimination value is structural (allocator pressure, zero-copy foundation for .8's parallel path) and shows up in CodSpeed instruction counts rather than wall-clock. Weaker hardware than M4 will see more of it. Worth re-measuring on x86 CI hardware before bd-dra6 publishes claims.

title: Buffer the RustFile backend
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PERF-1.

src-python/src/backend.rs:36 stores RustFile(File) raw and the per-record read loop (backend.rs:199-241) issues at least two read(2) syscalls per record on the default MARCReader(path) path — 2M+ syscalls for a million records.

What to do: RustFile(BufReader<File>) with 64-256KB capacity; check the core Rust MarcReader::from_path for the same issue. Highest win-to-effort ratio in the review.
close_reason: Merged in PR #285: 64 KiB BufReader on all file-path readers; +19.2%/+18.4% wall-clock read throughput at 10k/100k

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #281: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/281

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Instrumentation (agreed 2026-06-11): first commit of this PR adds the file-path benchmarks (pytest-benchmark via MARCReader(path) on a real temp file at 1k/10k; criterion from_path bench added to the cargo codspeed build list) so the affected path is sensed before it changes. Mechanistic proof: a CountingReader<R> Read-wrapper unit test asserting reads-per-underlying-call drop from ~2 per record to ~1 per buffer fill — cross-platform, runs as a normal cargo test, replaces any strace/container idea. Payoff numbers: wall-clock A/B per the epic protocol (see bd-0pg6 comment). Also seed scripts/bench_read_throughput.py (mrrc-only) here; bd-dra6 extends it with pymarc columns later.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: PR #285 open: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/pull/285. Wall-clock A/B (Apple M4, release wheel, scripts/bench_read_throughput.py, 5 runs, median): 10k fixture 279,328 -> 332,943 rec/s (+19.2%); 100k fixture 278,743 -> 329,915 rec/s (+18.4%). Mechanistic proof in tests/read_buffering_tests.rs (counting Read wrapper: >=2 reads/record unbuffered, ~1 per 64 KiB fill buffered) — placed in the core crate because cargo test cannot link mrrc-python (extension-module). Also buffered the authority/holdings Python readers, which had the same raw-File issue.

title: Performance grab bag: construction copies, zero-init buffers, get_fields call count
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PERF-10 items b, c, e, f (item a folds into the writer-consolidation bead; item d, streaming format APIs, folds into the format-front-door bead).

(b) source.extract::<Vec<u8>>() copies the whole bytes input at reader construction — one copy per reader, defensible, but a borrowed PyBytes-backed cursor is possible if large in-memory inputs matter. (c) vec![0u8; expected_len] zero-initializes before overwriting in read_record_data — revisit with uninit/read_buf APIs after the bigger copies are gone. (e) no-arg get_fields() makes 9 separate Rust calls for control tags plus a full deep clone of data fields — a single Rust call returning all triples would tidy it. (f) Leader::from_bytes allocates a small reserved String per record — listed only so it is not re-investigated.

title: Eliminate the set_parse_buffer happy-path copy
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PERF-2.

iso2709_skeleton.rs:285-286 calls ctx.set_parse_buffer(&record_data, ..), which does buffer.to_vec() (iso2709.rs:166-169) — a full alloc+memcpy of every record, paid by 100% of records on all paths (pure Rust included) solely so error constructors can render bytes_near hex dumps for the under-1% that error.

What to do: pass &record_data to the err_* helpers at raise sites (it is in scope at every raise) — mechanical, deletes the copy outright.
close_reason: Merged in PR #292: Arc-shared parse buffer, per-record alloc+memcpy deleted; +1.2% wall-clock at 100k, Arc::ptr_eq mechanism test

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: PR open. Arc-based design instead of err_* signature threading (35 raise sites untouched). Wall-clock: +1.2% at 100k/250B records, neutral at 10k; Arc::ptr_eq test pins the no-copy mechanism.

title: Collapse the per-record byte-copy chain; add a slice-based parse entry point
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PERF-3.

Roughly nine copies of each record's bytes between MARCReader(path) and a parsed record, through backend.rs:236-276, batched_unified_reader.rs:103/131/173, readers.rs:330/334/348, iso2709.rs:407, the skeleton's set_parse_buffer, and __init__.py:1727. readers.rs:348 Cursor::new(record_bytes_owned.to_vec()) is pure waste (already owned).

What to do: move owned Vec<u8> through the chain instead of SmallVec::from_slice/to_vec at each hop; add a parse_record_from_slice entry point so in-memory bytes skip the Cursor and read_record_data copies — this also makes the rayon path zero-copy (see the parallel-path bead).
close_reason: Merged in PR #294: parse_record_from_bytes + slice skeleton entry, copy chain ~9 -> 2; wall-clock neutral on M4 (see epic comment), parity tests over full 1k corpus

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: PR #294 open. ~9 copies -> 2 (backend assembly + current_chunk stash). parse_record_from_bytes public API added; parity tests over the full 1k corpus. Wall-clock neutral at 250B AND 4KB records on M4 (see epic comment) — value is structural + enables .8 zero-copy parallel path.

title: Make MARCReader.current_chunk lazy
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PERF-4.

readers.rs:334/215 stash a full copy per record and __init__.py:1727 materializes a fresh Python bytes per record — two extra full-record copies always, even when nobody reads current_chunk.

What to do: make MARCReader.current_chunk a lazy property fetching _inner.last_chunk on access; keep pymarc semantics (chunk of the most recent read, exception or not).

title: Read the Python-file backend in large chunks
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PERF-5.

buffered_reader.rs:31,105,152-165 (same shape in unified_reader.rs and backend.rs): two Python file.read() round-trips plus a fresh getattr("read") per record, all under the GIL; the batching layer changes when these calls happen but not how many.

What to do: read large chunks (e.g. 256KB — one Python call per ~150 records) and split records out in Rust (the boundary scanner already supports this); bind the read method once at construction; downcast to PyBytes and use as_bytes() instead of extract::<Vec<u8>>() to drop a copy.

title: Unify the batched readers; detach the GIL per batch
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PERF-6.

batched_reader.rs and batched_unified_reader.rs are a near line-for-line duplicated queue/batch state machine that has already drifted (only one tracks offsets). The GIL detach happens per record (readers.rs:347) while comments claim "N to N/100" amortization — the claim describes neither path. Queue elements are SmallVec<[u8; 4096]>: every queue operation is a 4KB memcpy and the 200-element backing is ~820KB.

What to do: one generic BatchedReader over a RecordByteSource trait; parse the whole batch inside one detach and queue parsed records (or plain Vec<u8>); keep the 200-record/300KB bounds; fix the comments and the matching docstrings in test_queue_state_machine.py.

title: Hoist hot-path regexes into LazyLock statics
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PERF-8.

field_linkage.rs:98 recompiles its regex on every LinkageInfo::parse, which Record::get_linked_field and friends call in loops (record.rs:503,535,587,670) — compilation costs more than the parse it serves on 880-heavy records. marcxml.rs:116,121 compile two regexes per document, and the replace_all preprocessing copies the whole XML document up to twice (~3x peak memory).

What to do: static LazyLock<Regex> (first-party precedent in marc8_tables.rs); longer term, replace the marcxml regex preprocessing with namespace handling inside the existing event loop to remove the document copies.

title: Fix parallel-path GIL holds and per-record channel sends
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PERF-7.

rayon_parser_pool_wrapper.rs:53 runs the whole parallel parse while holding the GIL (no detach) — other Python threads stall for the batch duration. producer_consumer_pipeline_wrapper.rs:154-205 blocks on recv() holding the GIL. producer_consumer_pipeline.rs:116-120 sends records one at a time over the channel (the unused config.batch_size suggests batch sends were intended) and clones a >=512KB buffer per chunk iteration (:98). rayon_parser_pool.rs:90-94 pays Cursor + read_record_data + set_parse_buffer copies on already-contiguous bytes. boundary_scanner.rs:109 clones the boundary Vec per scan.

What to do: detach around parallel work and recv; batch the channel sends; reuse the chunk buffer; adopt the slice-based parse entry point. Sequencing: blocks bd-ieoe — fix the parallel paths before building the measurement procedure for them.

title: Reduce per-field tag allocations; decide on a Tag newtype
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PERF-9.

Three String allocations per data field for a 3-byte tag: iso2709_skeleton.rs:382-384 (from_utf8().to_string()), iso2709.rs:779 (Field::new(tag.to_string(), ..)), record.rs:190 (entry(field.tag.clone())). Roughly 50-75 small mallocs per record.

What to do: short term, thread the already-owned tag String through (the built-and-discarded _tag parameter at reader.rs:331). Long term: a Tag([u8; 3]) Copy newtype for Field.tag and the IndexMap key — API-rippling, only gets cheaper before 1.0; make the call during the API epic's 1.0 surface work.

title: Review and wire v0.8 error-handling infrastructure
description:
## Status: 0.8.1 release scope

The original "review and wire v0.8 error-handling infrastructure" scope (Phase 1 audit + Phase 2 six numbered remediations, captured below as historical context) is largely complete. What remains is the long tail to ship 0.8.1: a few independent implementation beads, a pre-1.0 design decision, the perf-gate disposition, and the final release-readiness gate.

## Order of operations — remaining

Sequenced via `br dep` edges so `br ready` reflects the order automatically. Tier names are convenience labels; within a tier, beads have no ordering between them.

### Tier A — Implementation (any order)

- **bd-vwlp** (p2) — Align Python backend truncation detection with parser recovery_mode. Fixes E005 capture in lenient/permissive on the Python side; needed regardless of the .17 default decision.
- **bd-al66** (p2) — Expose `max_errors` kwarg on Python `MARCReader`. Unblocks Python E099 coverage left as a skip in bd-mk6z.
- **bd-rjl1** (p2) — Expand E404 manifest coverage to non-size `WriterError` triggers, plus the broader per-trigger audit sweep that bd-0x73.12's release gate then re-asserts.
- **bd-1a5a** (p3) — Add Python integration test for E007 OSError path. Self-contained.

### Tier B — Pre-1.0 design decision

- **bd-0x73.17** (p2) — Revisit default `recovery_mode` (strict vs permissive). Decide early: the answer drives CHANGELOG framing, example code in docs, and migration-guide language. Not a blocker for Tier A implementation, but a prerequisite for finalizing user-facing docs that .12 reconciles against.

### Tier C — Release-prep decisions

- **bd-0x73.15** (p2) — Decide retire/generalize/park for the perf-gate workflow.
- **bd-0x73.16** (p2) — Re-anchor or retire the perf baseline at v0.8.1 tag. Pair with .15; same evidence, same call.
- **bd-0x73.18** (p3) — Improve perf-gate signal-to-noise via median-of-N. Wired as `depends-on bd-0x73.15`; closes as moot if .15 retires the gate.

### Tier D — Final gate

- **bd-0x73.12** (p1) — Release-readiness gate (re-run unwired audit + perf + docs reconciliation). Wired as `depends-on` every other open child, so `br ready` only surfaces it once everything else closes. Re-asserts the per-trigger audit constructed in bd-rjl1.

### Tier E — Wrap-up

- **bd-0x73** (this umbrella) — close on tag.

## Historical scope (original 2026-04-29 framing)

GitHub issue #148 surfaced during launch-post drafting: v0.8 shipped the *infrastructure* for rich field-level error diagnostics (typed exception classes, error codes, slugs, snapshots, `to_dict()` shape, pickle round-trip, Python binding) but did not wire it to the parse paths that would emit it. The structural-error paths (leader, directory, base address, EOR, encoding) work as documented; the field-level paths (indicator, subfield code) did not.

GitHub: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/148

### Phase 1 — full inventory and audit (no code changes)

Cross-check **everything** error-related against what actually fires from the parse paths. The user's framing: "thorough review of the entire new error infrastructure to make sure it's all wired up correctly and assess where we are with it."

Concretely:

- Every `MarcError` variant: where defined, where constructed, what trips it. Mark unwired ones.
- Every Python exception class in `mrrc/exceptions.py`: same.
- Every error code (E001–E007, E099, E101, E105, E106, E201, E202, E301, E401, E402, E404): which parse paths emit it.
- All validators in `src/validation.rs`: `IndicatorValidator` and any others. Where called? What detects them?
- Recovery-mode behavior: `strict` / `lenient` / `permissive` — what each yields, what diagnostics survive.
- Reader-side accessors: `MARCReader.permissive=True` swallow path, any `current_exception` / `last_exception` / `last_chunk` shape.
- Documentation claims vs code reality: `docs/reference/error-handling.md`, `docs/reference/error-codes.md`, `docs/guides/migration-from-pymarc.md`.

Output: a single design doc (or sequence of beads) capturing every gap, not just the ones #148 enumerates. #148 explicitly listed six findings; the review confirmed the complete set and expanded it into the children spawned under this umbrella.

### Phase 2 — implementation

Per #148's recommended plan:

1. Wire `parse_data_field` to construct `MarcError::InvalidIndicator` on bad indicator bytes (universal "digit or space" check).
2. Wire `parse_subfields` to construct `MarcError::BadSubfieldCode` on bad subfield-code bytes (printable ASCII alphanumeric).
3. Add **new orthogonal axis** `validation_level: structural | strict_marc` (default `structural`) separate from `recovery_mode`. Don't conflate detection-strictness with recovery-policy.
4. Surface per-record diagnostics in lenient/permissive: attach `record.errors: list[MrrcException]` and add an `iter_with_errors()` / `(record, errors)` tuple iterator.
5. Add `MARCReader.last_exception` / `last_chunk` for pymarc-compat; populate on the `permissive=True` swallow path so the swallowed info is at least retrievable.
6. Update `error-handling.md` / `error-codes.md` / `migration-from-pymarc.md` to match the code that ships in 0.8.1.

## Done when

- E201 (`InvalidIndicator`) and E202 (`BadSubfieldCode`) fire from the actual parse path with full positional context, exercised by tests reading malformed records (not by `raise`-ing in test code).
- A user can drain a 50k-record stream and identify every record that had any defect and what the defect was, regardless of recovery mode.
- `permissive=True` gives users at least as much information as pymarc's `current_exception` / `current_chunk`.
- All advertised behavior in error-handling / error-codes / migration-from-pymarc docs is real.
- The launch blog post's central example (`:` at indicator 0 of 245 → `InvalidIndicator [E201]`) actually works against a 0.8.1 install.

## Out of scope (file separately if pursued)

- Per-tag indicator semantics (245 first ind must be 0/1, etc.). Start with universal "digit or space."
- Per-tag subfield-code semantics. Start with "printable ASCII alphanumeric."
- Full validator wiring for control fields (008, 006, 007).
- Encoding errors — already wired via `Utf8DecodeMode`.

## Non-goals

- No reverting v0.8.0 publish state. 0.8.0 stays on PyPI/crates.io; 0.8.1 ships the corrective state. Yanking 0.8.0 is not currently on the table — issue and bead document the gap; users encountering it via the docs are advised to upgrade to 0.8.1.

## References

- GitHub Issue #148 — full analysis with pymarc 5.3.1 baseline repro and source-level evidence.
- `src/iso2709.rs:292-311` — original unwired `err_invalid_indicator`.
- `src/iso2709.rs:316-327` — original unwired `err_bad_subfield_code`.
- `src/iso2709.rs:622-642` — original direct cast / "in future enrichment work" doc-comment.
- `mrrc/__init__.py:1453-1460` — original `permissive=True` swallow path that lost the exception.
- `src/validation.rs:77+` — original unwired `IndicatorValidator`.
- `src/error.rs:1325+` — `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` containing original `MarcError::InvalidIndicator` construction sites.
close_reason: All 20 children complete and merged; the release-readiness gate (bd-0x73.12) landed in PR #231. All five 'Done when' criteria verified: E201/E202 fire from the real parse path with positional context (tests, not raise-in-test); a user can drain a 50k-record stream and identify every defect regardless of recovery mode (verified empirically across strict/lenient/permissive); permissive gives at least pymarc's current_exception/current_chunk info (added in #182); advertised docs behavior is real (verify_error_docs.py reconciliation gate + the milestone quality review); and the launch blog's central example (':' at indicator 0 of 245 -> InvalidIndicator E201) works against a 0.8.1 install. v0.8 error-handling infrastructure is reviewed and wired.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: GH issue #148 (https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/148) is the GitHub face of this epic. 8 child beads elevated to issues #149-#156, all assigned to the v0.8.1 milestone.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## 2026-04-30 review re-sequenced this epic into Phases A–E

**Phase A — foundation (TDD red phase, no parser changes):**
- bd-0x73.8 — error coverage fixture + harness (`tests/error_coverage.toml` + parameterized Rust + Python harness; every documented code starts as `wired = false` skip)
- bd-0x73.9 — perf baseline + CI gate (2% warn / 5% fail, baseline locks at v0.8.0)
- bd-0x73.3 (re-scoped) — integration tests consume the fixture; lands here, not at the end

**Phase B — wire one code at a time, each in its own PR:**
1. E201 InvalidIndicator → fixture skip removed → tests turn green
2. E202 BadSubfieldCode → same
3. E001/E003/E004 (split `leader_msg` into specific helpers inheriting ParseContext) — bd-44z4 + bd-0x73.2 (the InvalidLeader positional-context fix folds in here naturally)
4. E006 EndOfRecordNotFound — bd-44z4
5. E105 FieldNotFound — bd-44z4 (decide wire vs fold)
After each: re-run benchmarks, post delta.

**Phase C — recovery surface (orthogonal):**
- bd-0x73.10 — `validation_level` axis (was step 3)
- bd-0x73.11 — `record.errors` + `iter_with_errors` (was step 4)
- bd-0x73.7 — pymarc-default mode + `last_exception`/`last_chunk` (was step 5; subset)

**Phase D — cross-cutting test depth:**
- bd-0x73.4 — proptest strategies seeded from fixture
- bd-0x73.5 — fuzz error-classification harness cross-checking fixture
- bd-0x73.1 — validators decision (a/b/c per validator); option (a) wire-as-parse-time becomes coherent only after bd-0x73.10 lands

**Phase E — release:**
- bd-0x73.12 — release-readiness gate (re-run unwired-code audit, perf delta, docs reconciliation)
- Tag 0.8.1

**Deferred to 0.8.2:** bd-0x73.6 (broader pymarc API audit) — too large to land alongside 0.8.1's corrective scope; bd-0x73.7's narrow strictness-mode parity stays in 0.8.1.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Phase B sequencing update (2026-04-30)

bd-0x73.13 belongs in Phase B alongside the Rust-core wiring beads. It is the FFI-layer analogue of bd-0x73.2: TruncatedRecord (E005) loses positional context across the Python binding's ParseError → MarcError conversion path the same way InvalidLeader (E002) lacks positional context in Rust core. Surfaced by bd-0x73.8's harness.

Updated Phase B list:
1. E201 InvalidIndicator (Rust core wiring) — bd-0x73 / bd-44z4
2. E202 BadSubfieldCode (Rust core wiring) — bd-44z4
3. E001/E003/E004 split from E002 leader_msg (Rust core) — bd-44z4 + bd-0x73.2
4. E006 EndOfRecordNotFound (Rust core wiring) — bd-44z4
5. E105 FieldNotFound (Rust core, accessor decision) — bd-44z4
6. **E005 TruncatedRecord positional context across FFI — bd-0x73.13** (new)

Dependencies: bd-0x73.13 is independent of the other Phase B beads (different layer, different fix); can land in parallel. bd-0x73.12 (release-readiness gate) blocks on bd-0x73.13 because the gate asserts both harnesses pass and the Python harness currently skips e005_truncated_record's expected_context check pending this fix.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Workflow lifecycle decision filed (2026-04-30)

bd-0x73.15 captures the keep-vs-retire decision for the
`error-handling-perf-gate.yml` workflow that landed alongside
bd-0x73.9. The decision is naturally bound to release-readiness
(bd-0x73.12) because that bead closes the epic this workflow was
scoped for; updating the umbrella sequencing to reflect that:

**Phase E — release:**
- bd-0x73.12 — release-readiness gate (re-run unwired audit + perf + docs reconciliation)
- **bd-0x73.15** — decide retire / generalize / park for the
  perf-gate workflow based on what the error-handling work actually
  produced as a cumulative perf delta. Should be resolved alongside
  bd-0x73.12 so the workflow's fate is settled before 0.8.1 tags.

title: 4 unwired validator structs in src/validation*.rs (decision needed)
description:
## Context

Surfaced during the 2026-04-29 thorough error-handling audit (bd-0x73
phase 1). #148 flagged `IndicatorValidator` as an unwired complement
to the unwired E201/E202 path. The audit found **three additional
validators** in the same shape — public structs in `src/`, re-exported
from `src/lib.rs`, **never called from production code**.

## The four unwired validators

| Validator | Module | Re-export | Production call sites |
|---|---|---|---|
| `IndicatorValidator` | `src/validation.rs` | `src/lib.rs` | **0** |
| `IsbnValidator` | `src/bibliographic_helpers.rs` | `src/lib.rs` | **0** |
| `RecordStructureValidator` | `src/record_validation.rs` | `src/lib.rs` | **0** |
| `EncodingValidator` | `src/encoding_validation.rs` | `src/lib.rs` | **0** |

Each is publicly exported and so available for external callers to
invoke directly. None is wired to the parser / reader / writer / any
default code path. So the library has two parallel error layers:

1. The `MarcError` parse-path layer (E201/E202 unwired per #148, plus
   E001/E003/E004/E006/E105 unwired per the audit; bd-44z4 covers
   those).
2. A validators-as-API layer that exists for opt-in use by callers but
   has no internal users.

## Decision needed

Three options, mutually exclusive:

a. **Wire as parse-time validation.** Run validators automatically
   during reading. Requires deciding *which* validation level (what
   bd-0x73's recommended `validation_level` axis would gate).
   Substantial code change; needs interaction with `recovery_mode`.

b. **Document as user-callable opt-in.** Keep them public, add docs
   pages explaining when to invoke them post-parse. Lower lift; matches
   the current state but requires actual docs.

c. **Remove from public API.** Drop the `pub use` re-exports; either
   make them private helpers or delete them entirely.

(b) is the fastest path to "honest." (a) is the user-aligned answer
that closes the gap with what `error-codes.md` *implies*. (c) is
viable for any validator with no clear use case.

**Constraint posture:** mrrc is pre-1.0; mrrc-vs-itself BC is not a
goal. Per bd-0x73.10, validators wired into the `strict_marc`
validation level are opt-in by default, so option (a) does not
break existing v0.8.0 callers. Pymarc-migration parity remains the
only external constraint that matters for these decisions.

## Specific notes per validator

- **`IndicatorValidator`** has per-tag indicator rules (e.g., 245
  first-indicator must be 0/1). Far richer than the universal
  "digit or space" check #148 / bd-0x73 will wire into the parser.
  Could be both: parser does universal check (E201), `IndicatorValidator`
  remains opt-in for per-tag semantics.
- **`IsbnValidator`** is a domain-specific helper, not a parser
  concern. Likely belongs in option (b).
- **`RecordStructureValidator`** overlaps with the structural
  parse-path errors (E001/E003/E004 etc.) being addressed in bd-44z4.
  Decide together.
- **`EncodingValidator`** overlaps with E301 `EncodingError` which
  *is* wired. Decide whether to fold or keep distinct.

## Done when

- A decision recorded for each of the four validators (a/b/c).
- For (a) wired: the parse-time integration shipped with regression
  tests.
- For (b) docs: `docs/reference/` page covering the validator API
  surface, with examples.
- For (c) remove: `pub use` removed from `src/lib.rs`; CHANGELOG
  `### Breaking` entry under `[Unreleased]` for any affected names.
- `error-codes.md` and `error-handling.md` reflect what's actually
  enforced vs what's opt-in.

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella for v0.8 error-handling re-evaluation.
- bd-44z4 — sibling: 5 unwired structural error codes.
- GH issue #148 — original surfacing, mentions IndicatorValidator
  briefly.
close_reason: Implemented by PR #175 (merged)

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/150

title: validation_level orthogonal axis (structural | strict_marc)
description:
## Context

bd-0x73 Phase 2 step 3 introduced this idea inside the umbrella's
description but never elevated it to its own bead. The 2026-04-30
review elevates it: the orthogonal axis is a meaty architectural
decision that interacts with bd-0x73.1 (validators), bd-0x73.14
(E301 strict-UTF-8 divergence — now folded in), and the per-record
diagnostic surface. It deserves its own scope, design notes, and PR.

## The idea

Today `MARCReader` has one knob: `recovery_mode` (`strict` / `lenient`
/ `permissive`). This conflates two orthogonal concerns:

- **What counts as an error** (detection strictness)
- **What to do when one fires** (recovery policy)

Conflating them means "I want strict detection but lenient recovery"
isn't expressible. With the wiring bead set landing E201/E202/E001/etc.,
this becomes the natural shape of the problem.

## Proposed shape

```python
MARCReader(
    file,
    validation_level="structural",   # or "strict_marc"
    recovery_mode="strict",          # or "lenient", "permissive"
)
```

Two axes, orthogonal.

## Single rule (consistency + clarity)

The target shape, statable in one sentence:

> **`structural` is lossy everywhere; `strict_marc` is strict
> everywhere — every reader behaves the same way at each level.**

Concretely:

- `validation_level="structural"` (default): only ISO 2709 structural
  errors fire — leader, directory, EOR, base address, truncation. UTF-8
  decode is **lossy** (U+FFFD substitution) across **all** readers
  (bibliographic, authority, holdings); E301 does not fire. E201/E202
  byte checks do not fire.
- `validation_level="strict_marc"`: enforces strict UTF-8 across
  **all** readers (E301 fires on invalid byte sequences); E201/E202
  fire on bad indicator / subfield-code bytes. May also run the opt-in
  validators per bd-0x73.1.

The `recovery_mode` axis is unchanged: strict raises, lenient yields
record + errors, permissive yields None + last_exception (subject to
bd-0x73.6 / bd-0x73.11 diagnostic surface).

## Folded in: bd-0x73.14 (E301 divergence)

bd-0x73.14 asked "what should bibliographic readers do about strict
UTF-8?" — three options were on the table: opt-in mode (a),
default-strict (b), docs-only (c). Resolution: option (a),
implemented as `strict_marc` enforcing strict UTF-8 across all
readers. This puts E301 on the same axis as E201/E202: no extra
knob, single rule. Holdings moves from "always strict" to "strict
at strict_marc only" for reader symmetry.

Pre-1.0; no v0.8.0-vs-v0.8.1 BC weighting. The criterion is
end-state consistency *and* clarity, not migration delta.

## Interaction with bd-0x73.1 validators

Three of the four unwired validators (`IndicatorValidator`,
`RecordStructureValidator`, `EncodingValidator`) are candidates for
"runs at `validation_level=strict_marc`" wiring. `IsbnValidator` is
domain-specific and probably stays opt-in regardless.

## Done when

- `validation_level` kwarg accepted on `MARCReader` (Python) and
  `MarcReader::builder()` (Rust); default `"structural"`.
- `"structural"`: bibliographic + authority + holdings readers all
  fall back to lossy UTF-8 decode; E201/E202/E301 do not fire.
- `"strict_marc"`: bibliographic + authority + holdings readers all
  enforce strict UTF-8 (E301 fires on bad bytes); E201/E202 fire on
  bad indicator/subfield bytes.
- The two axes interact correctly: each cell of the
  `{structural, strict_marc} × {strict, lenient, permissive}` matrix
  has a passing test, with separate cases per error code.
- Perf check: `from_utf8` (strict) vs `from_utf8_lossy` cost on a
  realistic corpus is measured and acceptable; if not, the design
  needs revisiting before shipping.
- bd-0x73.14 closed as absorbed.
- `permissive=True` legacy kwarg keeps mapping to
  `recovery_mode="permissive"` (separate concern from
  validation_level).
- Docs: `docs/reference/error-handling.md` adds a "Validation level
  vs recovery mode" section explaining the orthogonality and quoting
  the one-sentence rule.

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella; this was Phase 2 step 3.
- bd-0x73.1 — sibling: validator decisions; (a) wire-as-parse-time
  becomes coherent only after this axis exists.
- bd-0x73.14 — folded in; E301 / strict-UTF-8 enforcement is now part
  of strict_marc rather than a separate decision.
- bd-0x73.8 — sibling: fixture+harness; this bead's test matrix lives
  in the same harness.
- bd-0x73.11 — sibling: record.errors / iter_with_errors paired
  diagnostic surface.
- bd-0x73.7 — sibling: pymarc-default parity becomes the
  `(structural, permissive)` cell of the new matrix; reduces to
  test+docs once this bead lands.
close_reason: Implemented by PR #173 (merged 2026-05-07); GH #162 closed

title: record.errors + iter_with_errors per-record diagnostic surface
description:
## Context

bd-0x73 Phase 2 step 4 introduced this idea inside the umbrella's
description but never elevated it to its own bead. The 2026-04-30
review elevates it: it's the load-bearing surface for "drain a 50k
stream and identify every record that had any defect, regardless of
recovery mode" — and it interacts with bd-0x73.7 (pymarc-default
mode) and the validation_level axis (sibling bead). Deserves its own
scope.

## What to build

### `record.errors: list[MrrcException]`

Every yielded record carries a list of any non-fatal errors that
fired during its parse. In strict mode this is always empty (the
record either parses clean or raises). In lenient mode, recoverable
errors land here. The list is `[]` for clean records, never `None`.

Rust: `Record { ..., errors: Vec<MarcError> }`
Python: `record.errors -> list[MrrcException]`

The errors carry the same positional context as if they'd been
raised directly (record_index, byte_offset, field_tag, etc.).

### `iter_with_errors() -> Iterator[(Record, list[MrrcException])]`

Alternate iterator on `MARCReader` that yields `(record, errors)`
tuples instead of bare records. Equivalent to iterating + reading
`record.errors`, but more discoverable for the
"give-me-everything-defective" use case.

```python
for rec, errs in reader.iter_with_errors():
    if errs:
        log.warning(f"record {rec.title!r}: {len(errs)} issues")
        for e in errs:
            log.warning(f"  [{e.code}] {e}")
```

### Rust analogue

`MarcReader::iter_with_errors() -> impl Iterator<Item = (Record, Vec<MarcError>)>`.

## Recovery-mode interaction

| Mode | record.errors | iter_with_errors yields |
|---|---|---|
| strict | always `[]` (raises before yield) | same as iter() |
| lenient | populated with recoverable errors | same: (record, errors) |
| permissive | populated when record yielded; `None` for swallowed records (?) | needs decision: (None, errors) for swallowed? |

The swallowed-record case in permissive mode is the open design
question. Two options:
- Yield `(None, [exception])` from iter_with_errors so even swallowed
  records are observable.
- Skip swallowed records entirely; surface them only via
  `last_exception` (per bd-0x73.7).

Recommend (A) because it matches the use case: "I want to know about
every defective record, including the unsalvageable ones."

## Done when

- `Record { errors }` field added (Rust + Python).
- `iter_with_errors()` / `MARCReader.iter_with_errors()` available.
- Across the bd-0x73.8 fixture harness, every fixture's expected
  recovery-mode behavior is exercised through both `iter()` and
  `iter_with_errors()`.
- Each cell of the `{structural, strict_marc} × {strict, lenient,
  permissive} × {iter, iter_with_errors}` matrix has a test.
- Docs: `docs/reference/error-handling.md` adds an
  "Inspecting per-record errors" section.
- Migration doc updated: pymarc users should see this as the
  pymarc-default + extras path.

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella; this was Phase 2 step 4.
- bd-0x73.7 — sibling: pymarc-default-matching strictness mode; this
  surface is what makes mrrc strictly more informative than pymarc
  while remaining drop-in compatible.
- bd-0x73.10 — sibling: validation_level axis.
- bd-0x73.8 — sibling: fixture+harness drives the test matrix.
close_reason: Implemented by PR #174 (merged at 0fa3aa0). record.errors field on all three Record types; iter_with_errors() on bibliographic MARCReader; permissive=True yields (None, [exception]) for swallowed records. CI green; perf gate WARN softened to non-blocking annotation per bd-0x73.18 follow-up.
