title: Release-readiness gate (re-run unwired audit + docs reconciliation + pymarc parity)
description:
## Context

The v0.8 audit found that wiring gaps shipped because nothing in CI asserted "every documented error code has a real production call site." This bead is the gate that prevents 0.8.1 from shipping with the same shape of gap.

The principle: documentation-as-spec, enforced by tooling.

## What to run before tagging 0.8.1

### 1. Unwired-code re-audit

Re-run the same `rg` enumeration the 2026-04-29 audit used:

- For every variant in `MarcError`, count non-test call sites that construct it.
- For every code in `docs/reference/error-codes.md`, confirm the fixture in `tests/error_coverage.toml` has `wired = true`.
- Assert: zero variants with zero non-test call sites; zero codes with `wired = false`.

Output: a printable table. Failure: any `wired = false` row.

### 2. Coverage harness assertion

Run `cargo test --test error_coverage` and `pytest tests/python/test_error_coverage.py` (per bd-0x73.8). Assert:

- All wired cases pass; harness skips are limited to documented Python-binding-gap or unreachable-defensive-code reasons (post-bd-0x73.21.3 baseline: 1 harness-limitation for E402 Python-only + 2 cross-reader-type findings filed for #204).
- The harness's per-trigger tally — "wired in manifest: N/M | harness asserted: K/N" — is at the agreed final number. The number is per-distinct-trigger-path, not per-code, following the convention bd-rjl1 established for E404 and bd-0x73.21.3 generalized across E001/E002/E003/E101/E106/E301/E401 plus programmatic defensive checks.
- Every distinct production fire site for a `MarcError` variant has at least one manifest case (extends the per-trigger principle from bd-rjl1 across all variants per the bd-0x73.21.3 audit; this gate re-asserts that audit's output, it doesn't re-do it).

### 3. Documentation reconciliation

Diff the following pairs, fail if they disagree:

- `docs/reference/error-codes.md` listed codes ↔ `MarcError` variants ↔ `tests/error_coverage.toml` entries.
- `docs/reference/error-handling.md` "always-present" vs "may-be-present" context tables ↔ what the harness's `expected_context` lists assert.
- `docs/guides/migration-from-pymarc.md`'s claims about `permissive=True` ↔ the actual permissive-mode fixture-harness behavior.

Tooling: a simple `tools/verify_error_docs.py` (or rust binary in `xtask/`) that loads each source-of-truth and prints a diff. Run it in CI on every PR that touches `src/error*.rs`, `mrrc/exceptions.py`, or `docs/reference/error-*.md`.

### 5. Pymarc parity sanity check

For `permissive=True` (per bd-0x73.7), run a side-by-side parity test against pymarc 5.3.1 on a small mixed-quality corpus (say 100 records, 10% malformed). Assert iteration shape equivalence modulo documented divergences.

## Done when

- `tools/verify_error_docs.py` (or equivalent) exists and runs cleanly on main.
- A new check.sh section runs steps 1-4 above; failure blocks the release.
- 0.8.1 tag is cut only after all five steps pass.
- `docs/contributing/release-procedure.md` updated to include this gate as a pre-tag step.

## Why a separate bead

The per-piece beads (bd-44z4, bd-0x73.2, etc.) each carry their own "done when" — but none of them assert the *system* property that the release as a whole must satisfy. That property is what this bead exists to enforce.

Without this gate, the same wiring-gap-shipped-undetected outcome can recur in 0.9.

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella; see "Order of operations — remaining" for where this gate sits.
- bd-0x73.8 — fixture+harness; this gate consumes its outputs.
- bd-0x73.9 — perf baseline; this gate enforces the threshold.
- bd-rjl1 — constructs the per-trigger E404 audit + the broader per-variant sweep that this gate re-asserts in step 2.
- 2026-04-29 audit (the original `rg` analysis) — this gate re-runs that analysis as the explicit final check.
- `docs/contributing/release-procedure.md` — needs an addition.
close_reason: Release-readiness gate landed in PR #231 (merged). Scoped to option A: scripts/verify_error_docs.py reconciles MarcError codes <-> error-codes.md <-> error_coverage.toml + audits non-test construction sites (wired into .cargo/check.sh full mode); tests/python/test_pymarc_parity.py covers pymarc iteration-shape parity (importorskip-guarded); docs/contributing/testing.md notes it. Release-procedure pre-tag subsection and prose auto-diffs intentionally dropped (recorded in the scope-decision comment). Parent epic bd-0x73 stays open until the v0.8.1 close-out is confirmed.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Scope decision (2026-05-10): IN SCOPE for 0.8.1 — the close-out

This bead IS the release-readiness gate; bd-0x73 closes when this
closes. Comes after bd-vwlp + bd-mk6z + bd-0x73.17 land (the
remaining corrective work), and before bd-0x73.16 + bd-0x73.15 are
touched at the actual tag-cut.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Perf-gate final-check step removed from description (was step 3); the gate was retired in PR #202 (bd-0x73.15 + bd-0x73.16). Cumulative v0.8.0→v0.8.1 cost on parse_10k_clean_strict was +0.50%, recorded in CHANGELOG [Unreleased]. Title updated to match.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
Scope decision (2026-05-28): trimmed from a full release-procedure gate to a standing automated guard (option A), matching the solo-dev 'match ceremony to risk' posture. The point-in-time cleanup was already done by the per-piece beads + the 9lui review; what remains worth keeping is the cheap recurring check.

Delivered:
- scripts/verify_error_docs.py — reconciles MarcError codes (src/error.rs code() arms) <-> docs/reference/error-codes.md <-> tests/error_coverage.toml (wired status) + a non-test construction-site audit. Exit 1 on any disagreement (negative-tested).
- .cargo/check.sh full mode runs it every check — catches drift the error_coverage harness can't (a new code with no manifest case).
- tests/python/test_pymarc_parity.py — pymarc iteration-shape parity over a 100-record/10%-malformed corpus, importorskip-guarded (skips without pymarc). Validated against real pymarc 5.3.1.
- docs/contributing/testing.md — a short reminder that the parity test exists and how to run it with pymarc.

Intentionally NOT done (dropped from the original done-when): the docs/contributing/release-procedure.md pre-tag subsection and the prose-table auto-reconciliation (error-handling.md narrative / migration-from-pymarc.md claims are prose, not machine-diffable). Revised done-when is the four delivered items above.

title: Python binding drops positional context for TruncatedRecord across the FFI boundary
description:
## Context

Surfaced by the Phase A error-coverage harness (tests/error_coverage.rs
and tests/python/test_error_coverage.py) on 2026-04-30. The Rust core's
`MarcError::TruncatedRecord` correctly carries `record_index`,
`byte_offset`, and `record_byte_offset` for an EOF-truncated input;
the same exception observed through the Python binding has all three
fields set to `None`.

## Source-level evidence

`src-python/src/parse_error.rs::to_py_err` (lines ~198-210):

```rust
ParseErrorKind::TruncatedRecord {
    expected_length,
    actual_length,
} => MarcError::TruncatedRecord {
    record_index: self.context.record_index,
    byte_offset: self.context.byte_offset,
    record_byte_offset: None,         // hardcoded
    source_name: self.context.source_name.clone(),
    record_control_number: None,
    expected_length: *expected_length,
    actual_length: *actual_length,
    bytes_near: self.context.bytes_near.clone(),
},
```

Two issues compounding:

1. `record_byte_offset` is hardcoded `None`, so it is never populated
   for any TruncatedRecord that flows through this conversion path.
2. `record_index` and `byte_offset` are inherited from
   `self.context.record_index` / `self.context.byte_offset`. The
   `ParseErrorContext` carrying these is constructed at the boundary-
   scanner / buffered-reader call site that detects truncation; that
   site does not populate the context, so the values default to
   `None`.

The direct `MarcError` path (synchronous reader without ParseError
intermediate) populates all three fields correctly. The asymmetry is
why the regression only manifests for E005 from harnesses driving
ParseError-producing call sites.

Empirical reproduction (2026-04-30 main):

| Field             | Rust core | Python binding |
|---|---|---|
| `record_index`        | `Some(1)`  | `None` |
| `byte_offset`         | `Some(24)` | `None` |
| `record_byte_offset`  | `Some(24)` | `None` |
| `expected_length`     | `Some(126)`| `Some(126)` |
| `actual_length`       | `Some(76)` | `Some(126)` *(wrong value)* |

`actual_length` is also incorrect on the Python side (claims 126 when
the truncation occurred after 76 bytes of body, with 24-byte leader
already consumed). The TruncatedRecord ParseError construction is
likely measuring "bytes requested" rather than "bytes actually read."

## What to do

1. Audit ParseErrorContext call sites in src-python that produce
   TruncatedRecord ParseErrors; populate `record_index`,
   `byte_offset`, and (where derivable) the actual_length.
2. Fix `to_py_err` so `record_byte_offset` is computed from the
   context (or the conversion accepts it as an explicit field on the
   ParseError).
3. Re-enable the e005_truncated_record assertion in
   tests/python/test_error_coverage.py by removing its entry from
   `_PYTHON_BINDING_REGRESSIONS`.

## Done when

- `mrrc.MARCReader(bytes_, recovery_mode="strict")` over a truncated
  fixture raises `mrrc.TruncatedRecord` with `record_index`,
  `byte_offset`, `record_byte_offset`, and an accurate `actual_length`
  populated.
- `_PYTHON_BINDING_REGRESSIONS` in test_error_coverage.py is empty.
- The Python harness asserts the same expected_context shape as the
  Rust harness for TruncatedRecord.

## Reference

- Surfaced by the Phase A coverage harness (parent epic).
- Affected variant: only `MarcError::TruncatedRecord` so far. The
  ParseError conversion path may affect other variants if the same
  call sites produce them; audit the full set when fixing.
close_reason: Python TruncatedRecord FFI context loss fixed via PR #161

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Sequencing within bd-0x73 epic

This is a Phase B (variant-wiring) bead alongside bd-44z4 (5 unwired structural codes) and bd-0x73.2 (InvalidLeader positional context). The shape is parallel to bd-0x73.2 — that one fixes a Rust-core variant carrying no positional context; this one fixes a Python-binding variant losing positional context that the Rust core does populate.

Independence: no hard dep on the other Phase B beads — the FFI fix touches src-python/src/parse_error.rs and is orthogonal to the Rust-core wiring work. Can land in parallel.

Downstream: bd-0x73.12 (release-readiness gate) blocks on this. The gate's docs-vs-implementation reconciliation requires the Python harness to assert e005_truncated_record's full expected_context, which currently skips citing this regression in tests/python/test_error_coverage.py::_PYTHON_BINDING_REGRESSIONS.

title: 0.8.1 P2: E301 bibliographic-vs-holdings strict-UTF-8 divergence (decision needed)
description:
## Context

Surfaced by the bd-0x73.8 coverage harness on 2026-04-30. The
documented E301 (`utf8_invalid` / `EncodingError`) fires from the
holdings reader's strict-UTF-8 path but never from the bibliographic
or authority readers, which fall back to lossy decoding (substituting
U+FFFD for invalid byte sequences). A user reading
docs/reference/error-codes.md without context might reasonably assume
all readers enforce strict UTF-8 and emit this code.

## Source-level evidence

`docs/reference/error-codes.md#E301` already says:

> **Applies to:** Currently raised most often by the holdings reader,
> which uses strict UTF-8 decoding. The bibliographic and authority
> readers historically fall back to lossy decoding (replacement
> characters) and don't surface this code.

So the divergence is documented, but the design question — *should*
the bibliographic / authority readers also enforce strict UTF-8 (or
make it opt-in via a reader configuration) — is open.

The coverage harness's E301 case currently uses a bibliographic-style
fixture (a stray 0xFF byte inside a 100$a value) and is marked
`wired = false` with a skip_reason naming the lossy-decoding
divergence. A separate holdings-record fixture would exercise the
documented path.

## Decision needed

Three options:

a. **Add a bibliographic strict-UTF-8 mode.** New constructor flag
   (e.g., `MarcReader::with_strict_utf8(true)`) or a new
   `validation_level = "strict_marc"` setting per bd-0x73.10. When
   set, the bibliographic reader fires E301 on invalid UTF-8 instead
   of substituting U+FFFD.
b. **Make strict UTF-8 the default; document U+FFFD as opt-in.**
   Backward incompatible; needs a CHANGELOG `### Breaking` entry and
   a migration note in docs/guides/migration-from-pymarc.md (pymarc
   does *not* enforce strict UTF-8).
c. **Keep the divergence; clarify docs.** Update
   error-codes.md#E301 to make explicit that the bibliographic reader
   *does not* fire this code, and update the harness's E301 case to
   use a holdings-record fixture instead.

(c) is the smallest change and matches the current shape; (a) gives
users an opt-in for stricter behavior; (b) is the most consistent
spec but breaks pymarc-style users.

## Done when

- A decision recorded among (a) / (b) / (c) with rationale.
- For (a) wired: the new flag / mode shipped with regression tests
  and the harness's E301 case flipped to wired = true with a
  bibliographic fixture.
- For (b) wired: the breaking change shipped, CHANGELOG `### Breaking`
  entry recorded, migration doc updated.
- For (c) docs-only: error-codes.md#E301 explicitly names which
  readers fire it, the harness's E301 case is replaced with a
  holdings-record fixture, and the bibliographic-fixture case is
  documented (in the manifest's skip_reason or as a deliberate
  unwired entry) as expected behavior rather than a gap.

## Out of scope

- MARC-8 → UTF-8 conversion fidelity (separate concern; the
  bibliographic reader already does that conversion correctly when
  the source is identifiable as MARC-8).
- Encoding-detection heuristics. This bead is only about behavior
  when bytes are *already* in the input stream and fail strict UTF-8
  validation.

## Reference

- Surfaced by bd-0x73.8 coverage harness (sibling).
- Interaction: bd-0x73.10 (validation_level orthogonal axis) — option
  (a) most naturally lives there.
- docs/reference/error-codes.md#E301 — current docs already note the
  divergence but don't resolve the design question.
close_reason: Folded into bd-0x73.10. Resolution: option (a) — strict_marc enforces strict UTF-8 across all readers; structural is lossy everywhere. Holdings moved from 'always strict' to 'strict at strict_marc only' for reader symmetry. PR merged at d374afb; CI green on all platforms.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #164: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/164

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Folded into bd-0x73.10 (in_progress on feat/validation-level-axis). Resolution: option (a) — strict_marc enforces strict UTF-8 across all readers; structural is lossy everywhere. Holdings moves from 'always strict' to 'strict at strict_marc only' for reader symmetry. Will close when bd-0x73.10 lands.

title: Decide retire-vs-generalize-vs-park for the perf-gate workflow
description:
## Context

The `error-handling-perf-gate.yml` workflow added in PR #160 is
scoped to the error-handling detection work currently in flight: its
baseline file (`benches/baselines/error_handling_v080.json`) locks at
v0.8.0 parser code, its 2%/5% thresholds are framed as the cumulative
budget for adding per-byte detection to the hot path, and its trigger
paths cover only `src/`, `src-python/`, the bench file, and the
workflow itself.

When the error-handling epic closes (release-readiness gate landing
and 0.8.1 shipping), this workflow needs a deliberate keep-or-retire
decision rather than drifting into a stale-baseline gate that warns
or fails on regressions the team isn't actively budgeting against.

## Decision

Three options:

a. **Retire.** Delete the workflow, the baseline JSON, and the local
   comparison script. Codspeed remains for general perf awareness.
   Cheapest option; we get the workflow back from git history if a
   future hot-path effort wants it.

b. **Generalize.** Rename to `perf-gate.yml`; broaden trigger paths
   to anything that touches the parser or writer hot paths; refresh
   the baseline at each minor-release tag (procedure documented in
   `docs/contributing/benchmarking.md`); rename the threshold
   description from "error-handling cumulative budget" to a generic
   "release-cycle perf budget." Useful if perf-sensitive work happens
   continuously across releases.

c. **Park (keep but disable).** Leave the workflow file in place but
   set its trigger to `workflow_dispatch` only. Re-enable when the
   next perf-sensitive epic starts. Avoids losing the
   build/comparison logic but keeps it out of the way until needed.

## Recommendation seed

Decide based on what we learn from running the gate during the
error-handling work. If wiring PRs hit the threshold meaningfully
(useful signal, repeated saves), generalize. If they all sail through
under the budget, retire and reach for it again next time.

## Done when

- A decision recorded among (a)/(b)/(c) with rationale linked to the
  cumulative perf delta the error-handling work actually produced.
- For (a): workflow + baseline JSON + comparison script removed in a
  single PR; `docs/contributing/benchmarking.md` updated to remove
  the "cumulative-budget" section.
- For (b): rename + broaden + baseline-refresh procedure shipped;
  thresholds re-justified in the renamed workflow file's header.
- For (c): trigger narrowed to `workflow_dispatch`; banner comment
  in the workflow file naming the next anticipated re-enablement
  trigger.

## Reference

- PR #160 — original landing of the gate.
- `docs/contributing/benchmarking.md` — describes when to use which
  benchmark layer; the gate is the merge-blocking layer.
- The release-readiness gate (sibling) is the natural decision point
  because it closes the epic this workflow was built for.
close_reason: Retired the error-handling perf-gate workflow per option (a). Shipped via #202 (merge 9ed9823). The gate served the bd-0x73 epic (3/19 ≈ 16% catch rate, including one materially-prevented +10.72% happy-path regression on PR #174) and is recoverable from git history if a future hot-path epic needs it.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Scope decision (2026-05-10): IN SCOPE for 0.8.1 — touch at tag-cutting time

The `error-handling-perf-gate.yml` workflow's framing is the
error-handling-detection-work cumulative budget; that budget is
settled once bd-0x73.12 lands and the epic's cumulative perf delta
is known. Three options (retire / generalize / park), each a small
commit.

Pairs with bd-0x73.16 (perf baseline lifecycle) — decide both together
at tag time. Doesn't block bd-0x73.12's gate steps but must precede
the actual tag-cut.

title: Re-anchor or retire perf baseline at v0.8.1 tag
description:
## Context

`benches/baselines/error_handling_v080.json` is described as the
"cumulative perf budget reference for the error-handling detection
work" — pinned to v0.8.0 so each detection-wiring PR's cost is
measured against a stable anchor. The CI same-runner perf gate and
the local `scripts/check_error_handling_perf.py` both consume it.

Once the v0.8.1 milestone closes (every `MarcError` variant wired,
manifest at full coverage, bd-0x73.12 release-readiness gate
asserted), the "cumulative detection-wiring cost" framing has
nothing left to accumulate. The file's purpose-statement and the
v0.8.0 anchor commit reference will be historical artifacts rather
than active gates.

## Decision needed at release-prep time

a. **Re-anchor.** Rename to `error_handling_v081.json` (or
   generalize to `parse_perf_baseline.json`), re-capture
   `parse_10k_clean_strict` against the v0.8.1 tag commit, update
   `purpose` / `captured_at` / `captured_against_commit*` fields.
   The same-runner CI workflow and local script keep working
   unchanged; the JSON just rolls forward.

b. **Retire.** Drop the JSON, the workflow's perf-gate steps, and
   `scripts/check_error_handling_perf.py`. Reasonable if no
   comparable perf-sensitive epic is queued for v0.8.2+ and the
   per-PR perf gate is more friction than value going forward.
   The bench file itself stays; `cargo bench` still runs the
   scenarios, just without a CI threshold.

(c) Hybrid — keep the JSON pinned to v0.8.0 as a long-term
historical reference but drop the cumulative-budget framing — is
plausible but adds a third state and makes the file's role
ambiguous. Avoid unless we want a long-term v0.8.0-vs-now drift
metric for some other reason.

## Trigger

Run this bead **after** bd-0x73.12 (the v0.8.1 release-readiness
gate) has fired its perf check and the cumulative regression vs
the v0.8.0 baseline is recorded in CHANGELOG. That's the moment
the baseline has done the job it was designed for; what to do with
it next is a separate decision from "did we hit the budget?"

## Done when

- A decision recorded (a/b/c) with rationale.
- For (a): JSON renamed and re-captured against the v0.8.1 tag;
  `scripts/check_error_handling_perf.py` default path updated;
  `.github/workflows/*.yml` references the new filename;
  CHANGELOG notes the re-anchor.
- For (b): JSON, perf-gate workflow steps, and the script removed
  in a single PR; CHANGELOG notes the retirement and the v0.8.0→
  v0.8.1 cumulative cost as the historical record.
- For (c): JSON `purpose` rewritten to drop "cumulative budget"
  framing; threshold logic in workflow either removed or scoped
  to a different role.

## Reference

- `benches/baselines/error_handling_v080.json` — current file
- bd-0x73.12 — release-readiness gate (depends on this baseline)
- bd-0x73 — umbrella for the v0.8 error-handling re-evaluation
close_reason: Retired the v0.8.0 baseline JSON per option (b), cohesive with bd-0x73.15(a). Shipped via #202 (merge 9ed9823). Cumulative v0.8.0→v0.8.1 cost on parse_10k_clean_strict was +0.50% — well inside the 2%/5% budget — and is recorded in CHANGELOG [Unreleased].

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Scope decision (2026-05-10): IN SCOPE for 0.8.1 — touch at tag-cutting time

Mechanically required when cutting the v0.8.1 tag. Either re-anchor
(rename `error_handling_v080.json` → `error_handling_v081.json` or
generalize, re-capture against the v0.8.1 commit, update `purpose` /
`captured_at` / `captured_against_commit*` fields), or retire (delete
the JSON + the workflow's perf-gate steps + the local comparison
script). Either action is a small commit.

Pairs with bd-0x73.15 (perf-gate workflow lifecycle) — the two
decisions are typically made together since the workflow consumes the
baseline JSON. Doesn't block bd-0x73.12's gate steps but must precede
the actual tag-cut.

title: Revisit default recovery_mode (strict vs permissive)
description:
## Context

Side question surfaced during bd-0x73.10 (validation_level orthogonal
axis) design discussion. Independent of .10's scope — both can ship
independently. Filed inside the bd-0x73 epic because it's part of the
same "what's the right shape of error handling for 0.8.1 / pre-1.0"
conversation, judged under the same consistency-and-clarity lens.

mrrc's `MARCReader` defaults to `recovery_mode="strict"`: the first
malformed record in a stream raises and aborts iteration. For a user
processing a 50k-record file, this is a surprising failure mode —
one defective record kills the entire batch.

Comparable libraries default to error-tolerant iteration:

- **pymarc** — `MARCReader(file)` catches every exception, stashes on
  `current_exception`, yields None for the bad record, continues.
- **marc4j** — `MarcReader.next()` skips on parse errors by default.
- **libmarc** — similar error-tolerant default.

So mrrc-default-strict is the outlier. New users coming from any of
these libraries (and especially pymarc, since mrrc explicitly targets
pymarc-shape API) hit a surprising failure mode on day one unless
they know to add `permissive=True`.

## The question

Should mrrc's default `recovery_mode` flip from `strict` to something
more forgiving?

Variants:

- **(a) Flip default to `permissive`.** New users get pymarc-default
  iteration shape with no kwarg required. Existing v0.8.0 callers
  relying on default-strict get a surprise — under the pre-1.0 BC
  posture, that's not a cost worth weighing.
- **(b) Flip default to `lenient`.** Different shape: yields the
  record + errors instead of None. Probably the more *useful*
  default but the less *familiar* one (less pymarc-shaped).
- **(c) Keep default as `strict`.** Status quo. Defensible if the
  fail-fast posture is intentional and we want users to opt into
  recovery explicitly. Document the rationale.

## Interaction with bd-0x73.10

`recovery_mode` and `validation_level` are orthogonal (that's .10's
whole point). This decision is settle-able independently: flipping
the default doesn't require .10 to land first, and .10 doesn't need
this resolved to ship.

But there's a case for settling it *near* .10: the same
consistency-and-clarity lens applies. If we're optimizing for "what
does a fresh `MARCReader(file)` produce for a new user," that
question wants to be answered alongside the introduction of
validation_level — not as a separate later surprise.

## Done when

- Decision recorded among (a) / (b) / (c) with rationale.
- For (a) or (b): implementation shipped (Rust builder default +
  Python wrapper default), CHANGELOG line, `error-handling.md`
  updated to show the new default.
- For (c): rationale documented (probably in `error-handling.md`)
  so future maintainers understand the posture.

## Out of scope

- Renaming `recovery_mode`. Separate API question.
- Adding new recovery modes. The three existing modes
  (strict / lenient / permissive) are the design space.
- Changing `validation_level`'s default from `"structural"` —
  that's settled in bd-0x73.10.

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella; fits the "what's right for 0.8.1 error
  handling" lens.
- bd-0x73.10 — sibling: orthogonal but conceptually adjacent. Both
  shape the new-user experience.
- bd-0x73.7 — sibling: pymarc-default parity goal becomes much
  closer to "out of the box" if (a) is chosen.
- Pre-1.0 design posture (memory: feedback_pre_1_0_design_posture)
  — no v0.8.x BC weighting.
close_reason: Landed via PR #201 squash-merge; GH issue #188 auto-closed

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Scope decision (2026-05-10): IN SCOPE for 0.8.1 — decision required

Pre-1.0 is the right window to flip a default if the team is going to;
deferring postpones the decision into a future minor release that
carries stronger BC commitments.

The umbrella's done-when *"a user can drain a 50k-record stream and
identify every record that had any defect, regardless of recovery
mode"* is undermined today by the strict default — users coming from
pymarc / marc4j / libmarc (all error-tolerant by default) hit the
first malformed record and stop iterating unless they know to pass
`permissive=True`.

The decision is binary (flip default to `permissive`, or document the
choice to keep strict). Implementation is small if flipping (one-line
default change + tests + CHANGELOG entry). The act of *deciding* must
land before bd-0x73.12 closes the epic.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/188

title: Improve perf-gate signal-to-noise via median-of-N runs
description:
## Context

Surfaced during bd-0x73.11 (per-record diagnostics) PR review. The
`error-handling-perf-gate` workflow's 2%/5% thresholds are tight
relative to the run-to-run variance we observed on CI runners.

Two CI runs of the same PR commits produced:

- Run A: main 17.057 ms, PR 17.832 ms → +4.54%
- Run B: main 18.948 ms, PR 20.980 ms → +10.72%

Main itself ran ~11% slower in run B (different physical runner, different
load). The PR-vs-main delta swung by 6 percentage points across runs of
identical code. At those magnitudes, the 2% WARN threshold is below
the noise floor and the 5% FAIL threshold is occasionally above it.

For bd-0x73.11 this was worked around by softening the WARN tier to
a non-blocking GitHub annotation (the FAIL tier still blocks). That
preserves visibility but doesn't address the underlying flakiness.

## What to build

Modify `.github/workflows/error-handling-perf-gate.yml` to:

- Run each scenario N times (probably 3-5) for both baseline and PR.
- Take the median or trimmed mean of the runs.
- Use the median for the gate comparison.

This reduces single-run variance proportionally to N. Three runs cuts
the noise band roughly by sqrt(3) ≈ 1.7x; five runs by ~2.2x.

Trade-off: workflow runtime grows linearly with N. Currently the gate
takes ~3 minutes (single benchmark per side); 3 runs would push to
~9 minutes. That's tolerable on a per-PR check.

## Done when

- The gate's CI runtime is reasonable (under ~10 min) given N runs.
- Repeated runs of the same PR commit produce consistent verdicts
  (same WARN/FAIL/OK status across reruns); demonstrate via 3+ reruns
  on a stable PR.
- The 2% WARN threshold can be re-tightened to a meaningful signal
  (deliberate decision based on observed post-fix variance).

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella.
- bd-0x73.11 PR (#174) — surfaced the issue.
- `scripts/check_error_handling_perf.py` — the comparison driver
  (annotation-emitting WARN/FAIL behavior added in .11).
- `.github/workflows/error-handling-perf-gate.yml` — the workflow to
  modify.
close_reason: Moot: bd-0x73.15 retired the perf gate, so the median-of-N signal-to-noise improvement has nothing to improve. The median-of-N pattern itself stays in git history with the workflow file if a future hot-path epic wants to restore it.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Scope decision (2026-05-10): NOT in scope for 0.8.1

Perf-gate signal-to-noise polish (median-of-N runs). Not corrective
error-handling work — the 0.8.1 milestone covers the missing error
handling, not CI infrastructure polish.

There's no active follow-on milestone to attach this bead to. Keeping
the bead open without milestone as backlog. Only worth picking up if
the perf-gate cadence becomes valuable again post-0.8.1 (e.g., a
future hot-path effort with stricter budget). May also fall away
naturally if bd-0x73.15's "retire" option lands.

title: Differential fuzzing across recovery modes
description:
## Context

Sibling of bd-0x73.5 (split out 2026-05-10). bd-0x73.5 originally
listed three options for fuzz expansion:

- Option A: error-classification harness (bd-0x73.5, scoped to A only)
- Option B: differential fuzzing across recovery modes — **this bead**
- Option C: variant-targeted fuzzing — filed separately, no parent

This bead carries Option B, which has a real dependency on bd-0x73.7's
pymarc-default strictness mode plus `last_exception` / `last_chunk`
work — without those accessors, the per-mode contracts can't be fully
checked.

## What to do

`fuzz/fuzz_targets/recovery_mode_consistency.rs` — same input bytes
fed through `strict`, `lenient`, and `permissive` readers; assert
each respects its contract:

- strict: raises an error iff lenient/permissive yield None or partial
  record
- lenient: when it yields a record, the record's `errors` list matches
  what strict would have raised
- permissive: when it yields None, strict raised; when it yields a
  record, strict accepted

This is a structural cross-consistency check across the three modes.
`record.errors` (bd-0x73.11, closed) and `last_exception` /
`last_chunk` (bd-0x73.7, in flight) are both required for the
lenient and permissive contracts to be checkable.

## Done when

- New fuzz target asserts the cross-mode contracts above.
- Nightly `fuzz.yml` runs the new target alongside the bd-0x73.5
  classification target.
- Reproducers (if any) committed to
  `tests/data/fuzz-regressions/recovery_mode_consistency/`.
- `docs/contributing/fuzzing.md` updated to describe the new target.

## References

- bd-0x73 — umbrella.
- bd-0x73.5 — Option A foundation; this bead's harness reuses its
  structural-validation helpers where applicable.
- bd-0x73.7 — provides `last_exception` / `last_chunk` accessors used
  by the permissive-mode contract assertion.
- bd-0x73.11 (closed) — provides `record.errors` used by the lenient-
  mode contract assertion.
close_reason: Landed in #184 (commit d0cbcd9)

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/183

title: InvalidLeader (E002) carries no positional context (record_index / source / byte_offset all None)
description:
## Context

Surfaced during the 2026-04-29 thorough error-handling audit
(bd-0x73 phase 1). The leader-validation error path uses a different
construction helper than the rest of the parser, and that helper
discards the positional-context fields that are the whole point of
the v0.8 structured-error work.

## Source-level evidence

`src/error.rs:1171-1180`:

```rust
pub(crate) fn leader_msg(message: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
    MarcError::InvalidLeader {
        record_index: None,
        byte_offset: None,
        record_byte_offset: None,
        source_name: None,
        message: message.into(),
        bytes_near: None,
    }
}
```

Everything is `None`. Compare to `ParseContext::err_invalid_field`
(and the other `err_*` helpers) which inherit `record_index`,
`byte_offset`, `record_byte_offset`, `source_name` from the live
`ParseContext`.

The caller in `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:189-198` does enrich
`bytes_near` after the fact:

```rust
let leader_offset = ctx.stream_byte_offset;
let leader = Leader::from_bytes(&leader_bytes)
    .map_err(|e| e.with_bytes_near(&leader_bytes, leader_offset))?;
leader
    .validate_for_reading()
    .map_err(|e| e.with_bytes_near(&leader_bytes, leader_offset))?;
```

…but does **not** enrich `record_index`, `byte_offset`,
`record_byte_offset`, or `source_name`. So `InvalidLeader` errors
emit with hex-dump context but missing the metadata most useful for
"where in the harvest is this bad record?"

## Why this is a v0.8 regression

The whole motivation for the variant restructure (bd-qzew) was
"every parse error carries structured positional metadata." E002
shipped without it.

## What to do

Two equivalent paths; B is cleaner:

A. **Enrich at the caller.** Extend the `map_err` chain in
   `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:195-198` to fill in `record_index`,
   `byte_offset`, `record_byte_offset`, `source_name` from `ctx`.
   Add `MarcError::with_position(...)` builder methods if not
   already present.

B. **Pass `&ParseContext` through to `Leader::from_bytes` /
   `validate_for_reading`.** Have those methods construct
   `MarcError::InvalidLeader` with positional context already
   populated, the same way `err_*` helpers do. Removes `leader_msg`
   in favor of a context-aware constructor.

(B) is more invasive but eliminates the asymmetry between leader
and field error construction. (A) is the smaller patch. Either
satisfies the "structured positional context on every parse error"
promise.

## Done when

- `MarcError::InvalidLeader` errors raised from production parsing
  carry `record_index` (where applicable — first record may be 0),
  `byte_offset`, `source_name` matching the rest of the parser's
  errors.
- Tests confirm a malformed leader at record N produces an
  `InvalidLeader` with the right positional metadata, not just
  `bytes_near`.
- `docs/reference/error-handling.md`'s table of "Always-present vs
  may-be-present per variant" updated if the answer changes for
  E002.

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella tracker.
- bd-44z4 (sibling) — five additional unwired error codes,
  including E001/E003/E004 which currently collapse to E002 via
  the same `leader_msg` path.
- bd-0x73.1 (sibling) — four unwired validators.
close_reason: InvalidLeader (E002) positional context fix landed via PR #165

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/151

title: Reader accepts non-ASCII directory tag bytes; writer can't round-trip via UTF-8-lossy conversion
description:
## Context

Surfaced by the bd-0x73.5 error-classification fuzz target on first
60s of execution. The fuzz target asserts that any record the strict-
mode reader yields can round-trip through `MarcWriter` and re-parse to
a record. A reproducer at 106 bytes shows that this isn't the case
for inputs with non-ASCII bytes in the directory's 3-byte tag field.

Even at `ValidationLevel::StrictMarc`, where indicator and subfield-
code byte validity is enforced, the directory-entry tag is parsed via
UTF-8-lossy conversion that silently replaces invalid byte sequences
with U+FFFD. The resulting `Field::tag` is a valid Rust string but
contains characters that re-encode to multiple UTF-8 bytes.
`MarcWriter` then writes the 3-character string back as more than 3
bytes, overflowing the directory entry's tag field into the length
field — and the second parse fires `DirectoryInvalid` (E101) on the
synthesized non-digit length bytes.

## Reproducer

Input bytes (106): leader claiming `record_length=106`, `data_base
_address=49`, with a directory entry whose tag bytes are `0x31 0xCC
0x30` (ASCII '1', invalid UTF-8 lead byte 0xCC, ASCII '0'). Saved
locally at `fuzz/artifacts/error_classification/crash-31d768fb...`
(gitignored). The fix PR should commit a minimized copy under
`tests/data/fuzz-regressions/error_classification/non-ascii-tag-
roundtrip.mrc` per the fuzzing.md playbook.

## Root cause

Two interlocking issues:

1. The reader accepts non-ASCII bytes in directory tags at every
   validation level, converting via lossy UTF-8 decoding. The
   documented codec specifies tags as 3 ASCII characters; this is a
   wiring gap (similar in shape to the indicator/subfield-code gaps
   the bd-0x73 epic addressed for E201/E202).
2. The writer doesn't validate that field tags are 3 ASCII bytes
   before emitting. Even if (1) were fixed downstream, a record
   constructed in code with a non-ASCII tag would still emit
   un-parseable bytes.

## Fix scope (decide during implementation)

- Reject non-ASCII tag bytes at `StrictMarc` and surface as a
  documented variant (probably extend `DirectoryInvalid` E101 — same
  variant currently used for non-digit length and missing terminator
  — so the fixture-driven coverage harness picks it up automatically).
- At `Structural`, decide: also reject (consistent with control-fields
  having tags 001-009 = ASCII), or keep lossy and document the writer
  refusal as the boundary check.
- Writer-side: `write_record` should return
  `MarcError::WriterError` (or a more specific variant) when any field
  tag contains non-ASCII bytes.

Per the bd-0x73 epic: prefer wiring at parse time so the byte-level
diagnostic surfaces with positional context, rather than only at
write time.

## Done when

- Reader rejects non-ASCII directory tag bytes at `StrictMarc`,
  firing the appropriate variant with `record_index`, `byte_offset`,
  `record_byte_offset`, `field_tag` populated.
- Writer rejects records with non-ASCII tags before emitting.
- Reproducer fixture committed under
  `tests/data/fuzz-regressions/error_classification/`.
- `tests/fuzz_regressions.rs` (created in this fix PR if it doesn't
  yet exist) auto-discovers and runs the fixture; the test passes.
- `tests/error_coverage.toml` extended with a case for the new
  trigger if a new variant or new E-code is introduced; existing
  E101 case widened if reusing that variant.

## Out of scope

- Other Unicode-in-tag attack surfaces (e.g., zero-width characters
  that ARE valid ASCII per Rust's `is_ascii` but aren't allowed by
  the codec). The fix should accept anything `b.is_ascii()`-true; if
  further restriction (e.g., printable ASCII alphanumeric) is wanted,
  file separately.

## References

- bd-0x73.5 (closed) — the fuzz target that surfaced this finding.
- `fuzz/fuzz_targets/error_classification.rs` — the asserting harness.
- `docs/contributing/fuzzing.md` — triage playbook (steps 4-7).
close_reason: Landed in #181 (commit d46a74c)

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/180

title: Error-handling quality review — wiring completeness, caller handling, test/fuzz coverage
description:
## Context

bd-0x73.12 (release-readiness gate) is a mechanical completeness check: it asserts that every documented error code has at least one non-test call site, that the manifest matches the docs, that the perf budget held, and that internal sources of truth agree with each other. That's the *minimum bar* — "we shipped what we said we'd ship."

It does not ask the harder questions that pre-1.0 quality requires. This bead asks them, before .12 fires.

## Four axes of review

1. **Wiring completeness.** For each `MarcError` variant, is it fired in *every* place it should be — not just somewhere? The 04-29 audit found six unwired codes the first time it ran. That audit's discovery method was the rigorous part; bd-0x73.12 only re-runs the verification, assuming the discovery is settled. This bead re-runs the discovery itself, asking: are there call sites that should construct an error variant but currently swallow / convert / mask the condition instead?

2. **Caller-side handling.** When an error fires, does the calling code do the right thing? Are recovery paths in `try_recover_record` appropriate for each variant? Does positional context (`field_tag`, `byte_offset`, `record_control_number`) propagate intact through the PyO3 boundary? Does `permissive=True`'s swallow path expose enough information via `current_exception` / `current_chunk`? Are downstream interpretations of `record.errors` correct?

3. **Test coverage beyond the manifest.** The error-coverage manifest declares which codes are wired. That's a presence check, not a quality check. For each firing site: are there integration tests with realistic inputs? Golden-file tests over corpora? Regression tests for past bugs in this path? Negative tests asserting that *non*-error inputs do not fire the error?

4. **Fuzz coverage.** The differential recovery-mode fuzz target covers a slice. For each variant: does any fuzz target plausibly exercise this error path? Where it doesn't, is the gap acceptable (errors that require specific structural conditions hard to generate randomly) or should we add a targeted fuzz harness?

## Method

For each of the 17 documented error codes (E001–E007, E099, E101, E105, E106, E201, E202, E301, E401, E402, E404):

- Read the code's documented semantics in `docs/reference/error-codes.md` and `docs/reference/error-handling.md`.
- Inspect `src/`, `src-python/`, and the validators in `src/validation.rs` to identify every location where this error *should* fire under those semantics. Document each.
- Cross-reference with where it *does* fire (the manifest's wired sites). Flag any "should fire here, doesn't" gaps.
- Read the caller-side code paths for each firing site. Assess whether the handling is thoughtful — recovery, context propagation, downstream surfacing.
- Map firing sites to tests. Per-site tests? Integration tests? Negative tests?
- Map firing sites to fuzz coverage. Which targets exercise this path? Where uncovered, document and judge.

Output per code: a category from {OK, GAP, DEFER}.

## Output artifacts

- A per-code findings document (in-PR markdown or a long comment on this bead).
- One or more child beads or PRs implementing each GAP item.
- Updates to the manifest, docs, tests, and fuzz harnesses as appropriate.

## Done when

- All 17 codes reviewed with documented findings.
- Every GAP item either closed (fix landed) or DEFERred with explicit reason and a follow-up bead in 0.8.2 scope.
- A comment on this bead summarizes outcomes (codes reviewed, gaps found, gaps fixed, gaps deferred).
- bd-0x73.12 can then run cleanly against the upgraded spec.

## Why before .12, not after

Running .12 first would mean re-running it after every gap fix. Running the quality review first means .12 verifies the upgraded spec once, cleanly. Also: a passing .12 against an incomplete spec is false-comfort signal; better to know the spec is good first.

## Reference

- bd-0x73.12 — release-readiness gate (consumes the upgraded spec this bead produces; blocked on this bead).
- bd-rjl1 — per-trigger E404 audit (predecessor methodology, scoped to one code).
- 2026-04-29 audit (the original `rg` enumeration) — the rigorous discovery method this bead re-applies systematically across all variants.
- Companion bead: 0.8.1 milestone-wide quality review (different scope, different lens, can run in parallel).
close_reason: All children complete and merged. The error-handling quality review covered wiring completeness, caller handling, and test/fuzz coverage across E001-E402; per-finding follow-ups (.1-.11) all landed via their own PRs. Final child bd-0x73.21.10 (auth/holdings Python API docs + .pyi correction) merged in #229. Remaining v0.8.1 error-handling work is the one-time release-readiness gate, tracked separately at bd-0x73.12.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
# bd-0x73.21 — Error-handling quality review (Phase 1 findings)

Scratch doc. Final form posts as a bd-0x73.21 comment when complete.

## Method

For each of the 17 documented error codes, four axes:

1. **Wiring completeness** — every place it SHOULD fire vs every place it DOES fire.
2. **Caller-side handling** — recovery paths, context propagation, downstream surfacing.
3. **Test coverage** beyond the manifest's presence check — integration, golden-file, negative.
4. **Fuzz coverage** — which targets exercise this path; gaps acceptable or fixable.

Category per code: **OK** / **GAP** / **DEFER**.

## Codes

(written as I walk them; sections appended below)

---

### E001 — RecordLengthInvalid

**Documented semantics** (`docs/reference/error-codes.md:69`): leader bytes 0–4 not five ASCII digits, OR record_length < 24. **Applies to:** Bibliographic / Authority / Holdings readers.

**Variant** (`src/error.rs:109`). Context fields: `record_index`, `byte_offset`, `source_name`, `found`, `expected`, `bytes_near`.

**Production firing sites:**

1. `src/leader.rs:212` — `Leader::from_bytes`, non-digit bytes 0-4 (via `parse_digits`).
2. `src/leader.rs:294` — `validate_for_reading`, `record_length < 24`.

Both construct the error with `record_index: None, byte_offset: None, source_name: None`. Callers are expected to enrich.

**Caller enrichment status:**

- ✅ `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:211, 215` — wraps both via `e.with_position(ctx).with_bytes_near(...)`. Full context surfaces.
- ❌ `src/marcxml.rs:511` — bare `?` propagation. No record_index, byte_offset, source_name.
- ❌ `src/json.rs:139` — same.
- ❌ `src/marcjson.rs:117` — same.

**GAPs:**

1. **Wiring (caller enrichment):** MARCXML, JSON, and marcjson readers propagate E001 without positional context. `byte_offset` may not be meaningful for XML/JSON, but `record_index` and `source_name` are. Three call sites need a `with_position`-equivalent or explicit field population. **Severity:** Moderate — users hitting bad leaders in MARCXML streams get useless errors for multi-record files.
2. **Docs vs code:** Docs say E001 "Applies to: Bibliographic, Authority, Holdings readers" (i.e., the binary readers). Code fires it from MARCXML, JSON, marcjson too. Either the docs need a broader "Applies to" claim OR the non-binary readers should fire a different code. **Severity:** Low — code is more correct than docs claim.
3. **Helper dead code:** `ParseContext::err_record_length_invalid` at `src/iso2709.rs:191` is defined but never called (only the inline construction at leader.rs is used). Either delete the helper or refactor leader.rs/iso2709_skeleton.rs to use it for consistency. **Severity:** Cosmetic.
4. **Manifest coverage:** Only one trigger fixture (non-digit bytes 0-4). Missing:
   - `record_length < 24` trigger (path 2 via `validate_for_reading`).
   - MARCXML / JSON / marcjson triggers (if those paths stay in scope per GAP #2).
   **Severity:** Moderate — per-trigger manifest coverage is what bd-rjl1 set up; E001 should match.

**Test coverage beyond manifest:** `src/leader.rs:540, 553, 566, 578, 590` unit tests cover both firing paths in `from_bytes` and `validate_for_reading`. No integration tests exercising E001 from MARCXML/JSON/marcjson paths (would be needed if GAP #2 lands as "broaden scope").

**Fuzz coverage:** `fuzz/fuzz_targets/parse_record.rs` and `error_classification.rs` exercise random-byte inputs → leader malformations are reachable. Likely well-covered by random byte fuzzing.

**Category: GAP** — Four concrete deficiencies; #1 and #4 are the most consequential. Recommend addressing in Phase 2.

---

### E002 — InvalidLeader

**Documented semantics** (`docs/reference/error-codes.md:87`): two shapes — (1) structural (always-fires byte-level malformation), (2) MARC 21 semantic (fires only at `validation_level="strict_marc"`).

**Variant** (`src/error.rs:93`). Context fields: `record_index`, `byte_offset`, `record_byte_offset`, `source_name`, `message`, `bytes_near`.

**Production firing sites:**

- `src/leader.rs:205, 227, 232, 236, 242` — `Leader::from_bytes` structural checks (length too short, non-digit indicator/subfield-code count).
- `src/record_validation.rs:27, 38, 48, 58, 68, 76, 84, 92, 102, 112, 122` — `RecordStructureValidator::validate_leader` semantic checks (11 distinct rules across record_status, record_type, bibliographic_level, control_record_type, encoding_level, indicator_count == 2, subfield_code_count == 2, length_of_length_field, length_of_starting_position, length_of_implementation_defined, undefined).

All construct via `MarcError::leader_msg(...)` which yields the variant with `message` populated and all positional context as `None`.

**Caller enrichment status:**

- ✅ `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:211-218, 227-235` — both `Leader::from_bytes` errors and `validate_leader` errors are enriched via `with_position(ctx).with_bytes_near(...)`. Full context surfaces.
- ❌ Same MARCXML/JSON/marcjson gap as E001 — `Leader::from_bytes` can fire E002 via these paths without enrichment.
- ⚠️ `RecordStructureValidator::validate_record` at `src/record_validation.rs:138` calls `validate_leader`. Need to check who calls `validate_record` and whether they enrich.

**Test coverage beyond manifest:** Comprehensive unit coverage in `src/record_validation.rs:279-344` (~10 test cases, one per semantic rule). Strong.

**Fuzz coverage:** `parse_record.rs` exercises random byte leaders → both shapes plausibly reachable.

**GAPs:**

1. **Per-trigger manifest coverage:** Manifest has 2 entries (structural=indicator_count, semantic=record_status). The semantic validator has 11 distinct rules; only 1 is in the manifest. Per the per-trigger principle established in bd-rjl1 for E404, each semantic rule deserves a manifest entry. **Severity:** Moderate (per-trigger coverage matters for catching regression where one rule loses wiring).
2. **MARCXML/JSON/marcjson caller enrichment:** Same as E001 — `Leader::from_bytes` fires E002 from non-binary paths without positional context. **Severity:** Moderate.
3. **`validate_record` caller path:** `validate_record` (which calls `validate_leader`) — find callers, verify enrichment. **Severity:** TBD (needs investigation).

**Category: GAP** — Same shape as E001 but with stronger baseline. Per-trigger expansion is the main deficiency.

---

### E003 — BaseAddressInvalid

**Production firing sites:**
- `src/leader.rs:246` — from_bytes, non-digit bytes 12-16.
- `src/leader.rs:304` — validate_for_reading, `data_base_address < 24`.

**Helper:** `ParseContext::err_base_address_invalid` defined at `src/iso2709.rs:216`, **DEAD** (never called).

**Manifest:** 1 entry (non-digit case via from_bytes). Missing trigger: `data_base_address < 24` from validate_for_reading.

**Caller enrichment:** Same as E001 — iso2709_skeleton enriches, MARCXML/JSON/marcjson don't.

**Test coverage:** `src/leader.rs:555, 592` — unit tests cover both paths.

**Fuzz coverage:** Random leader bytes → reachable.

**Category: GAP** — Mirrors E001 (helper dead, MARCXML/JSON enrichment gap, per-trigger manifest gap).

---

### E004 — BaseAddressNotFound

**Production firing sites:**
- `src/leader.rs:315` — validate_for_reading, `data_base_address > record_length`.

**Helper:** `ParseContext::err_base_address_not_found` defined at `src/iso2709.rs:231`, **DEAD**.

**Manifest:** 1 entry, matches the single production path. ✅

**Caller enrichment:** `validate_for_reading` is only called from `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:215` which enriches. ✅ — MARCXML/JSON readers do not call `validate_for_reading`, so this path is binary-only.

**Test coverage:** `src/leader.rs:568` — unit test.

**Fuzz coverage:** Reachable.

**Category: OK-with-cosmetic-GAP** — Single production path, manifest match, caller enrichment fine. Only deficiency is the dead helper.

---

### E005 — TruncatedRecord

**Production firing sites (helper used — good pattern):**
- `src/iso2709.rs:461` — `read_record_data` strict-mode truncation in regular reader.
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:262` — `read_record_data` strict-mode truncation in skeleton path.
- `src/recovery.rs:158` — `try_recover_record` zero-bytes-read case.

**Helper:** `ParseContext::err_truncated_record` **LIVE** (3 call sites). ✅

**Manifest:** 1 entry — basic short-stream case. Missing: `try_recover_record` empty-read case (recovery.rs:158). **Per-trigger gap.**

**Caller enrichment:** Helper auto-fills positional context from ctx. ✅

**Test coverage:** Solid — `src/iso2709.rs:945-963` and similar tests in iso2709_skeleton.

**Fuzz coverage:** Reachable via `parse_record.rs` random truncation.

**Recovery handling:** Documented to salvage in lenient. Python wrapper handling was recently fixed (bd-vwlp, PR #196). ✅

**Category: GAP (small)** — Helper-driven construction is the model to emulate. Only deficiency is per-trigger manifest gap for the recovery-mode empty-read case.

---

### E006 — EndOfRecordNotFound

**Production firing sites:**
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:290` — only site, via `err_end_of_record_not_found` helper. ✅

**Helper:** **LIVE** (1 caller).

**Manifest:** 1 entry, matches the single production path. ✅

**Caller enrichment:** Helper auto-fills. ✅

**Test coverage:** Tested via fixture-driven harness + iso2709_skeleton unit tests.

**Fuzz coverage:** `parse_record.rs` random RECORD_TERMINATOR mutations reachable.

**Category: OK** — Clean: one path, one manifest entry, helper used, caller enrichment works.

---

### E007 — IoError

**Documented semantics:** Raised as Python's built-in `OSError` (per docs, intentional pymarc-compat decision).

**Production firing:** **Implicit via `impl From<std::io::Error> for MarcError`** at `src/error.rs:1449`. Wherever `?` is used on a `Result<_, io::Error>`, an `MarcError::IoError` is constructed with `record_index: None, byte_offset: None, source_name: None`. The From impl explicitly says "without surrounding context."

**Helper:** `ParseContext::err_io_error` defined at `src/iso2709.rs:373`, **DEAD** (never called).

**Manifest:** 1 entry — generic "reader source returns io::Error." `expected_context: []` — manifest explicitly does NOT expect positional context (because From impl strips it).

**GAPs:**

1. **Context propagation:** Every `?` on an `io::Error` strips positional context (`record_index`, `byte_offset`, `source_name`). For a multi-record stream this is severe — users see "IO error" with no idea which record was being parsed when the failure happened. Two ways to fix:
   - (a) Add an explicit "with_position via ParseContext" wrapper at io-fallible call sites, replacing `?` with `.map_err(|e| ctx.err_io_error(e))`. Use the LIVE helper. Eliminates the dead-helper issue too.
   - (b) Document this as a known limitation; OSError pymarc-compat doesn't carry positional context anyway. **Cheaper, less complete.**
2. **Python integration test:** bd-1a5a (closed) added one test for this path; verify it asserts that the raised OSError survives the FFI boundary correctly.

**Test coverage:** Manifest fixture. The bd-1a5a Python test (PR #200) adds end-to-end coverage.

**Fuzz coverage:** Hard to fuzz an external IO error without a mock; not pursued. Acceptable gap.

**Category: GAP** — Implicit From impl strips positional context system-wide. Fix is consistent with the E001/E003 pattern of "use the helper at firing sites." Could also defer to 0.8.2 if pymarc-compat OSError is judged sufficient.

---

### E099 — FatalReaderError

**Production firing sites:**
- `src/recovery.rs:81` — only production site. `RecoveryCap::note` exceeds `max_errors`.

(Other references in src/reader.rs, src/holdings_reader.rs, src/authority_reader.rs are all test code matching against the variant.)

**Manifest:** 1 entry, matches the single production path. ✅

**Caller enrichment:** Construction inline at recovery.rs:81 populates `cap`, `errors_seen`, and `record_index` directly from RecoveryCap state. ✅

**Python integration:**
- `mrrc.FatalReaderError` class in `mrrc/exceptions.py:487`. ✅
- `max_errors` kwarg on `MARCReader` (bd-al66, PR #199). ✅
- Tests in `tests/python/test_max_errors.py` cover cap-trip, `max_errors=0` disables cap, and exhausted-state semantics. ✅
- Pymarc parity test in `test_pymarc_exception_parity.py` includes E099. ✅

**Test coverage:** Comprehensive. Rust tests in reader.rs/holdings_reader.rs/authority_reader.rs for all three reader types, Python tests in test_max_errors.py.

**Fuzz coverage:** Not specifically targeted. E099 requires specific malformed-record-density that random fuzzing won't naturally produce. **Acceptable gap** — could add a targeted "fuzz a stream of N malformed records, assert cap trips at N+1" harness in 0.8.2 but low ROI.

**Category: OK** — Clean wiring, full Python integration, strong test coverage. Recommend no Phase 2 action.

---

### E101 — DirectoryInvalid

**Production firing sites (all via helper):**
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:324` — directory not 12-byte multiple.
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:344` — tag bytes not 3 ASCII.
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:367` — directory length field non-digit.
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:383` — directory start position field non-digit.

**Helper:** `ParseContext::err_directory_invalid` **LIVE** (4 call sites). ✅

**Manifest:** 2 entries (no-terminator, non-digit-length). **Per-trigger gap:** 4 production paths, 2 manifest. Missing: bad-tag-bytes path, non-digit-start path.

**Caller enrichment:** Helper populates context from ctx. ✅

**Test coverage:** Unit coverage in iso2709_skeleton tests. Per bd-44z4 PR γ (PR #168), the directory walker was specifically refactored for E101 — should have decent regression coverage.

**Fuzz coverage:** `parse_record.rs` random byte mutations would reach directory paths.

**Category: GAP (small)** — Per-trigger manifest expansion (2 more entries).

---

### E105 — FieldNotFound

**Production firing sites:**
- `src/record.rs:221` — `Record::get_field_or_err` only. ✅ (per bd-44z4 PR δ, PR #169)

**Helper:** None defined (no helper for accessor errors — appropriate).

**Manifest:** 1 entry — accessor pattern via `get_field_or_err("999")`. ✅

**Caller enrichment:** Accessor populates `field_tag` directly. No `record_index` / `byte_offset` (correctly per docs — "Never populates: byte_offset"). ✅

**Test coverage:** Manifest fixture + unit tests in record.rs.

**Fuzz coverage:** N/A — accessor errors, not parse errors.

**GAPs:**

1. **Docs claim vs code reality:** Docs say "Applies to: All record types." Code only implements `get_field_or_err` on `Record` (bibliographic). `AuthorityRecord` and `HoldingsRecord` have no `_or_err` accessor — they only expose `get_field() -> Option<&Field>`. Three options:
   - (a) Add `get_field_or_err` to `AuthorityRecord` and `HoldingsRecord` for parity.
   - (b) Narrow docs claim to "Applies to: Record (bibliographic)."
   - (c) Decide accessor errors aren't worth the API surface on Authority/Holdings and leave as-is.
   **Severity:** Moderate (pre-1.0 consistency: one rule should apply to all record types per [project posture](feedback_pre_1_0_design_posture)).

**Category: GAP** — Single deficiency (cross-record-type consistency).

---

### E106 — InvalidField

**Production firing sites (all via helper):**
- `src/iso2709.rs:796` — parse_data_field, "Data field too short (needs indicators)".
- `src/iso2709.rs:863` — parse_subfields, "Expected subfield delimiter".
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:401` — skeleton path equivalent.
- `src/holdings_reader.rs:198, 239` — holdings-specific field length errors.
- `src/authority_reader.rs:203, 255` — authority-specific.
- `src/recovery.rs:218` — recovery path.

**Helper:** `ParseContext::err_invalid_field` **LIVE** (7+ call sites across multiple modules). ✅ Best-used helper in the codebase.

**Manifest:** 1 entry (field length past data area). **Per-trigger gap (major):** 7+ distinct production paths, 1 manifest entry. Each path has a different error message but the same variant. The manifest's `expected_context` should also vary by path (e.g., authority/holdings paths populate different fields).

**Caller enrichment:** Helper handles. ✅

**Test coverage:** Strong via unit tests across the modules using the helper.

**Fuzz coverage:** `parse_record.rs` random mutations reach many of these paths.

**Category: GAP** — Per-trigger manifest expansion (need ~6 more entries to cover the distinct paths). Same shape as the E404 audit that bd-rjl1 did for the writer.

---

### E201 — InvalidIndicator

**Production firing sites (all via helper):**
- `src/iso2709.rs:806` — indicator 1 not digit/space (byte-level).
- `src/iso2709.rs:809` — indicator 2 not digit/space (byte-level).
- `src/iso2709.rs:815` — indicator 1 fails per-tag MARC 21 rule.
- `src/iso2709.rs:818` — indicator 2 fails per-tag MARC 21 rule.

**Gating:** All four wrapped in `if config.indicator == IndicatorMode::Strict` (i.e., `validation_level="strict_marc"`). At `structural` (default), indicators are accepted as-is. ✅

**Helper:** `ParseContext::err_invalid_indicator` **LIVE** (4 call sites). ✅

**Manifest:** 2 entries (one byte-level, one per-tag-rule). Per-trigger coverage matches both kinds of failure. ✅

**Caller enrichment:** Helper handles. ✅

**Test coverage:** Strong — PR #170 (bd-44z4-derived) specifically wired this with regression tests for both byte-level and per-tag cases.

**Fuzz coverage:** `parse_record.rs` random byte mutations reach indicator positions. The recovery-mode-consistency fuzz target also exercises E201.

**Category: OK** — Clean wiring, comprehensive coverage. The 04-29 audit's flagship "this should fire when blog-post-example fires" code, now working as documented. No Phase 2 action recommended.

---

### E202 — BadSubfieldCode

**Production firing sites (via helper):**
- `src/iso2709.rs:883` — subfield code byte not `is_ascii_graphic()`.

**Gating:** Inside `if config.subfield_code == SubfieldCodeMode::Strict` (i.e., `validation_level="strict_marc"`). ✅

**Helper:** `ParseContext::err_bad_subfield_code` **LIVE** (1 call site). ✅

**Manifest:** 1 entry — non-printable byte (0x00). Matches the single production path. ✅

**Caller enrichment:** Helper handles. ✅

**Test coverage:** PR #171 (bd-44z4-derived) wired this with regression tests.

**Fuzz coverage:** Reachable via random subfield-code byte mutations.

**Category: OK** — Single clean path. The 04-29 audit's other flagship, now working as documented.

---

### E301 — EncodingError

**Production firing sites (all via `err_encoding` helper):**
- `src/iso2709.rs:898` — subfield value invalid UTF-8 at `Utf8DecodeMode::Strict`.
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:110` — control field invalid UTF-8 (skeleton path).
- `src/holdings_reader.rs:228` — holdings control field invalid UTF-8.
- `src/authority_reader.rs:242` — authority control field invalid UTF-8.

**Helper:** `ParseContext::err_encoding` **LIVE** (4 call sites). Populates `record_index`, `byte_offset`, `source_name`, `record_control_number`, `field_tag`, `message`, `bytes_near`. ✅

**Gating:** All four wrapped in `Utf8DecodeMode::Strict` (mapped from `validation_level="strict_marc"`). At `Lossy` (default `structural`), `String::from_utf8_lossy` substitutes U+FFFD. ✅

**Manifest:** 1 entry — subfield-value UTF-8 case. **Per-trigger gap:** 4 production paths, 1 manifest entry. Missing: control field (3 reader types — bibliographic skeleton, holdings, authority).

**Caller enrichment:** Helper handles. ✅

**Test coverage:** Manifest fixture covers the subfield case. Unit tests in iso2709_skeleton, holdings, authority verify their respective UTF-8 paths fire.

**Fuzz coverage:** `parse_record.rs` random byte mutations reliably produce invalid UTF-8 sequences in subfield/control field positions. Well-covered.

**Category: GAP (small)** — Per-trigger manifest expansion (3 more entries for the control field paths).

---

### E401 — XmlError

**Production firing sites (all via `err_xml` helper):**
- `src/marcxml.rs:157, 230, 252, 278, 301, 454, 458, 497` — 8 call sites covering deserialize, element-state errors, character-reference resolution, attribute parsing.

**Helper:** `ParseContext::err_xml` **LIVE** (8 call sites). ✅ Wraps any `std::error::Error + Send + Sync + 'static` (handles both `quick_xml::Error` and `quick_xml::DeError`).

**Manifest:** 1 entry — close-tag mismatch. **Per-trigger gap:** 8 production paths, 1 manifest entry.

**Caller enrichment:** Helper populates `record_index` and `source_name`. `byte_offset: None` (deliberate — XML byte offsets don't map cleanly to mrrc's `stream_byte_offset` semantics).

**Test coverage:** Manifest fixture + marcxml.rs unit tests.

**Fuzz coverage:** No dedicated MARCXML fuzz target (existing fuzz targets are ISO 2709). Possible 0.8.2 follow-up.

**Category: GAP** — Per-trigger manifest expansion to cover more of the 8 distinct XML parse failure modes.

---

### E402 — JsonError

**Production firing sites:** **NONE.** The `err_json` helper at `src/iso2709.rs:400` is **DEAD**.

**How E402 actually fires:** **Unclear.** `src/marcjson.rs` and `src/json.rs` use `MarcError::invalid_field_msg(...)` (which constructs **E106 InvalidField**, not E402) for all their JSON-parsing errors. No `From<serde_json::Error>` impl exists. No call site constructs `MarcError::JsonError`.

**Manifest:** Claims 1 entry with `trigger_kind = "parse_marcjson"` and fixture `e402_malformed_marcjson.json`. If the harness exercises this fixture, what code actually fires? Almost certainly E106, not E402. **The manifest entry is wrong or the wiring is missing.**

**GAPs (significant):**

1. **Wiring missing:** `marcjson.rs` should fire E402 when serde_json deserialization fails, not collapse to E106. Either:
   - (a) Refactor `marcjson_to_record` to use `ctx.err_json(serde_json::Error)` (need to thread ParseContext through), OR
   - (b) Add `From<serde_json::Error> for MarcError` like the IoError pattern (simpler but loses positional context), OR
   - (c) Repurpose E402 as deprecated and re-categorize the JSON-shape errors as E106. Update docs.
2. **Manifest probably lying:** If the fixture actually fires E106, the manifest entry should report E106 — not E402. Either fix the wiring (option a or b above) or fix the manifest.
3. **bd-rjl1's per-trigger audit didn't catch this** because it was scoped to the WriterError variant, not all writer-adjacent codes.

**Test coverage:** Need to run the manifest harness against this fixture to see what code actually surfaces. (Phase 2 task.)

**Fuzz coverage:** No dedicated marcjson fuzz target.

**Category: GAP (significant)** — This is the strongest E2-04-29-audit-style finding this review has produced. Documented infrastructure, dead helper, wrong manifest entry, no production firing path. Behaves like the original E201/E202 wiring gap.

---

### E404 — WriterError

**Production firing sites:**
- `src/iso2709.rs:578` — record-too-large for ISO 2709 (length overflow).
- `src/iso2709.rs:612` — base-address overflow.
- `src/iso2709.rs:621` — additional ISO 2709 serialization constraint.
- `src/writer.rs:139` — writer reuse after finish.
- `src/writer.rs:223` — record_length u32 conversion failure.
- `src/writer.rs:229` — base_address u32 conversion failure.

**Helper:** No `err_writer` helper. All sites construct `MarcError::WriterError { ... }` directly with positional context (`record_index`, `byte_offset` as None — writer side doesn't have a parse position).

**Manifest:** 3 entries — record too large, non-ASCII tag, writer reuse. Per-trigger expanded by bd-rjl1 (PR #198). **Per-trigger gap remaining:** 6 production sites, 3 entries. Missing: base-address overflow (iso2709.rs:612), additional ISO 2709 constraint (iso2709.rs:621), record_length u32 conversion (writer.rs:223), base_address u32 conversion (writer.rs:229). Note the iso2709.rs sites overlap conceptually with "record too large" — may not need separate entries depending on interpretation.

**Caller enrichment:** Inline construction at each site. `record_index` and `byte_offset` left None — appropriate for writer-side errors.

**Test coverage:** Manifest covers 3 of 6 sites. The writer unit tests in writer.rs cover all 6 directly.

**Fuzz coverage:** `roundtrip_binary.rs` exercises writer paths; should reach E404 on pathological inputs.

**GAPs:**

1. **Per-trigger manifest expansion:** Add manifest entries for the remaining 3 sites OR consolidate where conceptually duplicate.
2. **Slug "record_too_large_for_iso2709" misrepresents the variant scope:** The variant fires for several distinct conditions, only one of which is "too large." Either rename the slug (breaking change) or split into multiple variants. **Defer to 0.9** — slug renames are user-visible.

**Category: GAP** — Per-trigger gap is real but bd-rjl1 made meaningful progress. Slug-rename consideration deferred.

---

## Phase 1 synthesis

### Category tally

| Category | Count | Codes |
|---|---|---|
| OK | 5 | E004, E006, E099, E201, E202 |
| GAP (small / cosmetic) | 3 | E005, E101, E301 |
| GAP | 7 | E001, E002, E003, E105, E106, E007, E401, E404 |
| GAP (significant) | 1 | E402 |
| **Total** | 16 / 17 with concrete deficiencies | |

(E001-E007 counted is 7 codes; E099 is 1; E101+E105+E106 is 3; E201+E202 is 2; E301 is 1; E401+E402+E404 is 3 → 17 total.)

The 04-29 audit's flagship codes (E201, E202) are now clean. The new audit surface is a different shape: per-trigger manifest coverage, dead helpers, caller-enrichment asymmetry across reader types, and one previously-undetected wiring gap (E402).

### Cross-cutting findings

1. **Dead error-construction helpers (5 of them).** `err_record_length_invalid`, `err_base_address_invalid`, `err_base_address_not_found`, `err_io_error`, `err_json` are defined in `ParseContext` but never called. The construction-via-inline-`MarcError { ... }` pattern at firing sites bypasses them and consequently doesn't auto-populate positional context.

2. **MARCXML / JSON / marcjson readers don't enrich leader errors.** Three call sites (`marcxml.rs:511`, `json.rs:139`, `marcjson.rs:117`) use bare `?` propagation on `Leader::from_bytes`. Errors E001/E002/E003 fire from these paths with `record_index = None`, `source_name = None`. The ISO 2709 path enriches correctly via `with_position(ctx)`.

3. **Per-trigger manifest coverage is undersized.** bd-rjl1 established the per-trigger principle for E404. The same principle applies to E001 (2 paths, 1 entry), E002 (13 paths, 2 entries), E003 (2 paths, 1 entry), E005 (3 paths, 1 entry), E101 (4 paths, 2 entries), E106 (7+ paths, 1 entry), E301 (4 paths, 1 entry), E401 (8 paths, 1 entry), E404 (6 paths, 3 entries). Roughly **25-30 new manifest fixtures** would close the gap.

4. **E105 not implemented on AuthorityRecord / HoldingsRecord.** Docs say "Applies to: All record types." Code only implements `get_field_or_err` on bibliographic `Record`. Pre-1.0 consistency wants one rule across all three readers.

5. **E007 strips positional context system-wide.** `impl From<std::io::Error> for MarcError` constructs IoError with all positional fields `None`. Wherever `?` is used on an `io::Error`, the user sees "IO error" with no record_index / byte_offset / source.

6. **E402 likely has zero production firing paths.** `err_json` helper is dead; `marcjson.rs` uses `invalid_field_msg` (E106) for JSON-shape errors. The manifest entry claims E402 fires from `e402_malformed_marcjson.json` but the harness probably actually observes E106. This is the closest analog to the original 04-29 E201/E202 wiring gap that this review found.

### Recommended Phase 2 bundles

**Bundle C: E402 wiring fix** — Priority 1.
- Run the manifest harness against `e402_malformed_marcjson.json`, confirm whether it actually fires E402 or E106.
- Decision: wire E402 properly (thread ParseContext through marcjson.rs error paths, use `err_json`) OR repurpose as deprecated and re-categorize as E106 with manifest correction.

**Bundle B: MARCXML / JSON / marcjson leader-error enrichment** — Priority 2.
- Add `with_position`-equivalent (or explicit field population for `record_index`/`source_name`) at `marcxml.rs:511`, `json.rs:139`, `marcjson.rs:117`.
- Small, localized, low risk.

**Bundle E: Per-trigger manifest expansion** — Priority 3 (biggest payoff for "is the spec complete?").
- Add ~25-30 new manifest fixtures + entries across E001/E002/E003/E005/E101/E106/E301/E401/E404.
- Update bd-0x73.12 step 2 to assert per-trigger coverage as a release-gate condition.

**Bundle A: Dead-helper cleanup** — Priority 4 (bundle with B/E or its own small PR).
- Refactor inline construction sites to use the existing helpers where appropriate (auto-enriches context).
- Delete any helpers that remain unused after refactoring.

**Bundle D: E105 cross-record-type consistency** — Priority 5.
- Add `get_field_or_err` to `AuthorityRecord` and `HoldingsRecord` (recommended per pre-1.0 posture) OR narrow docs claim to "Record (bibliographic) only."

**Bundle F: E007 positional context** — Priority 6 OR defer.
- Decide: enrich via `err_io` helper at call sites (large blast radius; touches every io-fallible site) OR accept pymarc-compat OSError as sufficient.
- **Recommend defer to 0.8.2** unless user wants positional context on IO errors badly enough to justify the diff size.

### Deferred to 0.8.2 (file beads)

- E404 slug rename (breaking change for users matching on `record_too_large_for_iso2709` slug across multiple distinct conditions).
- Dedicated MARCXML / marcjson fuzz targets (E401, E402).
- Per-variant fuzz harnesses for E099 (recovery-cap density) and a few others.

### Sequencing for Phase 2

Recommend Bundle C first (correctness), then B (small, isolated), then E (biggest spec-completion payoff), then A (cleanup naturally bundles with B+E refactors), then D (consistency call), then F or defer.

Each bundle is a single PR. Total estimated: 5 PRs in 0.8.1 scope (C, B, E, A, D) plus deferred follow-ups for 0.8.2 (F, fuzz expansion, slug rename).

While starting bd-0x73.21.1 (E402 wiring fix), discovered that my Phase 1 helper-liveness search only walked `src/` — not `src-python/`. Re-ran the audit across both:

**Correction:**

- `err_json` is **LIVE** (2 call sites in `src-python/src/formats.rs` — `mrrc.marcjson_to_record` and `mrrc.json_to_record` both use `ctx.err_json(e)` on `serde_json::from_str` failure). Phase 1's "5 dead helpers" finding was overstated.
- The helper for E007 is named `err_io`, not `err_io_error` as Phase 1 wrote.

**Verified correct in Phase 1:**

- `err_record_length_invalid` — dead.
- `err_base_address_invalid` — dead.
- `err_base_address_not_found` — dead.
- `err_io` — dead.
- E007 IoError context-strip via the From impl — still real (PyO3 layer translates to PyIOError; no enrichment).
- MARCXML/JSON/marcjson leader-error context-strip — still real (PyO3 wrappers handle deserialization via err_json/err_xml but propagate leader errors with stripped context).
- E402 manifest correctness — actually correct (the Python harness's `parse_marcjson` kind exercises `mrrc.marcjson_to_record`, which hits the LIVE err_json wiring).

**Consequence:**

- **bd-0x73.21.1 closed as verified-OK** (no code change needed; rationale recorded in bead closure + gh #205 close comment).
- **bd-0x73.21.4** description revised: dead-helper count 5 → 4, helper name corrected, dependency on .1 removed. GitHub issue #208 body synced.
- **bd-0x73.21.2, .6** scopes unchanged.

**Methodology fix:** going forward, all helper-liveness and call-site enrichment audits search `src/` AND `src-python/`. Phase 1 walk could plausibly have other src-python-related blind spots — will keep looking for them as Phase 2 progresses.
