title: E402 wiring fix — verify harness behavior, then wire properly or repurpose
description:
## Context

Phase 1 finding (bd-0x73.21 comment #97, "E402 — JsonError" section): `err_json` helper at `src/iso2709.rs:400` is defined but never called in production. `src/marcjson.rs` and `src/json.rs` use `MarcError::invalid_field_msg(...)` (which constructs **E106 InvalidField**, not E402) for all JSON-parsing errors. No `From<serde_json::Error>` impl exists. No call site constructs `MarcError::JsonError`. The manifest entry `e402_marcjson_truncated` claims E402 fires from `e402_malformed_marcjson.json` but the harness probably observes E106.

This is the closest analog to the original 04-29 audit's E201/E202 wiring gap that this Phase 1 review found.

## Method

1. **Verify first.** Run the error-coverage harness against `tests/data/error_fixtures/e402_malformed_marcjson.json` and observe what code actually surfaces. Two branches:
   - If E402 fires: there's a path not found by source inspection. Document where, verify wiring is sound, close as OK with an explanatory note.
   - If E106 (or anything else) fires: proceed with the wiring decision below.

2. **Wiring decision** (assuming E402 doesn't currently fire):
   - **(a) Wire properly.** Thread `ParseContext` (or a minimal context bag) through `marcjson_to_record` and `json_to_record`. Replace `?` on `serde_json` operations with `.map_err(|e| ctx.err_json(e))`. Preserves the typed JsonError class with positional context.
   - **(b) Add `From<serde_json::Error> for MarcError`.** Mirrors the `From<std::io::Error>` pattern. Simpler — no ParseContext threading — but strips positional context (same issue as E007). Acceptable only if pymarc parity doesn't expect context.
   - **(c) Repurpose E402 as deprecated.** Update docs to remove E402 from the documented set. Fix manifest to report E106 for the existing fixture. Justified if JSON-shape errors are conceptually `invalid_field` and don't deserve a separate code.
   - **Recommend (a)** for pre-1.0 consistency (typed errors with full context over generic ones).

3. Update manifest entry `e402_marcjson_truncated` to match chosen approach.

4. Add unit tests verifying the chosen behavior on both `marcjson.rs` and `json.rs` paths.

## Files likely touched

`src/marcjson.rs`, `src/json.rs`, `src/iso2709.rs` (err_json helper signature if changed), `src/error.rs` (From impl OR variant doc), `tests/error_coverage.toml`, possibly `tests/data/error_fixtures/e402_malformed_marcjson.json` (retire or update), `docs/reference/error-codes.md` (E402 section), `CHANGELOG.md`, new unit tests.

## Done when

- Manifest harness against `e402_malformed_marcjson.json` consistently fires the expected code (E402 if wired, E106 if repurposed).
- Decision recorded in commit message + bead closure comment.
- Manifest, docs, and code agree.
- bd-0x73.12 step 3 (docs reconciliation) would pass against this code path.

## Reference

- bd-0x73.21 comment #97 — Phase 1 findings (E402 section).
- `src/iso2709.rs:400` — `err_json` helper (currently dead).
- `src/marcjson.rs`, `src/json.rs` — current error paths.
- `tests/error_coverage.toml` — current E402 manifest entry.
close_reason: Verified — wiring is sound. The Python harness test passes because src-python/src/formats.rs:188-195 (mrrc.marcjson_to_record) uses ctx.err_json(e) on serde_json::from_str failure, correctly firing E402 for the truncated-JSON manifest fixture. Phase 1 finding was a false positive caused by searching only src/ (not src-python/). The Rust core's marcjson::marcjson_to_record(&Value) still fires E106 for shape errors, but that's an idiomatic-Rust asymmetry the user has explicitly accepted (Rust callers handle deserialization themselves; once in Value land, schema mismatches are conceptually invalid_field). No code change needed; no PR.

title: Document AuthorityRecord, HoldingsRecord, AuthorityMARCReader, HoldingsMARCReader in docs/reference/python-api.md
description:
## Context

Surfaced while documenting `get_field_or_err` for the cross-record-type parity addition (bd-0x73.21.5 / PR #216): `docs/reference/python-api.md` does not document non-bibliographic types at all. The "Core Classes" section covers `Record`, `Field`, `ControlField`, `Subfield`, `Leader`; the "Reader/Writer Classes" section covers only `MARCReader` and `MARCWriter`. There is no section for:

- `AuthorityRecord` (full API: control fields, heading, see-from / see-also tracings, notes, source-data accessors, kind-of-record, level-of-establishment, builder)
- `HoldingsRecord` (full API: control fields, locations, captions, enumerations, textual holdings, item information, holdings-type detection)
- `AuthorityMARCReader` (constructor signature, iteration protocol, recovery_mode/validation_level kwargs, error semantics)
- `HoldingsMARCReader` (same)
- `AuthorityMARCWriter` (if exposed)
- `HoldingsMARCWriter` (if exposed)

The PR #216 docs change adds minimal `AuthorityRecord` and `HoldingsRecord` sub-sections that document only the new parity methods (`get_field`, `get_field_or_err`, `get_fields`) and explicitly note "The full API surface is not yet covered in this reference." This bead is the follow-up to fill in the full surface.

## Method

1. Enumerate the public Python API for each of the four/six types. Use `mrrc/_mrrc.pyi` as the canonical source for what is exposed. Cross-check with `src-python/src/wrappers.rs`, `src-python/src/authority_readers.rs`, and `src-python/src/holdings_readers.rs`.

2. For each type, write a docs section matching the existing `Record` / `MARCReader` format: brief intro, usage example, properties table (if any), methods table (with return types and one-line descriptions).

3. Cross-link from `MARCReader` to the analogous `AuthorityMARCReader` / `HoldingsMARCReader` since users coming from pymarc may not realize the parallel exists.

4. Optionally add a short "Choosing a reader" subsection at the top of "Reader/Writer Classes" explaining which reader to use for which MARC record type.

5. Update the quickstart-python doc if it has a section about reading authority/holdings records that needs to point at the reference.

## Files likely touched

`docs/reference/python-api.md` (primary), possibly `docs/getting-started/quickstart-python.md`, possibly `docs/reference/formats.md`, possibly the project's mkdocs nav.

## Done when

- `AuthorityRecord`, `HoldingsRecord`, `AuthorityMARCReader`, `HoldingsMARCReader` (and any writer counterparts that exist) each have a documented section in `docs/reference/python-api.md` matching the depth of the existing `Record` / `MARCReader` sections.
- The "not yet covered" notes in the minimal sub-sections added by PR #216 are retired.
- Docs build cleanly.

## Reference

- `mrrc/_mrrc.pyi` — canonical Python API stubs.
- `docs/reference/python-api.md` — current doc with bibliographic-only coverage.
close_reason: Documented in PR #229 (merged 7eb45f6, closes GH #217). Full AuthorityRecord/HoldingsRecord sections + AuthorityMARCReader/HoldingsMARCReader sections in docs/reference/python-api.md; retired the 'not yet covered' placeholders. Also corrected the canonical _mrrc.pyi stubs for both record types (phantom fields()/control_field(), leader mistyped as method, omitted members) to match the PyO3 surface, verified at runtime.

title: validate_data_field_bytes guard's InvalidField error reports stale byte_offset and no field_tag
description:
## Context

Surfaced during bd-0x73.21.8 (PR #223) when wiring `e106_authority_field_too_short` in the error-coverage manifest. The newly-wired case asserts `expected_context = ["record_index", "byte_offset"]` and both are non-null, but the `byte_offset` value points at the *directory entry's* position rather than the field's actual data-area position, and `field_tag` is `None` on the error even though the parser knows the tag at that point.

Concrete reproduction from the wired fixture: the authority `< 2` guard fires on tag 100 (a data field). The fixture's directory entry for that field sits at file byte 60; the field's data starts at file byte 143 (record base address 73 + data-area offset 70). The raised `InvalidField` reports `byte_offset = 60` and `field_tag = None`. A consumer reading the error gets an offset that lands inside the directory, not the field.

## Root cause

`src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:492` invokes `B::validate_data_field_bytes(field_data, &tag, ctx)` *before* lines 504-505 set `ctx.current_field_tag = tag.as_bytes().try_into().ok()` and `ctx.stream_byte_offset = record_data_offset + data_start + start_position`. Those two assignments only execute on the post-guard parse path, so the guard's `ctx.err_invalid_field(...)` inherits whatever `current_field_tag` and `stream_byte_offset` were last set to (stale from directory walking or a prior field).

## Method

1. Move (or duplicate) the `ctx.current_field_tag` and `ctx.stream_byte_offset` assignments to run *before* the guard invocation at line 492 so the guard's `InvalidField` carries the correct field tag and absolute byte offset for the field being validated.
2. Reset `ctx.current_field_tag = None` on the strict-Err / lenient-skip-continue branches so field context does not leak into subsequent iterations (mirror the existing pattern at line 507).
3. Add `field_tag` to the `e106_authority_field_too_short` manifest case's `expected_context`, consistent with the sibling E106 case at `tests/error_coverage.toml:534` which asserts `field_tag`.
4. Verify the wired case's `byte_offset` now equals the field's data-area position (file byte 143 in the fixture).

## Files likely touched

`src/iso2709_skeleton.rs` (move the two ctx assignments before the guard), `tests/error_coverage.toml` (expand `expected_context` on `e106_authority_field_too_short`).

## Done when

- The `InvalidField` raised by the per-reader data-field-bytes guard carries the correct `field_tag` and a `byte_offset` pointing at the field's data-area position.
- The `e106_authority_field_too_short` manifest case asserts `field_tag` in its `expected_context`.

## Reference

- bd-0x73.21.8 (PR #223) — surfaced this finding.
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:492` — guard invocation site.
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:504-505` — where `current_field_tag` / `stream_byte_offset` get set for the post-guard parse path.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #228 (merged 5d415e4). Moved ctx.current_field_tag / stream_byte_offset assignments ahead of the per-reader minimum-bytes guard in iso2709_skeleton.rs so the guard's InvalidField carries the field's data-area byte_offset and correct field_tag; cleared current_field_tag on the guard error branch. Expanded e106_authority_field_too_short to assert field_tag and added a regression test pinning the exact offset/tag.

title: MARCXML/JSON/marcjson leader-error positional context enrichment
description:
## Context

Phase 1 finding (bd-0x73.21 comment #97, E001/E002/E003 sections + cross-cutting #2): three call sites use bare `?` propagation on `Leader::from_bytes`, stripping positional context (`record_index`, `source_name`) for E001/E002/E003 errors. The ISO 2709 path enriches correctly via `with_position(ctx)` at `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:211-218`. Non-binary readers (MARCXML, JSON, marcjson) leave these `None` — users hitting bad leaders in multi-record streams get unactionable errors.

## Method

For each of the three call sites — `src/marcxml.rs:511` (`marcxml_record_to_record`), `src/json.rs:139` (`json_to_record`), `src/marcjson.rs:117` (`marcjson_to_record`) — add positional-context enrichment.

Approach options:
- **(a)** Pass a `ParseContext` (or a minimal "context bag" with `record_index` + `source_name`) into the helper functions and use `e.with_position(ctx)`. Maximum consistency with the ISO 2709 pattern.
- **(b)** Inline `.map_err(|e| { ... mutate context fields ... })` at each call site. Simpler but less reusable.

**Recommend (a)** for consistency. May require small refactoring of the calling MARCXML / JSON / marcjson reader loops to track `record_index` and pass it down.

`byte_offset` may not map cleanly to XML/JSON streams (the parser's byte position vs the record's byte position differ); leaving it `None` is acceptable for these formats. `record_index` and `source_name` MUST populate.

## Files likely touched

`src/marcxml.rs`, `src/json.rs`, `src/marcjson.rs` (error enrichment at 3 sites + possibly the calling loops); possibly a small "context bag" type in `src/error.rs` if (a) is chosen and full ParseContext is too heavy; new unit tests asserting `record_index` and `source_name` populate; CHANGELOG entry under Changed.

## Done when

- E001/E002/E003 fired from MARCXML / JSON / marcjson paths surface `record_index` and `source_name`.
- Verified by new unit tests on each reader (parse a 3-record stream with bad leader on record 2; assert error carries `record_index = 1`).
- All three call sites use the same enrichment pattern (consistency).

## Reference

- bd-0x73.21 comment #97 — Phase 1 findings (E001, E002, E003 sections; cross-cutting #2).
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:211-218` — enrichment pattern to mirror.
close_reason: Shipped via PR #211 (merge edf47de, closes GH #206). MARCXML/JSON/marcjson leader errors now carry record_index — multi-record collections identify the failing record by index; single-record APIs carry record_index=1. byte_offset and source_name plumbing intentionally deferred (out of scope for this fix; would need API extensions). 3 Rust unit + 4 Python integration tests added. Codspeed dashboard flagged 19 regressions, ruled out as host fluctuation (regressions all in ISO 2709 paths this PR doesn't touch; Codspeed itself warned of unreliable Walltime measurement environment).

title: Per-trigger manifest expansion across E001/E002/E003/E005/E101/E106/E301/E401/E404
description:
## Context

Phase 1 finding (bd-0x73.21 comment #97, cross-cutting #3): bd-rjl1 established the per-trigger manifest principle for E404 but didn't generalize. Many codes have multiple distinct production firing paths with only one manifest entry each. The error-coverage harness's "wired = true" check is satisfied with one entry per code, masking regressions where individual paths lose wiring.

## Scope (counts from Phase 1)

| Code | Production paths | Current entries | New entries needed |
|---|---|---|---|
| E001 | 2 | 1 | +1 (record_length < 24 via validate_for_reading) |
| E002 | 13 (1 structural + 12 semantic) | 2 | +~10 (one per RecordStructureValidator semantic rule) |
| E003 | 2 | 1 | +1 (data_base_address < 24 via validate_for_reading) |
| E005 | 3 | 1 | +1 (try_recover_record empty-read case) |
| E101 | 4 | 2 | +2 (bad-tag-bytes path, non-digit-start path) |
| E106 | 7+ | 1 | +~6 (per distinct path: data field too short, expected subfield delimiter, holdings/authority-specific paths, recovery path) |
| E301 | 4 | 1 | +3 (control field invalid UTF-8 in bibliographic skeleton, holdings, authority) |
| E401 | 8 | 1 | +~7 (distinct XML parse failure modes) |
| E404 | 6 | 3 | +~3 (base-address overflow, additional ISO 2709 constraint, u32 conversion failures) |

Total: roughly **25-30 new fixtures + manifest entries**.

## Method

For each code:

1. List the distinct production firing paths (from Phase 1 findings).
2. For each path, construct a minimal fixture that exercises only that path.
3. Add manifest entry with appropriate `trigger_kind`, `trigger_fixture`, `description`, `expected_context`, and `validation_level` / `recovery_modes` constraints.
4. Run harness; verify the new fixture triggers the expected code AND the manifest assertion passes.

Where multiple paths produce conceptually-identical errors (e.g., u32 overflow vs ISO 2709 size limit might collapse to "record too large"), consolidate rather than duplicate.

## Update bd-0x73.12

bd-0x73.12 step 2 (coverage harness assertion) currently asserts "every variant has at least one wired entry." Expand it to assert "every distinct production trigger has a manifest entry" — per-trigger coverage as the release-gate condition.

## Files touched

`tests/error_coverage.toml` (~25-30 new entries); `tests/data/error_fixtures/*.bin`, `*.xml`, `*.json` (~25-30 new fixtures); possibly `tests/error_coverage.rs` and `tests/python/test_error_coverage.py` if the harness needs new plumbing for per-trigger assertion; bd-0x73.12 description update; CHANGELOG entry under Changed.

## Done when

- Every distinct production firing path identified in Phase 1 has at least one manifest entry.
- bd-0x73.12 step 2 re-runs with the expanded manifest and passes.
- Harness's "X/N wired" tally reflects per-trigger coverage.

## Reference

- bd-0x73.21 comment #97 — Phase 1 findings (per-code sections).
- bd-rjl1 (closed) — original per-trigger principle for E404.
- `tests/error_coverage.toml` — current manifest.
close_reason: Shipped via PR #212 (merge 6681cd8, closes GH #207). Per-trigger manifest expansion: 23 → 44 entries (42 wired, 2 unwired-with-skip pointing to dedicated follow-up beads). Generator script lands the binary-fixture mutation logic as the single source of truth. Harness extended with parse_holdings / parse_authority / programmatic_validator / programmatic_writer_check trigger kinds. bd-0x73.12 step 2 wording updated to require per-trigger coverage as the release-gate condition. Four findings surfaced and tracked as dedicated beads: bd-0x73.21.7 (cross-reader-type validation), bd-0x73.21.8 (authority field-too-short guard), bd-0x73.21.9 (MarcError Clone), and bd-0x73.21.4 widened to cover the writer.rs u32 dead-code finding.

title: Dead defensive code cleanup — err_* helpers + writer u32 conversion checks
description:
## Context

Phase 1 finding (bd-0x73.21 comment #97, cross-cutting #1) — REVISED after the 2026-05-17 src-python/ methodology fix: **4 helpers** (originally claimed 5) defined in `ParseContext` but never called in production: `err_record_length_invalid`, `err_base_address_invalid`, `err_base_address_not_found`, `err_io` (note: original Phase 1 comment misnamed this `err_io_error`). The fifth originally-claimed dead helper, `err_json`, is in fact LIVE — used 2× in `src-python/src/formats.rs` (mrrc.marcjson_to_record and mrrc.json_to_record). Firing sites for the 4 truly-dead helpers construct errors directly via inline `MarcError { ... }`, bypassing the helpers and skipping auto-enrichment of positional context.

**Sequencing note:** Run AFTER bd-0x73.21.6 (which may give `err_io` callers). bd-0x73.21.2 (MARCXML/JSON enrichment) may also reshape the helper landscape for E001/E003. bd-0x73.21.1 closed as verified-OK (no helper landscape change). This bead's scope is whatever remains dead after .2 and .6 land.

## Additional dead defensive code surfaced by bd-0x73.21.3 (PR #212)

Per the bd-0x73.21.3 audit, two `u32::try_from` checks inside `MarcWriter::write_record` (`src/writer.rs:223, 229`) are unreachable from the public API:

```rust
leader.record_length = u32::try_from(record_length).map_err(|_| MarcError::WriterError { ... });
leader.data_base_address = u32::try_from(base_address).map_err(|_| MarcError::WriterError { ... });
```

`write_record` calls `iso2709::check_iso2709_size` immediately before these conversions, which already caps both values at 99999 — well under `u32::MAX` (4 billion). On 32-bit systems `usize == u32` so `try_from` is a no-op; on 64-bit systems no value that passes the 99999 cap can fail the conversion. Result: the `try_from`'s error arms are dead defensive code with no test reachability and no real-world fire condition.

In scope for this bead: decide per check — keep with a code comment explaining the check is a redundant runtime guard that would only fire if a future refactor bypasses `check_iso2709_size`, OR hoist the check into a separately-testable public helper, OR delete.

## Method

For each helper that remains dead after .2 and .6 land, AND for the two writer.rs u32 conversion checks:

1. Decide:
   - **(a) Refactor** existing inline-construction sites to use the helper (auto-enriches positional context, improves consistency).
   - **(b) Delete** the helper (genuinely redundant; helper was speculative).
   - **(c) Keep with a code comment** stating the check is a redundant runtime guard whose error arm is unreachable under the current upstream guard but would catch a future refactor that bypasses the upstream guard. Appropriate for the writer.rs cases where removing the check would lose protection against a future bug, even though no test can reach the error arm today.

2. Apply the decision. Refactor diffs should also delete the inline `record_index: None, byte_offset: None, source_name: None` field-setting where the helper now provides them.

**Recommendation:**
- For the 4 dead `ParseContext::err_*` helpers: prefer (a) where it adds context-enrichment value; (b) where the helper signature genuinely doesn't fit any real call site.
- For the writer.rs u32 checks: prefer (c) with a code comment, since these are redundant runtime guards that catch a class of future refactor bug (anyone calling the inner conversion without going through `check_iso2709_size` first), and removing them removes that protection even though no test can reach them today.

## Files likely touched

`src/iso2709.rs` (helper definitions; possibly delete), `src/leader.rs` (inline construction sites for E001/E003), `src/writer.rs` (the two u32 conversion checks — comment annotation), other source files as discovered during refactor, possibly the `from_bytes` API surface if signatures change.

## Done when

- Zero defined `ParseContext::err_*` helpers without callers (verified by `rg "fn err_" --type rust src/iso2709.rs | while read line; ...` style audit).
- The two writer.rs u32 conversion checks either have a code comment stating they are redundant runtime guards (and naming the upstream guard that makes them unreachable today) OR are removed/hoisted with the rationale captured in the commit message.
- Decision recorded per helper / per check in commit message.
- Refactored sites tested (existing test suite + any new asserts that positional context survives).

## Reference

- bd-0x73.21 comment #97 — Phase 1 findings (cross-cutting #1; per-code sections for E001/E003/E007/E402).
- bd-0x73.21.3 (PR #212) — surfaced the writer.rs u32 dead-code finding.
- `src/iso2709.rs` — `ParseContext` helper definitions.
- `src/writer.rs:215-233` — `MarcWriter::write_record`'s u32 conversion checks.
close_reason: Dead defensive code cleanup landed in PR #227 (merged f696955, closes GH #208). Deleted 3 unused ParseContext helpers (err_record_length_invalid, err_base_address_invalid, err_base_address_not_found); err_io kept (live after E007 PR #222). writer.rs u32 guards kept with explanatory comment (option c).

title: E105 cross-record-type consistency — add get_field_or_err to AuthorityRecord and HoldingsRecord
description:
## Context

Phase 1 finding (bd-0x73.21 comment #97, E105 section): docs claim `E105 FieldNotFound` "Applies to: All record types." Code only implements `Record::get_field_or_err` on bibliographic `Record` (`src/record.rs:219`, added in PR #169). `AuthorityRecord` and `HoldingsRecord` only expose `get_field(tag) -> Option<&Field>` — no strict-mode counterpart.

Pre-1.0 consistency posture: one rule across all three record types. This bead closes the asymmetry by adding `get_field_or_err` to the two remaining record types and the corresponding PyO3 bindings.

## Method

1. Add `get_field_or_err(tag: &str) -> Result<&Field>` to `AuthorityRecord` (`src/authority_record.rs`) mirroring `Record::get_field_or_err`. Populate `field_tag` and `record_control_number` in the constructed `MarcError::FieldNotFound`.

2. Same for `HoldingsRecord` (`src/holdings_record.rs`).

3. Add PyO3 `#[pymethods]` exposing the new methods on the Python wrappers (`src-python/src/`). Match the existing `Record.get_field_or_err` signature exactly.

4. Update `mrrc/_mrrc.pyi` with the new method signatures on `_AuthorityRecord` and `_HoldingsRecord` (if they're exposed).

5. Update Python wrapper layer (`mrrc/__init__.py`) if needed for delegation parity.

6. Add manifest entries for E105 triggered from each of the three record types (extending `e105_field_not_found` into three entries or adding two more with appropriate `trigger_kind` qualifiers).

7. Add Rust unit tests on each new method + Python integration test exercising all three record types.

8. CHANGELOG entry under Added.

## Files touched

`src/authority_record.rs`, `src/holdings_record.rs`, `src-python/src/{authority_readers,holdings_readers,...}.rs` (or wherever the PyO3 record bindings live), `mrrc/_mrrc.pyi`, `mrrc/__init__.py` (if delegation needed), `tests/error_coverage.toml`, unit tests in Rust + `tests/python/`, `CHANGELOG.md`.

## Done when

- `get_field_or_err(tag)` available on all three record types in both Rust and Python.
- E105 fires from all three; manifest covers all three triggers.
- Docs claim "Applies to: All record types" verifies as true via the harness.
- Existing `get_field` accessors continue to return `Option<&Field>` unchanged (pymarc-compat preserved).

## Reference

- bd-0x73.21 comment #97 — Phase 1 findings (E105 section).
- `src/record.rs:219` — pattern to mirror.
- PR #169 (bd-44z4 PR δ) — original implementation for `Record`.
close_reason: Shipped via PR #216 (merge 8532045, closes GH #209). get_field_or_err now available on all three record types (Record, AuthorityRecord, HoldingsRecord) in both Rust and Python with the same call shape. Logic centralized as a default method on the MarcRecord trait; each record type exposes thin inherent shims that delegate to the trait so Rust callers don't need use mrrc::MarcRecord in scope for the common accessor path. PyO3 wrappers expose get_field and get_field_or_err on PyAuthorityRecord/PyHoldingsRecord (previously only get_fields was exposed). Manifest grew 44 -> 46 wired entries; harness asserts E105 fires from all three record types. simple_holdings.mrc fixture added. docs/reference/python-api.md gained minimal AuthorityRecord/HoldingsRecord sections documenting the parity accessors (full per-type API documentation tracked separately under bd-0x73.21.10).

title: E007 positional context propagation — enrich IoError at all io-fallible parse sites
description:
## Context

Phase 1 finding (bd-0x73.21 comment #97, E007 section + cross-cutting #5): `impl From<std::io::Error> for MarcError` at `src/error.rs:1449` constructs `MarcError::IoError` with all positional fields `None`. The impl's own comment says "without surrounding context." Every `?` on an `io::Error` strips positional context — for multi-record streams, users see "IO error" with no record_index, byte_offset, or source.

This is the largest-blast-radius bundle in Phase 2 because it touches every io-fallible call site in every reader.

## Method

1. **Inventory.** Find every site in `src/` where `?` is used on a `Result<_, io::Error>` (or equivalent) in a context where `ParseContext` is available or could be made available. Approximate scope:
   - `src/iso2709.rs`, `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs` — main parse loops.
   - `src/reader.rs`, `src/authority_reader.rs`, `src/holdings_reader.rs` — reader entry points.
   - `src/recovery.rs` — recovery-path io.
   - Possibly more discovered during the audit.

2. **Replace** bare `?` with `.map_err(|e| ctx.err_io(e))` at each site. Use the LIVE `ParseContext::err_io` helper at `src/iso2709.rs:373`.

3. **Where `ParseContext` isn't directly available** (e.g., low-level utility functions called from many places): thread it in if practical, OR document the site as From-impl fallback (acceptable for utility code that doesn't know the stream context).

4. **Update the From impl** (`src/error.rs:1449`) to add a doc-comment warning: callers in parse paths should prefer `ctx.err_io(...)` for context. Keep the From impl for backwards-compat and for non-parse io contexts.

5. **Update manifest E007 entry.** Currently `expected_context = []`. After enrichment, expected_context should include `record_index` and `source_name` for parse-path triggers. Either expand the existing entry's `expected_context` (if all triggers now carry context) or add separate manifest entries for parse-path vs raw-io triggers.

6. **Add regression test.** Parse a multi-record stream where one record's read fails partway (e.g., via a mock Reader that errors after N bytes). Assert the surfaced E007 carries `record_index` matching the failing record.

## Files likely touched

Wide. Every io-fallible call site in readers: `src/iso2709.rs`, `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs`, `src/reader.rs`, `src/authority_reader.rs`, `src/holdings_reader.rs`, `src/recovery.rs`, possibly `src/leader.rs`. Plus `src/error.rs` (From impl doc update), `tests/error_coverage.toml` (E007 entry expansion), `tests/data/error_fixtures/` (possibly new fixtures), regression tests in `tests/` and `tests/python/`, CHANGELOG.

## Risk

Largest diff in Phase 2. Take care to:
- Not change semantics — only enrich context.
- Verify each call site has `ctx` in scope; some may not (use From impl fallback there).
- Run full test suite to catch any subtle changes.
- Verify Python OSError surface still works (E007 fires as Python `OSError` per docs; the wrapping should preserve that).

## Done when

- All io-fallible call sites in readers use `ctx.err_io(...)` (or are documented as From-impl-only with reason).
- E007 fired from a parse path carries `record_index` and `source_name` (verified by regression test).
- Manifest E007 entry's `expected_context` updated to match.
- Python OSError surface unchanged for end users.

## Reference

- bd-0x73.21 comment #97 — Phase 1 findings (E007 section; cross-cutting #5).
- `src/error.rs:1449` — current From impl.
- `src/iso2709.rs:373` — `err_io` helper (LIVE).
close_reason: E007 IoError positional-context enrichment landed in PR #222 (merged), closing issue #210

title: RecordStructureValidator's bibliographic rules fire on authority/holdings records at strict_marc
description:
## Context

Surfaced during bd-0x73.21.3 (PR #212) when adding an E301 manifest case for authority-record control-field invalid UTF-8 at `validation_level=strict_marc`. The fixture parsed and triggered **E002 InvalidLeader** ("Invalid bibliographic level in leader: '|'") instead of E301 EncodingError.

Root cause: `src/record_validation.rs` defines `RecordStructureValidator::validate_leader` with the MARC 21 **bibliographic** allowed-value sets (record_status in `{a,c,d,n,p}`, bibliographic_level in `{a,b,c,d,i,m,s}`, etc.). At `validation_level=strict_marc`, `iso2709_skeleton.rs:227` calls this validator on every leader, including those read by `AuthorityMarcReader` and `HoldingsMarcReader`. Authority records typically have `bibliographic_level='|'` (undefined for authority records per MARC 21), which the bibliographic validator rejects.

Practical impact: at `strict_marc`, the authority and holdings readers cannot parse otherwise-valid records without tripping E002 on leader bytes that are valid for their own record type. Detection-strictness becomes unusable for non-bibliographic readers.

## Method

1. Confirm reproduction: parse `tests/data/simple_authority.mrc` through `AuthorityMarcReader::with_validation_level(StrictMarc)` and observe E002 on the leader.

2. Decide the validator design:
   - **(a)** Split into `validate_leader_bibliographic`, `validate_leader_authority`, `validate_leader_holdings` with the MARC 21 leader rules for each record type. Skeleton dispatches by reader type.
   - **(b)** Generalize `validate_leader` to accept a per-record-type allowed-set parameter (closure or trait object).
   - **(c)** Keep the current `validate_leader` as the bibliographic case; add the two record-type variants alongside it. Skeleton dispatches.
   - Recommend **(c)** as least-disruptive: existing code continues to work for bibliographic.

3. Wire the per-type variants into the skeleton's `validation_level=strict_marc` branch via a generic parameter on the reader (similar to how `B::validate_record_type` is dispatched today).

4. Add per-reader-type tests verifying:
   - Bibliographic at strict_marc accepts bibliographic-valid leaders, rejects bibliographic-invalid ones.
   - Authority at strict_marc accepts authority-valid leaders (incl. `bibliographic_level='|'`), rejects authority-invalid ones.
   - Holdings at strict_marc accepts holdings-valid leaders, rejects holdings-invalid ones.

5. Re-wire bd-0x73.21.3's two unwired-with-skip entries:
   - `e106_authority_field_too_short` (also reveals issue #2 — see bd-0x73.21.8)
   - `e301_invalid_utf8_in_authority_control_field` — once authority leaders pass at strict_marc, this should fire E301 as documented.

## Files likely touched

`src/record_validation.rs` (per-type validators), `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs` (dispatch at the strict_marc branch), per-reader-type unit tests, `tests/error_coverage.toml` (flip the two unwired entries to wired), CHANGELOG entry.

## Done when

- Authority and Holdings readers can be driven at `validation_level=strict_marc` against their own valid records without spurious E002.
- Each reader type has documented MARC 21 leader rules with per-type validation coverage.
- bd-0x73.21.3's `e301_invalid_utf8_in_authority_control_field` flips to `wired=true` and the harness asserts E301 fires.
- Docs `docs/reference/error-codes.md` E002 section is updated to clarify which leader rules apply to which reader type.

## Reference

- bd-0x73.21.3 (PR #212) — surfaced this finding.
- `src/record_validation.rs:14-129` — current bibliographic-only validator.
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:226-237` — strict_marc dispatch site.
- MARC 21 Authority Format leader doc — for authority allowed-value sets.
- MARC 21 Holdings Format leader doc — for holdings allowed-value sets.
close_reason: Per-record-type MARC 21 leader validation landed in PR #220 (merged), closing issue #213

title: Authority field-too-short guard's gating doesn't match the comment at iso2709_skeleton.rs:121-122
description:
## Context

Surfaced during bd-0x73.21.3 (PR #212) when constructing an E106 fixture for the documented "authority returns `Err` when `field_bytes.len() < 2`" guard. Per the docstring at `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:121-122`:

> Authority returns `Err` when `field_bytes.len() < 2` (can't read indicators); holdings returns `Err` when `< 3`.

A trial fixture (`tests/data/error_fixtures/e106_authority_field_too_short.bin`) was constructed by taking `tests/data/simple_authority.mrc` and mutating the second directory entry's length field from `0017` to `0001` — claiming a 1-byte field, which should fail the authority reader's `< 2` guard.

Result: the fixture parsed cleanly, no E106 fired. The guard's actual gating differs from what the comment claims.

Possible explanations:
- The guard exists but is in a code path the harness's `parse_authority` (strict mode) doesn't reach.
- The guard fires only at certain `validation_level` settings (likely lenient mode only).
- The guard was refactored out at some point and the docstring is stale.
- The fixture mutation does something other than what's intended (e.g., changing the length cascades into the directory walker hitting a different error first).

## Method

1. Read `iso2709_skeleton.rs`'s per-reader-type `min_data_field_bytes` callback (or equivalent) and confirm where it's invoked.
2. Read `AuthorityReaderBackend::min_data_field_bytes` (or its equivalent trait impl) to confirm the documented `2` value and where it's enforced.
3. Trace what happens when an authority data field reports length 1 (or 0): does it fire E106 unconditionally? Only in lenient mode? Only when other invariants hold? Or does the directory walker reject the entry first via E101?
4. Reconcile: either fix the comment to match the actual gating, or fix the gating to match the documented behavior.
5. Rebuild the fixture (or use a different mutation) so it actually trips the documented guard.
6. Flip `e106_authority_field_too_short` from `wired=false` to `wired=true` in the manifest.

## Files likely touched

`src/iso2709_skeleton.rs` (gating fix or comment fix), `src/authority_reader.rs` (where the `min_data_field_bytes` is set), the fixture (if a different mutation is needed), `tests/error_coverage.toml` (flip wired), CHANGELOG entry if behavior changes.

## Note on coupling to bd-0x73.21.7

This guard's strict-mode path may also be intercepted by the cross-reader-type validation bug (bd-0x73.21.7) when `validation_level=strict_marc`. Sequence: land .7 first, then re-test this guard at strict_marc to see whether the gating issue persists once leader validation isn't intercepting.

## Done when

- The comment at `iso2709_skeleton.rs:121-122` accurately describes when the authority `< 2` and holdings `< 3` guards fire.
- A fixture exists that demonstrably trips the documented guard.
- `e106_authority_field_too_short` is wired and asserted by the harness.

## Reference

- bd-0x73.21.3 (PR #212) — surfaced this finding.
- `src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:116-130` — `min_data_field_bytes` docstring.
- bd-0x73.21.7 — likely sequenced first.
close_reason: Authority field-too-short E106 manifest case wired with a correct (data-field) fixture in PR #223 (merged), closing issue #214

title: Add Clone to MarcError to unblock lenient-mode harness arms and other downstream uses
description:
## Context

Surfaced during bd-0x73.21.3 (PR #212) when trying to add `parse_iso2709_lenient` as a harness trigger_kind. The intended harness arm would drive `MarcReader` in lenient mode and return the first error pushed to a yielded `record.errors: Arc<Vec<MarcError>>`. The shape:

```rust
if let Some(err) = record.errors.first() {
    return Some(err.clone());  // <-- blocked: MarcError doesn't implement Clone
}
```

The harness's `TriggerOutcome::Fired(MarcError)` is an owned variant, but `record.errors` is `Arc<Vec<MarcError>>` — there's no way to move out of it, and without `Clone` there's no way to copy from a borrow either.

Root cause: `MarcError` contains an `IoError { cause: std::io::Error, ... }` variant, and `std::io::Error` does not implement `Clone` (intentional design — `io::Error` may wrap arbitrary errors, including non-Clone ones).

## Impact

bd-0x73.21.3 was forced to defer lenient-mode per-trigger entries:
- E005 lenient skeleton path (`iso2709_skeleton.rs:262`) — distinct fire site from the strict path that the existing E005 manifest entry covers.
- E106 recovery path (`recovery.rs:218`) — distinct fire site from the strict E106 entries.

Both fire the same observable variant as their strict counterparts, but per-trigger coverage strictly applied requires distinct manifest entries that the harness can independently exercise. Without `MarcError: Clone`, the harness cannot assert lenient-mode errors that surface via `record.errors`.

There are also broader downstream uses: any test that wants to inspect a recovered error after the fact, any caller that wants to forward a typed `mrrc.MarcError` through a layer that doesn't own it, etc.

## Method

1. Implement `Clone for MarcError` manually, handling the `IoError { cause: io::Error, ... }` variant by reconstructing via `io::Error::new(orig.kind(), orig.to_string())`. This preserves the kind discriminant and the rendered message; loses any non-string error payload the original wrapped (acceptable; the same trade-off is documented in `std::io::Error`'s own `Clone`-via-`From<&io::Error>` ecosystem patterns).

2. Add unit tests verifying:
   - `Clone` produces an equivalent error for every variant (round-trip via `==` if `PartialEq` is implemented, otherwise via field-by-field assertion).
   - `IoError`'s clone preserves the `kind()` and the rendered string.

3. Add a `parse_iso2709_lenient` trigger_kind to the harness (`tests/error_coverage.rs`) that drives `MarcReader::with_recovery_mode(Lenient).with_max_errors(0)` and returns `record.errors.first().cloned()`.

4. Add a `parse_iso2709_lenient_recovery` trigger_kind (or fold into the same) for `recovery.rs:218` path coverage.

5. Add manifest entries:
   - `e005_truncated_record_lenient` — same fixture as `e005_truncated_record`, `trigger_kind=parse_iso2709_lenient`. Asserts the lenient skeleton path fires E005.
   - `e106_recovery_invalid_field` — distinct fixture exercising `recovery.rs:218`. Asserts E106 fires via the recovery path.

6. CHANGELOG entry under Changed (semi-public API addition: `MarcError: Clone`).

## Files touched

`src/error.rs` (Clone impl + tests), `tests/error_coverage.rs` (new trigger_kind dispatch), `tests/error_coverage.toml` (new entries), possibly a new fixture, CHANGELOG.

## Done when

- `MarcError: Clone` is implemented and tested.
- The two manifest entries (E005 lenient, E106 recovery) are wired and harness-asserted.
- bd-0x73.21.3's comment noting the gap can be retired.

## Reference

- bd-0x73.21.3 (PR #212) — surfaced the blocker.
- `src/error.rs:1449` — `impl From<std::io::Error> for MarcError` (precedent for handling `io::Error` in custom impls).
- `tests/error_coverage.rs` (the commented-out `first_iso2709_lenient_error` design noted at the top of the new helpers section).
close_reason: MarcError Clone impl + lenient harness arm (E005/E106) landed in PR #224 (merged), closing issue #215

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #215 (v0.8.1 milestone).

title: Integration tests for every error variant + every recovery mode
description:
## Context

The 2026-04-29 audit revealed a structural test-suite gap: tests for
the typed-error infrastructure exercise the exception classes in
isolation (construction, formatting, dict shape, pickle round-trip)
but **don't exercise the parser-→-error wiring end-to-end**. A
variant could be defined, have a Python class, have a stable code,
have a documented behavior — and the test suite would still pass
even if no parse path ever constructed it. That's exactly how
E201/E202/E001/E003/E004/E006/E105 shipped unwired without test
failures.

## What to do

Add integration tests under `tests/python/test_error_integration.py`
and `tests/error_integration.rs` (or extend existing test files)
that feed **malformed input bytes** to a reader and assert:

1. The expected variant fires.
2. With the expected error code (`e.code == "E201"`) and slug.
3. With the expected positional context (`record_index`,
   `byte_offset`, `record_byte_offset`, `field_tag`,
   `indicator_position`, `subfield_code`, `found`, `expected`,
   `source_name`, `bytes_near` — the subset that's defined for the
   variant per `error-handling.md`).
4. Across all four behaviors (strict / lenient / permissive /
   pymarc-compat once that mode lands per bd-0x73.6 once filed).

## Coverage matrix

For each error code documented in `error-codes.md`, exactly one
integration test exists feeding bytes that trigger it:

| Code | Trigger input |
|---|---|
| E001 RecordLengthInvalid | leader bytes 0-4 contain non-digit |
| E002 InvalidLeader | leader bytes 5-23 fail other checks |
| E003 BaseAddressInvalid | leader bytes 12-16 contain non-digit |
| E004 BaseAddressNotFound | leader's base-address > total bytes |
| E005 TruncatedRecord | stream EOF before declared record length |
| E006 EndOfRecordNotFound | byte at record-length-minus-1 is not 0x1D |
| E007 IoError | reader's underlying stream raises I/O |
| E099 FatalReaderError | recovery cap exceeded |
| E101 DirectoryInvalid | directory size not a multiple of 12 |
| E105 FieldNotFound | accessor hits a missing field |
| E106 InvalidField | various field-shape malformations |
| E201 InvalidIndicator | indicator byte not digit/space |
| E202 BadSubfieldCode | byte after 0x1F not printable-alnum |
| E301 EncodingError | non-UTF-8 byte sequence in subfield value |
| E401 XmlError | malformed MARCXML input |
| E402 JsonError | malformed MARCJSON input |
| E404 WriterError | writer fails (e.g., I/O on output) |

Each row is one Rust test + one Python test (since the Python
binding is its own integration surface).

## Recovery-mode dimension

For variants where recovery applies, also assert behavior in each
mode:

- `strict`: variant raised
- `lenient`: variant recovered (record yielded with diagnostic
  attached per `record.errors` once bd-0x73 lands that), or skipped
  if unrecoverable
- `permissive`: `None` yielded; diagnostic accessible via
  `reader.last_exception` once bd-0x73 lands that
- `pymarc-compat` (if filed): same as pymarc 5.3.1 default (yield
  `None`, stash on `reader.current_exception` / `current_chunk`)

## Done when

- Every error code in `docs/reference/error-codes.md` has at least
  one integration test that triggers it via real malformed input.
- Recovery-mode dimension covered for variants where it applies.
- Test failure precisely identifies "code X is documented as fireable
  but no input we can construct triggers it" if a variant remains
  unwired despite bd-0x73 / bd-44z4 / bd-0x73.1 work.
- Tests are organized so adding a new error code requires adding a
  new test (e.g., parameterized fixtures, or one-test-per-code
  layout).

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella; depends on completion of E201/E202 wiring.
- bd-44z4 — sibling: 5 unwired structural codes need wiring or
  fold/remove decision before integration tests can target them.
- bd-0x73.2 — sibling: InvalidLeader positional context fix —
  integration tests need to assert the right context fields are set.
- This is the systemic fix for the test-suite gap: integration tests
  enforce documentation-as-spec.
close_reason: Implemented by PR #176 (merged); follow-ups bd-mk6z + bd-vwlp filed for deferred trigger mechanisms and Python backend alignment

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Status (2026-04-30): substantially subsumed by bd-0x73.8

bd-0x73.8's manifest-driven harness (tests/error_coverage.rs and tests/python/test_error_coverage.py) IS the integration test layer this bead originally called for: feed malformed input bytes to a reader, assert the documented variant fires with matching code, slug, and positional context. Every documented error code in docs/reference/error-codes.md has at least one case in the manifest; cases marked wired = false describe the docs-vs-code gap in technical terms.

What remains:
- The recovery-mode dimension (strict / lenient / permissive). The manifest's recovery_modes field declares which modes a case's contract covers, but the harness today only asserts strict mode. Lenient and permissive contracts depend on per-record diagnostic surfaces (record.errors, iter_with_errors, last_exception) that are tracked in bd-0x73.10 / bd-0x73.11 / bd-0x73.7. When those land, this bead extends the harness to assert non-strict behavior against the same fixtures.
- A pymarc-compat mode dimension (per bd-0x73.7) — same shape: declared in recovery_modes, asserted once the mode exists.

In other words: bd-0x73.3 = bd-0x73.8 (strict mode, today) + extensions when bd-0x73.10/.11/.7 land. The residual scope is small enough to fold rather than tracked as a separate bead, but keeping it open until those Phase C beads complete is the cleaner shape.

title: Property-test strategies for malformed inputs (proptest expansion)
description:
## Context

`tests/properties.rs` has 8 properties at `ProptestConfig { cases: 64 }`.
All current strategies (`arb_record`, `arb_subfield_value_xml`, etc.)
generate **structurally valid** records. They probe the round-trip
codec and structural invariants on emitted bytes, but they never
feed malformed inputs to the parser and assert specific errors fire.

Combined with the integration-test expansion (bd-0x73.3 once filed),
this closes the gap on "we test the exception classes in isolation
but not the parser-→-error wiring."

## What to do

Add new strategies that generate **invalid** inputs in deliberate,
shrinkable ways:

- `arb_invalid_leader_truncated` (< 24 bytes)
- `arb_invalid_leader_bad_length_field` (non-digit in bytes 0-4)
- `arb_invalid_leader_bad_base_address` (non-digit in bytes 12-16)
- `arb_record_with_bad_indicator` (valid record + one indicator
  byte mutated to a non-digit-non-space value)
- `arb_record_with_bad_subfield_code` (valid record + one subfield
  delimiter followed by a non-printable-alnum byte)
- `arb_record_with_directory_violation` (directory size mod 12 ≠ 0
  or directory tile sums don't match data area)
- `arb_record_truncated_after_offset` (EOF in middle of valid record
  at a parameterized offset)
- `arb_record_missing_record_terminator` (byte at record-length-1
  is not 0x1D)

For each, add a property:

```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn malformed_indicator_yields_invalid_indicator(
        bytes in arb_record_with_bad_indicator()
    ) {
        let mut reader = MarcReader::new(Cursor::new(&bytes[..]));
        let result = reader.read_record();
        prop_assert!(matches!(
            result,
            Err(MarcError::InvalidIndicator { .. })
        ));
        // also verify positional context populated correctly
    }
}
```

Plus property-level invariants on recovery modes:

- `lenient_mode_never_panics_on_arbitrary_bytes` (similar to
  fuzz target, but with proptest's shrinking)
- `permissive_mode_yields_none_for_every_unrecoverable_record`
- `recovery_cap_eventually_trips_on_pathological_inputs`

## Done when

- New strategies in `tests/properties.rs` generate every malformation
  type from the integration-test coverage matrix (bd-0x73.3).
- One property per malformation type asserts the right variant fires
  with right positional context.
- Recovery-mode invariants are properties (not just integration
  tests), so shrinking surfaces minimal counter-examples.
- `PROPTEST_CASES=2000 cargo test --test properties` runs cleanly.
- Any seeds that surface bugs get committed to
  `tests/proptest-regressions/properties.txt` per existing convention.

## Why proptest is right for this

The combinatorial space of "any byte-position × any mutation × any
recovery mode" is too large to enumerate as integration tests.
Proptest's shrinking finds minimal counter-examples for "we missed
this combination" cases. Integration tests (bd-0x73.3) cover the
named, documented cases; properties cover the long tail.

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella.
- bd-0x73.3 — sibling: integration tests for named cases.
- `tests/properties.rs` and `docs/contributing/formal-methods.md` for
  current proptest setup and conventions.
- bd-3t62, bd-2dia, bd-uss1, bd-gbgx — existing fuzz target beads
  for parse_marcxml, decode_marc8, parse_json, parse_leader. Some
  overlap with proptest territory; coordinate scope.

title: Fuzz coverage for error-detection paths
description:
## Context

Current fuzz targets (`fuzz/fuzz_targets/parse_record.rs`,
`roundtrip_binary.rs`) assert "no panic / no OOM / no hang" on
arbitrary bytes. They wouldn't surface "we never construct E201" or
"we silently accept malformed records and produce wrong-shaped
records" because not raising isn't a panic.

The thing the audit revealed — that wiring gaps are invisible to
unit tests — applies equally to fuzz tests as currently structured.
A wiring gap is a *behavioral* gap, not a panic.

## Scope (revised 2026-05-10)

Originally listed three options (A: error-classification, B:
differential cross-mode, C: variant-targeted). Now scoped to
**Option A only**. B and C split out:

- **Option B** filed as bd-0x73.19 — stays in this epic; depends on
  bd-0x73.7 for the `last_exception` / `last_chunk` accessors needed
  to fully assert per-mode contracts.
- **Option C** filed as bd-bfrh — standalone bead, no parent.
  Decision triggered after this bead's classification fuzzer has
  produced a corpus to analyze; kept out of the epic so it doesn't
  gate release-readiness.

## What to do

`fuzz/fuzz_targets/error_classification.rs` — for arbitrary input
bytes, run the reader in strict mode and assert one of:

- `Ok(record)` → record passes structural invariants (every byte
  is well-formed per the documented codec)
- `Err(MarcError::*)` → the variant matches a documented
  malformation category for the actual byte pattern (verifiable via
  `tests/error_coverage.toml` from bd-0x73.8)

This catches "wrong error class fired for input X" cases that the
panic-only fuzz doesn't catch.

## Done when

- New fuzz target `error_classification.rs` exercises
  error-classification beyond panic detection.
- Nightly `fuzz.yml` runs the new target in addition to the
  existing two.
- Reproducers (if any) committed to
  `tests/data/fuzz-regressions/error_classification/` per existing
  convention.
- `docs/contributing/fuzzing.md` updated to describe the new target
  and what it asserts.

## References

- bd-0x73 — umbrella.
- bd-0x73.3 (closed) / bd-0x73.4 — siblings: integration tests +
  property tests cover the named-case dimension. Fuzzing covers the
  long tail of byte-pattern combinations.
- `tests/error_coverage.toml` — single source of truth for documented
  variants; fuzz target cross-checks Ok/Err outputs against it.
- `fuzz/fuzz_targets/parse_record.rs` for the existing harness shape;
  reuse the libfuzzer setup.
- bd-3t62, bd-2dia, bd-uss1, bd-gbgx — pre-existing fuzz target beads
  for additional parsers; coordinate scope so this doesn't duplicate
  them.

title: pymarc-compat for error handling (current_exception, current_chunk, except-clause parity)
description:
## Context

This bead originally drifted to "broader pymarc API compatibility
audit." Re-scoped on 2026-05-03 to the actual user need: a pymarc
user dropping mrrc into existing pymarc-shaped error-handling code
should find their patterns work without surprises. The broader-API
audit is out of scope here and will be its own bead if and when it's
needed.

## What "pymarc-compat for error handling" means

Pymarc 5.3.1 default `MARCReader` behavior, which existing pymarc
codebases assume:

- iteration yields records OR `None` for unparseable records — never
  raises to the caller in default mode
- the offending exception is stashed on `reader.current_exception`
- the offending raw bytes are stashed on `reader.current_chunk`
- iteration continues past bad records
- the documented exception classes (`RecordDirectoryInvalid`,
  `BaseAddressInvalid`, `BaseAddressNotFound`, `RecordLeaderInvalid`,
  `EndOfRecordNotFound`, etc.) are caught via `except` clauses
  written against those names

Concretely: a typical pymarc loop looks like

```python
reader = pymarc.MARCReader(open("harvest.mrc", "rb"))
for rec in reader:
    if rec is None:
        log.warning("bad record at %s: %s",
                    reader.current_chunk[:80], reader.current_exception)
        continue
    process(rec)
```

A user who writes that loop against `mrrc.MARCReader` should get
behavior they'd describe as "the same" — same iteration shape, same
ability to inspect the bad record and the exception, same exception
class names matching their `except` clauses.

## What to deliver

At least one mrrc reader mode that reproduces the above. The closest
existing surface is `MARCReader(file, permissive=True)`; the
shortest path is to enrich it. Specifically:

1. **`reader.current_exception` accessor** populated with the most
   recent exception when permissive iteration yielded `None` for a
   record.
2. **`reader.current_chunk` accessor** populated with the offending
   raw bytes for that same record.
3. **Encoding-strictness divergence documented and minimized.**
   pymarc default doesn't fire `EncodingError`; mrrc's permissive
   currently does. Either suppress encoding errors in the
   pymarc-shape mode or document the divergence prominently in
   `migration-from-pymarc.md`.
4. **Exception-class-name parity verified** for every error a
   pymarc-default loop could plausibly catch by name. Existing
   typed-exception class names already extend the pymarc-shape
   parents; this is a verification + documentation pass.

## Done when

- `mrrc.MARCReader(file, permissive=True)` produces output a
  reasonable pymarc user would describe as "the same" as
  `pymarc.MARCReader(file)` for default-mode error handling: same
  iteration shape, populated `current_exception` / `current_chunk`,
  catchable by the same `except` clauses.
- One end-to-end test reads a mixed-quality corpus through both
  libraries and asserts iteration parity record-by-record (count,
  None-vs-record at each position, attributes on yielded records,
  exception classes attached to swallowed records).
- `docs/guides/migration-from-pymarc.md` documents the
  pymarc-default-equivalent recipe and any remaining divergences
  (most likely encoding strictness).

## Out of scope

- The broader pymarc public-API audit (record/field accessors,
  serialization helpers, MARC-8 conversion, etc.). If that becomes
  needed it will be its own bead.
- Reproducing every pymarc behavior identically. mrrc and
  pymarc differ in places (e.g., mrrc applies stricter byte-level
  validation in non-permissive modes). The goal here is that
  pymarc-shaped *error-handling* code keeps working unchanged —
  not full behavioral parity. Where the two libraries differ,
  document the difference in migration-from-pymarc.md.

## Reference

- GitHub issue #148 — original surfacing of `current_exception` /
  `current_chunk` gap.
- `mrrc/__init__.py:1453-1460` — current `permissive=True` swallow
  path; the locus of the fix.
- bd-0x73.7 (sibling, GH #156) — overlaps significantly. Decide
  whether to fold into bd-0x73.7 or keep this as the broader
  "rounds out the pymarc-compat error story" bead.
close_reason: Landed in #185 (commit a58fc07)

GitHub issue #155 retitled and rewritten to match.

title: At-least-one strictness mode that matches pymarc default behavior
description:
## Context

User-stated 0.8.1 goal: "at least one of our strictness modes
matches pymarc as closely as possible."

Pymarc's default `MARCReader` behavior (verified 2026-04-29 against
pymarc 5.3.1):

- `__next__` catches every `Exception` raised from `Record(chunk)`.
- Stashes the exception on `reader.current_exception`.
- Stashes the offending bytes on `reader.current_chunk`.
- Yields the bad record as `None` (does not raise to the caller).
- Continues iteration on the next record.
- Logs warnings for *missing* / *extra* indicators (not malformed).
- Does not validate indicator byte values beyond presence/count.
- Does not validate subfield-code byte values.
- Hardcoded message strings on the documented exception classes
  (e.g., `RecordDirectoryInvalid` always says "Invalid directory").

mrrc's current `permissive=True` is the closest mode but:

- Discards the exception (no `current_exception` accessor).
- Doesn't preserve the offending bytes (no `current_chunk`).
- Doesn't validate indicators (matches pymarc accidentally — this
  is the bd-0x73 / GH #148 finding).
- Validates subfield-codes? Same — currently no.
- Encoding errors *do* fire in mrrc and *don't* in pymarc — minor
  divergence.

So mrrc's permissive ≈ pymarc default minus the `current_exception` /
`current_chunk` machinery, plus encoding strictness.

## What to do

Decide the implementation shape first:

### Option A: enrich `permissive=True` to match pymarc default

- Add `MARCReader.current_exception` / `current_chunk` per
  bd-0x73 #5.
- On exception swallow path, stash both before returning `None`.
- Keep encoding strictness unchanged (encoding errors still fire);
  document the divergence in `migration-from-pymarc.md`.
- Net: `permissive=True` becomes the pymarc-compat default.

### Option B: new explicit `pymarc_compat` mode

- Adds a third value to `MARCReader`'s recovery axis (or a separate
  `pymarc_compat=True` kwarg).
- Encapsulates *all* the pymarc-default behaviors including
  loosening encoding to match pymarc.
- More precise; more API surface.

### Recommendation

**Option A** for 0.8.1. Reasons:

- `permissive=True` is already the closest user-facing mode and is
  documented as "pymarc-compat behavior."
- Adding `current_exception` / `current_chunk` is independently
  required (per bd-0x73 / GH #148).
- A separate `pymarc_compat` mode adds API surface for marginal
  precision.
- Encoding strictness divergence is a documented difference, not a
  user-visible breakage at the iteration shape (the exception class
  caught is just `EncodingError` instead of `Exception` — caller's
  `except Exception:` still catches it).

If we later find users specifically want pymarc's encoding lenience,
add Option B in 0.9 as a follow-up.

## Done when

- `MARCReader(file, permissive=True)` produces output indistinguishable
  from `pymarc.MARCReader(file)` for the cases where pymarc accepts
  records — same iteration shape (None-yielded for bad records),
  same `reader.current_exception` and `reader.current_chunk`
  semantics.
- One end-to-end test reads a corpus through both libraries (mrrc
  permissive vs pymarc default) and asserts iteration parity for
  every record (count, type at each position, attributes on
  yielded records).
- `migration-from-pymarc.md` documents:
  - "for 1:1 pymarc-equivalent behavior, use `permissive=True`"
  - the encoding-strictness divergence and how to handle it
  - any remaining behavioral gaps from bd-0x73.6 audit

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella.
- bd-0x73.6 — sibling: broader pymarc API compatibility audit. This
  bead specifically targets reader iteration shape; bd-0x73.6 covers
  everything else.
- GH issue #148 — original surfacing including pymarc 5.3.1 baseline
  repro (`pymarc/reader.py:190-191` — `current_exception` /
  `current_chunk` plumbing).
- `mrrc/__init__.py:1453-1460` — current `permissive=True` swallow
  path that needs the enrichment.
- `pymarc/exceptions.py` for the hardcoded-message exception
  classes; mrrc's typed exceptions extend those names so existing
  pymarc-shape `except` clauses keep working.
