title: Error coverage fixture + harness
description:
## Context

Phase A foundation for the bd-0x73 epic. The 2026-04-30 review chose
a TDD-inverted approach: build the failing tests first, watch every
unwired error code show up red, then wire the parse paths green one
at a time. This bead establishes the **single source of truth** all
later test/wiring work consumes.

## What to build

1. `tests/error_coverage.toml` — one entry per documented error code
   in `docs/reference/error-codes.md`. Each entry:

   ```toml
   [[case]]
   code = "E201"
   variant = "InvalidIndicator"
   slug = "indicator-not-digit-or-space"
   trigger_fixture = "tests/data/error_fixtures/e201_bad_indicator.bin"
   expected_context = ["record_index", "byte_offset",
                       "record_byte_offset", "field_tag",
                       "indicator_position", "found", "source_name",
                       "bytes_near"]
   wired = false   # flips to true as Phase B lands the wiring
   skip_reason = "unwired (bd-44z4 / bd-0x73)"
   recovery_modes = ["strict", "lenient", "permissive"]
   ```

2. `tests/data/error_fixtures/<code>_<slug>.bin` — minimal real
   malformed-MARC bytes that trigger each code. Hand-crafted, small
   (typically <500 bytes), checked in.

3. **Rust harness** (`tests/error_coverage.rs`) and **Python harness**
   (`tests/python/test_error_coverage.py`) that:
   - parameterize over the TOML entries
   - feed `trigger_fixture` bytes to a reader
   - assert the `variant` fires with `code` and `slug` matching
   - assert every field listed in `expected_context` is `Some(...)` /
     not None
   - run across each `recovery_mode` and assert the per-mode contract
     (strict raises; lenient yields with errors attached if defined;
     permissive yields None with last_exception populated if defined)
   - SKIPS cases where `wired = false` with a clear skip message that
     includes the bead reference

4. CI surfaces the **count of skipped cases** as a single number
   ("X/17 error codes wired"). The release gate (separate bead) asserts
   that count is 17/17 before tagging 0.8.1.

## Coverage matrix (initial set; mirrors error-codes.md)

E001 RecordLengthInvalid · E002 InvalidLeader · E003 BaseAddressInvalid ·
E004 BaseAddressNotFound · E005 TruncatedRecord · E006 EndOfRecordNotFound ·
E007 IoError · E099 FatalReaderError · E101 DirectoryInvalid ·
E105 FieldNotFound · E106 InvalidField · E201 InvalidIndicator ·
E202 BadSubfieldCode · E301 EncodingError · E401 XmlError ·
E402 JsonError · E404 WriterError

## Done when

- `tests/error_coverage.toml` exists with one entry per documented code.
- Each `wired = true` entry has a real `trigger_fixture` that the
  harness exercises end-to-end.
- Each `wired = false` entry has a fixture (or a clear placeholder)
  and a `skip_reason` referencing the wiring bead.
- Both harnesses run cleanly under `.cargo/check.sh`.
- A new line in CI output: "error coverage: X/17 codes wired".

## Why this matters

The v0.8 audit showed that wiring gaps are invisible to unit tests
because not-raising is not a panic. This fixture + harness makes
"absence of behavior" a first-class assertion.

Downstream consumers of this fixture:
- bd-0x73.3 (integration tests) — parameterizes over the same TOML
- bd-0x73.4 (proptest strategies) — seeds malformation strategies
  from the trigger_fixture entries
- bd-0x73.5 (fuzz error-classification harness) — cross-checks
  classifications against the same TOML
- 0.8.1 release-readiness gate (separate bead) — asserts wired = true
  for every entry

Single source of truth is the load-bearing property — change the
docs, change the TOML, and every test layer is regenerated.

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella tracker.
- bd-0x73.3 / bd-0x73.4 / bd-0x73.5 — sibling test layers; will be
  re-sequenced to consume this fixture.
- `docs/reference/error-codes.md` — the spec this fixture pins down.
close_reason: Phase A foundation merged in PR #159 (commit a52338a)

title: Baseline + incremental error-handling perf benchmarks + CI gate
description:
## Context

The bd-0x73 epic wires per-byte indicator and subfield-code checks into
the hot ISO 2709 parse path. This will cost something — we just don't
know how much. Without baseline numbers and a CI gate, perf regressions
ride along with correctness fixes invisibly.

The 2026-04-30 review set the threshold:

- **2% regression**: pause and assess before accepting.
- **5% regression**: hard fail.

## What to build

1. **Baseline benchmarks** committed before any wiring lands. Use the
   existing `benches/` Rust harness and `tests/python/test_benchmarks.py`
   (Python pytest-benchmark) infrastructure. Add at minimum:
   - `parse_50k_valid_records` (happy path; the regression-sensitive
     case)
   - `parse_50k_with_1pct_malformed` (recovery-mode performance — what
     does lenient cost when 1% of records have errors)
   - `parse_50k_all_strict` (all clean, strict mode; should match
     `parse_50k_valid_records` modulo overhead)
   - One Python-binding equivalent so we capture cross-FFI cost.

2. **Baseline file** committed under `benches/baselines/error_handling_v080.json`
   (or similar) capturing the numbers as of the pre-wiring main commit.
   Format: criterion-compatible or pytest-benchmark-compatible JSON.

3. **CI gate** in `.cargo/check.sh` (or a separate workflow step) that:
   - Runs the same benchmarks.
   - Compares against the baseline file.
   - Outputs a delta table (mean, ±%, vs baseline).
   - Exits with **warning** at >2% regression.
   - Exits with **failure** at >5% regression.
   - Both thresholds cite this bead in the failure message.

4. **Per-PR delta posting**: each Phase B wiring PR re-runs benchmarks
   in CI and posts the delta vs baseline as a comment (or just in the
   PR body). Keeps the cost of each individual wiring step visible.

## Done when

- Baseline benchmarks captured and committed before bd-44z4 / bd-0x73's
  E201/E202 wiring lands.
- CI gate runs benchmarks on every PR that touches `src/` and reports
  delta vs baseline.
- 2% warning / 5% failure thresholds enforced and cite this bead.
- `docs/contributing/benchmarking.md` (or equivalent) documents how to
  re-baseline when a deliberate trade-off is accepted.

## Open question

**When does the baseline reset?** Two options:
- (A) Baseline locks at v0.8.0; every Phase B PR compares against it,
  budget accumulates across the epic.
- (B) Baseline rolls forward after each merged PR; we only catch
  per-step regressions, not cumulative.

(A) is stricter and matches the spirit of "5% total budget across the
0.8.1 work." Recommend (A); document re-baseline procedure for 0.8.2.

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella; this gate enforces perf bound on the wiring beads.
- `benches/` — existing Rust criterion suite.
- `tests/python/` benchmarks marked with `-m benchmark` — existing
  Python suite.
- 2026-04-30 review (this conversation) — set 2%/5% thresholds.
close_reason: Phase A perf baseline + CI gate landed via PR #160

title: Pymarc API compatibility: comprehensive gap analysis and implementation plan
close_reason: Replaced by bd-NEW — found JSON schema mismatch and additional gaps during verification

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
Full pymarc API surface comparison (pymarc installed via uv, verified against live introspection) identified 24 gaps organized by implementation impact. Replaces bd-qjjs. Elevated to GitHub issue #65: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/65

---

## Tier 1: Structural / semantic divergences

These affect the fundamental object model and break the most pymarc-compatible code.

### A. Separate ControlField class

pymarc has no ControlField — both control and data fields are `pymarc.Field`, distinguished by `is_control_field()`. mrrc exposes a separate `ControlField` class in the Python wrapper. This breaks `isinstance(f, Field)` checks and means `get_fields()` returns mixed types.

**Fix:** Unify into a single Field class in the wrapper, with `is_control_field()` dispatching behavior.

### B. ControlField uses .value attribute; pymarc uses .data

pymarc control fields store content in `.data`. mrrc's ControlField uses `.value`. Also creates a naming collision with the `Field.value()` method (Gap G).

**Fix:** Rename to `.data` when unifying Field (Gap A). Provides both pymarc compat and clears the way for `value()`.

### C. Record convenience accessors are methods; pymarc uses @property

These 17 pymarc properties are all methods in mrrc: `title`, `author`, `isbn`, `issn`, `issn_title`, `issnl`, `subjects`, `notes`, `location`, `series`, `sudoc`, `publisher`, `pubyear`, `physicaldescription`, `uniformtitle`, `addedentries`.

**Fix:** Convert to `@property` in `mrrc/__init__.py`. Rust `_inner` methods stay as-is. Breaking change for `record.title()` callers — consider deprecation shim.

### D. Record.__getitem__ returns None for missing tags; pymarc raises KeyError

pymarc `record['999']` raises `KeyError`. mrrc returns `None`. Silently changes control flow for code using try/except KeyError.

**Fix:** Raise `KeyError` for missing tags. `Record.get(tag, default)` already exists for the None-returning pattern.

### E. Field.__str__ returns useless default repr

pymarc `str(field)` returns MARC display format: `=245  10$aThe Great Gatsby$cF. Scott Fitzgerald`. mrrc returns `<mrrc.Field object at 0x...>` because the Python wrapper has no `__str__` and `__getattr__` doesn't intercept dunder methods. The Rust `_inner.__str__` returns `Field(245)` which is also not pymarc-compatible.

**Fix:** Add `__str__` and `__repr__` to the wrapper Field class matching pymarc's format.

### F. Record.as_marc() / as_marc21() missing

No way to serialize a Record to ISO 2709 bytes. MARCWriter writes to files but there's no Record-level method returning `bytes`. Critical for round-trip workflows.

**Fix:** Implement in Rust (pure serialization, no GIL needed), expose via PyO3, add wrapper method.

---

## Tier 2: Missing methods and signature mismatches

### G. Field.value() missing

pymarc `field.value()` returns space-joined subfield values (or `.data` for control fields). mrrc has no equivalent. Depends on Gap B being resolved first (`.value` attribute → `.data`).

**Fix:** Add `def value(self) -> str` to the unified Field class.

### H. Field.format_field() missing

pymarc `field.format_field()` returns human-readable text without indicators or subfield codes. Used internally by Record property helpers like `title`.

**Fix:** Add `def format_field(self) -> str` to Field.

### I. Record.as_json(**kwargs) missing

pymarc `record.as_json(**kwargs)` wraps `json.dumps(record.as_dict(), **kwargs)`. mrrc only has `to_json()` with no kwargs. Keep `to_json()` in our public API.

**Fix:** Add `as_json(**kwargs)` built on `as_dict()`.

### J. Record.as_dict() missing

pymarc `record.as_dict()` returns a plain dict. `as_json()` is built on it.

**Fix:** Implement `as_dict()` in the wrapper, then build `as_json()` on top.

### K. parse_xml_to_array() missing

pymarc top-level function accepting file paths, file handles, and StringIO. mrrc has `xml_to_records()` (string-only input).

**Fix:** Add `parse_xml_to_array()` to `mrrc/__init__.py`. GIL policy: file paths and string content → entirely Rust, zero GIL. Python file objects → GIL only for `.read()`, then Rust parsing. Document preferred path-based usage with explicit examples and comments explaining why, consistent with MARCReader docs.

### L. add_field(*fields) signature mismatch

pymarc `add_field(self, *fields)` accepts multiple fields. mrrc accepts exactly one.

**Fix:** Change signature to `*fields` with a loop.

### M. remove_field(*fields) + remove_fields(*tags)

pymarc `remove_field` accepts multiple fields. mrrc accepts one (Field or str). pymarc also has `remove_fields(*tags)` for bulk removal by tag — mrrc has no equivalent.

**Fix:** Change `remove_field` to accept `*fields`. Add `remove_fields(*tags)`.

### N. add_ordered_field() / add_grouped_field() missing

pymarc has both for inserting fields in tag-sorted or tag-grouped positions.

**Fix:** Implement in wrapper. `add_ordered_field` inserts maintaining tag sort order. `add_grouped_field` inserts after the last field with the same tag.

### O. Field.add_subfield() missing pos parameter

pymarc: `add_subfield(self, code, value, pos=None)`. mrrc only supports append.

**Fix:** Add optional `pos` parameter. May need Rust-side support for positional insert.

### P. Field.as_marc() / as_marc21() missing

pymarc can serialize individual fields to ISO 2709 bytes.

**Fix:** Implement in Rust, expose via PyO3. No GIL needed.

---

## Tier 3: Naming and return type mismatches

### Q. Property name mismatches

- pymarc `physicaldescription` → mrrc `physical_description` (extra underscore)
- pymarc `uniformtitle` → mrrc `uniform_title` (extra underscore)
- pymarc `addedentries` → mrrc: missing entirely (returns 700/710/711/730 fields)

**Fix:** Add pymarc-compatible names as properties (Gap C). Keep mrrc names as aliases if desired.

### R. pubyear return type

pymarc returns `Optional[str]` (e.g. `"2020"`). mrrc returns `Optional[int]` (`2020`). Confirmed via live test.

**Fix:** Return `str` from the property wrapper to match pymarc.

---

## Tier 4: Lower priority

These are real gaps but unlikely to block most pymarc-compatible code.

**S.** `decode_marc()` — semi-internal, populates Record from raw bytes. Uncommon in user code.
**T.** Reader/writer variants — JSONReader, JSONWriter, MARCMakerReader, XMLWriter, TextWriter.
**U.** Exception hierarchy — pymarc has ~15 specific exception classes. mrrc exposes generic errors.
**V.** MARC8 character encoding support.
**W.** Convenience functions — `map_records()`, `map_xml()`, `parse_json_to_array()`, `record_to_xml_node()`.
**X.** Constants — `LEADER_LEN`, `DIRECTORY_ENTRY_LEN`, `END_OF_FIELD`, `END_OF_RECORD`, `SUBFIELD_INDICATOR`, etc.

---

## GIL policy

Avoid holding the GIL whenever possible.

- **as_json(), as_marc(), as_marc21(), as_dict():** In-memory, single record — no GIL concern.
- **parse_xml_to_array():** File paths and string content → entirely Rust, zero GIL. Python file objects → GIL only for `.read()`, minimize window.
- **Field.value(), format_field(), as_marc():** Pure computation, no GIL concern.

## Documentation

For parse_xml_to_array() and any other new functions accepting flexible input types, document the preferred GIL-free approach with explicit examples and comments, consistent with existing MARCReader docs:

```python
# Preferred: file path — read + parse entirely in Rust, zero GIL
records = mrrc.parse_xml_to_array("collection.xml")

# Also good: string content — parse entirely in Rust, zero GIL
records = mrrc.parse_xml_to_array(xml_string)

# Works but slower: Python file object requires GIL for .read()
with open("collection.xml") as f:
    records = mrrc.parse_xml_to_array(f)
```

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Correction: Gap V (MARC8 encoding) removed — mrrc has had MARC8 support for a long time. pymarc exports specific convenience names (MARC8ToUnicode, marc8_to_unicode, map_marc8_field, map_marc8_record) that mrrc doesn't re-export at the top level, but the underlying functionality is there. Not a real gap.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
Correction to the correction: Gap V IS a real gap. mrrc has MARC8 support in the Rust core but exports NOTHING MARC8-related at the Python level. pymarc exports 4 things:

- MARC8ToUnicode class — stateful converter (G0/G1 charset tracking)
- marc8_to_unicode(marc8, hide_utf8_warnings=False) → str — standalone conversion function
- map_marc8_field(field) → Field — convert a single field's MARC8 data to Unicode
- map_marc8_record(record) → Record — convert all fields in a record

Fix: Expose mrrc's Rust MARC8 functionality via PyO3 bindings, then add pymarc-compatible wrapper names in mrrc/__init__.py. The Rust implementation likely handles the actual conversion already; the gap is purely in the Python API surface.

title: Thread ParseContext through recovery.rs salvage path
close_reason: Duplicate of bd-3ois (created accidentally by a failed jq parse during follow-up bead filing). Work delivered in PR #100 commit 60fd27d.

title: Add Python integration test for failing-read OSError path (E007)
description:
## Context

E007 `IoError` is documented in `docs/reference/error-codes.md#E007` as raising Python's built-in `OSError` rather than a typed `mrrc.IoError` — a deliberate pymarc-compat decision implemented at `src-python/src/error.rs:381-385` (the `describe()` function returns `Err` for the `IoError` variant, routing through the fallback to `PyIOError`).

The Rust harness in `tests/error_coverage.rs` asserts `MarcError::IoError` fires from `MarcReader::read_record` via a `FailingReader`. The Python harness in `tests/python/test_error_coverage.py` skips with `"Python-side OSError is covered by an integration test rather than this typed-class harness"` — but no such integration test exists today. The contract is unverified from the Python side, so a future regression that flips `MARCReader` to swallow, retype, or otherwise diverge from `OSError` would slip through.

Surfaced reviewing PR #193 (bd-mk6z); the skip reason in the harness over-promises and should be tightened to reference this bead.

## What needs to change

1. **New test in `tests/python/test_errors.py`** (or a focused new module): inject a Python file-like whose `read(n)` raises `OSError("synthetic read failure")`, hand it to `mrrc.MARCReader` in `recovery_mode="strict"`, iterate, and assert:
   - `OSError` is raised (catching `mrrc.MrrcException` would *not* match — that distinction is the test's whole point).
   - The exception message threads through the binding (per `src-python/src/buffered_reader.rs:35-39`, the wrapper produces `"Failed to read leader from Python file: OSError: <orig>"` — assert the original message appears as a substring).
2. **Same coverage in `recovery_mode="lenient"`** if applicable — the failing-read path bypasses recovery entirely (errors at the byte-read layer never reach `note_recovery_error`), so the lenient mode case should also raise `OSError`. Confirm this is the contract or revise.
3. **Tighten the harness skip reason** at `tests/python/test_error_coverage.py:122-128` to reference this bead and drop the "covered by an integration test" wording — replace with "Python-side OSError is asserted by `tests/python/test_errors.py::test_failing_read_raises_oserror` (bd-1a5a)" once the test lands.

## Done when

- A test exists that asserts `MARCReader` raises plain `OSError` (not `mrrc.MrrcException`) when the underlying file-like's `read()` raises.
- The harness skip reason no longer claims a non-existent integration test.

## Why P3

Pure docs-vs-test gap; the production code path is exercised by `mrrc/__init__.py:1490` whenever real I/O fails, and the Rust core's `MarcError::IoError` typed variant is already harness-asserted. This bead closes a verification gap, not a behavior gap.

## References

- `docs/reference/error-codes.md:196-213` — E007 docs.
- `src-python/src/error.rs:381-385` — deliberate route-through-fallback for IoError.
- `src-python/src/buffered_reader.rs:35-39` — wrapper that turns failing-read into `ParseError::io_error`.
- `tests/python/test_error_coverage.py:122-128` — over-promising skip reason to tighten.
close_reason: Landed via PR #200 squash-merge; GH issue #199 auto-closed

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/199

title: Remove dead profiling harnesses and fix broken references in the bench target
description:
Four of the seven [[bench]] entries are harness=false main() programs left over from early-development manual profiling: profiling_harness.rs, detailed_profiling.rs, rayon_profiling.rs, rayon_file_io_profiling.rs. They produce no CodSpeed data and no CI signal; they only compile (and would slow bd-9wk8's planned 'cargo bench --no-run'). Two carry active problems: detailed_profiling.rs prints pointers to docs/design/PROFILING_PLAN.md and docs/design/profiling/RUST_SINGLE_THREADED_PROFILING_RESULTS.md, neither of which exists, and reaches into record.fields directly (an internal surface that can drift); rayon_file_io_profiling.rs writes scratch files to /tmp/mrrc_profile_test, violating the project's never-write-to-/tmp convention.

What to do: delete all four .rs files and their [[bench]] stanzas in Cargo.toml, OR, if any are still wanted for manual profiling, move them out of the bench target (e.g. into examples/ or a scripts/profiling area), fix the broken doc references and the /tmp write (use a project-local gitignored tmp/ dir), and document them as manual-only. Default to deletion unless a concrete current use is identified.

Done when: the bench target contains only benches that produce CI signal; no bench file references a nonexistent doc; no bench writes to /tmp; '.cargo/check.sh' passes; CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased].

Do not sacrifice performance or pymarc API compatibility. Goal: easier for new devs and agents to understand. comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #81: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/81 --json

title: Add batch corpus-validation diagnostic report API
description:
## Problem

Validators exist per-item (`IndicatorValidator` in `src/validation.rs`, `IsbnValidator` in `src/bibliographic_helpers.rs`, `RecordStructureValidator` in `src/record_validation.rs`, `EncodingValidator` in `src/encoding_validation.rs`) but there is no batch entry point that runs all validators over a corpus and aggregates findings into a structured report. This is a natural fit for ETL use cases where users want to answer "what's wrong with my catalog?" and get a shaped answer, not per-record stack traces.

## What to do

1. Create `src/diagnostics.rs` with:
   - `DiagnosticReport` struct — aggregate stats: total records processed, error counts per validator category, sample offending records (e.g., first N per category), per-tag error breakdown
   - `validate_corpus<I: Iterator<Item = Result<Record>>>(iter: I, config: DiagnosticConfig) -> DiagnosticReport`
   - `DiagnosticConfig` — which validators to run, sample retention budget, how to treat read errors
   - `impl Serialize for DiagnosticReport` via serde so reports can be written as JSON
2. Expose in Python bindings: `mrrc.validate_corpus(reader)` returning a `DiagnosticReport` object with attributes/methods matching the Rust struct.
3. Integrate with the CLI from the separate CLI bead (`mrrc validate` subcommand should use this API internally) — flag this bead as depending on or providing the backend for that one.
4. New docs page `docs/guides/validating-large-corpora.md` with worked examples.
5. Tests:
   - Corpus of known-bad records with mixed error types; verify counts match expectations
   - JSON serialization roundtrip
   - Python binding exposes all fields

## Done when

- `src/diagnostics.rs` with `DiagnosticReport`, `DiagnosticConfig`, and `validate_corpus()`
- Python bindings expose the same functionality
- `DiagnosticReport` serializes to JSON cleanly
- Tests cover: aggregation correctness, sample retention, JSON roundtrip, Python interop
- `docs/guides/validating-large-corpora.md` with examples
- Integration with `mrrc validate` CLI subcommand (if CLI bead done) or standalone usage documented
- CHANGELOG entry under next version
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes

title: Decide handle semantics for query-DSL field results (fields_matching, fields_by_indicator, fields_in_range)
description: bd-blja made the pymarc-compatible accessors return live write-through handles, but query/filter results (fields_matching, fields_matching_range, fields_matching_pattern, fields_matching_value, fields_by_indicator, fields_in_range) remain detached snapshots: the PyO3 methods return filtered Vec<PyField> with no occurrence info, so the Python wrapper cannot bind them to record positions. Either extend the PyO3 query methods to return (occurrence, field) pairs and wrap as handles (one-sentence contract, no carve-outs), or commit to documenting query results as snapshots permanently. Currently documented as snapshots in docs/reference/python-api.md (Field handles paragraph).

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Design input to bd-d7aq.7 (query/helper trait consolidation) — handle-vs-snapshot semantics for query results should be decided as part of that consolidation.

title: Add Field.is_control_field() for pymarc compatibility
description:
pymarc's Field.is_control_field() returns True if the field is a control field (tag 001-009). In mrrc, control fields and data fields are separate types (ControlField vs Field), so there's no is_control_field() method on either.

For pymarc compatibility, add is_control_field() to both classes in mrrc/__init__.py:
- ControlField.is_control_field() -> returns True
- Field.is_control_field() -> returns False

This mirrors pymarc's unified Field type where is_control_field() checks the tag. Since mrrc already separates the types, the return values are static per class.

pymarc also has Field.is_subject_field() (checks tag == '6xx') — consider adding that too for completeness, though it's less commonly used.

Pure Python wrapper additions — no Rust changes needed. Update _mrrc.pyi stubs accordingly.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #60, merged

title: Add a type annotation to the MARCReader wrapper's file_obj parameter
description:
griffe emits 'No type or annotation for parameter' warnings on every docs build (bd-kxkq) for several public wrapper constructors in `mrrc/__init__.py`:

- `MARCReader.__init__` parameter `file_obj` (line ~1605)
- `Record.__init__` parameter `fields` (line ~893)
- `Field.__init__` parameters `subfields` and `indicators` (lines ~214-215)

Each of these is a public constructor parameter left without a type annotation while its siblings are annotated. Add annotations matching the accepted types (e.g. `file_obj: Union[str, os.PathLike[str], bytes, bytearray, BinaryIO]` or `Any`; `fields: Optional[List['Field']]`; `subfields: Optional[List['Subfield']]`; `indicators: Optional[List[str]]`). Silences the griffe warnings and improves type-checker coverage of the public constructors. Trivial annotations-only change; surfaced by bd-kxkq, out of scope for the docs PR itself.
close_reason: Annotated previously-bare wrapper constructor params (MARCReader/MARCWriter file_obj, Record.fields, Field.subfields/indicators); griffe warnings cleared. Merged in PR #263.

title: Add __str__ and __repr__ to Python Record wrapper
description: The Python Record wrapper class in mrrc/__init__.py does not define __str__ or __repr__. Python's data model skips __getattr__ for dunder methods, so str(rec) returns the default '<mrrc.Record object at 0x...>' instead of delegating to the Rust PyRecord's __str__ (which returns 'Record(type=a)'). Need to add explicit __str__ and __repr__ methods that delegate to self._inner. Reported in https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/57 comment.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #60, merged

title: Add decode_marc8 fuzz target
description:
## Context

Follow-up to bd-dfn2 (cargo-fuzz infrastructure). bd-dfn2's "Add additional fuzz targets over time" list explicitly named `decode_marc8` as a follow-up alongside `parse_marcxml` (bd-3t62) and `parse_leader` (bd-gbgx). The formal-methods verification strategy in mrrc-testbed groups MARC-8 with the high-bug-risk encoding paths.

## Why

MARC-8 is a stateful, escape-driven multi-byte encoding (ISO 2022 family) layered on top of MARC. The decoder is one of the highest-bug-risk surfaces in the crate: shift-in/shift-out sequences, undefined codepoints, truncated escapes, and pathological state-machine transitions are all input-shaped attack surfaces a generic ISO 2709 fuzzer (`parse_record`) reaches only incidentally. Targeted fuzzing on the encoding state machine surfaces panics and infinite loops faster than the broader target.

## What to do

1. Add `fuzz/fuzz_targets/decode_marc8.rs` calling the public MARC-8 decode entry point (`mrrc::encoding::*` — confirm the exact public API when starting).
2. Seed corpus with small MARC-8-encoded byte sequences (extract from existing fixtures in `tests/data/` that contain MARC-8 fields, or hand-craft a few covering G0/G1 set transitions, ESC-based set designations, and truncated escape sequences).
3. Add the `[[bin]]` entry in `fuzz/Cargo.toml` and extend `.github/workflows/fuzz.yml` to run the new target (matrix entry alongside the existing targets).
4. Update the "What is tested" table in `docs/contributing/fuzzing.md` to mark `decode_marc8` Active and reference this bead.

## Done when

- `cd fuzz && cargo +nightly fuzz run decode_marc8 -- -max_total_time=60` builds and runs locally.
- Nightly CI exercises the target alongside `parse_record` and `roundtrip_binary`.
- Any findings fixed (with reproducer copied to `tests/data/fuzz-regressions/decode_marc8/` as a regression) or filed as separate beads.
- CHANGELOG entry under `[Unreleased]` (or the next applicable section).

## Out of scope (post-v0.8)

This is post-v0.8 follow-up; v0.8 is consolidating the binary ISO 2709 reader/writer/error story and the MARC-8 decoder is an encoding-layer concern outside that scope.
close_reason: Done in #255: decode_marc8 fuzz target added via encoding::decode_bytes(.., MarcEncoding::Marc8); EACC ESC multibyte seed. Smoke-ran clean.

comments.text: Detached the 'blocks: bd-bfrh' edge (2026-05-28). bd-bfrh decided the Option C question (variant-targeted fuzzing — closed as moot); this bead is an Option B format-surface fuzz target that delivers value independently of that decision, so it should not have been gated on it. Parent-child link to bd-sdz8 retained.

title: ci: add paths-ignore to skip workflows on docs-only changes
close_reason: Duplicate of bd-loe6 (delivered in PR #136); empty description, same title/priority/date.

title: ci: add concurrency-cancel and timeout-minutes to PR workflows
description:
The seven PR-triggered workflows (build.yml, python-build.yml, test.yml, benchmark-rust.yml, benchmark-python.yml, lint.yml, semver.yml) have neither a concurrency group with cancel-in-progress nor a job-level timeout-minutes. Two consequences, both zero-coverage-loss to fix:

1. A force-push to an open PR leaves the prior commit's jobs running to completion — the assessment measured ~46 jobs/PR, so a quick succession of pushes can pile up dozens of stale runs. Add a per-workflow concurrency group keyed on workflow + ref (e.g. group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}, cancel-in-progress: true) so a new push cancels the superseded run. Note: docs.yml already has a concurrency group with cancel-in-progress: false on purpose (pages deploys should not be interrupted) — do not change that one; this is about the PR CI workflows.

2. No timeout-minutes means jobs inherit GitHub's 6-hour default. There is history of 45-48 minute hangs on these workflows; a hung job holds a runner for hours. Add a sensible timeout-minutes to each job (well above normal run time — e.g. 30-45 min depending on the workflow's measured duration) as a safety net.

Done when: every PR-triggered CI workflow has a cancel-in-progress concurrency group and a timeout-minutes on its jobs; docs.yml's deliberate cancel-in-progress: false is left intact; CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] if any contributor-visible behavior changes (likely none — these are CI-internal).
close_reason: Done in #258: all PR-triggered workflows gained cancel-in-progress concurrency groups + per-job timeout-minutes; docs.yml's deliberate cancel-in-progress:false left intact.

title: Evaluate multi-subfield matching for SubfieldPatternQuery and SubfieldValueQuery for 1.0
description:
GitHub issue #225 (https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/225) flags an API-shape asymmetry across the query DSL types as a potential 1.0 improvement. This is a design consideration to evaluate, not yet an agreed change.

`FieldQuery` lets a caller require multiple subfields via `has_subfield(code)` chained calls and the `has_subfields(["a", "x", "v"])` shorthand. By contrast, `SubfieldPatternQuery(tag, subfield_code, pattern)` and `SubfieldValueQuery(tag, subfield_code, value)` each accept only a single subfield code and a single pattern/value. There is no way to express "match this pattern/value across any of several subfields" in one query object.

The reporter notes this is both a small learning curve and a possible area for improvement, but explicitly does not want to change anything yet: the motivating examples for matching a pattern or value across multiple subfields are not yet clear.

Relevant stubs in `mrrc/_mrrc.pyi`:

- `FieldQuery`: `has_subfield(code)` (single, chainable) plus `has_subfields` shorthand
- `SubfieldPatternQuery.__init__(self, tag, subfield_code, pattern, *, negate=False)`
- `SubfieldValueQuery.__init__(self, tag, subfield_code, value, *, partial=False, negate=False)`

The underlying Rust implementations live in `src/` (query DSL module) with PyO3 wrappers in `src-python/src/`.

## What to decide

- Are there real cataloging cases where matching one pattern/value across several subfields in a single query is genuinely useful (versus composing multiple single-subfield queries)?
- If yes, what is the cleanest API: accept a list of subfield codes on these two query types, or a separate builder, while keeping the four query types coherent with each other?
- Any change touches all four layers (Rust core, PyO3 bindings, Python wrapper/re-export, type stubs) plus the query-dsl guide.

## Done when

- A decision is recorded: either concrete motivating examples plus a chosen API shape (which would then spawn an implementation ticket), or a documented rationale for leaving the single-subfield shape as-is for 1.0.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Tracks GitHub issue #225 (https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/225) — the query DSL multi-subfield API-shape point flagged for 1.0.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Design input to bd-d7aq.7 (query/helper trait consolidation) — decide multi-subfield matching as part of that consolidation rather than in isolation.

title: Add parse_mods fuzz target
description:
## Context

Added to epic bd-sdz8 after the 2026-06-04 scope assessment: the MODS reader is the only XML read surface with no fuzz target and no tracking bead — the epic's original scope note listed CSV/DC/BIBFRAME as out of scope but omitted MODS entirely.

## Why

`mods_xml_to_record` and `mods_xml_to_records` (src/mods.rs) consume arbitrary XML byte streams via quick-xml and apply ~76 KB of bespoke structural mapping logic on top — a third XML reader alongside marcxml (bd-3t62 covers that one). MODS fuzzing reaches namespace handling, malformed CDATA, unbalanced tags, deep nesting, and MODS-specific structural mapping paths that neither parse_record nor parse_marcxml can exercise.

## What to do

1. Add `fuzz/fuzz_targets/parse_mods.rs` calling `mods_xml_to_record` (and/or `mods_xml_to_records`). Discard the `Result` so libfuzzer only flags panics, OOMs, and timeouts — `Err` on malformed XML is correct behavior, not a bug.
2. Seed corpus from MODS fixtures under `tests/`, or generate a handful by converting existing binary MARC fixtures through the library's MODS writer and committing the output.
3. Add the `[[bin]]` entry in `fuzz/Cargo.toml` and extend `.github/workflows/fuzz.yml` to run the new target alongside the existing matrix entries.
4. Update the "What is tested" table in `docs/contributing/fuzzing.md` to mark `parse_mods` Active and reference this bead.

## Done when

- `cd fuzz && cargo +nightly fuzz run parse_mods -- -max_total_time=60` builds and runs locally.
- Nightly CI exercises the target alongside the existing targets.
- Any findings fixed (with reproducer copied to `tests/data/fuzz-regressions/parse_mods/` as a regression) or filed as separate beads.
- CHANGELOG entry under `[Unreleased]` (or the next applicable section).
close_reason: Done in #255: parse_mods fuzz target via mods::mods_xml_to_record. Smoke-ran clean.

title: Add Record.get(tag) alias for pymarc compatibility
description:
pymarc's Record.get(tag, default=None) returns the first field matching a tag, or a default value. mrrc has the equivalent as Record.get_field(tag) but lacks the .get() alias.

Add a Record.get() method to the Python wrapper in mrrc/__init__.py that delegates to get_field() with an optional default parameter:

    def get(self, tag: str, default=None):
        return self.get_field(tag) or default

This is a pure Python wrapper addition — no Rust changes needed. Update _mrrc.pyi stubs accordingly.

pymarc source: Record.get() delegates to Record.__getitem__ with a try/except KeyError fallback.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #60, merged

title: Thread ParseContext through recovery.rs salvage path
description:
## Problem

src/recovery.rs constructs MarcError values via the legacy migration helpers (truncated_msg, invalid_field_msg) which produce errors with all positional fields set to None. Callers of recovery.rs receive errors with no record index, byte offset, or source filename — contradicting bd-qzew's contract that errors carry positional metadata in all recovery modes.

Note: as of bd-qzew, the recovery dispatch path from the readers is currently dead code (the buffer-length comparison can never trigger because read_record_data returns a buffer of full length even on a short read). So the practical runtime impact today is zero, but when the dead-code path is properly wired up in a future change, recovery.rs will need ParseContext threading.

## Done when

- recovery.rs functions accept &ParseContext and use ctx.err_* methods to construct MarcError values with positional metadata.
- Callers (the 3 readers) pass their ctx through the recovery dispatch.
- Tests in lenient/permissive mode verify recovered errors carry record_index, byte_offset, source_name, and (where applicable) record_control_number.
- The dead-code dispatch path is either fixed (so recovery actually fires) or explicitly removed with a clear note about the latent bug.
close_reason: Delivered in PR2; try_recover_record now takes &ParseContext and emits enriched errors. Dispatch path from reader still dead code (latent bug pre-existing this PR).

title: Review/remove "Out of scope for this guide" section in error-handling.md
description:
## Context

`docs/reference/error-handling.md` currently contains a standalone "Out of
scope for this guide" subsection (currently ~lines 155-165) that lists what
the page does *not* claim compatibility for:

- Reader/writer constructor signatures or behavior differences.
- `Record`, `Field`, `Subfield` API differences.
- Format-coverage differences (MARCXML/MARCJSON edge cases, encoding, etc.).
- Performance and memory behavior.

This was added in response to over-stated "compatibility" claims in an
earlier draft. Now that the page has been scoped down elsewhere, this
standalone disclaimer may no longer earn its keep.

## Why review

1. **Likely redundant with an earlier passage.** The "Pymarc exception
   compatibility" intro (currently ~lines 56-60) already says:

   > Other compatibility surfaces — record APIs, reader/writer constructor
   > shapes, format coverage, and performance characteristics — are out of
   > scope for this page; consult the Python API reference and Rust API
   > reference for those.

   The standalone "Out of scope" subsection repeats the same list in a
   different format. Two disclaimers, same content.

2. **Feels defensive.** A dedicated subsection titled "Out of scope for
   this guide" reads more like a response to past reviewer feedback than
   a natural part of the document. Readers reaching a page called
   "Error Handling" don't typically need a formal denial that it covers
   record APIs.

3. **Placement is awkward.** It sits between "Subclass behavior reference"
   (a factual catch-hierarchy table, currently ~lines 167-174) and
   "`MARCReader.current_exception` / `current_chunk`" (a specific
   compatibility note), interrupting a flow of concrete content with a
   generic boundary statement.

## What to do

1. Re-read the current state of `docs/reference/error-handling.md` (the
   file may have moved on since this bead was filed).
2. Decide one of:
   - **Remove it outright.** The lines 56-60 passage already sets scope;
     the standalone subsection is redundant.
   - **Fold it into the intro.** If the intro passage needs the
     bullet-list specificity, lift the bullets up there and drop the
     standalone subsection.
   - **Keep it but shrink.** If there's a reason it earns its keep
     (e.g., a specific reviewer concern still applies), tighten it to
     a single sentence and justify in the commit message.
3. Run `.cargo/check.sh --quick` (docs build is part of the full check,
   not quick, but make sure nothing else regressed).
4. Spot-check the rendered mkdocs page if docs are being actively
   reviewed in this cycle.

## Done when

- No redundant "out of scope" disclaimers in the page.
- Any remaining scope/boundary language lives in one place, not two.
- No referenced URLs (Python API reference, Rust API reference) are broken
  by the edit.
close_reason: Delivered in #108 (merged to main)

title: Reconcile orphaned Python performance tests with the benchmark marker scheme
description:
The Python CodSpeed run selects '-m "benchmark and not slow"'. test_memory_profiling.py has zero @pytest.mark.benchmark markers and test_performance_analysis.py has one of roughly nine tests marked. Those unmarked tests run in neither the pytest-benchmark matrix (which uses --benchmark-only and so skips non-benchmark tests) nor CodSpeed, so their assertions about queue capacity, batch boundaries, GIL-release overhead, and thread speedup execute in no perf gate at all. Several of them are really correctness/invariant checks (batch boundary at 100/200/201, no-leak-on-iteration, empty-file-no-crash) rather than throughput benchmarks.

What to do: triage each test in these two files. Promote genuine throughput benchmarks to @pytest.mark.benchmark so CodSpeed sees them; move correctness/invariant tests into the normal pytest suite (a non-benchmark module) so they run in the standard test job; delete anything redundant. Record the disposition so it is clear nothing was silently dropped.

Done when: every test in test_memory_profiling.py and test_performance_analysis.py either runs in the CodSpeed gate (benchmark marker) or runs in the normal correctness suite, with no test stranded outside both; '.cargo/check.sh' passes (python tests included).

title: Add parse_marcxml fuzz target
description:
## Context

Follow-up to bd-dfn2 (cargo-fuzz infrastructure). bd-dfn2's "Add additional fuzz targets over time" list explicitly named `parse_marcxml` as a follow-up alongside `parse_record` (active) and `decode_marc8` (bd-2dia). The formal-methods verification strategy in mrrc-testbed groups XML reader fuzzing with the high-bug-risk parser surfaces.

## Why

mrrc's MARCXML reader (`mrrc::MarcXmlReader`) consumes arbitrary XML byte streams via a third-party XML library (`quick-xml` or similar — confirm at implementation time) and applies its own structural validation on top of generic XML parsing. XML fuzzing exercises edge cases that binary MARC fuzzing won't reach: namespace handling, malformed CDATA, unbalanced tags, deeply nested structures, encoding-declaration mismatches, and the boundary between XML-spec parsing errors and MARCXML-spec structural errors.

## What to do

1. Add `fuzz/fuzz_targets/parse_marcxml.rs` calling the public MARCXML reader entry point. The harness should discard the `Result` so libfuzzer only flags panics, OOMs, and timeouts — `Err(MarcError)` on malformed XML is correct behavior, not a bug.
2. Seed corpus from any small MARCXML fixtures under `tests/` (or generate a handful by serializing existing binary MARC fixtures via the library's MARCXML writer and committing the output).
3. Add the `[[bin]]` entry in `fuzz/Cargo.toml` and extend `.github/workflows/fuzz.yml` to run the new target (matrix entry alongside the existing targets).
4. Update the "What is tested" table in `docs/contributing/fuzzing.md` to mark `parse_marcxml` Active and reference this bead.

## Done when

- `cd fuzz && cargo +nightly fuzz run parse_marcxml -- -max_total_time=60` builds and runs locally.
- Nightly CI exercises the target alongside `parse_record` and `roundtrip_binary`.
- Any findings fixed (with reproducer copied to `tests/data/fuzz-regressions/parse_marcxml/` as a regression) or filed as separate beads.
- CHANGELOG entry under `[Unreleased]` (or the next applicable section).

## Out of scope (post-v0.8)

This is post-v0.8 follow-up; v0.8 is consolidating the binary ISO 2709 reader/writer/error story and the MARCXML reader is a separate format-layer concern outside that scope. (Note: GitHub issue #112 is a v0.8 MARCXML reader bug fix, narrower than full fuzz coverage.)
close_reason: Done in #255: parse_marcxml fuzz target via marcxml::marcxml_to_record. Smoke-ran clean.

title: Stable error codes with help URLs and reference docs page
description:
## Problem

With core error enrichment (bd-qzew) landed, error messages carry rich context — but there's no stable identifier users can match against programmatically, and no canonical URL pointing to an explanation of each error. Rust-compiler-style codes (`E0601`) and help URLs solve both: `err.code() == "E201"` enables programmatic handling without class-name sniffing, and `err.help_url()` gives `mrrc explain E201`-style ergonomics.

## Background

Searched the ecosystem — no shared MARC parser error-code standard exists. MARC 21 / ISO 2709 are silent on parser errors; pymarc uses class names; marc4j uses free-form messages via a SAX-style `ErrorHandler` (`warning`/`error`/`fatalError`); MARCEdit has internal negative error numbers that aren't publicly documented and use a non-colliding namespace. We have freedom to design our own scheme.

## Design

**Numeric + slug, both stable, both useful** — matches Rust's `E0601` + "unused-variable" convention.

```rust
err.code()       // "E201"              — canonical stable ID
err.slug()       // "invalid_indicator" — human-friendly, also stable
err.help_url()   // "https://mrrc.dev/reference/error-codes/#E201"
```

Python equivalent: `err.code`, `err.slug`, `err.help_url` exposed as instance attributes.

### Code namespace spans all three record types

Errors from `MarcReader` (Bibliographic), `AuthorityReader`, and `HoldingsReader` share the same code namespace. A `record_length_invalid` condition gets `E001` regardless of which record type was being decoded — the failure mode is the same at the ISO 2709 level. This is consistent with the shared `ParseContext` abstraction bd-qzew establishes.

### Parse-side vs writer-side errors

The code table includes both parse-path and writer-path errors. Parse-path errors (E0xx–E3xx) always populate `byte_offset`; writer-path errors (e.g., `E403 write_needs_record`) have `byte_offset: None` since they don't occur during byte consumption. The docs entry for each code should make this clear so downstream handlers don't treat `byte_offset: None` as a bug.

## Initial code table

Grouped by parse phase, staggered ranges for expansion room (~80 slots per range):

| Range | Phase | Example codes |
|---|---|---|
| **E0xx** | Stream / leader | E001 `record_length_invalid`, E002 `leader_invalid`, E003 `base_address_invalid`, E004 `base_address_not_found`, E005 `truncated_record`, E006 `end_of_record_not_found`, E007 `io_error` |
| **E1xx** | Directory / field header | E101 `directory_invalid`, E102 `directory_entry_out_of_bounds`, E103 `invalid_tag`, E104 `field_terminator_missing`, E105 `field_not_found` |
| **E2xx** | Subfield / indicator | E201 `invalid_indicator`, E202 `bad_subfield_code`, E203 `subfield_delimiter_missing`, E204 `empty_subfield_value` |
| **E3xx** | Encoding | E301 `utf8_invalid`, E302 `marc8_invalid_escape`, E303 `marc8_unknown_charset` |
| **E4xx** | Serialization / writer | E401 `marcxml_invalid`, E402 `marcjson_invalid`, E403 `write_needs_record`, E404 `record_too_large_for_iso2709` |
| **Wxxx** | Warnings (pymarc parity) | W001 `bad_subfield_code_warning`, W002 `missing_linked_fields` |

~23 codes at launch, ranges reserve ~80 slots each for future growth.

## Docs structure

Single reference page, manual maintenance (Option A — docs are source of truth), explicit numeric anchors via mkdocs-material's attribute-list extension so anchors are decoupled from heading slug. If we rename a slug later, the URL still resolves.

`docs/reference/error-codes.md` — one page, one `## E0xx — `slug` { #E0xx }` heading per code.

Each entry shape:

```markdown
## E201 — `invalid_indicator` { #E201 }

Raised when a variable-data field's indicator byte is not valid for the given tag.

**Context:** Parse-side.
**Applies to:** Bibliographic, Authority, Holdings readers.
**Populates:** `record_index`, `field_tag`, `indicator_position`, `found`, `expected`, `byte_offset`, `record_byte_offset`. May also populate: `record_control_number`, `source`.

**Common causes.** Source systems emitting local-use indicators; records round-tripped through non-conformant ILSes; sloppy cataloging from pre-2000s records.

**How to recover.** Use `recovery_mode="lenient"` to coerce unknown indicators to space (0x20). Use `recovery_mode="strict"` to reject.

**Example error:**
[code block with rendered one-liner]

**Related:** [E202](#E202), [E203](#E203).
```

The three metadata lines (`Context`, `Applies to`, `Populates`) give users writing programmatic handlers everything they need to know before reading prose: which reader surfaces it, whether `byte_offset` will be populated, and which fields they can reliably destructure.

## Stability policy

Two rules, non-negotiable, documented in CONTRIBUTING.md:

1. **Never re-purpose a retired code.** If a check goes away, the docs entry stays and reads "retired in v0.x; see [EYYY](#EYYY) for the replacement".
2. **Never renumber.** If the grouping feels wrong later, it stays wrong — URLs users paste into chat have to keep resolving.

## What to do

1. Add `code()`, `slug()`, `help_url()` methods to `MarcError` in `src/error.rs`, each a match expression over the variants.
2. Expose equivalent `code`, `slug`, `help_url` attributes on each Python exception class in `mrrc/__init__.py`, with matching constants.
3. Decide whether the code appears in the default `Display` one-liner (e.g., `[E201] [record 847 · ...]`) or only in `repr`/`detailed()`. Pick based on what looks cleanest during implementation.
4. Write `docs/reference/error-codes.md` with all ~23 initial codes using the entry shape above — including the `Context` / `Applies to` / `Populates` metadata lines per entry.
5. Configure mkdocs-material's attribute-list extension if not already enabled. Add nav entry under Reference.
6. Add stability policy section to CONTRIBUTING.md.
7. Optional follow-up (not required to close): `mrrc explain E201` CLI subcommand (if the CLI bead lands first).

## Depends on

- **bd-qzew** (core error enrichment must land first to provide the error shape this attaches to, including the `ParseContext` abstraction and the per-variant field-requirements table).

## Done when

- Every `MarcError` variant has a stable code + slug returned by `code()` / `slug()`.
- Every Python exception class has matching `code` / `slug` / `help_url` attributes.
- `docs/reference/error-codes.md` documents all initial codes with explicit anchors + `Context`/`Applies to`/`Populates` metadata lines.
- `err.help_url()` resolves to a live anchor on the docs site (verified by clicking through a few).
- Stability policy documented in CONTRIBUTING.md.
- Unit tests verify each variant's code/slug via snapshot tests (both Rust and Python).
- **FFI integration test**: a parse error on the Rust side propagates to a Python exception with `code` / `slug` / `help_url` populated.
- CHANGELOG entry under next version.
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes.
close_reason: Delivered in PR #101 (merged). Stable Exxx codes + slugs on every MarcError variant; matching code/slug/help_url() on Python exceptions; configurable docs base URL via MRRC_DOCS_BASE_URL; new docs/reference/error-codes.md page; stability policy in CONTRIBUTING.md.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/95

title: 5 additional unwired structural error codes (E001/E003/E004/E006/E105)
description:
## Context

Surfaced during the 2026-04-29 thorough error-handling audit, expanding
GH issue #148 / bd-0x73 scope. #148 flagged E201 (`InvalidIndicator`) and
E202 (`BadSubfieldCode`) as unwired. The audit found **five additional
error codes** in the same shape — defined as `MarcError` variants, with
public Python exception classes, codes, slugs, dict serialization, and
`ParseContext::err_*` helpers — that are **never constructed in
production code**.

## The five unwired codes

| Code | Variant | Helper exists? | Where docs claim it fires | Where it actually fires |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E001** | `RecordLengthInvalid` | `err_record_length_invalid` | "leader's record-length field (bytes 0-4) is invalid: not five ASCII digits" | nowhere — `Leader::validate_for_reading` raises E002 `InvalidLeader` instead |
| **E003** | `BaseAddressInvalid` | `err_base_address_invalid` | "leader's base-address-of-data (bytes 12-16) is not five ASCII digits" | nowhere — same E002 path |
| **E004** | `BaseAddressNotFound` | `err_base_address_not_found` | "leader claims a base address that exceeds the available bytes" | nowhere — same E002 path |
| **E006** | `EndOfRecordNotFound` | `err_end_of_record_not_found` | "end-of-record byte (`0x1D`) was not found at the leader's claimed position" | nowhere — no code path checks `RECORD_TERMINATOR` and raises this |
| **E105** | `FieldNotFound` | (no helper) | "a requested field was not present in the parsed record" (accessor error) | nowhere — `Record::get_field` and friends never raise it |

Same wiring asymmetry as E201/E202: the infrastructure (variant, helper,
code, slug, Python class, docs entry) is present; the parse path that
should fire it is missing.

## Source-level evidence

- E001: `src/error.rs` defines `MarcError::RecordLengthInvalid`. `ParseContext::err_record_length_invalid` defined at `src/iso2709.rs`. **Zero non-test call sites** (rg confirmed). The actual leader validation at `src/leader.rs:265-279` uses `MarcError::leader_msg` which always builds E002 `InvalidLeader`.
- E003 / E004: same shape — defined, helper present, zero calls. `Leader::validate_for_reading` only checks `< 24` and uses `leader_msg`.
- E006: helper at `src/iso2709.rs`, zero calls. `RECORD_TERMINATOR = 0x1D` is defined at `src/iso2709.rs` but no code path verifies it.
- E105: no `err_field_not_found` helper exists. The variant is in `error.rs` and re-exported in `mrrc/exceptions.py`, but no `Record` method raises it (they return `Option<&Field>` / `None` instead).

## Decision needed

For each code, choose:

a. **Wire it** — add the appropriate detection path in the parser/reader/accessor. E001/E003/E004 would mean splitting the current generic `Leader::validate_for_reading` into more specific checks; E006 means adding a `RECORD_TERMINATOR` verification step; E105 means changing accessor return types or adding `*_or_err` variants.

b. **Fold into existing variant** — accept that the granularity is wider than the codes suggest. E001/E003/E004 already collapse to E002; E006 would similarly collapse to E005 `TruncatedRecord` or E101 `DirectoryInvalid`; E105 doesn't have a natural alternative.

c. **Remove from public API** — drop the variant + Python class + code + slug + docs entry. Only viable if no users depend on the type names (most are pymarc-shape names so likely some compat sensitivity).

The "stable codes never get renumbered" promise in `docs/reference/error-codes.md#stability` makes (c) costly. (a) is the most user-aligned answer; (b) is fastest but contradicts the docs.

## Done when

- A decision recorded for each of E001/E003/E004/E006/E105.
- For codes chosen (a) wire: parse-path detection added with positional
  context, exercised by tests reading malformed records.
- For codes chosen (b) fold: docs updated to remove the code, or
  re-document as "fires for [actual subset]" if partial wiring.
- For codes chosen (c) remove: variant removed from `MarcError`,
  Python class removed, code-table entry retired with breadcrumb in
  `error-codes.md`.
- `error-codes.md` and `error-handling.md` accurately describe what
  fires from production code paths.

## Reference

- bd-0x73 — umbrella tracker for v0.8 error-handling re-evaluation.
- GH issue #148 — original surfacing of E201/E202.
- This audit's verification: ran 2026-04-29 against main commit 9e2e7f3
  using rg to enumerate non-test call sites for each variant.
close_reason:
All 6 codes resolved across four PRs:
- E001/E003/E004 wired in leader-validation path (#166)
- E006 RECORD_TERMINATOR check in iso2709_skeleton (#167)
- E101 directory-walker recharacterization (#168, expanded scope from 2026-05-01 comment)
- E105 via Record::get_field_or_err accessor (#169)
Coverage harness tally moved 9/18 → 15/18. Both Rust and Python harnesses assert these cases on every CI run.
