comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Scope expansion (2026-04-30): include E101 vs E106 directory-walker recharacterization

Surfaced by the bd-0x73.8 coverage harness: docs/reference/error-codes.md describes E101 (DirectoryInvalid) as firing on "non-numeric length or start" in a directory entry, but the parser today routes those bytes to E106 (InvalidField) regardless of caller context. The cause: parse_4digits / parse_5digits in src/iso2709.rs return MarcError::InvalidField unconditionally; the directory walker in src/iso2709_skeleton.rs forwards that error without recharacterizing it as DirectoryInvalid for directory-entry parsing.

This belongs in scope here because it is the same shape as the other 5 unwired structural codes — variant + code + Python class exist, ParseContext helper exists (err_directory_invalid), but the parse path constructs a different variant.

Resolution options (mirroring the per-code decision frame already in this bead):
a. **Wire it.** Wrap parse_4digits / parse_5digits errors at the directory-walker call site as DirectoryInvalid; leave them as InvalidField when called from data-field parsing. Matches the docs.
b. **Fold into E106.** Update docs/reference/error-codes.md to describe E101 as firing only on "directory size not a multiple of 12 / no terminator before base address" and document that non-digit length/start fires E106 by design.
c. **Remove from public API.** Same constraints as before — costly given the stable-codes promise.

Recommend (a): the variant should match where the malformation occurred (directory entry vs data field), not which numeric helper detected the bad byte. The directory walker has the context to make that call.

Coverage harness already has both a wired E101 case (no-terminator) and an unwired E101 case (non-digit length) for this; flipping wired = true on the latter is the test ratchet.

title: Correct the declared MSRV and enforce it with a CI check
description:
The declared MSRV is false and nothing would catch it (source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PKG-1 / BP-H1).

Cargo.toml:16 declares rust-version = "1.71", but locked dependencies require far newer toolchains: oxrdf/oxrdfio/oxttl/oxrdfxml need 1.87, pyo3 0.28 needs 1.83, indexmap 2.13 needs 1.82, rayon 1.80, csv 1.73. The workspace cannot compile on 1.71. No CI job tests any MSRV — test.yml uses dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable and rust-toolchain.toml pins 1.95.0, so exactly one toolchain is ever exercised. Beyond being wrong, the false claim is hazardous: cargo's MSRV-aware resolver uses rust-version to steer downstream users toward old dependency versions mrrc has never been tested against.

What to do:
- Set rust-version to the real floor (1.87 today; could drop to 1.85 later if the BIBFRAME/oxrdf stack moves behind a feature flag — that is separate work, do not couple it here). Verify the floor empirically: install the candidate toolchain and run cargo check --workspace --all-targets with it.
- Update the other declarations to match: fuzz/Cargo.toml:6 and the MSRV mentions in docs/contributing/release-procedure.md:151 and docs/getting-started/installation.md:36, plus any README mention.
- Add a CI job (in test.yml or lint.yml) that pins the declared MSRV toolchain and runs cargo check --workspace (check is enough; full test run on MSRV is not required). The job should read the version from one place if practical so the declaration and the check cannot drift apart.

Note: this corrects the premise of bd-j01q ("quick-xml 0.40 requires MSRV bump to 1.79") — the de facto floor is already past 1.79, so that migration's MSRV concern is moot; the quick-xml bump itself remains valid.

Done when: rust-version in both manifests matches a toolchain that demonstrably builds the workspace; a CI job verifies it on PRs; stale 1.71 references in docs are updated; .cargo/check.sh passes.
close_reason: Merged in PR #280; GitHub issue #277 auto-closed. MSRV now declared 1.87 with a CI msrv job; lazy_static dependency removed as lint fallout

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #277: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/277 (milestone v0.8.3)

title: Make Rust doc examples compile-checked so they cannot silently go stale
description:
Make canonical Rust doc examples compile-checked so they cannot silently go stale. DEFERRED — not in current release scope; revisit as its own focused effort once we decide the next release's shape. Investigation results below so this doesn't need re-discovering.

Finding: the active mkdocs docs hold ~78 ```rust fences. Running them through `rustdoc --test` (the natural mechanism — reuses doctest machinery, respects no_run/ignore, compiles against the crate) shows only ~11 compile as standalone units; the other ~67 are NARRATIVE snippets that reference record/reader/File defined in surrounding prose, so each fails in isolation ('cannot find value record'), plus a few do file I/O that would need no_run. Making every fence individually compilable means stuffing hidden '# let record = ...;' scaffolding into ~70 fences — hours of work, ongoing maintenance, and it litters the .md sources with doctest boilerplate to catch a failure mode (undefined-in-isolation) that is not the real drift risk.

Preferred approach when revisited: do NOT compile ~70 in-place snippets. Instead move the canonical, complete Rust examples into compiled examples/*.rs (already compiled in CI) and pull them into the mkdocs pages via pymdownx.snippets, giving genuine single-source compile-checking for the copy-paste-critical code without boilerplating every illustrative fragment. First decide which fences are canonical complete examples (worth a compiled source) vs illustrative fragments (left inline).

Done when: the canonical Rust doc examples are sourced from compiled examples/*.rs (or rustdoc doctests) and included via snippets so CI fails when they stop compiling against src/, and the workflow is documented for contributors.

Context: surfaced by the docs-drift work (bd-nj22). The docs are API-correct as of the v0.8.x reconciliation (bd-nj22, bd-du5n), so there is no active rot — this is future-proofing, to be done deliberately. Detached from the bd-c7ok docs epic (closed on its generation/verification goals) to stand alone.

title: Populate ParseError positional metadata at boundary-scanner sites
description:
## Problem

ParseError construction sites in src-python/src/{backend,unified_reader,batched_reader}.rs currently pass only string messages, even though the surrounding code has access to records-read counters, byte position, and source identifier. The bd-qzew commit 6 unified ParseError → MarcError mapping but didn't enrich the construction sites.

## Done when

- ParseError construction sites populate record_index (from records-read counter), byte_offset, and source where available.
- Either: ParseError variants gain optional positional fields, or callers construct MarcError directly and the ParseError enum is removed.
- Boundary scanner errors carry the same positional shape as synchronous reader errors.
close_reason: Delivered in PR2; ParseError now carries optional positional context with builder methods. Per-site population of context at boundary-scanner sites tracked separately when reader iteration counters are added.

title: Fix CI Clippy drift (collapsible_match errors in bibframe/converter.rs) + pin Rust toolchain
description:
## Problem

CI `Clippy` job is failing on main because of a **Rust toolchain version drift** between the local pre-push hook environment and the GitHub Actions runners, exposing 4 pre-existing `clippy::collapsible_match` lints in `src/bibframe/converter.rs`.

**Failing run:** https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/actions/runs/24541173652/job/71747095028 (exit code 101, 4 errors)

The commit that first turned CI red was `chore(beads): add maturity-gap beads for tiers 1-3` — but that commit only touched `.beads/issues.jsonl`. The lints are in existing code; they're only surfaced now because the stable Rust channel advanced.

## Failing lints

All in `src/bibframe/converter.rs`, all `clippy::collapsible_match`:

| Line | Match arm pattern |
|---|---|
| ~558 | `'e' => { if !field.subfields.iter().any(\|s\| s.code == '4') { ... } }` |
| 1912 | `'a' => { if self.config.include_bflc { ... } }` |
| 1918 | `'b' => { if self.config.include_bflc { ... } }` |
| 1924 | `'c' => { if self.config.include_bflc { ... } }` |

Clippy wants each collapsed into a match guard: `'a' if self.config.include_bflc => { ... }`.

## Root cause — toolchain drift

- **Local**: `rustc 1.94.1` (Homebrew, 2026-03-25), `clippy 0.1.94`. This version does not flag the nested-if pattern. `.cargo/check.sh` and the pre-push hook both pass locally.
- **CI**: `.github/workflows/lint.yml:34` uses `dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable`, which on the failing run resolved to **Rust 1.95.0**. The 1.95 release expanded `collapsible_match` to catch these cases. With `-D warnings` in the clippy command (`.github/workflows/lint.yml:53`), lints become hard errors.
- No `rust-toolchain.toml` in the repo → local and CI will drift forever, every time stable ticks on either side.

## Significance

CI is broken on main until this is fixed. Every push (code or not) will fail the Clippy job. This blocks:

- Any future PR merge (CI-required status check).
- Work on bd-qzew, bd-44wb, bd-kodg, and other maturity-gap beads — their PRs will all fail CI too.
- Routine dependabot updates.

## Fix options

Three levers, not mutually exclusive:

1. **Fix the 4 lints** in `src/bibframe/converter.rs` — collapse each `'x' => { if cond { ... } }` into `'x' if cond => { ... }`. Mechanical edit, ~5 minutes. Gets CI green immediately.
2. **Pin a toolchain** via a `rust-toolchain.toml` at repo root (e.g., `channel = "1.95"` or whatever local-and-CI should agree on). Prevents future drift surprises. Trade-off: bumps become explicit/manual, and clippy-version regressions show up at bump-time rather than at CI-time.
3. **Relax `-D warnings`** — keep warnings visible but don't fail CI on them. Not recommended; loses the quality gate that got us here.

## Recommended approach

Do **#1 now** (fix the lints — fast, unblocks everyone) AND **#2** (pin a toolchain — prevents the next drift surprise). That way:

- CI goes green immediately.
- Local hook and CI agree on the same clippy version.
- New clippy lints surface at deliberate bump-time, not at push-time.

After pinning, running `brew upgrade rust` locally stops silently de-syncing from CI; running `rustup update` with a `rust-toolchain.toml` in place uses the pinned channel.

## Done when

- All 4 clippy errors in `src/bibframe/converter.rs` are fixed.
- Either a `rust-toolchain.toml` is added pinning to a specific version, OR a deliberate decision is documented not to pin (with rationale).
- CI's Clippy job passes on main.
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes locally.
- CHANGELOG entry under next version noting the CI fix and toolchain policy change.

## Priority rationale

Filed P0 because CI is actively broken on main and every subsequent push will fail. Unblocking this is prerequisite to merging any of the other maturity-gap beads (bd-qzew, bd-44wb, bd-kodg, bd-ouji, etc.) via PR.
close_reason: CI drift fixed + toolchain pinned via PR #97 (merged). GH #96 auto-closed on merge.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/96

title: Fix user-facing Python documentation errors that break copy-pasted code
description:
Four defects in high-traffic doc pages, all verified by executing the documented code against the installed extension (source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, DOC-1..DOC-4).

1. README.md:46 calls `record.title()` but `title` is a property on the wrapper Record — raises TypeError. Same bug in the `mrrc.read()` docstring example (mrrc/__init__.py:1972), which renders into the API reference via mkdocstrings. This is the first code a new user copies.

2. docs/reference/python-api.md:487-505 (Exceptions section) and docs/guides/migration-from-pymarc.md:330 show `from mrrc import MrrcException, MarcError`; MarcError does not exist in the Python package — ImportError. python-api.md's 3-class exception hierarchy sketch also contradicts the accurate hierarchy documented in error-handling.md; replace the sketch with a link to that reference.

3. docs/reference/error-handling.md:271, 288-289, 316-333 document `MARCReader.from_path()` and `.with_source()` in Python; neither exists on the wrapper, the extension, or _mrrc.pyi — both examples raise AttributeError (these are Rust builder methods transplanted into Python prose). Rewrite the section to describe what Python actually does today; exposing source plumbing through the bindings is separate future work (reader-configuration parity, ARCH-7 in the findings doc).

4. docs/reference/python-api.md:226 and its kwargs table state the recovery_mode default is "strict"; the actual default is "permissive" (mrrc/__init__.py:1678-1679), as error-handling.md and migration-from-pymarc.md correctly document — the API reference inverts the safety story. The table also omits the validation_level and max_errors keyword arguments entirely.

Done when: every corrected example executes successfully against the installed extension; no doc page contradicts another on defaults or available APIs; .cargo/check.sh passes (includes mkdocs --strict).
close_reason: Merged in PR #278; GitHub issue #275 auto-closed

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #275: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/275 (milestone v0.8.3)

title: Remove 'currently/not yet' process wording from error-handling.md
description:
Three spots in docs/reference/error-handling.md use 'currently' / 'not yet' wording that implies pending work instead of stating the literal behavior. Each fix is a one-line rewording; the technical claims stay unchanged.

1. Position semantics by format, MARCXML bullet (~line 304-307): 'The underlying quick_xml parser does not expose a byte position from its deserializer error type, so byte_offset is currently None.' Drop 'currently' -> '...so byte_offset is None.' The sentence already gives the timeless reason (quick_xml's error type carries no position).

2. Position semantics by format, MARCJSON bullet (~line 308-311): 'byte_offset is currently None because translating (line, column) to a byte offset requires the original input bytes.' Drop 'currently' -> 'byte_offset is None because...'.

3. Hex dump in detailed(), closing paragraph (~line 603-606): '...for wrapping variants like IoError / XmlError / JsonError, or for error paths that do not yet plumb the buffer through.' Rewrite the final clause to state the condition, not promise future plumbing: '...or for error paths that do not have buffer access at error time.'

Deliberately NOT in scope: line ~569 'Pre-1.0, the shape may still evolve' — that is an intentional API-stability statement to users (the reason schema_version exists), not process wording.

Optional follow-on in the same pass: sweep the rest of docs/ (excluding docs/history/ and docs/design/) for the same 'currently / not yet' pattern and fix anything comparable.
close_reason: Done in #261: dropped 'currently/not yet' process wording from the three error-handling.md spots (MARCXML/MARCJSON byte_offset, bytes_near note); optional sweep also fixed validators.md (IndicatorValidator re-export) and error-codes.md E021 recovery. Intentional roadmap/availability/conditional uses left as-is. mkdocs build warning-free.

title: ci: one-shot cargo-semver-checks audit before v0.8 tag
description:
## Problem

v0.8 has multiple documented breaking changes in `CHANGELOG.md`
`[Unreleased]` `### Breaking` (the `MarcError` variant restructure to
struct form, `MarcError::InvalidRecord` removal, `recovery::RecoveryContext`
removed from public re-exports). CI's `semver.yml` workflow runs
`cargo-semver-checks` per-PR when `src/**` or `Cargo.*` change, but the
breaking changes landed across many PRs over the cycle. A single
cumulative run against v0.7.6 is the only way to verify the breaking
list is complete and accurate before tagging.

## What to do

1. `cargo install cargo-semver-checks` (one-time on dev machine).
2. From main, run: `cargo semver-checks check-release --baseline-rev v0.7.6`.
3. Compare output against the `### Breaking` section in
   `CHANGELOG.md`.
4. Categorize findings:
   - Already documented in `### Breaking` → confirm.
   - Undocumented breaking change → add to `### Breaking` before tag.
   - False positive (e.g., re-export move that semver-checks flags but
     wasn't a real API loss) → annotate the close reason with
     justification.

## Done when

- cargo-semver-checks completed against v0.7.6 baseline.
- CHANGELOG `### Breaking` reconciled — every flagged change is either
  already listed or has been added.
- Bead closed with the diff (or "no surprises") in close reason.

## Out of scope

- Permanent inclusion in `check.sh`. The recurring use case is for the
  next breaking-change cycle, which is far off (post-1.0 in fact, if we
  hold the line on additive-only minor bumps). File a follow-up bead
  if/when we want to bake it in.
- CI gating semver-checks failures (currently a hint, not a hard fail) —
  separate decision.
close_reason: cargo-semver-checks 0.47.0 audit against v0.7.6 baseline run with --release-type patch to enumerate every breaking change. 5 categories surfaced; 3 already documented; 2 gaps caught and added in PR #143 (MarcError::ParseError removal, recovery::try_recover_record 4->5 params). Default-mode cargo semver-checks now reports clean for the v0.7.6 -> v0.8 bump. ### Breaking section reconciled.

title: Enrich MARCJSON parse-path errors via JsonError variant
description:
## Problem

The actual serde_json::from_str call for MARCJSON parsing lives in src-python/src/formats.rs (line ~187), not in src/marcjson.rs. It currently produces a bare PyValueError("Invalid MARCJSON: ..."), bypassing the typed JsonError variant entirely.

The bd-qzew commit 4 added MarcError::JsonError and ParseContext::err_json infrastructure, but this call site was missed because src/marcjson.rs operates on already-parsed serde_json::Value inputs.

## Done when

- src-python/src/formats.rs MARCJSON parse sites construct MarcError::JsonError via ctx.err_json() and route through marc_error_to_py_err.
- The wrapped serde_json::Error is preserved via #[source] so callers can downcast.
- Test verifies a malformed MARCJSON input raises mrrc.JsonError with cause chain.
close_reason: Delivered in PR2; JSON parse sites in src-python/src/formats.rs now route through ctx.err_json

title: Fix copy.copy(Record) RecursionError and define copy/deepcopy semantics for wrapper types
description: Found during the field-handle semantics test work (PR #301). copy.copy(Record) raises RecursionError: the wrapper's __getattr__ delegates to self._inner, which recurses when copy protocols construct an instance without _inner set. The recursion fix is to make __getattr__ raise AttributeError when _inner is absent (object.__getattribute__ guard) instead of self-recursing. Beyond the crash, decide and implement copy semantics: copy.deepcopy currently raises TypeError (PyO3 objects are unpicklable) for Record, Field, detached fields, and handles, but pymarc records support deepcopy — per the pymarc-parity principle, Record and Field likely want explicit __copy__/__deepcopy__ implementations. The 34 tests in tests/python/test_field_handle_writeback.py pin today's observed behavior (RecursionError excluded, TypeError pinned), so implementing deepcopy means deliberately updating those pins.

title: Fix repeated control fields being dropped (e.g. multiple 007s)
close_reason: Fixed in PR #79, merged to main

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #77: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/77

title: docs: resolve mkdocs warnings in docs/design/profiling/
description:
## Problem

After PR #138 cleaned up mkdocs warnings (`docs/history/` excluded,
active-doc cross-links fixed), four warnings remain — all in
`docs/design/profiling/`:

```
WARNING - design/profiling/PHASE_2_DETAILED_ANALYSIS.md
  -> ../PROFILING_PLAN.md (target not found)
WARNING - design/profiling/RUST_SINGLE_THREADED_PROFILING_RESULTS.md
  -> ../PERFORMANCE.md (target not found)
WARNING - design/profiling/RUST_SINGLE_THREADED_PROFILING_RESULTS.md
  -> ../benchmarks/RESULTS.md (target not found)
WARNING - design/profiling/pymrrc_concurrent_profile.md
  -> ./../../../.benchmarks/pymrrc_concurrent_profile.json (target not found)
```

The contents look archival (PHASE_1, PHASE_2, RESULTS, PROFILING_PLAN —
similar shape to the `docs/history/` files), but `docs/design/` is not
flagged in `CLAUDE.md` as archival the way `docs/history/` is. Worth a
deliberate decision rather than a default.

## What to do

Pick one:

- **Option A (consistent with #138):** add `design/profiling/` to
  `exclude_docs:` in `mkdocs.yml`. Treats it as archival like
  `docs/history/`. One-line change.
- **Option B:** fix the broken cross-references in-place. Two are to
  files that no longer exist in the docs tree (`PERFORMANCE.md`,
  `PROFILING_PLAN.md`, `benchmarks/RESULTS.md`); the fourth is to a
  `.benchmarks/*.json` artifact outside the docs tree (likely a
  source-file link that should be a GitHub URL or removed).
- **Option C:** move `docs/design/profiling/` under `docs/history/` so
  it's excluded automatically. Reflects intent if the content is in
  fact archival.

Recommend A or C if the content is truly archival; B if it's still
relevant reference material.

## Done when

- `mkdocs build` produces zero warnings on current main.
- Decision documented in commit message or close reason.
- Once clean, the `--strict` flag in both `docs.yml` and `check.sh`
  becomes safely turn-on-able (separate bead — that was mrrc-s3ug,
  which doesn't actually exist as a bead and should be replaced with a
  real ID at that time).

## Out of scope

- Enabling `--strict` mode in `docs.yml` / `check.sh` — separate
  decision once warnings are at zero.
close_reason: Done in #260 (Option C): the 16 dated profiling files moved to docs/history/profiling/ (excluded from the site build), clearing all four mkdocs warnings; PROFILING_GUIDE.md salvaged to docs/contributing/profiling.md and added to the nav; docs/design/index.md repointed. mkdocs build now warning-free. Modernization tracked as bd-l9zh.1.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Adjacent to bd-1dbu (benchmarking epic bd-l9zh): the dead profiling harnesses bd-1dbu removes reference these same docs/design/profiling/ files. Not epic membership — this is archival-docs hygiene — but coordinate if bd-1dbu's reference fixes touch the same links.

title: Plumb positional context through buffered-reader ParseError path
description:
## Problem

The `src-python/src/buffered_reader.rs` + `batched_reader.rs` + `backend.rs` paths do pre-parse validation (record_length < 24, missing 0x1D terminator, short record-length header, non-ASCII-digit length) via `ParseError::invalid_record(...)` / `record_boundary_error(...)`. These errors surface as `mrrc.InvalidField` on the Python side with no positional context — `byte_offset`, `bytes_near`, `record_index`, `source` are all None.

The core parse path now preserves typed `MarcError` end-to-end (bd-kodg / PR #102), so this ParseError-mediated path is the only remaining gap where bytes_near and positional fields are dropped.

Affects the buffered-reader path used for Python file objects (fallback from BatchedUnifiedReader) and any pre-parse boundary condition.

## What to do

1. Extend `ParseErrorContext` (src-python/src/parse_error.rs) to carry `bytes_near: Option<Vec<u8>>` + `bytes_near_offset: Option<usize>` alongside existing `record_index` / `byte_offset` / `source_name`.
2. At each `ParseError::invalid_record` / `ParseError::record_boundary_error` site in buffered_reader.rs / batched_reader.rs / backend.rs, populate the context with whatever buffer is available (typically the 5-byte length header or the partial record buffer).
3. Update `ParseError::to_py_err` (or `ParseErrorKind → MarcError` conversion in `to_py_err`) to forward the bytes_near fields into the reconstructed MarcError variant.
4. Add an integration test: feed a record with record_length < 24 (triggers buffered_reader's pre-parse check) and verify the surfaced Python exception carries bytes_near over the length header.

## Done when

- ParseErrorContext carries bytes_near + bytes_near_offset
- All ParseError construction sites populate bytes_near from the available buffer (or explicitly None where no buffer exists, e.g. IO errors)
- to_py_err forwards bytes_near through to the typed Python exception
- Integration test: malformed record via buffered_reader path surfaces with bytes_near populated
- CHANGELOG entry under next version
- .cargo/check.sh passes

## Depends on

- bd-kodg (PR #102) — this task builds on the MarcError bytes_near field and with_bytes_near helper.
close_reason: Delivered in PR #102 as part of bd-kodg

title: Continue read-path optimization (post-v0.8 follow-up to bd-tcym)
description:
## Context

Follow-up to bd-tcym (#116) / bd-9kdt (#117). bd-tcym identified a ~15-17% read-path regression vs v0.7.6 caused by the v0.8-cycle `ParseContext` refactor disabling natural compiler inlining of `iso2709::parse_data_field`. bd-9kdt (#117) shipped the fix: `#[inline(always)]` on `parse_data_field` paired with shrinking `current_field_tag` to `Option<[u8; 3]>` so forced inlining does not balloon L1-i cache usage.

That fix lands reads at +9-11% vs v0.7.6 (from main's +15-17%). The residual +9-11% gap is *inherent* to the v0.8 structured-error feature — ParseContext threading, positional-error construction, `set_parse_buffer` per-record buffer copy, etc. — and is documented in CHANGELOG.md under `### Fixed` alongside the inlining recovery. Not blocking v0.8.

This bead captures the threads post-v0.8 work could pull on to recover more of that gap. Do NOT start any of them until v0.8 has shipped and real-world perf has been observed; some of these may turn out not to matter after measuring.

## Threads, in suggested execution order

### 1. Re-measure first; decide which threads are worth pursuing

Before touching code, re-run the bd-tcym Criterion comparison (v0.7.6 vs then-current main, toolchain 1.95.0, clean back-to-back baselines). If the gap has closed on its own (new stable Rust, other incidental optimizations), the rest may be unnecessary. If it persists at ≥5%, proceed.

Done when: fresh per-benchmark % change numbers recorded in this bead.

### 2. Profile the hot path with L1-i cache awareness

On macOS, `samply` or `sample` can surface hot functions but not cache-miss counts directly. `dtrace`-driven tooling or Linux `perf stat -e L1-icache-load-misses` gives explicit cache-miss counts. Compare current main vs v0.7.6 on `read_10k_records` to localize where time goes.

Done when: profile output identifies the top 2-3 hot sites beyond parse_data_field itself.

### 3. `set_parse_buffer` refactor to avoid per-record `Vec<u8>` clone

`iso2709.rs::set_parse_buffer` currently owns a heap-allocated copy of each record's bytes (`current_buffer: Option<Vec<u8>>`) purely so `err_*` helpers can capture a `bytes_near` window on failure. 10k records × ~300-byte record = ~3 MB of per-run memcpy + 10k small allocs on the success path.

Design alternatives:

- Pass `bytes` into err_* helpers at call time (lifetimes on ParseContext or pass-through parameter).
- Keep `current_buffer` but switch to `Cow<[u8]>` or `&[u8]` with a lifetime (bigger API change).
- Keep the owned representation but only populate it when an error is about to be raised (move `set_parse_buffer` from pre-parse to per-error-construction; deferred).

Done when: a benchmark comparison shows the measured impact; either PR lands the refactor or bead closes with a "not worth it" note.

### 4. Remove `current_field_tag` from `ParseContext`; pass as explicit err_* parameter

Even with the compact `Option<[u8; 3]>` representation, every data field parse writes the field then clears it. Removing the field entirely and requiring callers to pass the tag as an argument to `err_invalid_field` / `err_bad_subfield_code` / `err_encoding` / etc. eliminates the writes altogether.

Design note: this grows err_* helper signatures by 1 parameter but makes the zero-cost-on-success guarantee explicit in the type system.

Done when: a benchmark comparison shows the measured impact; either PR lands the refactor or bead closes with a "not worth it" note.

### 5. `#[cold]` annotation on error-construction tails in `parse_subfields`

`parse_subfields` has two error paths (`ctx.err_invalid_field` / `ctx.err_encoding`). LLVM does not always recognize these as cold from the function-call shape alone. Explicit `#[cold]` on helper functions that wrap the err_* calls would nudge the register allocator and branch predictor.

Done when: a benchmark comparison shows the measured impact.

## Out of scope

- Reverting any part of the v0.8 structured-error feature.
- Any code change before measurement confirms it's worth doing.
- Rust compiler / stdlib / dependency bumps as a perf vector — handled in routine dep-update cycles, not here.
close_reason: Absorbed into the read-path performance epic bd-0pg6, whose children carry the concrete optimization list from the v0.8.2 code review (PERF-1..PERF-10)

title: Reorganize [Unreleased] CHANGELOG section: fix order, dedupe headings, hard-wrap long lines
description:
## Problem

The `[Unreleased]` section at the top of `CHANGELOG.md` (lines 8–116) is out of order, contains a duplicate heading, and has several bullets that are single unwrapped paragraphs 300–900 characters long. It does not flow naturally and is hard to review in a diff.

## Specific issues

1. **Duplicate `### Changed` heading.** Lines 103 and 114 are both `### Changed`. They must merge into a single section.
2. **Section order violates Keep-a-Changelog convention.** Current order:
   `Added — property tests` → `Added — error serialization` → `Added — error codes` → `Breaking` → `Added` → `Changed` → `Fixed` → `Changed`.
   Keep-a-Changelog expects: **Added → Changed → Deprecated → Removed → Fixed → Security**, with breaking changes flagged inline (e.g. `**BREAKING:**` prefix) rather than in a standalone section. `Breaking` should either move to the top of the unreleased block as a callout or be folded into Changed with explicit markers.
3. **Topic-grouped vs flat Added sections.** The three `Added — <topic>` subsections narrate different features; the bare `### Added` below them is a second flat list that overlaps topically (positional error context, `ParseContext`, typed Python exceptions, `detailed()` output) with the earlier error-serialization / error-codes topics. Decide on one structure:
   - **Option A (recommended):** Fold the flat `### Added` entries into the appropriate `Added — <topic>` subsection, so each feature area has a single home.
   - **Option B:** Demote all three topic headings to bold lead-ins inside a single flat `### Added` list, and order strictly chronologically.
   Either way, no information is dropped.
4. **Long unwrapped lines.** Wrap at ~80 columns (the top three topic-grouped sections already wrap to ~72; match that). Line lengths measured on 2026-04-19:
   - L86 `Breaking MarcError variants...` — 908 chars
   - L90 `Structured positional context...` — 338 chars
   - L91 `ParseContext parsing context...` — 279 chars
   - L92 `Shared subfield-parsing primitives...` — 323 chars
   - L96 `mrrc-specific Python exception subclasses...` — 351 chars
   - L97 `Positional attributes on every Python exception...` — 307 chars
   - L99 `PyO3 typed-exception construction...` — 343 chars
   - L105 `MarcError source-error chain...` — 231 chars
   - L107 `src-python/src/parse_error.rs...` — 336 chars
   - L108 `Shared ISO 2709 parsing primitives...` — 419 chars
   - L112 `CI Clippy failure on main...` — 537 chars
   - L116 `Pinned Rust toolchain to 1.95.0...` — 638 chars

## What to do

1. Merge the two `### Changed` sections into one.
2. Reorder headings under `[Unreleased]` to: Breaking (if retained as a top callout), Added, Changed, Fixed. Resolve topic-grouped vs flat Added sections per option A or B.
3. Hard-wrap every bullet in the unreleased section to ~72–80 columns, matching the indentation style of the existing top three subsections (two-space continuation indent under the bullet `-`).
4. Verify the Keep-a-Changelog link at the top of the file still reflects the chosen structure (no schema change needed; just the convention).
5. Do NOT edit any released version section (`[0.7.x]` and below). Scope is limited to `[Unreleased]`.

## Done when

- `## [Unreleased]` contains no duplicate subsection headings.
- Sections appear in Keep-a-Changelog order.
- No line under `[Unreleased]` exceeds ~80 columns (except unbreakable URLs or code spans).
- All pre-existing content is preserved — this is a reformatting task, not a trim.
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes (no source changes expected, but run it anyway).
close_reason: Delivered in #107 (merged to main)

title: Restore parser inlining lost in v0.8 structured-error refactor
description:
## Context

Discovered during bd-tcym (#116) investigation. Measurement confirmed ~15-17% read-path regression on main vs v0.7.6.

## What we initially thought

The original hypothesis: `self.ctx.current_field_tag = Some(tag.clone())` on every data field heap-allocated a `String` per field parse — so eliminating that allocation by storing the tag as `Option<[u8; 3]>` (MARC tags are format-mandated to be exactly 3 ASCII chars) would recover most of the regression.

## What we actually found

The String allocation was **not** the hot cost. Experiments:

- Commenting out the `current_field_tag` writes entirely (eliminating all per-field allocations) barely moved the needle: read_1k_records went from +32% to +30.58% vs v0.7.6.
- Applying the type change alone made read_1k/10k_records *worse* (+32% vs +15% on main). That was confusing until we checked `-Zprint-type-sizes`: shrinking `current_field_tag` from 24 bytes (`Option<String>`) to 4 bytes (`Option<[u8; 3]>`) shrank `ParseContext` from 144 bytes to 120 bytes, and this tripped the compiler's inlining cost heuristic from "yes inline" to "no inline" on `iso2709::parse_data_field`.
- Adding `#[inline(always)]` on `parse_data_field` + keeping the type change recovered plain reads to +11% vs v0.7.6.
- Applying `#[inline(always)]` *alone* to main (without the type change) caused **30-44% regressions on parallel benchmarks**, because forcing inline on the 144-byte ctx blows up the L1 instruction cache footprint when multiple threads run the expanded `read_record`.
- Applying `#[inline(always)]` on `parse_subfields` *too* (inlining further) produced unstable numbers and field-access regressions (+22% vs +11%) — the hot loop was fine fully inlined once but not twice.

## What this actually fixes

The v0.8-cycle structured-error refactor (bd-qzew, PR #100) reshaped `parse_data_field`'s call sites enough that the compiler's default inlining heuristic stopped inlining it. The ~15-17% read-path regression is almost entirely that lost inlining.

Two-part fix, both required:

1. **`#[inline(always)]` on `iso2709::parse_data_field`** (with `#[allow(clippy::inline_always)]` and a measured-justification comment). Plain `#[inline]` was insufficient — the cost model still refused.

2. **`ParseContext::current_field_tag: Option<String>` → `Option<[u8; 3]>`** and a private `field_tag_as_string()` helper for err_* callers. Not primarily a write-cost savings — the compact context is the prerequisite that lets forced inlining be a net win rather than a parallel-workload disaster.

## Measured outcome

Vs v0.7.6, Criterion toolchain 1.95.0, fresh back-to-back baselines:

- **Reads: +9.5% to +10.8%** (main was +15% to +17%) — recovers 5-7%.
- **Reads w/ field access: +10.8%** (main was +16-17%) — recovers 5-6%.
- **Sequential: +10.6% to +11.4%** (main was +18-19%) — recovers 7-8%.
- **Parallel: +7% to +12%** (main was +5% to +9%) — 2-3% *worse* than main.
- **Serialize/roundtrip: +3% to +6%** (main was +2% to +5%) — 1-2% worse than main.
- **`parallel_8x_1k_records`**: measurement-unstable across runs, ±15% swing. Exact number varies.

Net: read-heavy and sequential workloads (the dominant real-world use) beat current main by 5-8%. Heavy-parallel workloads pay a modest additional penalty (2-12% worse than main) for the read-path recovery.

## Out of scope

- Further performance investigation. Deeper profiling (L1-i cache measurements, dead-code-elimination in parse_data_field's error paths, `#[cold]` annotation on error construction) is tracked for post-v0.8 development.
- Eliminating the remaining +9-11% read gap vs v0.7.6. The `ParseContext` threading and positional-error cost is inherent to the v0.8 feature; this PR minimizes but does not eliminate it.

## Done when

- PR merges.
- CHANGELOG entry under `[Unreleased]` `### Fixed` citing the measured numbers (done).
- Summary comment on bd-tcym (#116) consolidating the investigation (this PR).
close_reason: Delivered in PR #118 (merged as c38ef81)

title: ci: add small compile-only checks to check.sh (examples + bench compile)
description:
## Problem

Two small gaps where CI catches things `.cargo/check.sh` doesn't:

1. **`cargo build --examples`** — `build.yml` runs this on all 3 OSes;
   `check.sh` doesn't compile the `examples/` tree. A broken example
   slips past every local push.
2. **`cargo bench --no-run`** — benchmark code in `benches/` compiles
   in a separate target from `cargo test`; if benchmark APIs drift,
   only CI's `cargo codspeed build` step catches it. Local check
   passes; push fails.

## What to do

Add two lines to `.cargo/check.sh` full mode (after the existing
Rust test step, skipped under `--quick`):

```bash
echo ""
echo "=== Compile examples ==="
cargo build --examples --quiet

echo ""
echo "=== Compile benchmarks (no run) ==="
cargo bench --no-run --quiet
```

Both compile-only — no execution overhead beyond the build step.

## Done when

- Both lines added.
- Local pre-push run completes them cleanly on current main.
- Optionally verify each by deliberately breaking an example or bench
  and confirming the check fails.
- CHANGELOG entry under `[Unreleased]` `### Changed`.

## Out of scope

- Running benchmarks locally — too slow for routine pre-push (CI's
  CodSpeed run is the right home).
- Refactoring or expanding `examples/` or `benches/`.
close_reason: Done in #257: .cargo/check.sh full mode now runs cargo build --examples and cargo bench --no-run. Also enabled build.yml removal (bd-qbne deferral) by folding examples-compile into check.sh + a Build examples step in test.yml.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Sequencing with bd-qbne (2026-05-28): build.yml is currently the only place CI compiles the examples. If bd-qbne deletes build.yml, this bead (adding examples-compile to check.sh) should land first or together so examples-compile coverage isn't dropped.

title: Add mypy.stubtest to CI to verify _mrrc.pyi matches the compiled extension
description:
## Context

`mrrc/_mrrc.pyi` is declared the canonical Python API (CLAUDE.md: "ground-truth type stubs"), but nothing checks it against the actual compiled `_mrrc` extension. In PR #229 the stubs for AuthorityRecord/HoldingsRecord were found materially wrong — a phantom `fields()` method, a `control_field()` that is actually `get_control_field()`, `leader` declared as a method when it is a property, `get_fields()` typed `List[Field]` when it returns `Optional[List[Field]]`, and ~9-13 real members omitted. The drift was invisible because no tool compares the stub to the runtime surface.

## Approach

Add `stubtest` (ships with mypy) to CI: it imports the compiled module and diffs every name/signature against the `.pyi`, failing on mismatch. Run it in the Python lint/test workflow and add it to `.cargo/check.sh` so it runs locally too. Seed an allowlist only for genuinely unavoidable cases (PyO3 constructs stubtest cannot introspect), each with a comment.

This is also the gating prerequisite for the mkdocstrings work (the generated docs would be sourced from the `.pyi`, so the stub must be verified first), and it directly advances bd-0x73.12's "documentation-as-spec, enforced by tooling" principle for the Python API.

## Done when

- `stubtest` runs in CI and locally and passes against the current extension.
- A documented allowlist (if any) captures only unavoidable PyO3-introspection gaps.
- CI fails on any future stub/runtime divergence.
close_reason: stubtest wired into CI (lint.yml) + .cargo/check.sh; 156→0 errors, no allowlist. Merged in PR #262.

title: Expose max_errors kwarg on Python MARCReader
description:
## Context

The Rust core's `MarcReader::with_max_errors(N)` configures the per-stream recovered-error cap; once `N` errors have accumulated in lenient/permissive mode, the next read returns `MarcError::FatalReaderError` (E099). The Python `MARCReader` binding does not expose this kwarg, so the cap-tripping path is unreachable from Python.

Surfaced by bd-mk6z: the Rust harness in `tests/error_coverage.rs` now exercises E099 directly; the Python harness in `tests/python/test_error_coverage.py` still skips with `"the Rust core supports max_errors but the Python MARCReader binding does not yet expose it"`. The skip should drop once the binding lands.

## What needs to change

1. **`src-python/src/readers.rs`** — accept a `max_errors: Option<usize>` kwarg on `_MARCReader.__new__` and thread it into the inner `MarcReader::with_max_errors(...)` builder (skip the builder call when `None` to preserve the current default of "no cap").
2. **`mrrc/__init__.py`** — add `max_errors: int | None = None` to `MARCReader.__init__` and pass through to `_MARCReader`. Document that 0 means "no cap" per the Rust API.
3. **`mrrc/_mrrc.pyi`** — add the kwarg to the `_MARCReader` constructor signature.
4. **`tests/python/test_error_coverage.py`** — replace the recovery_cap skip with an actual driver (mirror `tests/error_coverage.rs`'s `recovery_cap` branch: concatenate N+2 malformed records, `max_errors=1`, iterate, assert `mrrc.FatalReaderError`).
5. **`docs/reference/error-handling.md`** + **`docs/guides/migration-from-pymarc.md`** — document the new kwarg.

## Done when

- The Python harness's recovery_cap skip is removed; the case asserts `mrrc.FatalReaderError` (code `E099`, slug `fatal_reader_error`) fires.
- New test in `tests/python/` covering at least: cap trips at the documented record index; `max_errors=0` disables the cap; cap is inert in strict mode (matching the Rust unit tests in `src/reader.rs`).

## References

- `src/reader.rs:182` — `with_max_errors` builder.
- `src/reader.rs:665-737` — Rust unit tests covering cap trip / cap=0 / strict-mode inert behavior.
- `mrrc/exceptions.py:487-495` — `FatalReaderError` class docstring already mentions `max_errors`; reality should match.
close_reason: Landed in #197 (squash-merge f125f3a); GH issue #194 auto-closed

title: Leader parity with pymarc: attribute access and str rendering
description: Surfaced by activating the pymarc oracle (bd-pk26.1). pymarc exposes record.leader as an attribute whose Leader subclasses str ('00136nam a2200061   4500') with positional accessors; mrrc's Python Record.leader is a method, and mrrc.Leader renders as a bare object with no __str__, no positional accessors, and only validation helpers (describe_value, get_valid_values, is_valid_value) visible from Python. pymarc-shaped code touching the leader cannot port. What to do: decide the leader surface per the pymarc-north-star policy (leader as property; Leader.__str__ rendering the 24 bytes; consider str subclass or at least indexable), implement across the four layers, and extend the oracle's value-level comparison to include str(record.leader) once it renders.
close_reason: Merged in PR #290: record.leader property + assignable, str-shaped Leader (str/repr/len/eq/ctor), Display impl in core, oracle extended with leader comparison

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Correction to the description: Leader positional accessors (record_type, record_status, ...) already existed at runtime — the earlier dir() probe misread the wrapper class. The real gaps were: record.leader as method (not property/assignable), no 24-char str rendering (str/repr/len/eq-with-str), no string constructor. All fixed; oracle extended with str(record.leader) comparison.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: PR #290 open: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/pull/290

title: ci: misc check-stack cosmetic fixes (PYO3 abi3 env, stale pre-push comment)
description:
## Problem

Two small cosmetic items found during the check-stack review:

1. **PYO3 abi3 forward-compat env var.** `python-build.yml` sets
   `PYO3_USE_ABI3_FORWARD_COMPATIBILITY: "1"` at the workflow level;
   `.cargo/check.sh` does not. Inert today (Python 3.13 dev venv) but
   will matter when building locally against Python 3.14+ before pyo3
   adds explicit support.

2. **Stale `.githooks` comment in `pre-push` hook.** `.git/hooks/pre-push`
   contains the boilerplate comment `# Activate with: git config
   core.hooksPath .githooks` — there is no `.githooks/` directory in the
   repo; the hook lives in `.git/hooks/`. Leftover from a never-followed-
   through plan to track hooks under version control.

## What to do

1. Add `export PYO3_USE_ABI3_FORWARD_COMPATIBILITY=1` near the top of
   `.cargo/check.sh` (or just before the maturin step).
2. Remove or correct the stale `.githooks` comment in
   `.git/hooks/pre-push`. Note: the hook lives in `.git/hooks/` which
   is not version-controlled, so this is a one-time local edit on each
   contributor's machine — minor.

## Done when

- Both fixes applied.
- pre-push comment is accurate or removed.

## Out of scope

- Tracking git hooks under version control (e.g., via a `.githooks/`
  directory + `core.hooksPath`) — would be a separate bigger
  discussion about contributor onboarding.
close_reason: Done in #258: PYO3_USE_ABI3_FORWARD_COMPATIBILITY=1 exported in check.sh; stale .githooks comment in .git/hooks/pre-push corrected locally (not version-controlled, so not in the diff).

title: Decide whether variant-targeted fuzzing is needed
description:
## Context

Decision bead split out from bd-0x73.5 (2026-05-10). bd-0x73.5
originally listed three options for fuzz expansion; Option C was
"variant-targeted fuzzing": for each documented error variant in
`tests/error_coverage.toml`, a small fuzz target that mutates a
valid record at a specific position with constrained byte ranges,
and asserts the targeted variant fires (with positional context).

bd-0x73.5's own assessment was that C is "probably unnecessary if
A and the integration tests cover the named cases." That's a
data-driven question that can't be answered until bd-0x73.5's
classification fuzz target has produced a corpus to analyze, so
this bead exists separately to capture the decision.

This bead is intentionally filed without a parent: the underlying
data (a meaningful nightly-fuzz corpus) is only available after the
classification target lands and runs for a while, and that timeline
shouldn't gate any release-readiness work elsewhere.

## Trigger

Re-evaluate after bd-0x73.5's classification fuzz target has run
≥2 weeks of nightly fuzz against the production corpus. Until then,
this bead stays open as a placeholder for the decision; no work
required.

## What to do (when triggered)

Inspect the reproducer corpus and the integration-test coverage:

- Are there variants the classification fuzzer surfaced that the
  integration tests in `tests/error_coverage.toml` don't exercise?
- Are there variants in the fixture that the fuzzer never produced
  organically?
- For either gap, would variant-targeted fuzzing close it more
  efficiently than expanding integration coverage or proptest
  strategies (bd-0x73.4)?

If gaps exist that variant-targeted fuzzing is the right tool for,
file a follow-up bead (or PR directly) to add per-variant fuzz
targets. If no gaps exist, close this bead with a brief note
describing what was checked.

## Out of scope

This bead is the *decision*, not the implementation. Implementation
(if any) goes in a follow-up bead.

## References

- bd-0x73.5 — Option A produces the fuzz corpus this bead analyzes.
- `tests/error_coverage.toml` — single source of truth for documented
  variants + expected positional context.
close_reason: Decided: variant-targeted fuzzing (Option C) unnecessary/moot. The variant-fires-with-context assurance is covered deterministically by the tests/error_coverage.toml wired manifest; behavioral/differential fuzz coverage is covered by error_classification (#178) and recovery_mode_consistency (#184). Full rationale in comments.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Consequence for the bd-sdz8 epic: the four format-backfill beads (bd-gbgx parse_leader, bd-uss1 parse_json, bd-2dia decode_marc8, bd-3t62 parse_marcxml) are Option B (format-surface coverage), independent of this Option C decision. They proceed on their own merits; detaching their blocks edge on this bead is a separate gardening step.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Decision (2026-05-28): variant-targeted fuzzing (Option C) is unnecessary — closing as moot. The premise has shifted since this bead was filed: it assumed parse_record was the only fuzz target, but two behavioral targets have since landed and run nightly — error_classification (#178, 2026-05-10; per-input behavioral assertions in strict mode) and recovery_mode_consistency (#184, 2026-05-10; differential consistency across strict/lenient/permissive). The 2-week observation trigger has long elapsed (18 days).

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Rationale: Option C's goal was to assert, per documented variant, that the variant fires with correct positional context. That assurance already exists deterministically in tests/error_coverage.toml — the wired manifest asserts each variant fires with its expected_context (record_index/byte_offset/field_tag/etc.), and recent work (bd-0x73.21.8, bd-0x73.21.11) has been actively tightening that expected_context coverage. A deterministic, maintained manifest is a better fit for that assertion than per-variant mutation fuzzers, which would duplicate the manifest probabilistically and carry constrained-byte-range seeding maintenance. Meanwhile error_classification + recovery_mode_consistency cover the behavioral/differential surface, and parse_record + roundtrip_binary cover panics/OOM/timeout and reader/writer coupling. The marginal value of Option C is low.

title: Python in-place field edits are not persisted to the record
description: Editing a field obtained from a Python Record does not write back. After f = record['245']; f.indicator1 = '1' (or f.add_subfield(...)), re-reading record['245'] still shows the old value; the same holds for fields obtained via get_fields() and fields_by_tag(). pymarc users expect these edits to mutate the record in place, so this is a silent correctness/compatibility gap — the only working modification path is remove-and-re-add (record.remove_field(...) then record.add_field(...)), which the Python writing-records tutorial was updated to use in the doc-accuracy PR. Leader edits via record.leader() DO persist, so the inconsistency is field-specific. Decide whether field accessors should return live references that write through to the record, or whether remove/re-add should be the documented contract (and enforce one consistently).
close_reason: Fixed in PR #247, merged

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #241: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/241

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Implemented as live write-through handles at the Python wrapper layer over new PyO3 positional accessors (field_at / replace_field_at / control_field_value_at / set_control_field_value_at) plus a removal-bumped generation counter; stale handles raise mrrc.StaleFieldError (subclass of MrrcException). Read overhead on the wrapper convenience path is ~1.9x (each attached-handle read re-fetches for alias correctness, +0.24us per subfield read); deliberately NOT adding a mutation-counter cache — a missed bump would silently break alias visibility, recreating the bug class this fixes. Raw _mrrc and Rust paths unaffected. Follow-ups filed: bd-u92v (remove_field semantics + control-field removal no-op + fields() repeated-control-tag enumeration), bd-1pej (query-DSL results remain snapshots).

title: ci: clean up mypy --strict in mrrc/ for permanent strict-mode gating
description:
## Problem

The bd-mgfg audit cycle (PRs #139, #140, #142) drove `mypy mrrc/`
default-mode and `pyright mrrc/` to zero errors and gated both in
`.cargo/check.sh` full mode. `mypy mrrc/ --strict` was deferred —
it had 61 remaining errors after the cleanup, mostly in different
categories from the default-mode and pyright work.

Permanent `--strict` gating (running it as a third typing step in
`.cargo/check.sh`) is desirable for long-term hygiene, but the
cleanup is a longer arc than fit in the v0.8 cycle.

## What's in the 61 remaining errors

Bucketed (categories from the audit):

- `[no-untyped-def]` (~36): functions in `mrrc/` missing parameter
  or return-type annotations. Mostly internal helpers and
  `__init__` methods that take untyped `_inner` Rust objects.
- `[no-untyped-call]` (~8): cascading from above — calls into
  untyped functions.
- `[type-arg]` (~13): bare `List`, `tuple`, `dict` without type
  parameters in older annotations.
- A small tail of `[no-any-return]`, `[arg-type]`, etc., from the
  Rust-bridge boundary.

None of these are real bugs — they're documentation/typing-fidelity
gaps. The Rust types are typed; the wrapper layer doesn't always
re-declare them.

## What to do

Likely a multi-PR effort, taken in slices:

1. **Annotation pass on `mrrc/__init__.py`** — add return types and
   parameter types to every wrapper function. The biggest single
   chunk; may need a `# type: ignore[no-untyped-call]` here and there
   for the `_inner.foo()` Rust-bridge calls until the .pyi covers
   them.
2. **Type-arg widening** — switch bare `List` / `tuple` / `dict` to
   parameterized forms (`List[Subfield]`, `tuple[str, str]`, etc.).
   Mechanical.
3. **Re-audit and fix the remaining tail** — once the bulk is done,
   the leftover errors will be specific enough to address point-by-
   point.
4. **Add `uv run mypy mrrc/ --strict` to `check.sh` full mode** as
   the third typing step. Skipped under `--quick`.

## Done when

- `mypy mrrc/ --strict` reports zero errors.
- `--strict` invocation added to `.cargo/check.sh` full mode.
- CHANGELOG entry under `[Unreleased]` `### Changed` (one terse
  paragraph per the Conventions in `release-procedure.md`).

## Out of scope

- `mypy tests/python/ --strict` — tests have ~1500 missing-annotation
  errors under strict; lint of tests is a separate (much bigger)
  question.
- Permanent `pyright --strict` mode — pyright's basic mode is already
  gated and passing; pyright strict is a different conversation.

## Reference

- bd-mgfg's audit log: PRs #139, #140, #142, #141 (CHANGELOG cleanup).
- Permanent gating decision and rationale lives in #142's PR
  description.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Count refresh (2026-05-28): `uv run mypy mrrc/ --strict` now reports 58 errors in 4 files (the bead body says 61). Error categories unchanged (no-untyped-call, no-untyped-def, type-arg); current head of the list is mrrc/formats/marc.py. Treat 58 as the working number.

comments.author: dchud
