title: Unify UTF-8 decode policy across the three ISO 2709 readers
description:
## Problem

The three ISO 2709 readers have inconsistent UTF-8 decode policy on **both** dimensions: control-field text and data-field subfield content. Holdings is the outlier — strict on both, where bib + authority are lossy.

## Today's state

**Control fields (inline string decode in each reader's `read_record`):**

| Reader | Behavior | Code |
|---|---|---|
| `MarcReader` | `String::from_utf8_lossy(...)` — never fails | `src/reader.rs` |
| `AuthorityMarcReader` | `String::from_utf8_lossy(...).trim_end_matches(...)` — never fails | `src/authority_reader.rs` |
| `HoldingsMarcReader` | `std::str::from_utf8(...)?` → `MarcError::EncodingError` on bad UTF-8 | `src/holdings_reader.rs` |

**Data-field subfield content (via `iso2709::DataFieldParseConfig`):**

| Reader | `Utf8DecodeMode` | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `MarcReader` | `Lossy` | Bad UTF-8 → replacement chars |
| `AuthorityMarcReader` | `Lossy` | Bad UTF-8 → replacement chars |
| `HoldingsMarcReader` | `Strict` | Bad UTF-8 → `MarcError::EncodingError` |

The current code calls this out as deliberate ("Holdings historically used strict UTF-8 here") but it's deliberate-by-accident — the per-reader policies were set independently as the readers were written, not chosen against a unified design rubric.

## Why it matters

MARC records often have dirty UTF-8 (legacy MARC-8 transcoding artifacts, mixed encoding sources). pymarc is broadly lossy. A user processing the same dirty stream gets:

- bib + authority: silent replacement chars in subfields, parsing succeeds
- holdings: `EncodingError` raised, parsing aborts (in strict mode) — or, after bd-jmrt (#124), falls into the new lenient branch which silently skips the offending field

That last point is worth flagging: bd-jmrt made holdings's `parse_data_field` failure (which fires on strict-UTF-8 violations) silently skip in lenient mode. So holdings users who relied on strict UTF-8 catching dirty data are now silently losing fields when they set `RecoveryMode::Lenient`. Previously they got an error; now they get a partial record. Documented in CHANGELOG but worth re-stating.

## Design options

### Option 1 — Make everything lossy

Match bib + authority's existing policy. Holdings becomes lossy on both control fields and subfield content.

- **Pro:** Consistent. Matches pymarc. Real-world dirty data parses.
- **Con:** Breaking change for any holdings user who relied on strict-mode detection of bad UTF-8. Silently loses signal.

### Option 2 — Make everything strict

Match holdings's existing policy. Bib + authority become strict on both control fields and subfield content.

- **Pro:** Consistent. Bad data gets caught instead of silently corrupted.
- **Con:** Breaking change for everyone using bib/authority on real-world data — they suddenly start seeing parse errors on streams that worked fine before. Almost certainly the wrong direction.

### Option 3 — Per-reader UTF-8 mode option

Add a builder method `with_utf8_mode(Utf8DecodeMode)` (or similar) to all three readers. Default to whatever each currently does for backward compatibility.

- **Pro:** No behavior change by default. Users opt in to the policy they want. Aligns with the existing `with_recovery_mode` / `with_max_errors` pattern.
- **Con:** Adds API surface. Two configuration knobs (recovery + utf8) start interacting in ways that need documentation.

## Recommended direction

Option 3, but only after bd-lkcy (the skeleton consolidation) settles. The skeleton work will touch `DataFieldParseConfig` plumbing, may naturally relocate the per-reader UTF-8 choice into a single place, and may suggest the cleanest API. Trying to design this in isolation risks rework once the skeleton lands.

## Out of scope

- Adding strict/lossy decoding for *control field tags themselves* (the 3-byte tag bytes). Bib uses `String::from_utf8_lossy` for tags; authority and holdings use strict `std::str::from_utf8`. Same asymmetry but a different decision (tags should arguably always be ASCII per the spec; non-ASCII tag bytes are pathological data either way).

## Done when

- One uniform policy across the three readers, OR a per-reader configuration knob with documented defaults.
- CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] noting the change (especially if the default behavior changes for any reader).
- Tests covering the policy (whichever direction) on bad-UTF-8 inputs in both control fields and subfield content for all three readers.
- `docs/reference/error-handling.md` updated to reflect the policy.
close_reason: Premise no longer holds: UTF-8 decode policy was unified by the ValidationLevel refactor. All three ISO 2709 readers derive Utf8DecodeMode from the shared DataFieldParseConfig::modes_for (src/iso2709.rs:662) — Structural→Lossy, StrictMarc→Strict — for control fields and subfields alike; holdings is no longer a strict-on-both outlier. Readers now differ only in SubfieldStructureMode, which is intentional per-format behavior. The tag-byte decode asymmetry mentioned in this bead's out-of-scope note still exists; open a new bead if it becomes worth addressing.

title: Add a str-to-Record MARCJSON API for parity with marcxml_to_record, or document the asymmetry
description:
Surfaced while reviewing the error-coverage harness during the v0.8.1 milestone quality review (9lui).

## Asymmetry

MARCXML exposes a public Rust string entry point — `mrrc::marcxml::marcxml_to_record(&str)` — that parses the XML internally. MARCJSON does not: the public Rust API is `mrrc::marcjson::marcjson_to_record(json: &serde_json::Value)`, which takes an already-parsed `Value`. A Rust caller holding a JSON string must run `serde_json::from_str` themselves before calling it.

The Python binding does not have this asymmetry — `mrrc.marcjson_to_record(text)` takes a string and parses internally.

## Consequences observed

- Rust ergonomics: callers parsing MARCJSON from a string do an extra explicit `serde_json` step that the MARCXML path hides.
- Error-coverage harness: the `parse_marcjson` trigger_kind is `UnsupportedKind` in the Rust harness (tests/error_coverage.rs) precisely because there is no `str -> Record` entry point — a malformed/truncated JSON string fails inside serde_json before reaching mrrc. The E402 case is fully exercised in the Python harness instead, so this is NOT a coverage gap; a Rust str API would simply let the Rust harness cover E402 marcjson natively too.

## Decision to make

Either:
- **Implement** `marcjson_str_to_record(&str) -> Result<Record>` (parse the string, then delegate to the existing `marcjson_to_record(&Value)`), wiring it through the error-coverage harness's `parse_marcjson` arm; or
- **Document** the asymmetry as deliberate (Rust callers pass a `Value`; string parsing is the caller's concern) and leave the harness's Python-side coverage as the canonical E402 marcjson check.

Same read-path-asymmetry flavor as bd-i7j6 (Dublin Core / CSV: implement or document) — worth deciding consistently with that one.

## Done when

The asymmetry is resolved one way or the other: a public Rust string entry point exists (and the Rust harness exercises parse_marcjson), or the asymmetry is documented as intentional.

Not v0.8.1-blocking; unmilestoned backlog.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Related to bd-i7j6 (Dublin Core + CSV read-path asymmetry — implement or document): same class of format-parse-from-string asymmetry; decide the two consistently.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Absorbed as one concrete instance of bd-d7aq.3 (format front door / round-trip contract). Keep open until that design lands; if the contract declines a MARCJSON str entry point, close this with that rationale instead.

title: Generate and verify API reference docs to prevent drift
description:
## Context

The API reference is hand-maintained markdown with nothing in tooling tying it to the implementation, and it has now drifted from the real API **twice**:

- The Python `mrrc/_mrrc.pyi` stubs and `docs/reference/python-api.md` had both independently drifted from the PyO3 surface for AuthorityRecord/HoldingsRecord — a phantom `fields()` method, a `control_field()` that is actually `get_control_field()`, `leader` typed as a method when it is a property, and ~9-13 omitted members. Caught and corrected by hand in PR #229.
- The Rust code fences in the mkdocs docs reference methods that do not exist and use wrong call shapes (e.g. `field.subfield("a")` instead of `get_subfield('a')`), because rustdoc doc-tests run in CI but mkdocs Markdown fences are never compiled. Tracked by bd-nj22 (fix) and bd-4ap2 (prevention).

Both are the same root cause on different language surfaces: API docs are hand-written and silently drift. This epic groups the move to **generated + CI-verified** API reference docs.

## Scope

- Python: generate the reference from the `.pyi` via mkdocstrings (already installed and configured), and verify the `.pyi` against the compiled extension in CI so it cannot lie.
- Rust: fix the current stale examples and add a compile-only guard so they cannot regress.

## Children

- Generate the Python API reference with mkdocstrings instead of hand-written tables.
- Add `mypy.stubtest` to CI to verify `_mrrc.pyi` matches the compiled `_mrrc` extension. (Gating prerequisite for the mkdocstrings work, since the generated docs would be sourced from the `.pyi`.)
- bd-nj22 — fix stale Rust API references in docs markdown examples.
- bd-4ap2 — make Rust doc examples compile-checked so they cannot silently go stale.

## Related (not children)

This epic advances the "documentation-as-spec, enforced by tooling" principle from bd-0x73.12 (the v0.8.1 release-readiness gate) for the Python API, but that gate is a one-time release checkpoint while this epic is ongoing post-0.8.1 tooling, so there is no dependency edge. Out of scope and left standalone: bd-8in1 (mkdocs build warnings in archival profiling docs), bd-pebk (query DSL guide cross-linking), mrrc-1qku (Context7/Docfork submission).

## Done when

- The Python API reference is generated from the `.pyi` (hybrid: autodoc members/signatures, retain hand-written narrative), and CI fails if the `.pyi` diverges from the compiled extension.
- The Rust doc examples are correct and compile-checked in CI.

## Note

Post-0.8.1 follow-up; not in the v0.8.1 milestone.
close_reason: API reference generation + drift-verification delivered: the Python reference is generated from the .pyi via mkdocstrings (bd-kxkq), stubtest fails CI/check.sh on any .pyi-vs-extension divergence (bd-9zoo), and mkdocs build --strict gates the docs build on PRs (bd-pwq8). The Rust and Python doc examples were reconciled to the real API (bd-nj22, bd-du5n, bd-tqus), and docstring rendering was cleaned (bd-q0lu). The remaining done-when sub-goal — Rust doc fences compile-CHECKED in CI — is detached to standalone bd-4ap2 and deferred: investigation found full per-fence compilation is overkill (most fences are narrative snippets); the right approach is compiled examples/*.rs included via snippets, which deserves a deliberate pass. Docs are API-correct now, so nothing is rotting.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Related to bd-0x73.12 (v0.8.1 release-readiness gate): this epic advances that gate's 'documentation-as-spec, enforced by tooling' principle for the Python API (the stubtest child in particular). No dependency edge — the gate is a one-time v0.8.1 checkpoint and this epic is ongoing post-0.8.1 tooling.

title: Add criterion benches for uncovered serialization formats and the single-thread parser-pool path
description:
marc_benchmarks.rs covers ISO 2709 read/write/roundtrip, JSON, and MARCXML, but the supported CSV, MODS, Dublin Core, BIBFRAME (RDF), and marcjson serialization paths have no bench, and the rayon_parser_pool / ProducerConsumerPipeline single-thread path has no instruction-count signal under simulation (only the now-excluded multithread paths exercise it). Without coverage, regressions in those paths are invisible to the CodSpeed gate.

What to do: add criterion benches (harness=false, picked up by 'cargo codspeed') for serialize-to and, where applicable, parse-from CSV, MODS, Dublin Core, BIBFRAME, and marcjson over the existing 1k fixture, mirroring the structure of the JSON/XML benches in marc_benchmarks.rs. Add a single-thread bench that drives the parser pool / pipeline with one worker so its per-record instruction cost is tracked under simulation (this is a deterministic single-thread signal, distinct from the excluded parallel-throughput measurement). Keep additions in marc_benchmarks.rs or a new appropriately named bench file with a [[bench]] stanza.

Done when: each listed format has a criterion bench collected by 'cargo codspeed build'; the single-thread parser-pool path has a simulation bench; 'cargo bench --no-run' compiles them; '.cargo/check.sh' passes.
close_reason: Merged in PR #312

title: Wire MARC-8 encoding into the binary writer (currently UTF-8 only)
description: The UTF-8 to MARC-8 encoder exists (encode_string / private encode_marc8 in src/encoding.rs) but is never called by any writer: MarcWriter::write_record (src/writer.rs) always emits the record's internal UTF-8 bytes regardless of leader position 9, and the Python MARCWriter(file_obj) constructor has no encoding option. So MRRC currently writes UTF-8 only and MARC-8 output is unsupported. No bead (open or closed) records a deliberate UTF-8-only-output decision — the closed MARC-8 epics (mrrc-c98, mrrc-vx7, mrrc-wgm) covered decoding and roundtrip-in-tests only. Discovered during the doc-accuracy sweep that removed the fictional 'Writing with Specific Encoding' section (with nonexistent MarcWriter::with_encoding and Python Encoding.MARC8 APIs) from docs/reference/encoding.md. Decide whether to implement MARC-8 output (wire encode_string into MarcWriter, add an encoding option to the Rust writer and the Python MARCWriter, then restore accurate docs) or to commit to UTF-8-only and remove the now-dead encode_string/encode_marc8 code. Until then the docs state UTF-8-only, per the policy of not documenting capabilities that do not exist.

title: Correct or down-scope published benchmark claims until real walltime numbers exist
description:
docs/benchmarks/results.md (dated 2026-01-26) and README line 19 publish hardcoded figures as fact: '~1M rec/s' Rust, '~300k rec/s' Python, '~4x pymarc', '~3.74x' multithread, plus a 'Rust 1.71+' environment line (toolchain is now 1.95). None are reproducible from the repo, no benchmark imports pymarc (so the 'vs pymarc' columns and 'direct comparison for pymarc' note describe code that does not exist), and simulation-mode CodSpeed produces model-estimated instruction counts, not wall-clock records/sec, so it cannot back these claims either.

This overlaps bd-dra6 (publish empirical pymarc comparison); coordinate so the two do not conflict. The honest interim state is: either (a) replace the figures with numbers from a dated, documented real-walltime run on stated hardware against a pinned pymarc version, or (b) down-scope the published claims (remove the specific multipliers, replace with a link to the measurement procedure from the parallel-throughput child) until such a run exists. Do not leave unreproducible quantitative claims in user-facing docs.

What to do: reconcile results.md and README line 19 with reality per (a) or (b); fix the stale toolchain/environment line; remove or clearly mark the fictional pymarc-comparison content; ensure any retained number cites its source run (hardware, versions, date). Coordinate the pymarc-comparison work with bd-dra6 (this can become its concrete first step or supersede part of it).

Done when: no user-facing doc or README line states an unreproducible or simulation-derived records/sec or Nx-pymarc figure; retained numbers cite a dated real-walltime run, or claims link to the measurement procedure; the bd-dra6 overlap is resolved (cross-referenced or merged); CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased].
close_reason: Completed: PR #253 merged. User-facing docs carry hedged historical statements instead of unreproducible figures; results.md rewritten as methodology page; fictional harness references removed; bd-dra6 refreshed and parented under bd-l9zh as the restore-real-figures successor.

title: 0.8.1: python-release.yml: actions/checkout wipes dist/ before gh-release runs (zero assets attached)
description:
## Problem

`.github/workflows/python-release.yml`'s `publish-pypi` job runs
steps in an order that empties `dist/` between PyPI publication and
GitHub Release creation, so the release page is created with the
correct CHANGELOG-extracted notes but **zero asset wheels attached**.

## Source-level evidence

`.github/workflows/python-release.yml:168-199` (current `publish-pypi`
job step order):

```yaml
- uses: actions/download-artifact@v8       # populates dist/
  with:
    pattern: release-wheels-*
    path: dist
    merge-multiple: true

- name: Publish to PyPI                    # uploads dist/*
  uses: pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1

- uses: actions/checkout@v6                # WIPES WORKSPACE
  with:
    sparse-checkout: CHANGELOG.md
    sparse-checkout-cone-mode: false

- name: Extract release notes from CHANGELOG
  ...

- name: Create GitHub Release
  uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v3
  with:
    files: dist/*                          # dist/ is now empty
```

`actions/checkout@v6` defaults to cleaning the workspace before
checkout. `sparse-checkout: CHANGELOG.md` then materializes only
that file. Result: `dist/` is gone by the time
`softprops/action-gh-release@v3` runs.

Verified empirically on the v0.8.0 release run (workflow run
`25091714810`):

> `Pattern 'dist/*' does not match any files.`
> `🤔 dist/* does not include a valid file.`

PyPI publication itself succeeded (it ran while `dist/` still had
the wheels). The GitHub Release was created with notes but
`asset_count: 0`. Wheels were attached manually after the fact via
`gh release upload v0.8.0 ...`.

## Fix options

### Option A (recommended): reorder steps

Checkout first, then download artifacts. The workflow becomes:

```yaml
- uses: actions/checkout@v6
  with:
    sparse-checkout: CHANGELOG.md
    sparse-checkout-cone-mode: false

- uses: actions/download-artifact@v8
  with:
    pattern: release-wheels-*
    path: dist
    merge-multiple: true

- name: Extract release notes from CHANGELOG
  ...

- name: Publish to PyPI
  uses: pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1

- name: Create GitHub Release
  uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v3
  with:
    files: dist/*
    body_path: release_notes.md
```

Both `dist/` and `release_notes.md` exist before either publish step
runs. Cleanest fix; no flag tuning.

### Option B: pass `clean: false` to checkout

```yaml
- uses: actions/checkout@v6
  with:
    sparse-checkout: CHANGELOG.md
    sparse-checkout-cone-mode: false
    clean: false      # keeps dist/ from the prior step
```

One-line change. Relies on `clean: false` actually preserving
`dist/` under sparse-checkout — verify this works before
committing.

### Option C: re-download artifacts after checkout

Add a second `download-artifact` step between checkout and
gh-release. Wasteful (downloads the same wheels twice) but
unambiguous if A/B don't pan out.

## Done when

- Workflow change merged.
- Verified by either:
  - Re-running the workflow on the existing v0.8.0 tag (manually
    re-attaches wheels, but proves the workflow fix); OR
  - Tagging a test prerelease (e.g., `v0.8.1-rc.0`) on a fork or
    test branch and confirming the GitHub Release has all 25 assets
    attached automatically; OR
  - Confirming the v0.8.1 release at tag time has all assets.
- `softprops/action-gh-release@v3` log no longer shows
  "`Pattern 'dist/*' does not match any files`".

## Out of scope

- Cross-compiled Linux wheel job — that path uploads wheels to its
  own artifact and `download-artifact` collects them via the
  `release-wheels-*` pattern; wiring is correct, just affected by
  the same wipe.
- Pre-1.0 release-channel handling.
- Test-wheels job ordering (independent matrix).

## Reference

- workflow run 25091714810 — first observation, v0.8.0 release.
- workflow log: "Pattern 'dist/*' does not match any files."
- v0.8.0 GitHub Release with manually-attached wheels:
  https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/releases/tag/v0.8.0
- `actions/checkout` clean-by-default behavior:
  https://github.com/actions/checkout#fetch-all-history-for-all-tags-and-branches
close_reason: Resolved by #158 (commit 97ee0ee)

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/157

title: Add CHANGELOG lint checks (order, dedupe, line length) to release procedure
description:
## Problem

`docs/contributing/release-procedure.md` currently validates a few structural properties of `CHANGELOG.md` (exactly one `## [Unreleased]`, new `## [X.Y.Z]` header present) but does not catch the class of drift bd-95ho surfaced:

- Duplicate `### <category>` subsections under `[Unreleased]` (two `### Changed` sections).
- Section order violating Keep-a-Changelog (Added → Changed → Deprecated → Removed → Fixed → Security); `### Breaking` mixed in as a standalone heading.
- Bullet lines 300–900+ characters wide instead of hard-wrapped at ~72–80.
- Mixed structure inside `### Added` — topic-grouped (`### Added — <topic>`) alongside a flat `### Added` list.

Catching these at release time (or earlier, during each merge) keeps the released changelogs clean without manual review.

## What to do

Add a new subsection under **Pre-Release Verification** (around 1.3 / 1.4) with executable lint checks, and reference them from the **Final Sanity Check** (section 6). Suggested checks:

1. **Duplicate-subsection check.** Within each version block (`## [Unreleased]` through the next `## [`), fail if any `### ` heading text appears more than once (case-insensitive, ignoring the `### Added — <topic>` suffix so topic-grouped subsections remain allowed).

   ```bash
   awk "/^## \[/{blk++} blk==1 && /^### /{print}" CHANGELOG.md \
       | sed "s/ — .*//" \
       | sort | uniq -d
   # Expect: empty output
   ```

2. **Keep-a-Changelog ordering check.** Within `[Unreleased]`, confirm subsections appear in canonical order (Added, Changed, Deprecated, Removed, Fixed, Security). Topic-grouped `### Added — <topic>` variants collapse to Added for ordering purposes.

3. **Line-length check.** Flag any line in the `[Unreleased]` block longer than ~100 columns. URLs and fenced code blocks are legitimate exceptions — either skip them or pin the threshold generous enough to accommodate them.

   ```bash
   awk "/^## \[Unreleased\]/{in_block=1; next} /^## \[/{in_block=0} in_block && length > 100" CHANGELOG.md
   # Expect: empty output
   ```

4. **Canonical-subsection whitelist.** Warn (not fail) on `### ` headings that are not in the Keep-a-Changelog set or the `### Added — <topic>` form. This catches drift like `### Breaking` landing as its own section instead of being folded into Changed with a `**BREAKING:**` prefix.

5. **Doc the conventions.** A short paragraph at the top of the procedure pointing at `CHANGELOG.md`s format (Keep-a-Changelog link already exists) and stating the wrap-width target + topic-grouped naming convention, so contributors know the rules before the lint fails on them.

## Where to put it

- Add executable snippets to `docs/contributing/release-procedure.md` in section **1.3 Verify Documentation Completeness** (or a new **1.4 Lint Changelog**).
- Reference the lint from the **Definition of Done: "Prepare Release X.Y.Z"** block near the top so its machine-checkable criteria include changelog lint.
- Optionally: extract the checks into a standalone script at `scripts/lint-changelog.sh` and call it from both the release procedure and `.cargo/check.sh` (then changelog drift is caught on every PR, not only at release).

## Done when

- `docs/contributing/release-procedure.md` contains executable lint checks covering duplicate subsections, ordering, and line length.
- The Definition of Done block references the new checks.
- Conventions (wrap width, topic-grouped naming, Keep-a-Changelog mapping) are documented alongside the checks.
- Running the checks against a known-bad changelog (e.g., the current [Unreleased] before bd-95ho lands) fails with actionable output.
- Running them against the post-bd-95ho changelog passes.

## Out of scope

- Actually fixing the current [Unreleased] drift — that is bd-95ho.
- Enforcing the lint in CI — optional stretch goal, noted above as a follow-up.
- Rewriting historical release sections to conform; scope the lint to `[Unreleased]` only.
close_reason: Delivered in #108 (merged to main)

title: Coordinate parallel work tracks A-D (post-bd-0pg6.3)
description:
Execution plan from the 2026-06-12 queue analysis: once bd-0pg6.3 (PR #294) merges, run up to four concurrent subagents, each in an isolated git worktree on its own branch, each producing its own PRs. Tracks were constructed for zero file overlap; the only shared merge point is CHANGELOG.md [Unreleased] (trivial rebase per PR).

Track A — Test integrity (tests/ + fuzz/ only): bd-pk26.4 -> bd-pk26.5 -> bd-pk26.3 -> bd-pk26.2 -> bd-pk26.6 -> bd-3pd5.

Track B — CI/release hardening (workflows only, small-to-sweeping): bd-oayg.3 -> bd-oayg.8 (cancel-in-progress only; registry cleanup done) -> bd-oayg.2 -> bd-oayg.4 -> bd-oayg.5 -> bd-oayg.6 -> bd-oayg.1 (permissions/pinning sweep last) -> bd-oayg.7 (defer item h, readers.rs comment, if perf work still active).

Track C — Packaging manifests + error API: bd-yfe6.2 (Cargo.lock first) -> bd-yfe6.3 -> bd-yfe6.5 -> bd-yfe6.6 -> bd-yfe6.7 -> bd-hgv6 -> bd-yfe6.8 (paired with hgv6) -> bd-h8m8 (last; its lib.rs README-embed edit collides with yfe6.6).

Track D — New surfaces, two sub-streams: D1 bench chain bd-1dbu -> bd-c7sh -> bd-l9zh.1 (guide must follow 1dbu deletions); D2 features bd-e13s -> bd-d7aq.2 -> bd-g2xo -> mrrc-b7l -> bd-1ncs -> bd-tfre (collide only at lib.rs/__init__.py/.pyi registration lines).

MUST STAY SERIAL / excluded from tracks: bd-0pg6.4/.5/.6/.8/.9 (read path, mine), bd-qhll + bd-pk26.7 (both rework recovery skeleton, contend with each other), bd-d7aq.5/.6/.7/.8 (repo-wide regrouping), bd-mcei (rewrites __init__.py — needs quiet window), bd-e24e (after g2xo + h8m8 + manifests), bd-dra6 (gated on .2/.3 + yfe6.4, all now merged or in PR #294; re-measure on x86 before publishing — see bd-0pg6 epic comment), mrrc-3ivc (blocked upstream), bd-4ap2 (deferred).

Stale-bead fixes to apply before launching: mark bd-c5sh blocked-by bd-d7aq.3 (absorbed per its own comment; sits in ready queue inviting duplicate work); bd-oayg.8 description still presents the CodSpeed registry cleanup as pending (done; remaining scope is cancel-in-progress only); bd-l9zh epic's first must-fix item is largely resolved (README hedging already landed via bd-cpom).

Mark this bead in_progress while tracks run; close when all four tracks are drained or the plan is superseded.

title: API coherence for 1.0
description: Epic. Finish the abstractions that were started: one writer skeleton, a complete format front door, equal reader capability across record types, a consolidated query surface, and field-handle ownership solved on the Rust side. Children cover ARCH-1 through ARCH-9 and PAR-1 in Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md; the 1.0 end-state sketch is at the end of that doc's Theme 1.

title: Consolidate the three ISO 2709 writers onto a shared serialization skeleton
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, ARCH-3.

writer.rs:137-259, authority_writer.rs:41-148, and holdings_writer.rs:121-133 independently reimplement directory/data-area serialization. The bibliographic writer uses checked u32::try_from with typed WriterError and per-record error context; authority/holdings use unchecked casts under a clippy allow and pass None for record index (currently unreachable-as-truncating because check_iso2709_size runs first — a quality hazard, not a live bug). The readers already solved this with the shared Iso2709Builder skeleton.

What to do: one generic write_iso2709_record over MarcRecord (or a writer trait mirroring the reader skeleton); all three writers delegate; include the format!-per-directory-entry allocation fix (PERF-10a) while there. Deletes ~200 duplicated lines.

Sequencing: blocked by the roundtrip-property bead (land it first or together); unblocks bd-cdey (wire MARC-8 once, not three times).

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Absorbed from bd-pk26.3 / PR #306 scope deviation: add PartialEq to Record/AuthorityRecord/HoldingsRecord as part of this consolidation. A plain derive does not compile — all three structs carry errors: Arc<Vec<MarcError>> and MarcError wraps io::Error sources with no equality — so this needs a manual impl that deliberately ignores the parse-diagnostics field (decide and document that semantic). The roundtrip properties in tests/properties.rs compare component structures directly and do not depend on record-level equality, so there is no test blocker.

title: Align format_field across Rust, Python, and pymarc with parity tests
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PAR-1.

Rust Field::format_field (record.rs:1175-1197) skips subfield 6 and inserts " -- " before 6xx subject subdivisions; the Python wrapper reimplements it as a plain space-join (__init__.py:368-372). The same record formats differently per layer, and existing tests cover only the cases where both layers and pymarc agree.

What to do: check pymarc's actual behavior and make both layers match it (ideally delegate the Python method to the Rust implementation via PyO3); add parametrized parity tests (6xx with subdivisions, fields containing subfield 6, control fields); sweep other reimplemented wrapper methods (value(), title-ish helpers) the same way.

title: Design the format front door: complete or remove the formats trait; declare the round-trip contract
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, ARCH-1 and ARCH-2.

src/formats/mod.rs defines FormatReader/FormatWriter/RecordIterator traits and a Format enum with exactly one variant (Iso2709); seven of eight formats are free functions that bypass it; Python read()/write() accept only "marc". Dublin Core and CSV are encode-only with no declared round-trip contract — the absence of a function is the only API-level signal.

What to do: pick one model and apply it uniformly (pragmatic: a standardized record_to_<fmt> / <fmt>_to_record free-function convention routed through a completed Format enum for the generic entry points; delete the unused trait surface if it is not the chosen model). A capability table in formats/ rustdoc as the normative contract; implement or explicitly decline each missing direction; streaming variants (PERF-10d) fold in here.

Reframes GitHub issue #92; absorbs bd-c5sh as one concrete instance. Design-first; do not ship 1.0 with both halves half-built.

title: Decide field-handle ownership at the Rust binding boundary
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, ARCH-6.

Rust stores fields by value, so Python field handles resync via a generation counter — _refresh deep-clones the field (tag + all subfield Strings) on every delegated attribute access, __getitem__, get, __contains__, and value(); the canonical pymarc loop clones each field at least twice and crosses the PyO3 boundary 3-4 times per field; add_ordered_field/add_grouped_field are O(n^2) deep clones.

What to do: design spike first — evaluate solving ownership on the Rust side (live field proxies indexing back into the record, or shared interior mutability at the binding boundary) before investing further in the Python-side protocol. If the redesign is deferred, cheap interim mitigations: skip _refresh when the cached generation matches, Rust-side subfield_first(code), Rust-side positional insert.

Sequencing: blocked by the live-handle edge-case matrix bead (pin current semantics first); sequenced with bd-mcei (isolate the handle machinery during the __init__.py split regardless of outcome).

title: Lift reader builder options to all record types; expose source plumbing in Python
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, ARCH-7.

with_recovery_mode/with_validation_level/with_source/with_max_errors/from_path exist on the bibliographic MarcReader only, although the shared skeleton already threads the plumbing — authority/holdings expose a narrower surface for no structural reason. On the Python side, the source attribute on exceptions is never populated (the docs now state this honestly; this bead makes the feature real).

What to do: lift the builder options to all three Rust readers; expose source plumbing through the bindings on all three Python reader classes; update error-handling.md's source-plumbing section when it ships. Related: bd-fe3l (ParseContext threading).

title: Regroup src/ into formats, records, io, and query submodules
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, ARCH-4.

44 flat files sit beside the bibframe/ and formats/ subdirectories with no rule for the boundary: six format modules live flat while formats/ holds only traits; the three record types and their readers/writers are scattered; the query cluster is eight flat modules.

What to do: re-export-only regrouping into formats/ (the format modules join the traits), records/ (record types + builder + MarcRecord trait), io/ (readers/writers/skeleton/pipeline/pool/scanner), query/ (the helper cluster). One PR per group; pairs with the front-door and query-consolidation beads.

title: Consolidate the query and helper traits; delete field_collection
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, ARCH-5.

Eight overlapping modules (field_query, field_query_helpers, format_queries, authority_queries, field_collection, record_helpers, bibliographic_helpers, field_linkage) mean a user must discover and import up to eight similarly-named traits to query records; GitHub issue #225 (query discoverability) is the downstream symptom. field_collection.rs is exported, referenced nowhere, and untested — delete rather than document.

What to do: consolidate under query/: the FieldQuery DSL, one RecordHelpers, per-record-type query traits; target 2-3 imports. bd-2snh (multi-subfield matching) and bd-1pej (handle semantics for query results) are design inputs — decide them as part of this consolidation.

title: Add a prelude; make the parse skeleton crate-private
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, ARCH-8 and ARCH-9.

lib.rs re-exports roughly 30 types flat with no tiering — no "this is the 10% you need" path. iso2709/iso2709_skeleton are doc(hidden) but pub, making the internal sharing mechanism SemVer surface; external implementors of Iso2709Builder are not an intended use case.

What to do: a prelude module for the common path (Record, Field, Subfield, Leader, MarcReader, MarcWriter, core helpers); pub(crate) the skeleton at the 1.0 boundary. Do alongside the module regrouping.

title: Set up cargo-fuzz with parse_record fuzz target
description:
## Problem

ASAN runs nightly (`memory-safety.yml`) and Miri runs on PRs (`miri.yml`), but there is no continuous fuzzing infrastructure. For a parser that consumes arbitrary byte streams, fuzzing is the highest-leverage way to find panics, infinite loops, and memory issues in the parsing code.

## What to do

1. Install and set up cargo-fuzz at the repo root:
   - `fuzz/` directory as a **standalone Cargo workspace** (not a member of the root workspace — full rationale in Design decision 2 below).
   - `fuzz/Cargo.toml` with `cargo-fuzz` and `libfuzzer-sys` dependencies.
   - `fuzz/rust-toolchain.toml` pinning nightly, scoped to the fuzz workspace only so the root stable pin (1.95.0) stays unaffected.
   - `fuzz/fuzz_targets/parse_record.rs` harness that discards the `Result` so only panics count as findings — see Design decision 1 below:
     ```rust
     fuzz_target!(|data: &[u8]| {
         let mut reader = mrrc::MarcReader::new(std::io::Cursor::new(data));
         let _ = reader.read_record();
     });
     ```
2. Populate seed corpus in `fuzz/corpus/parse_record/` from existing fixtures in `tests/data/fixtures/`. Optional polish later: add a handful of deliberate edge-case seeds (truncated leader, bad directory, zero-length data area) to accelerate coverage.
3. Add additional fuzz targets over time — start with:
   - `parse_record` — full ISO 2709 reader (**required for this bead**).
   - `parse_marcxml` — MARCXML reader (follow-up).
   - `decode_marc8` — MARC-8 encoding path, high bug-risk due to escape sequences (follow-up).
4. Add a CI job in `.github/workflows/fuzz.yml` that runs `parse_record` for a short budget (e.g., 5 minutes) on a nightly schedule. On a crash: **fail the job** (so it surfaces on the Actions dashboard) and **upload `fuzz/artifacts/*`** as a workflow artifact so reproducers are retrievable. **No auto-filing** — someone notices a failed nightly run and investigates. Not a PR-blocker.
5. Document local usage in `docs/contributing/fuzzing.md`:
   - How to install cargo-fuzz.
   - How to run targets locally (`cd fuzz && cargo +nightly fuzz run parse_record`).
   - **Finding triage flow**: reproduce from `fuzz/artifacts/<target>/crash-<hash>`, fix the bug, then copy the reproducer into `tests/` as a regression test so it runs on every PR — not only during nightly fuzz.

## Design decisions

1. **Panics are findings; `Err(MarcError)` is normal.** The parser returning `Err` on malformed input is correct behavior, not a bug. The harness must drop the `Result` (`let _ = ...`) so libfuzzer only flags actual panics, OOMs, and timeouts.

2. **`fuzz/` is a standalone Cargo workspace, not a member of the root workspace.** The root pins stable 1.95.0 via `rust-toolchain.toml`; cargo-fuzz requires nightly. Keeping `fuzz/` standalone means stable tooling (`cargo build`, `cargo clippy`, `cargo test` from the repo root) does not descend into it, and `fuzz/rust-toolchain.toml` can pin nightly without affecting the rest of the repo. This also matches cargo-fuzz's default layout.

3. **Commit seed corpus; gitignore mutator-discovered inputs and crash artifacts.** Add a `fuzz/.gitignore` covering:
   - `artifacts/` (crash reproducers — local-only, potentially large, and get copied into `tests/` as regressions anyway).
   - Mutator-discovered corpus additions (the exact pattern to decide during implementation — either ignore `corpus/*/` with an explicit `!corpus/<target>/.gitkeep`, or only ignore files beyond the curated seed set).

4. **CI findings policy: fail-the-job + upload-artifacts + no auto-file.** A scheduled fuzz run that finds a crash fails so the red mark surfaces on the Actions dashboard. `fuzz/artifacts/*` uploads as a workflow artifact so the reproducer survives. Filing issues is manual — fits the original plan's "any panics found during initial fuzzing runs either fixed or filed as separate bugs".

## Done when

- `fuzz/` directory (standalone workspace, own `rust-toolchain.toml`) with at least one working target (`parse_record`).
- Seed corpus populated from test fixtures; `fuzz/.gitignore` correctly scopes what is tracked vs. local-only.
- Running `cd fuzz && cargo +nightly fuzz run parse_record` works locally.
- Nightly CI workflow runs `parse_record` for a bounded time budget, fails on crash, uploads `fuzz/artifacts/*`.
- `docs/contributing/fuzzing.md` covers install, local run, and the triage-to-regression flow.
- Any panics found during initial fuzzing runs either fixed (with the reproducer copied into `tests/` as a regression) or filed as separate bugs.
- CHANGELOG entry under next version.
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes (fuzz targets compile under nightly but must not break stable build).
close_reason: Delivered in PR #114 (merged as f3bd200)

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/90

title: Publish empirical benchmark results comparing mrrc vs pymarc
description:
Produce a real, reproducible mrrc-vs-pymarc benchmark comparison and restore citable figures to the documentation. User-facing docs carry a hedged historical statement (early benchmarking suggested at least a 4x single-threaded speedup over pymarc; needs re-measurement) with no specific figures; docs/benchmarks/results.md documents what a citable run must record. No comparison harness exists in the repository — the scripts/benchmark_comparison.py referenced by older docs was never in this repo's history — so the harness must be written from scratch.

## What to do

1. Write a comparison harness (committed to the repo, e.g. under scripts/) that measures mrrc (Rust), pymrrc, and a pinned pymarc version over the same fixtures: read, read+field extraction, roundtrip at minimum; MARCXML parse/emit if practical.
2. Run it per the citable-run procedure in docs/benchmarks/results.md: record date, hardware (CPU/RAM/OS), Rust toolchain, Python version, pinned versions of every library, fixture provenance. Real walltime on a quiet machine — CI CodSpeed simulation numbers cannot back records/sec claims.
3. Consider running against representative real library data in addition to the synthetic fixtures (generate_benchmark_fixtures.py output), and at more than one scale (10k minimum; 100k ideally).
4. Publish the results in docs/benchmarks/ with the full run context, replace the hedged statements in README/docs with figures citing the run, and document the regeneration procedure (manual per-release refresh is fine; CI artifact automation is optional).
5. For parallel-throughput figures, coordinate with the measurement procedure from bd-ieoe.

## Done when

docs/benchmarks/ contains a comparison table with numbers, hardware, versions, and date from a committed, re-runnable harness; README/docs hedged claims are replaced with figures citing that run; the regeneration procedure is documented; CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased].

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Coordination with bd-cpom (benchmark-claims down-scope): user-facing docs no longer publish specific throughput or pymarc multipliers; they carry a hedged historical statement (early benchmarking suggested at least 4x over pymarc; needs re-measurement). No comparison harness exists in the repo — scripts/benchmark_comparison.py referenced by the old docs was never in this repo's history. This bead's deliverable is therefore: write the harness from scratch, run it per the citable-run procedure in docs/benchmarks/results.md (date, hardware, OS, toolchain, pinned pymarc version, committed harness, fixture provenance), and restore real figures to the docs from that run.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: The bd-0pg6.1 PR will seed scripts/bench_read_throughput.py (mrrc-only file-path read throughput at multiple scales, used for per-child wall-clock A/B during the perf epic). Build the comparison harness here on top of that script — add pymarc columns and the citable-run context recording — rather than starting from scratch.

title: Reconcile rust-api.md reference tables and two Python-fence stale refs with the real API
description:
Surfaced during bd-nj22 / #233 (PR #238), which corrected every Rust *code-fence example* across the docs but deliberately left two non-example artifacts:

## rust-api.md prose 'Key Methods' tables

The Record-methods table (~lines 40-48) and Field-accessors table (~95-101) in docs/reference/rust-api.md use method-call notation that no longer matches the API the corrected examples on the same page now use:

- Names: `field(tag)` should be `get_field(tag)`; `control_field(tag)` should be `get_control_field(tag)`; `subfield(code)` should be `get_subfield(code)`.
- Field-vs-method: `leader()`, `tag()`, `indicator1()`, `indicator2()`, `subfields()` are presented as methods but `leader`, `tag`, `indicator1`, `indicator2`, `subfields` are public fields.
- Return types: e.g. `title()` is listed as `Option<String>` but `RecordHelpers::title` returns `Option<&str>`; `fields_by_tag` is listed as `Vec<&Field>` but returns an iterator. Verify every row's return type against src/.

Reconcile each row against src/record.rs / record_helpers.rs (names, field-vs-method, return types).

## Two Python-fence stale refs

- docs/reference/encoding.md:106 — `leader = record.leader()` in a Python fence (Python's leader is a property; also confirm the Python Record exposes `leader` at all).
- docs/guides/migration-from-pymarc.md:110 — the pymarc-vs-mrrc comparison table cites `record.control_field('001')` as the mrrc Python equivalent; confirm whether the Python Record has `control_field` or only `get_control_field`, and fix the table accordingly.

## Done when

- The rust-api.md Key Methods tables match the real API (names, field/method distinction, and return types).
- The two Python refs are corrected or removed.

Not v0.8.1-blocking; unmilestoned. Same doc-vs-reality drift class as bd-nj22 (examples) and bd-4ap2 (compile-checking, which would not catch prose tables).
close_reason: Verified already resolved (read-only check, no change needed). The docs/reference/rust-api.md 'Key members' tables already match src exactly: get_control_field->Option<&str> (record.rs:180), get_field->Option<&Field> (203), fields()/fields_by_tag()->impl Iterator (229/246), get_subfield->Option<&str> (1063), subfields()->impl Iterator (1079), title/author->Option<&str> (record_helpers.rs:62/82), isbns->Vec<&str> (165); leader/tag/indicator1/indicator2 are marked as fields. Both flagged Python fences are also correct against the runtime: Record.leader IS a method (so encoding.md 'record.leader()' is right) and Record.control_field exists while get_control_field does not (so migration-from-pymarc.md 'record.control_field("001")' is right). The bead's assumptions predated a later docs reconciliation; nothing left to fix.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Corrected during the doc-accuracy sweep (this PR). Two premises in this bead were wrong against the compiled extension: (1) Python Record.leader is a METHOD, not a property — record.leader() is correct, so docs/reference/encoding.md:106 was already right; (2) the Python Record control-field accessor is control_field (NOT get_control_field), so migration-from-pymarc.md's record.control_field('001') was already correct. get_control_field is correct only for AuthorityRecord/HoldingsRecord. This PR resolves the bead's actual scope: the rust-api.md Key Methods tables (Record + Field) are reconciled against src/ (names get_field/get_control_field/get_subfield, field-vs-method for tag/indicator1/indicator2/leader/subfields, return types such as Option<&str> and iterators) and the fake AuthorityRecordBuilder::new()/HoldingsRecordBuilder::new() examples are replaced with the real ::builder(leader) API. It also fixed a broader set of Python fences than originally listed (record.leader used without parens, nonexistent record.isbns(), field.ind1/ind2 aliases, and fields_by_tag results used with pymarc subscripting). Underlying code issues found during the sweep are filed separately: bd-cdey (MARC-8 write unsupported), bd-gmax (fields_by_tag returns unwrapped fields), bd-blja (in-place field edits not persisted).

title: ci: add CHANGELOG lint to lint.yml
description:
## Problem

`scripts/lint-changelog.sh` enforces Keep-a-Changelog ordering, line
length (≤100 cols), and duplicate-subsection detection on the
`[Unreleased]` block of `CHANGELOG.md`. It runs locally via
`.cargo/check.sh` but is not in any CI workflow. PRs from contributors
who bypass local hooks (Dependabot, occasional one-offs from external
contributors) ship with un-linted CHANGELOG drift — order violations
or >100-column lines won't be caught until someone re-runs `check.sh`
locally.

## What to do

Add a job to `.github/workflows/lint.yml`:

```yaml
changelog:
  name: CHANGELOG lint
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v6
    - run: bash scripts/lint-changelog.sh
```

No new dependencies — `lint-changelog.sh` is bash + awk only.

## Done when

- Job added to `lint.yml`.
- Verified by deliberately introducing a violation in a draft PR
  (e.g., a line over 100 cols in `[Unreleased]`) and confirming the
  job fails.
- CHANGELOG entry under `[Unreleased]` `### Changed`.

## Out of scope

- Changing the lint script behavior — separate bead if rules need to
  evolve.
- Linting the released sections (the script intentionally only checks
  `[Unreleased]` because released sections are frozen).
close_reason: Done in #257: dedicated changelog.yml lints CHANGELOG.md in CI (separate workflow because lint.yml ignores **.md). Verified the job fires and passes on the PR.

title: Add community infrastructure files (issue/PR templates, SECURITY.md, CoC)
description:
## Problem

CONTRIBUTING.md (332 lines) is comprehensive, LICENSE is present, but the repo is missing standard community signaling files expected by potential contributors and security researchers:

- No `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/` directory
- No `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md`
- No `SECURITY.md` (vulnerability reporting policy)
- No `CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md`

These are ~1 hour of work but they signal "this is a real project" to anyone evaluating the library as a dependency.

## What to do

1. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md` — include fields for: mrrc version, Python version (if applicable), OS, minimal reproducer, expected vs actual behavior, sample MARC if possible.
2. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/feature_request.md` — include fields for: use case, proposed API, alternatives considered, willingness to contribute.
3. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml` — optional, to disable blank issues and link to discussions/beads if applicable.
4. `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` — checklist for: `.cargo/check.sh` passed, CHANGELOG updated, docs updated, tests added, linked beads ID.
5. `SECURITY.md` — short policy: supported versions, how to report (email or private advisory), expected response time.
6. `CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md` — adopt Contributor Covenant 2.1 (standard, well-accepted).
7. Link the new files appropriately from README and CONTRIBUTING.md.

## Done when

- All four files/directories present
- Issue templates tested by opening a test issue in a draft state (or via `gh issue create`) to verify rendering
- PR template tested by opening a draft PR
- `SECURITY.md` includes real contact info (email or GitHub private advisory pointer)
- CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md references Contributor Covenant with enforcement contact
- README links to the new files under a Community/Contributing section
- CHANGELOG entry under next version

title: Add mrrc CLI binary with cat/convert/validate/stats subcommands
description:
## Problem

There is no `[[bin]]` target in `Cargo.toml` — mrrc is a library only. A small CLI would be a high-leverage showcase for the Rust core, support simple interactive use by catalogers and data engineers, and demonstrate real-world API ergonomics.

## What to do

1. Add a binary target to `Cargo.toml` (either `src/bin/mrrc.rs` single-file, or a separate `mrrc-cli` crate in a new workspace member if we want to keep CLI deps out of the library).
2. Use `clap` v4 with derive for arg parsing.
3. Subcommands:
   - `mrrc cat <file>` — print records in human-readable form (line-mode/.mrk style ideally, so this depends on or precedes bead for .mrk support)
   - `mrrc convert <input> <output>` — convert between formats (autodetect by extension: .mrc, .xml, .json, .mrk, .csv, .ttl for BIBFRAME Turtle)
   - `mrrc validate <file>` — run all validators (IndicatorValidator, IsbnValidator, RecordStructureValidator, EncodingValidator), emit report to stdout or file
   - `mrrc stats <file>` — record count, tag-frequency histogram, encoding breakdown, average record size
4. Global flags: `--recovery-mode {strict,lenient,permissive}`, `--output-format {text,json}`, `--quiet`, `--verbose`.
5. Integration tests using `assert_cmd` + `predicates` under `tests/cli.rs`.
6. Document CLI in `docs/cli.md` with example invocations.
7. Update README with a CLI section showing the most common use: `mrrc convert input.mrc output.xml`.

## Done when

- `mrrc` binary builds and installs via `cargo install mrrc`
- All four subcommands work on the test fixtures
- `mrrc --help` and each subcommand's `--help` are self-documenting
- Integration tests cover each subcommand's happy path and at least one error path
- README shows CLI examples
- `docs/cli.md` with examples for each subcommand
- CHANGELOG entry under next version
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes (including the binary target)

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/94

title: Post-merge CI follow-ups: add new fuzz targets to the fuzz matrix; run integration tests under ASAN
description: Two small workflow edits left over from Track A PRs that could not touch .github/workflows/, shippable as one PR once both parents merge. (1) PR #315 adds fuzz targets parse_authority, parse_holdings, parse_bibframe, and parse_lenient, but fuzz.yml runs a hardcoded 9-target matrix — add the four targets AFTER #315 merges (adding them first would break the scheduled run, which builds from main). (2) PR #310 rebuilt tests/memory_safety_asan.rs around real mrrc workloads, but memory-safety.yml runs cargo test --lib only, so integration tests never execute under ASAN — add --tests AFTER #310 merges. Expect trivial conflicts with the open CI-hardening stack (#308, #311) if unmerged when this lands.

title: ci: gate heavy workflows on the dependabot actor
description:
Dependabot PRs (dependency/lockfile bumps) currently trigger the full PR matrix, including the 30-job wheel build+test matrix in python-build.yml and the CodSpeed benchmark jobs — heavy workflows that rarely add signal for a routine version bump. Gate the expensive jobs on the actor so dependabot PRs skip them (e.g. a job-level if: github.actor != 'dependabot[bot]', or condition the heavy matrix legs). Keep the lightweight correctness gates (lint, core tests, semver) running on dependabot PRs so a breaking bump is still caught.

Decide per-workflow which jobs are heavy enough to gate: python-build.yml's full wheel matrix and the benchmark-python/benchmark-rust CodSpeed jobs are the obvious candidates; build.yml and test.yml are cheaper and arguably should still run. Document the choice in the PR.

Done when: dependabot PRs no longer trigger the 30-job wheel matrix and CodSpeed jobs (verify on a dependabot PR or a manual actor-spoofed test), while correctness gates still run; rationale for which jobs are gated is in the PR description.
close_reason: Done in #258: heavy jobs (python-build PR wheel matrix, benchmark-python CodSpeed, benchmark-rust) gated with if: github.actor != 'dependabot[bot]'; lint/tests/semver still run on dependabot PRs.

title: Thread ParseContext through Leader / encoding / marcjson / validation paths
description:
## Context

v0.8 quality review (bd-z8oc) audited the call sites of `MarcError::leader_msg`, `MarcError::invalid_field_msg`, and `MarcError::encoding_msg` — `pub(crate)` constructors that bypass the structured-positional-context promise of v0.8 by setting `record_index = None`, `byte_offset = None`, `field_tag = None`, `source_name = None`, etc.

Bypass call sites by module:

- `src/leader.rs` — 10 calls in `Leader::from_bytes` and validation. No `ParseContext` available; threading it requires changing the public `Leader::from_bytes` signature.
- `src/encoding.rs` — 3 calls (`encoding_msg`). MARC-8 / UTF-8 decode helpers, called from parsing paths but `encoding.rs` doesn't take `ParseContext`.
- `src/marcjson.rs` — 5 calls. JSON reader. Has access to record bytes but doesn't currently thread `ParseContext`.
- `src/validation.rs`, `src/record_validation.rs` — small validation routines.
- `src/recovery.rs` — bypass calls inside `try_parse_field` and `parse_4digits/5digits` helpers; **errors never escape the surrounding salvage `if let Ok(...)` patterns**, so they're internal sentinels with no user-visible impact (no fix needed unless the helpers are reused elsewhere).
- `src/authority_writer.rs` — writer path, no `ParseContext` is appropriate here (writer errors aren't parse errors).

## Why low priority

Most bypass call sites in real user-visible paths are in `src/leader.rs` — these emit `\"invalid leader: <message>\"` errors that lack byte-offset and source-name. v0.8 already ships these as-is, and the structured-error promise is honored everywhere ParseContext IS threaded. Filling in these gaps is incremental polish, not a correctness fix.

## What to do

For each module, decide whether to:
- Thread `ParseContext` through (substantive API change to public functions like `Leader::from_bytes`).
- Add a new public constructor `Leader::from_bytes_with_context(bytes, &ctx)` and deprecate the bare form.
- Leave alone (writer-side, internal-sentinel cases).

Consider this bead as the planning item; the actual implementation is per-module.

## Done when

- Each module audited; decision recorded for each (thread / new constructor / leave).
- Implementations land per-module as separate small PRs.
- No `pub(crate) fn *_msg` constructor remains in `src/error.rs` whose call sites have a usable `ParseContext` in scope but aren't using it.

title: Add line-mode MARC (.mrk / .mrk8) read+write support
description:
## Problem

Line-mode MARC (`.mrk` for UTF-8, `.mrk8` for MARC-8 encoded) is the human-readable text format produced and consumed by MARCEdit — the dominant desktop tool used by catalogers. Not supporting it is the largest interop gap with the real-world cataloging workflow. The format looks like:

```
=LDR  00000cam a2200000 a 4500
=008  230101s2023    nyu           000 0 eng d
=245  10$aExample Title $bsubtitle /$cresponsibility.
=300  \\$a300 pages :$bill. ;$c24 cm.
```

Records separated by blank line. Tags `=LDR` and `=00X` hold leader/control fields; others use indicators in columns 5-6 with `\` for blank indicators.

## What to do

1. Create `src/line_mode.rs` with:
   - `LineModeReader<R: Read>` — streams records from `.mrk`/`.mrk8` input
   - `LineModeWriter<W: Write>` — emits records in MARCEdit format
   - `record_to_mrk(&Record) -> String`
   - `record_from_mrk(&str) -> Result<Record>`
2. Handle both `.mrk` (UTF-8) and `.mrk8` (MARC-8) variants — `.mrk8` requires re-encoding subfield content to MARC-8 via existing `src/encoding.rs`.
3. Handle edge cases: blank indicator representation (`\` or space), embedded dollar signs in subfield content (MARCEdit escapes as `{dollar}`), multi-line fields (MARCEdit wraps long fields — decide whether to emit wrapped or unwrapped).
4. Add PyO3 bindings in `src-python/src/` exposing `LineModeReader`, `LineModeWriter`, plus free functions `record_to_mrk()`/`record_from_mrk()`.
5. Update `mrrc/__init__.py` exports and `mrrc/_mrrc.pyi` type stubs.
6. Cross-format tests: ISO 2709 → .mrk → ISO 2709 produces byte-identical output for well-formed input.
7. Obtain a real MARCEdit sample file (or generate one via MARCEdit on a test record) and verify parse-then-emit matches the input.
8. Update README feature table to show .mrk R/W.
9. New docs page: `docs/guides/line-mode-marc.md`.

## Done when

- `src/line_mode.rs` implements reader, writer, and conversion functions
- Python bindings exposed and tested
- Type stubs complete
- Roundtrip tests: ISO 2709 ↔ .mrk, MARCXML ↔ .mrk, both byte-equal for well-formed input
- MARCEdit interop test: at least one sample .mrk file from MARCEdit parses cleanly
- README feature table updated
- `docs/guides/line-mode-marc.md` with examples
- CHANGELOG entry under next version
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/93

title: Add parse_leader fuzz target
description:
## Context

Follow-up to bd-dfn2 (cargo-fuzz infrastructure). The formal-methods verification strategy in mrrc-testbed (`formal-methods-verification-strategy.md`) recommends a `parse_leader` target beyond the initial `parse_record`, `parse_marcxml`, and `decode_marc8` set noted in bd-dfn2.

## Why

Leader parsing has a **tiny fixed state space** (24 bytes). That makes it the fastest-converging fuzz target in mrrc: libfuzzer saturates the input space quickly, so any panic or off-by-one that exists will surface fast. It's also a natural companion to a future Kani proof over the same 24-byte input space.

## What to do

1. Add `fuzz/fuzz_targets/parse_leader.rs` exercising `Leader::from_bytes` (or whichever public API the reader uses for the 24-byte leader).
2. Seed `fuzz/corpus/parse_leader/` with 24-byte leaders extracted from the existing `fuzz/corpus/parse_record/*` fixtures.
3. Add a `[[bin]]` entry for `parse_leader` in `fuzz/Cargo.toml`.
4. Extend `.github/workflows/fuzz.yml` to run `parse_leader` alongside `parse_record` (or in a matrix).
5. Document the new target in `docs/contributing/fuzzing.md`.

## Done when

- `cd fuzz && cargo +nightly fuzz run parse_leader` runs locally.
- Nightly CI exercises the target.
- Any panics found are fixed (with reproducer copied to `tests/` as a regression) or filed.
close_reason: Moot: parse_leader fuzz coverage is subsumed by the active parse_record target (full reader drives Leader::from_bytes over the 24-byte leader). Distinct Kani-proof-companion idea belongs to a future formal-methods bead, not this fuzzing epic. See comments.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Detached the 'blocks: bd-bfrh' edge (2026-05-28). bd-bfrh decided the Option C question (variant-targeted fuzzing — closed as moot); this bead is an Option B format-surface fuzz target that delivers value independently of that decision, so it should not have been gated on it. Parent-child link to bd-sdz8 retained.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Decision (2026-05-28): closing as moot. A standalone parse_leader fuzz target is redundant with the active parse_record target, which already drives the full ISO 2709 reader — including Leader::from_bytes over the 24-byte leader — so libfuzzer already explores the leader input space within parse_record. The epic's Done-when explicitly anticipates closing targets that are redundant with existing coverage. The one distinct justification noted in this bead — a fast-converging companion to a future Kani proof over the 24-byte leader — is a formal-verification concern, not a fuzzing one, and no Kani work is currently scoped. If formal verification of leader parsing is taken up later, file a fresh bead on the formal-methods track rather than reviving this fuzzing target.

title: Python Record.fields_by_tag returns unwrapped fields, unlike get_fields
description: In the Python bindings, Record.get_fields(tag) returns pymarc-compatible mrrc.Field objects that support field['a'] subscripting and the pymarc accessors, but Record.fields_by_tag(tag) returns lower-level _mrrc field objects that are not subscriptable, so record.fields_by_tag('245')[0]['a'] raises TypeError while record.get_fields('245')[0]['a'] works. The two look interchangeable but behave differently. fields_by_indicator, fields_in_range, and fields_matching all return the wrapped (subscriptable) fields — fields_by_tag is the lone exception. This silently broke several Python doc examples (fixed in the doc-accuracy PR by switching them to get_fields). Either wrap fields_by_tag results the same way get_fields does, or remove/deprecate fields_by_tag from the Python surface in favor of get_fields.
close_reason: Removed Python Record.fields_by_tag; consolidated on pymarc-standard get_fields across all Python layers (wrapper, PyO3, stub). Merged in PR #274 (Closes #240).

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #240: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/240

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: DEFERRED DECISION (resume fresh): the redundancy between Python Record.fields_by_tag and get_fields is unresolved. Facts: (1) pymarc has get_fields(*args) + indexing/iteration but NO fields_by_tag, so get_fields is the pymarc-standard accessor; (2) in the Python wrapper get_fields is a strict superset of fields_by_tag (variadic, handles control 00X + data; fields_by_tag is single-tag data-only); (3) Rust core Record has BOTH (get_fields -> Option<&[Field]>, fields_by_tag -> impl Iterator) and PyO3 exposes both; (4) ~7 Python call sites use fields_by_tag (tests, examples/python/bibframe_roundtrip.py, docs/reference/python-api.md). User steer: pymarc compatibility is the major focus of the Python wrapper; backward-compat is NOT a concern (new library, few users) -> points to REMOVING the non-pymarc fields_by_tag from the Python surface and consolidating on get_fields, keeping Rust's fields_by_tag. Open sub-question: remove from wrapper only, or also from the _mrrc PyO3 binding + stub. IMPLICATION FOR #266: it adds a regression test that locks IN fields_by_tag parity; if we remove the method, CLOSE #266 UNMERGED and supersede with a removal PR (resolves bd-gmax via the remove/deprecate path). User has NOT decided yet; do not merge #266 or start removal until they do.

title: Repository cleanup: scratch dirs, placeholder binary, orphaned test scripts, docs.rs README embed
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, CLEAN-1..3, TEST-7a/c, DOC-6d.

Delete: empty untracked examples_tmp/; the stray test-output/walkthrough-pr79-*.md; the placeholder src/main.rs (5-line banner — bd-e24e's CLI is its successor or its replacement); orphaned tests/create_sample_data.py (fixtures come from scripts/generate_benchmark_fixtures.py per tests/python/README.md); dead tmp/stubtest-baseline.txt. Also trim the docs.rs front page: src/lib.rs:1 embeds the full README via include_str!, so docs.rs carries CI badges and repo-relative links that 404 off GitHub — split the crate docs from the README or curate what is included. (field_collection.rs deletion lives in the query-consolidation bead.)

title: Include regex pattern in SubfieldPatternQuery repr
description:
GitHub issue #226. Introspecting a `SubfieldPatternQuery` currently produces `<SubfieldPatternQuery tag=084 subfield=a>`, which omits the regex pattern — arguably the most useful part for debugging a query at the REPL or in logs.

## Problem

The pattern is stored in the Rust core as a compiled `regex::Regex` in a private field (`src/field_query.rs`, `struct SubfieldPatternQuery`). The PyO3 `__repr__` in `src-python/src/query.rs` only formats `tag`, `subfield_code`, and `negate`, so there is no way to recover the pattern from the repr or from a getter. The PyO3 wrapper also exposes only a `negate` getter (no `tag`, `subfield_code`, or `pattern` getter), and the type stub in `mrrc/_mrrc.pyi` mirrors that gap.

## Proposed approach

1. Add a public accessor on the Rust struct, e.g. `pub fn pattern(&self) -> &str { self.pattern.as_str() }` — `regex::Regex::as_str()` returns the original pattern string, so no extra storage is needed.
2. Add a PyO3 `#[getter] pattern` in `src-python/src/query.rs` returning that string.
3. Update both `__repr__` branches (negated and non-negated) to include the pattern, e.g. `<SubfieldPatternQuery tag=084 subfield=a pattern=^abc>`. Decide whether the pattern should be quoted for readability when it contains spaces or special characters.
4. Add the `pattern` property (and, while there, the missing `tag`/`subfield_code` getters if we want the repr-backing fields exposed) to `mrrc/_mrrc.pyi`. Confirm whether `mrrc/__init__.py` needs any change — the class is re-exported directly, so likely not.
5. Add or extend a test in `tests/python/test_query_dsl.py` asserting the pattern appears in the repr for both negated and non-negated queries.

## Done when

- `repr(SubfieldPatternQuery("084", "a", r"^abc"))` includes the pattern string.
- A `pattern` getter is available on the Python object and reflected in `mrrc/_mrrc.pyi`.
- Rust and Python tests cover the repr/getter, and `.cargo/check.sh` passes.
close_reason: SubfieldPatternQuery now exposes a pattern getter and includes the regex in its repr; landed in PR #237 (merged, closes GH #235). Credited @acdha (report #226).

title: Consolidate per-variant MarcError accessors via fn metadata() -> ErrorMetadata
description:
## Context

v0.8 quality review (bd-z8oc) identified that `MarcError`'s structured-error refactor + stable-codes work added per-variant `match` arms across ~17 sites. Each new variant requires touching:

- `src/error.rs::code()`, `slug()`, `kind_name()`, `body_text()`, `bytes_near()`, `with_bytes_near()`, `record_index()`, `record_control_number()`, `field_tag()`, `byte_offset()`, `record_byte_offset()`, `source_name()`, `message_text()`, `to_json_value` extras, the `ERROR_CODES` test table
- `mrrc/exceptions.py` Python mirror class
- `src-python/src/error.rs::describe()` PyO3 mapping
- `tests/python/test_errors.py::_CODE_TABLE`

That's 17+ places per variant. The right consolidation isn't to collapse the trait or the enum — it's to extract a single `fn metadata(&self) -> ErrorMetadata` returning a struct of `Option`s, then write each accessor as a one-liner over that struct. The Python and PyO3 sides could then be driven from a single shared table.

## Why a focused PR

This is a non-trivial refactor (~200-line diff across 4 files). Doing it inside the v0.8 quality-review PR would either bloat that diff to where it's hard to review, or rush the refactor under deadline pressure.

## What to do

1. Define `ErrorMetadata` struct in `src/error.rs` with all the per-variant `Option<>` fields currently exposed as accessors.
2. Implement `MarcError::metadata(&self) -> ErrorMetadata` with one big `match` over all variants.
3. Rewrite each accessor (`record_index`, `byte_offset`, etc.) as a one-liner reading from `metadata()`. Keep the public accessor signatures unchanged so external callers see no change.
4. Mirror the same shape in `mrrc/exceptions.py` so the Python side has a single source of truth too.
5. Update `src-python/src/error.rs::describe()` to drive PyO3 conversion from a shared table where possible.
6. Verify the snapshot tests (`insta`/`syrupy`) still produce identical output.

## Done when

- `src/error.rs` per-variant accessor methods each become a one-liner over `metadata()`.
- New variant addition requires touching `MarcError` enum + `metadata()` match arm + Python mirror — substantially fewer than the current 17 places.
- Tests pass; snapshot output unchanged (the consolidation is internal).
- CHANGELOG entry under v0.9.

## Scope discipline

Do **not** restructure the variant set itself, change accessor public signatures, or rename anything. The goal is purely internal consolidation of the `match` arms.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Pair with bd-yfe6.8 (mark MarcError and grower enums non_exhaustive) as one error-API pass — both touch the same enum surface and the semver-checks allows.

title: Fix Rust API reference drift, documentation strays, and release-procedure inaccuracies
description:
Documentation drift findings from docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md (DOC-5, DOC-6, OPS-7); none break copy-pasted Python code (those are a separate bead) but several mislead.

Rust API reference (docs/reference/rust-api.md):
- Lines 333-338: `parse_batch_parallel` example calls it with one argument; the real signature is `parse_batch_parallel(record_boundaries: &[(usize, usize)], buffer: &[u8])` (src/rayon_parser_pool.rs:65-68). The Rust concurrency tutorial (docs/tutorials/rust/concurrency.md:58-71) shows the correct shape — copy it.
- The MarcError variants table lists 5 variants including InvalidRecord, which was removed in 0.8.0; the enum has 17 variants. Replace the table with a pointer to error-codes.md (accurate, per-variant) and docs.rs.
- `parse_batch_parallel` / `parse_batch_parallel_limited` are public module functions in mrrc/_mrrc.pyi (:951, :955) but missing from python-api.md's Parallel Processing section — add them with a boundary-scanner example.

Doc strays:
- Five pages build but are not in mkdocs.yml nav (contributing/project-layout.md and four design/ files); they are reachable via links from in-nav pages. Decide deliberately: add to nav or leave link-only (no change needed if intentional — record the decision in the nav comment).
- mkdocs.yml:2 comment points at docs/design/DOCS-REORG.md; the file lives at docs/history/DOCS-REORG.md.
- docs/getting-started/quickstart-python.md:28-29: comment says "raises KeyError if missing; use .get()" while annotating a .get() call that returns None — reword so the KeyError remark clearly refers to subscripting.

Release procedure (docs/contributing/release-procedure.md):
- Says "15 wheels total" expected; the workflow publishes 25 (15 native + 10 cross-compiled Linux).
- Still commands `bd sync` / `bd create`; the project migrated to `br` (br sync --flush-only etc.).
- Says "Rust 1.71+"; the toolchain pins 1.95 and the MSRV declaration is being corrected in its own bead — align the wording with whatever that bead lands.

Done when: every claim above matches the code/workflows it describes; .cargo/check.sh passes (includes mkdocs --strict).
