comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #276: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/276 (milestone v0.8.3)

title: Resolve Dublin Core + CSV read-path asymmetry (implement or document)
description:
## Decision (2026-06-07)

Dublin Core and CSV are **write-only by design** in built-in mrrc. No read path will be implemented for either. Both are lossy projections of a MARC record (CSV is a flat, user-chosen-column table; Dublin Core collapses MARC's hundreds of fields into 15 elements), so there is no canonical, round-trippable mapping back to MARC. Callers who need to import CSV or harvested Dublin Core (e.g. OAI-PMH) write their own reader matched to their source data — mrrc deliberately does not impose one.

## State at decision time

Both formats were already strictly write-only in code: `src/dublin_core.rs` and `src/csv.rs` expose only emit functions (record_to_dublin_core / record_to_dublin_core_xml / dublin_core_to_xml; record_to_csv / records_to_csv / records_to_csv_filtered), with no reader at any layer (Rust core, PyO3, Python wrapper, stubs, tests). The asymmetry was purely documentation: format lists presented them without stating they are export-only. So this resolves as documentation, not code.

## Done

- Documented write-only intent + rationale + 'bring your own reader' guidance in: README.md Format Support table, docs/reference/formats.md (matrix note + per-format Direction notes), docs/guides/format-selection.md (comparison-table note + CSV/Dublin Core profile callouts).
- CHANGELOG [Unreleased] ### Changed entry.
- No code change; no read path added.
- .cargo/check.sh passes (builds the docs site).
close_reason: Resolved in #256 as documentation: Dublin Core and CSV are write-only by design (both lossy projections of MARC, no canonical reader). Documented write-only intent + rationale + bring-your-own-reader guidance in README format table, docs/reference/formats.md, and docs/guides/format-selection.md. No read path implemented; no code change.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/92

title: Establish a parallel-throughput measurement procedure outside CodSpeed
description:
With the Python parallel files (bd-ipu0) and the Rust parallel bench (sibling child) excluded from simulation CodSpeed, the project's headline concurrency feature (GIL-release multithreading, rayon parser pool, ProducerConsumerPipeline) has no measurement anywhere. Simulation numbers cannot capture it (Valgrind serializes threads), so it needs a real-walltime path on controlled hardware. For a solo-dev repo, match ceremony to risk: a documented local-run procedure plus a workflow_dispatch-only walltime job, not a dedicated rig.

What to do: write a short procedure (a docs/contributing page, e.g. running-benchmarks.md, or extend an existing benchmarks doc) covering how to run the parallel-throughput benchmarks locally for real numbers: which hardware to record (CPU model, core count, RAM, OS), which fixtures, the exact pytest/cargo invocations for the parallel files, how to interpret output, and the explicit caveat that results are wall-clock and machine-specific (not a portable 'records/sec' claim). Optionally add a workflow_dispatch-only job (no PR trigger) that runs the parallel benches on a hosted runner for rough trend tracking, clearly labeled as walltime/advisory. Reuse the existing synthetic fixtures; note their synthetic nature.

Done when: a reproducible parallel-throughput procedure is documented (hardware, fixtures, commands, interpretation, caveats); if a walltime job is added it is workflow_dispatch-only and does not run on PRs; '.cargo/check.sh' passes.

title: ci: switch Python CodSpeed job to simulation mode and exclude parallel benchmarks
description:
The codspeed job in benchmark-python.yml runs in walltime mode on a standard hosted runner (ubuntu-22.04). Walltime on hosted runners cannot distinguish real regressions from runner-speed variance — PR #249 reported 57 benches uniformly -20..-28% across different runners, which is runner noise, not a regression. The job is also the PR wall-clock long pole at ~10.7 min because walltime runs many warmup/measurement rounds per bench.

Switch the job to mode: simulation, matching benchmark-rust.yml (3.4 min, no false regressions). Simulation measures instruction counts + cache behavior under Valgrind — deterministic, one pass per bench, sub-1% variance, so small real regressions become detectable.

Valgrind serializes threads, so parallel-throughput benchmarks are meaningless under simulation. Exclude tests/python/test_benchmark_parallel.py and tests/python/test_benchmark_pipeline_parallel.py from the CodSpeed run. Parallel-throughput measurement moves to a separate benchmarking effort on real hardware (assessment in progress).

Also remove the incorrect comment claiming walltime is required for v4 on standard runners — CodSpeed docs show mode: simulation on ubuntu-latest as the standard setup.

This resolves the CodSpeed-usefulness decision noted on bd-no9e and unblocks bd-u2or (the pytest-benchmark matrix has no baseline comparison; CodSpeed becomes the trustworthy gate). Note: simulation and walltime are different metrics, so CodSpeed history resets; trigger workflow_dispatch on main to seed the new baseline.
close_reason: Completed: PR #251 merged. Python CodSpeed job now runs simulation mode with parallel benchmark files excluded; job time 10.7 -> 6.8 min, regression detection deterministic.

title: Migrate to quick-xml 0.40
description:
## Context

quick-xml 0.40 introduces breaking API changes that prevent a drop-in version bump. Dependabot PR #191 surfaced the breakage and was closed; dependabot PR #221 re-opened the same bump and was rebased onto current main on 2026-06-11 — it fails CI with the expected compile error (E0061 at src/marcxml.rs:259), confirming this analysis. The open #221 branch can carry the fix: push the code changes below onto dependabot/cargo/quick-xml-0.40 and merge #221, rather than opening a third PR for the same bump.

## What needs to change

1. **src/marcxml.rs:259 and :269** — BytesText::xml_content() and BytesCData::xml_content() now take a required XmlVersion argument. Pass quick_xml::XmlVersion::Edition10 (MARCXML is XML 1.0). Tighten the surrounding comment at lines 255-258 while here — it currently says "XML 1.1 §2.11" but both 1.0 and 1.1 define the same EOL normalization pass, and our intent is 1.0.

2. **src/marcxml.rs:320** — Attribute::unescape_value() is deprecated in 0.40. Rename to Attribute::decoded_and_normalized_value(). Per the 0.40 release notes the two are behaviorally identical; pure rename. Required because clippy in CI runs with -D warnings.

3. **Cargo.toml deps** — bump quick-xml = "0.39" to "0.40" (this is what #221 already does).

4. **CHANGELOG.md [Unreleased]** — terse note: bumped quick-xml to 0.40.

## MSRV: no action needed

This ticket originally required bumping rust-version 1.71 → 1.79 (quick-xml 0.40's MSRV). The declared MSRV is now 1.87, above 1.79, and the msrv CI job in lint.yml enforces it — that job runs on #221 and gates this migration automatically.

## What does NOT need to change (verified during the PR #191 review)

- mrrc's own read_text helper in src/mods.rs is *not* quick_xml::Reader::read_text(), so the upstream return-type change there doesn't affect us.
- No NsReader usage anywhere in the tree; deleted .prefixes() / .resolve() / .resolve_attribute() / .resolve_element() methods are not called.
- The XML 1.0 deserializer EOL rule fix in 0.40 only affects quick_xml::de; mrrc uses quick_xml::se (serializer) on the write path, so behavior there is unchanged.
- The duplicate quick-xml 0.37.5 via oxrdfxml is out of scope; it converges only when oxrdfxml updates upstream.

## Done when

- cargo build and cargo test pass against quick-xml 0.40.
- .cargo/check.sh is clean.
- CHANGELOG [Unreleased] notes the bump.
- PR #221 merges green (all jobs including the msrv check).

## Why not security-driven

quick-xml 0.39.x has no open GHSA / RustSec advisory. The 0.40 release is feature work (UTF-16 / ISO-2022-JP support via a new DecodingReader, typed XmlVersion, normalized_value() API family) plus a deserializer EOL-normalization correctness fix. No CVE. Routine dependency-currency work.

## References

- Dependabot PR #191 (closed): https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/pull/191
- Dependabot PR #221 (open, rebased, carries the bump): https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/pull/221
- GitHub issue #192
close_reason: Completed in PR #221 (merged); GitHub issue #192 closed

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #192: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/192

comments.text: MSRV premise resolved: the declared MSRV is now 1.87 (was falsely 1.71), enforced by the msrv CI job in lint.yml. The 'MSRV bump to 1.79' concern in this ticket is moot; the quick-xml 0.40 migration itself remains valid.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Closing notes — two corrections to this bead's prescription, discovered during execution: (1) quick_xml::XmlVersion has no Edition10 variant; the real variants are Implicit1_0 / Explicit1_0 / Explicit1_1, and Implicit1_0 was used (MARCXML is XML 1.0 per the LOC schema and examples — the version="1.1" attribute on the xsd:schema element is the MARC21slim schema revision, not the XML language version). (2) The unescape_value replacement is not a behavior-identical pure rename: the lib-safe decoded_and_normalized_value() requires a Decoder argument (decoder-less variants are compiled out under quick-xml's encoding feature unification), so attr_value() gained a Decoder parameter passed from reader.decoder() at all ten call sites; 0.40 also adds XML 1.0 attribute-value normalization (inert for valid MARCXML attributes). Behavior note recorded in CHANGELOG: EOL normalization now follows XML 1.0 (NEL/LSEP are no longer folded — they are legitimate data characters in XML 1.0 content).

title: HoldingsMarcReader: add per-field lenient/permissive recovery parity
description:
## Problem

`HoldingsMarcReader` exposes `with_recovery_mode(Lenient)` and (via bd-uj7c) `with_max_errors(n)`, but neither has real effect on per-field parsing. `recovery_mode` is consulted only at:

- `iso2709::read_record_data(..., self.recovery_mode, ...)` — passed to the shared read primitive
- the truncated-record branch at `src/holdings_reader.rs:~186`, which is unreachable per the comment and inverted (returns Err in non-strict)

The directory-walk loop has **zero per-field strict/lenient branches**. Every per-field error — field-exceeds-data, data-too-short-for-indicators, `parse_data_field` failure — propagates regardless of mode. Users asking for lenient holdings parsing get strict behavior silently.

Surfaced while scoping bd-uj7c (PR #120): `HoldingsMarcReader::with_max_errors` had to be documented as inert because there were no recovery sites to cap.

## What to do

1. Add standard lenient/permissive branches at each per-field error site:
   - field-exceeds-data (`end > data_section.len()`) — today unconditional Err; split into strict-Err vs lenient-skip + `note_recovery_error()?`
   - data-too-short-for-indicators (`field_data.len() < 3`) — same split
   - `parse_data_field` failure (`let field = parsed?`) — same split
2. Fix the inverted truncated-record branch to match whichever shape bib/authority settle on (see the authority-parity bead).
3. Wire `HoldingsMarcReader::with_max_errors` to real `note_recovery_error` calls. Remove the `#\[allow(dead_code)\]` and the "inert today" docstring caveat.
4. Tests: strict errors at each site; lenient salvages; cap trips after N events.

## Done when

- `HoldingsMarcReader` honors `RecoveryMode::Lenient` / `Permissive` at every per-field error site, symmetric with `MarcReader`.
- `with_max_errors` is active (not inert) and exercised by a test on a stream of malformed holdings records.
- Docstring on `HoldingsMarcReader::with_max_errors` is updated to remove the "inert" caveat.
- CHANGELOG entry under \[Unreleased\].

## Out of scope

- Skeleton consolidation across readers — separate bead (depends on this + authority parity).
- Redesigning the semantic field-dispatch by tag (852 → location, 853-855 → captions, etc.) — that's already fine as-is; this bead only adds recovery branches around it.
close_reason: Completed — merged in PR #124

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #122: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/122

title: Remove pointless private-alias double-indirection for AuthorityRecord/HoldingsRecord re-exports
description:
In `mrrc/__init__.py`, `AuthorityRecord` and `HoldingsRecord` are imported from `._mrrc` under private aliases (`AuthorityRecord as _AuthorityRecord` at line 13, `HoldingsRecord as _HoldingsRecord` at line 16) and then re-bound to their public names at module level (`AuthorityRecord = _AuthorityRecord` / `HoldingsRecord = _HoldingsRecord`, lines 1790-1791). Every other re-exported `_mrrc` type (`Subfield`, `FieldQuery`, `TagRangeQuery`, `BibframeConfig`, etc.) is imported directly under its public name. The `_AuthorityRecord`/`_HoldingsRecord` aliases are not referenced anywhere else, so the double-indirection is pointless.

Beyond being dead indirection, it breaks static analysis: griffe (and therefore mkdocstrings) sees `mrrc.AuthorityRecord` as a module attribute assigned an alias rather than as a class, so `::: mrrc.AuthorityRecord` renders an empty attribute stub with no members. Surfaced while wiring up bd-kxkq (mkdocstrings Python API reference); that page currently works around it by autodoc-ing these two types via the canonical `mrrc._mrrc.AuthorityRecord` / `mrrc._mrrc.HoldingsRecord` path instead of the public `mrrc.AuthorityRecord` path used for every other type.

Fix: import `AuthorityRecord` and `HoldingsRecord` directly under their public names like the other re-exports, and delete the line-1790-1791 reassignments. Then switch the bd-kxkq doc blocks for these two back to the public `mrrc.X` path for consistency. Verify the public API is unchanged (`mrrc.AuthorityRecord is mrrc._mrrc.AuthorityRecord` stays True) and stubtest/tests pass.

Related to bd-mcei (the broader `mrrc/__init__.py` reorganization) but independent and much smaller; can land on its own.
close_reason: AuthorityRecord/HoldingsRecord now imported directly under public names; private-alias double-indirection removed. Public API identity preserved. Merged in PR #263.

title: Structured error serialization (to_dict/to_json) and hex-dump diagnostics
description:
## Problem

With core error enrichment (bd-qzew) landed, errors carry rich context — but users wanting to emit errors to structured logging platforms (ELK, Datadog, Splunk, JSON-line pipelines) still have to write their own adapter because Python exceptions aren't JSON-serializable by default. And for truly gnarly parse failures, `detailed()` output could do more: a hex dump of bytes around the error offset turns the error into a self-contained replacement for opening the file in a hex editor.

## Design

### Structured serialization

Add `to_dict()` and `to_json()` to the base `MrrcException` with a versioned schema. Delegates to a class-level `_diagnostic_fields` tuple that subclasses extend.

```python
class MrrcException(Exception):
    _diagnostic_fields = (
        "record_index", "record_control_number", "field_tag",
        "indicator_position", "subfield_code", "found", "expected",
        "byte_offset", "record_byte_offset", "source",
    )

    def to_dict(self, *, include_traceback: bool = False) -> dict:
        result = {
            "schema_version": 1,
            "class": type(self).__name__,
            "code": getattr(self, "code", None),
            "slug": getattr(self, "slug", None),
            "severity": getattr(self, "severity", "error"),
            "help_url": getattr(self, "help_url", None),
        }
        for field in self._diagnostic_fields:
            value = getattr(self, field, None)
            if isinstance(value, bytes):
                result[f"{field}_hex"] = value.hex()
            else:
                result[field] = value
        # Cause chain (bd-qzew adds #[source] to wrapping variants; surface here)
        cause = self.__cause__ or self.__context__
        result["_cause"] = str(cause) if cause is not None else None
        if include_traceback and self.__traceback__:
            import traceback
            result["traceback"] = traceback.format_exception(type(self), self, self.__traceback__)
        return result

    def to_json(self, **kwargs) -> str:
        import json
        return json.dumps(self.to_dict(**kwargs))
```

Rust equivalent: `MarcError::to_json()` via serde, same schema shape.

### Schema stability

`schema_version: 1` is the initial contract. Guarantees that downstream log pipelines can rely on:

- **Bounded payload size**: `found` is truncated to 32 bytes by bd-qzew, so `found_hex` is at most ~64 hex characters. No field in the dict is unbounded. This is important for ELK/Datadog ingestion limits and for predictable storage cost.
- **All top-level keys always present**: even when the underlying attribute is `None`, the key appears with a `null` value. Downstream consumers never need to branch on key existence.
- **`_cause` is the single source-chain field**: always a string or `null`. Not a nested structure (keeps JSON flat for easy indexing in log platforms). Users who want the full chain can call `include_traceback=True` or walk `__cause__` themselves.

Any schema change must bump `schema_version` and bump the crate's minor version (pre-1.0) or major (post-1.0).

### Sample output

```python
>>> err.to_dict()
{
  "schema_version": 1,
  "class": "InvalidIndicator",
  "code": "E201",
  "slug": "invalid_indicator",
  "severity": "error",
  "help_url": "https://mrrc.dev/reference/error-codes/#E201",
  "record_index": 847,
  "record_control_number": "ocm01234567",
  "field_tag": "245",
  "indicator_position": 0,
  "found_hex": "3a",
  "expected": "digit or space",
  "byte_offset": 7217,
  "record_byte_offset": 42,
  "source": "harvest.mrc",
  "_cause": null,
}
```

For an `IoError` that wraps a `std::io::Error` (per bd-qzew's `#[source]` convention):

```python
>>> io_err.to_dict()["_cause"]
"No such file or directory (os error 2)"
```

### Hex dump in .detailed()

Add 16 bytes before + 16 after the error offset, rendered as hex + ASCII, to `detailed()` output:

```
InvalidIndicator at record 847, field 245
  source:   harvest.mrc
  001:      ocm01234567
  indicator 1: found ':', expected digit or space
  byte offset: 0x1C31 (7217) in stream
  record-relative: byte 42

  bytes near offset 0x1C31:
    0x1C21:  32 30 32 33 6e 79 75 20  20 20 20 20 20 20 20 20  |2023nyu         |
    0x1C31:  3a 30 00 30 20 30 20 65  6e 67 20 64 1e 32 34 35  |:0.0 0 eng d.245|
                ^^ offending byte
```

Caret points to the exact offending byte within the dump.

## What to do

1. Add `_diagnostic_fields` class attribute to `MrrcException` base; subclasses extend as needed.
2. Implement `to_dict(include_traceback=False)` on `MrrcException` per the sketch above. Bytes values get hex-encoded with a `_hex` suffix on the key for JSON-serializability.
3. Populate the `_cause` key from `self.__cause__ or self.__context__` — always a string or `null`, never nested.
4. Implement `to_json(**kwargs)` as a one-line convenience wrapper.
5. Implement matching Rust `MarcError::to_json()` via serde. Same schema shape; docs note that any schema change must bump `schema_version` and bump the crate's minor version.
6. Document the dict schema and the stability contract in `docs/reference/error-handling.md` — including the bounded-payload guarantee that depends on bd-qzew's `found` truncation.
7. Add hex-dump rendering to the `detailed()` output (both Rust and Python). Requires the parser/reader to retain a short byte-window around the error offset (already knowable from the reader buffer — bd-qzew's `ParseContext` can be extended to hold this).
8. Make the hex-dump window size a constant (default 16 bytes before + 16 after). No need for it to be user-configurable initially.
9. Use the same snapshot testing crates bd-qzew commits to (`insta` for Rust, `syrupy` or `pytest-snapshot` for Python — whichever bd-qzew picks) for both dict-shape and hex-dump rendering tests.

## Depends on

- **bd-qzew** (core error enrichment must land first — the fields this serializes must exist, and `found` must be bounded for the schema-stability guarantee to hold).

Benefits from the stable-error-codes bead landing first (so `code`, `slug`, `help_url` show up populated in `to_dict` output), but doesn't strictly require it — emits `None` for those keys until the codes bead lands.

## Done when

- `MrrcException.to_dict()` returns the versioned schema dict with all diagnostic fields, including `_cause` populated from the exception chain.
- `MrrcException.to_json()` returns valid JSON parseable by `json.loads`.
- Rust `MarcError::to_json()` emits matching schema.
- Bytes fields are hex-encoded with `_hex` suffix.
- `_cause` key present and populated for errors with a `#[source]` chain; `null` otherwise.
- `include_traceback=True` option works for the Python side.
- `detailed()` output includes a 32-byte hex dump with offset caret (both Rust and Python).
- Unit tests verify schema keys/values for each variant.
- Snapshot tests using the same crates bd-qzew chose (`insta` for Rust, `syrupy` or `pytest-snapshot` for Python) for both `to_dict` output and hex-dump rendering.
- **FFI integration test**: `to_dict()` called on a Python exception surfaced from a Rust parse error includes all positional fields plus `_cause`.
- `docs/reference/error-handling.md` documents the schema, the stability contract (`schema_version` bump rules), and the bounded-payload guarantee.
- CHANGELOG entry under next version.
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes.
notes: Delivered in PR #102 (merged 2026-04-19). Includes: to_dict/to_json on Rust and Python, hex-dump in detailed(), bytes_near plumbing across all 3 readers + leader errors + boundary-scan ParseError path, typed MarcError preserved across Python FFI.
