title: Generate the Python API reference with mkdocstrings instead of hand-written tables
description:
## Context

`docs/reference/python-api.md` is entirely hand-written markdown tables, while `mkdocstrings[python]` is already a `docs`-extra dependency and already enabled in `mkdocs.yml` (`handlers.python.paths: [mrrc]`) — with zero `:::` autodoc directives anywhere in `docs/`. The engine is wired up but unused, so the reference drifts by hand (see PR #229).

## Approach

Drive the API reference from a single source via mkdocstrings/griffe. Because the public API is the compiled `_mrrc` extension re-exported through `mrrc/__init__.py` (which griffe cannot statically parse), source the docs from the `.pyi` stubs. Adopt a **hybrid**: autodoc the member/signature tables with `:::` blocks, but keep the hand-written narrative the autodoc cannot produce (the "choosing a reader" table, recovery-mode semantics, thread-safety notes, format-conversion examples). Interleave prose with autodoc blocks rather than replacing the page wholesale.

## Dependency

Depends on stubtest landing first: once the reference is generated from the `.pyi`, an unverified stub would just publish drift automatically. With stubtest gating CI, this closes the loop — one source, verified against runtime.

## Done when

- The per-type member/signature tables in `python-api.md` are generated by mkdocstrings from the `.pyi` (no hand-maintained signatures), with narrative prose retained around them.
- Docs build cleanly and the rendered reference matches the runtime API.
- The hand-written signature tables that autodoc now covers are removed to avoid double-maintenance.
close_reason: Python API reference now generated from source via mkdocstrings (hybrid: wrapper types from __init__.py, re-exported types from stubtest-verified _mrrc.pyi); hand-written signature tables removed; query-DSL + parallel sections added. Merged in PR #263.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Docstring-coverage consideration (2026-05-28): generated Python docs are only as good as the docstrings behind them. Audit found the .pyi is partially documented (~137 `"""` markers across 222 members; many bare `def ...: ...`), while the PyO3 `///` comments are rich (458 doc lines over 125 `pub fn` in wrappers.rs). This couples to the source decision: (a) source from the .pyi → docstrings must be filled in there, creating a DRY problem since they also live in the PyO3 `///` comments; (b) source from runtime `__doc__` → the `///` comments already flow in, but griffe's dynamic mode on a compiled extension is finicky. Resolve the source decision first, then decide whether closing the .pyi docstring gap is part of this bead or warrants its own sub-task. (Rust side needs nothing — `#![warn(missing_docs)]` + clippy `-D warnings` already enforce full public-API doc coverage.)

title: Benchmarking infrastructure coherence: realign benches, fixtures, CI, and the parallel-throughput story with the current code
description:
Epic. The benchmarking infrastructure has drifted from the current code across several releases (read-path optimization, the rayon parser pool, ProducerConsumerPipeline, GIL-release work, and added formats: CSV, MODS, Dublin Core, BIBFRAME, marcjson). At the same time the CI perf gate is changing: the Python CodSpeed job moves to simulation mode (deterministic Valgrind instruction counting) and excludes the two parallel-throughput files (bd-ipu0). This epic makes the whole benchmarking story coherent again: trim what is dead or misleading, fix what is broken, fill the gaps that matter, and give parallel-throughput measurement a real home outside CI. It complements rather than duplicates bd-dra6 (publish empirical pymarc comparison), bd-u2or (reduce/drop the pytest-benchmark matrix in benchmark-python.yml), bd-9wk8 (add cargo bench --no-run to check.sh), and bd-ipu0 (the simulation-mode switch driving the parallel exclusion).

## Assessment findings

### Actively misleading (must fix)
- docs/benchmarks/results.md is dated 2026-01-26 and asserts hardcoded numbers as fact: '~1M rec/s' Rust, '~300k rec/s' Python, '~4x pymarc', '~3.74x' multithread, plus 'Rust 1.71+' (the toolchain is now 1.95). README line 19 repeats '~4x pymarc throughput in Python, ~1M records/sec in Rust' as a headline claim. None of these are reproducible from anything in the repo. Under simulation mode the CodSpeed numbers are model-estimated instruction counts, not wall-clock, so they cannot back a 'records/sec' claim at all. This is the bd-dra6 problem; this epic should drive bd-dra6 to resolution or down-scope the published claim until real walltime numbers exist.
- No benchmark anywhere imports pymarc. The 'direct comparison for pymarc' line in results.md, the per-row 'vs pymarc' columns, and the 'pymrrc vs pymarc' docstrings in test_benchmark_parallel.py / test_benchmark_pipeline_parallel.py describe a comparison whose code does not exist. Any pymarc comparison has to be written, not merely re-run.

### Broken references / convention violations
- benches/detailed_profiling.rs prints pointers to docs/design/PROFILING_PLAN.md and docs/design/profiling/RUST_SINGLE_THREADED_PROFILING_RESULTS.md; neither file exists.
- benches/rayon_file_io_profiling.rs writes scratch files to /tmp/mrrc_profile_test, violating the project's never-write-to-/tmp convention.

### Dead weight in the bench target
- Of the seven [[bench]] entries, only three are criterion-based and therefore picked up by 'cargo codspeed' (marc_benchmarks, error_handling_benchmarks, parallel_benchmarks). The other four (profiling_harness, detailed_profiling, rayon_profiling, rayon_file_io_profiling) are harness=false main() programs that print tables; they compile under the bench target (and under bd-9wk8's planned cargo bench --no-run) but produce no CodSpeed data and no CI signal. They are manual one-off profiling scratch from early development; detailed_profiling.rs also reaches into record.fields directly, an internal surface that can drift.

### CI / marker gaps
- The Python CodSpeed run selects '-m "benchmark and not slow"'. test_memory_profiling.py has zero @benchmark markers and test_performance_analysis.py has one of ~nine; these files run in neither the pytest-benchmark matrix (--benchmark-only skips non-benchmark tests) nor CodSpeed, so most of their assertions about queue capacity, batch boundaries, GIL-release overhead, and thread speedup execute nowhere in the perf gate. Decide per file: promote to the benchmark marker, move to the normal correctness suite, or delete.
- parallel_benchmarks.rs (Rust criterion) is still collected by 'cargo codspeed' under simulation, where Valgrind serializes threads and rayon speedup is meaningless. The Python parallel files are now excluded by bd-ipu0; the Rust parallel bench should get the same treatment for consistency.

### Coverage gaps (current code with no bench)
- No bench exercises CSV, MODS, Dublin Core, BIBFRAME (RDF), or marcjson serialization, despite all being supported formats.
- No single-thread bench exercises rayon_parser_pool / ProducerConsumerPipeline as an instruction-count signal under simulation (only the excluded multithread paths touch it).

### Fixtures
- Fixtures (1k/10k/5k_records.mrc, fidelity_test_100.mrc) are synthetic, built by scripts/generate_benchmark_fixtures.py, not real-world MARC. Adequate for relative regression detection under simulation, but not representative enough to publish as a 'records/sec on real data' figure. Note this explicitly wherever numbers are published.

### What is fine, leave alone
- marc_benchmarks.rs and error_handling_benchmarks.rs are well-targeted at the hot read/write/roundtrip/JSON/XML and recovery paths and exercise the current MarcReader/MarcWriter API; they are the right core to keep.
- benchmark-rust.yml already runs simulation mode and is minimal.
- The conftest.py fixture loaders and the benchmark/slow marker registration are sound.

### The parallel-throughput gap
With both Python parallel files and (recommended) the Rust parallel bench out of CodSpeed, there is no measurement of the project's headline concurrency feature. For a solo-dev repo, match ceremony to risk: a documented local-run procedure plus a workflow_dispatch-only walltime job is the right weight, not a dedicated rig. Capture the procedure (hardware to use, fixtures, how to read the numbers, that they are wall-clock and machine-specific) so concurrency claims have a reproducible basis.

## Children, suggested order
1. Remove dead profiling harnesses and fix broken/convention-violating references in the bench target.
2. Reconcile orphaned Python perf tests (test_memory_profiling.py, test_performance_analysis.py) with the marker scheme.
3. Exclude the Rust parallel bench from simulation CodSpeed for consistency with bd-ipu0.
4. Add criterion benches for the uncovered formats and the single-thread parser-pool path.
5. Establish a parallel-throughput measurement procedure (local + workflow_dispatch walltime) outside CodSpeed.
6. Correct or down-scope the published benchmark claims in docs/benchmarks/results.md and README until real walltime numbers exist (coordinate with bd-dra6).

## Done when
- No bench file references a nonexistent doc or writes to /tmp; the bench target contains only benches that produce CI signal or are explicitly documented as manual.
- Every Python perf test either runs in the CodSpeed gate or has a deliberate, recorded reason it does not.
- No parallel-throughput bench runs under simulation; the Rust parallel bench is excluded alongside the Python ones.
- Supported formats and the single-thread parser-pool path have instruction-count coverage under simulation.
- A reproducible parallel-throughput measurement procedure exists and is documented.
- No published doc or README line states an unreproducible or simulation-derived 'records/sec' / 'Nx pymarc' figure; claims either cite a dated real-walltime run or link to the procedure (resolving the conflict bd-dra6 raises).

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Relationship to bd-0pg6 (read-path performance epic, from the v0.8.2 review): this epic fixes the measurement story; bd-0pg6 changes the code being measured. Interlocks: bd-dra6 needs both (honest infrastructure here, optimized baseline there — bd-dra6 now carries explicit dependencies on bd-0pg6.1-3 and bd-yfe6.4); bd-ieoe is blocked by bd-0pg6.8 (fix the parallel paths before building their measurement procedure); sequencing preference is this epic's coherence work early so bd-0pg6's wins are visible in trustworthy benchmarks. The CodSpeed registry cleanup and main-push cancellation fix (bd-oayg.8) also belong to the same measurement story.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Status check 2026-06-12: the epic's first must-fix item (README perf hedging) is largely resolved — landed via bd-cpom. Remaining guide work proceeds via bd-l9zh.1 in Track D after bd-1dbu deletions.

title: Modernize the salvaged profiling guide and integrate it with the benchmarking docs
description:
## Context

PR #260 salvaged the local CPU-profiling guide to `docs/contributing/profiling.md`, lifted verbatim (minus two dead sections) from the 2026-02 `docs/design/profiling/PROFILING_GUIDE.md` (now archived under `docs/history/profiling/`). It is accurate enough to keep live — the benches it names still exist and the `cargo flamegraph` commands work — but it has not been modernized against the current benchmarking story this epic (bd-l9zh) is realigning.

## What to do

- Cross-link `docs/contributing/profiling.md` with `docs/contributing/benchmarking.md`: profiling.md covers local flamegraph/CPU profiling; benchmarking.md covers criterion / pytest-benchmark / CodSpeed. Each should point at the other so contributors land in the right place.
- Align the flamegraph guidance with the current perf-measurement workflow (CodSpeed simulation mode + criterion), so the guide doesn't read as a standalone 2026-02 process.
- Re-verify every bench command against the current harnesses. The guide names `profiling_harness`, `parallel_benchmarks`, `detailed_profiling`; the repo now also has `rayon_file_io_profiling` and `rayon_profiling`. Confirm the named targets are still the right ones to profile and mention any newer ones worth flamegraphing.
- Optionally distill the reusable 'profiling checklist' / 'measure allocations on the hot path, don't optimize by assumption' lesson from the archived `docs/history/profiling/SINGLE_THREADED_BOTTLENECK_ANALYSIS_2026.md` into a short section, rather than keeping that whole dated report live.

## Done when

- `docs/contributing/profiling.md` reflects the current bench set and perf-measurement workflow, cross-links benchmarking.md, and has no stale references.
- `mkdocs build` stays warning-free.

## Notes

Child of bd-l9zh. Deliberately deferred from PR #260, which only archived the dated material and salvaged the guide verbatim to clear the mkdocs warnings (bd-8in1).

title: AuthorityMarcReader: full per-field lenient recovery parity with MarcReader
description:
## Problem

`AuthorityMarcReader` has only partial per-field lenient/permissive recovery compared to `MarcReader`. Surfaced while scoping bd-uj7c (PR #120).

## Gaps vs bib reader

Looking at `src/reader.rs` vs `src/authority_reader.rs` side-by-side in `read_record`:

| Recovery site | Bibliographic | Authority |
|---|---|---|
| incomplete directory entry | lenient branch + cap | lenient branch + cap |
| bad field_length digits | lenient branch + cap | **missing** — `iso2709::parse_4digits(...)?` propagates in all modes |
| bad start_position digits | lenient branch + cap | **missing** — same |
| field-exceeds-data | lenient branch + cap (salvages partial) | **missing** — not present |
| parse_data_field failure | lenient branch + cap | lenient branch + cap |
| truncated record in non-strict | dispatches to `try_recover_record` | **inverted** — returns Err in non-strict (line ~168); comment says branch is unreachable in practice |

Net: authority users who set `with_recovery_mode(Lenient)` get strict behavior for three of the five per-field error classes, silently.

## What to do

1. Add the three missing lenient/permissive branches, mirroring `reader.rs`'s pattern:
   - bad field_length → `self.note_recovery_error()?; pos += 12; continue;`
   - bad start_position → same
   - field-exceeds-data → salvage partial field + `self.note_recovery_error()?; continue;`
2. Fix the inverted truncated-record branch. Options:
   a. Call `try_recover_record` the way bib does (if the recovery logic generalizes).
   b. Keep the current "return Err" shape but in the *strict* branch, making non-strict fall through to normal parsing (matches bib structurally).
   Pick whichever matches the real intent — confirm with a test.
3. Hook `self.note_recovery_error()?` at each new site. The helper is already present from bd-uj7c.
4. Tests per new site: strict errors, lenient salvages, cap trips after N events.

## Done when

- `AuthorityMarcReader` and `MarcReader` behave identically for per-field error recovery in `Lenient` / `Permissive` modes (modulo the per-type record assembly).
- Cap is exercised at each new recovery site (verified by a test that counts events).
- Truncated-record branch is either symmetric with bib or documented as deliberately different.
- CHANGELOG entry under \[Unreleased\].

## Out of scope

- Skeleton consolidation across the three readers — separate bead (follow-up to this + holdings parity).
- Adding lenient recovery to holdings — separate bead.
close_reason: Completed — merged in PR #123

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #121: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/121

title: Consolidate ISO 2709 directory-walk skeleton across three readers
description:
## Problem

`MarcReader::read_record` (`src/reader.rs`), `AuthorityMarcReader::read_record` (`src/authority_reader.rs`), and `HoldingsMarcReader::read_record` (`src/holdings_reader.rs`) share a near-identical ~200-line skeleton:

1. Cap-exhausted short-circuit
2. Read leader → `Leader::from_bytes` + `validate_for_reading`
3. Record-type validation (per-type: none / 'z' / x/y/v/u)
4. Compute `record_length`, `base_address`, `directory_size`
5. `ctx.advance(LEADER_LEN)`
6. `iso2709::read_record_data` + `ctx.set_parse_buffer`
7. Truncated-record dispatch (per-type)
8. Directory walk: parse 12-byte entries, handle each recovery site with strict-Err / lenient-skip + `note_recovery_error`
9. Per-entry: dispatch tag → control-field-add vs data-field-add (per-type field dispatch)
10. Restore `stream_byte_offset`; return Ok(Some(record))

What actually varies per type:

- **Record-type validation** (which leader char is allowed)
- **What a parsed field becomes** (`Record::add_field` vs `AuthorityRecord::add_heading/add_see_from_tracing/...` vs `HoldingsRecord::add_location/add_captions_basic/...`)
- **Truncated-record recovery** (`try_recover_record` for bib; per-type policy for others)

Plus duplication in the cap state machine itself: each reader has `max_errors`, `error_count`, `cap_exceeded`, and an identical `note_recovery_error` method (introduced in bd-uj7c).

## Prerequisites

Both prerequisite parity beads are now merged on main:

- **bd-lbsi** (#121, PR #123) — authority per-field recovery parity ✓
- **bd-jmrt** (#122, PR #124) — holdings per-field recovery parity ✓

The three readers now share the same recovery-branch shape at every per-field error site, so the abstraction can be designed against a single behavioral target rather than accommodating divergence.

## What to do

1. Design a shared skeleton. Sketch:
   - A `RecordBuilder` trait (or equivalent) with associated `Output` type, `validate_leader(leader) -> Result<()>`, `add_control_field(tag, value)`, `add_data_field(tag, field)`, `finalize() -> Output`.
   - A `RecoveryCap` struct owning the counter/cap state + `note(&ctx) -> Result<()>`.
   - A generic `parse_iso2709_record<B: RecordBuilder>(reader: &mut impl Read, ctx, cap, builder) -> Result<Option<B::Output>>` in `iso2709.rs` (or a new module).
2. Each public reader's `read_record` collapses to ~20 lines: construct builder, call skeleton, return the output.
3. Subsumes the small `RecoveryCap` struct consolidation mentioned in bd-uj7c.
4. No behavior change — all existing tests must pass unchanged.

## Design questions to resolve

- **Trait with associated types vs. generic skeleton taking closures.** Trait is cleaner; closures are lower friction for one-off tweaks.
- **Where the cap lives** — on the reader struct, on the builder, or on a borrowed `RecoveryCap`. Borrow probably cleanest.
- **How to handle the bib-specific `try_recover_record` path.** Could be a trait method `fn recover_truncated(leader, partial, base_address, mode, ctx) -> Result<Self::Output>` with a default impl that returns Err (authority/holdings) and a bib-specific override.
- **Performance preservation on the bib hot path** (lesson from bd-tcym / bd-9kdt). The bib reader's `parse_data_field` was annotated with `#[inline(always)]` to recover a 15–17% read-path regression caused by an earlier refactor. The skeleton must keep `parse_data_field` (and any per-field hot-path code) inlinable through whatever abstraction is chosen. Concretely:
  - Prefer `impl Trait` / monomorphized generics over `dyn Trait` for the skeleton's builder parameter — `dyn` boxes the dispatch and disables inlining.
  - Trait methods on the hot path (e.g., `add_data_field`) may need explicit `#[inline]` hints.
  - **Verify with codspeed on every PR push.** Treat any regression on `bench_read_*` as a hard blocker, not a tolerable cost.

## Done when

- One shared skeleton in `iso2709.rs` (or new module).
- Each reader's `read_record` is ~20 lines of thin per-type code (builder setup + skeleton call + finalize).
- `RecoveryCap` lives in one place.
- All existing tests pass unchanged — zero behavior change.
- Property / fuzz coverage still holds.
- No clippy regressions.
- **No codspeed regression on the bib read path.**
- CHANGELOG entry under [Unreleased] (internal refactor, no public API change if done right).
close_reason: Completed — merged in PR #127

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #125: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/125

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
Baseline read-path benchmarks (pre-refactor reference for comparison).

**Environment:**
- commit: 0409d49 (main, post-bd-jmrt merge)
- rustc 1.95.0 (pinned via rust-toolchain.toml)
- Darwin arm64 (M-series, on AC power)
- criterion default settings (100 samples, 3s warmup, 5s measurement)

**Bibliographic read path (`marc_benchmarks.rs`):**

| Benchmark | Median | 95% CI |
|---|---|---|
| read_1k_records           | 1.1874 ms | [1.1838, 1.1922] |
| read_10k_records          | 11.629 ms | [11.614, 11.644] |
| read_1k_with_field_access | 1.1829 ms | [1.1814, 1.1843] |
| read_10k_with_field_access| 11.877 ms | [11.861, 11.894] |

Throughput sanity: ~860k records/sec for the pure read path at 10k scale.

The bd-tcym/bd-9kdt regression was 15-17% on these same benches; treat any change >2-3% as a real signal worth investigating, anything >5% as a hard blocker that needs a fix before merge.

No equivalent benches exist for `AuthorityMarcReader` or `HoldingsMarcReader` today. Bib is the perf-critical hot path so this is the bar that matters; if the skeleton refactor regresses bib, that's the signal. If we want authority/holdings parity benches too, that's separate scope (could be a follow-up bead).

title: ci: add paths-ignore to skip workflows on docs-only changes
description:
## Problem

Push or PR of any docs/markdown-only change fires the full per-PR workflow set
(lint, test, build, python-build, benchmark-python, benchmark-rust) — ~47 jobs
for a CHANGELOG.md edit. `semver.yml` and `docs.yml` already use path filters;
the rest do not.

## What to do

Add `paths-ignore` to the `push` and `pull_request` triggers in:

- `.github/workflows/lint.yml`
- `.github/workflows/test.yml`
- `.github/workflows/build.yml`
- `.github/workflows/python-build.yml`
- `.github/workflows/benchmark-python.yml`
- `.github/workflows/benchmark-rust.yml`

Ignore set:

```
paths-ignore:
  - '**.md'
  - 'docs/**'
  - 'mkdocs.yml'
  - 'LICENSE'
  - '.gitignore'
```

Rationale: `**.md` covers CHANGELOG.md, README.md, AGENTS.md, etc. `docs/**`
is owned by `docs.yml`. Mixed PRs (code + docs) still run because at least one
non-ignored path changed — paths-ignore only skips when *every* changed path
matches.

## Done when

- All six workflows have matching paths-ignore on push + pull_request triggers.
- A docs-only PR fires zero jobs from these workflows.
- Mixed PRs continue to fire normally.
- CHANGELOG entry under `[Unreleased]` `### Changed`.
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes.

## Out of scope

- Slimming the python-build matrix (separate bead).
- Removing build.yml (separate bead).
- Reducing benchmark cadence (separate bead).
close_reason: Delivered in PR #136 (merged); main at 10c9d16 includes paths-ignore on lint/test/build/python-build/benchmark-python/benchmark-rust.

title: Refactor mrrc/__init__.py: extract Field, Record, Leader, MARCReader, MARCWriter to dedicated modules
description:
## Problem

The exception hierarchy already lives in `mrrc/exceptions.py`, but `mrrc/__init__.py` is still **2234 lines** and holds several large, conceptually distinct class definitions:

- `Indicators` (~47 lines)
- `Field` + `ControlField` (~395 lines)
- `Leader` (~291 lines)
- `Record` (~716 lines)
- `MARCReader` (~157 lines)
- `MARCWriter` (~36 lines)

Plus top-level helper functions (`_wrap_field`, `_wrap_record`, `_is_control_tag`, etc.) and the module-level IO/format functions (`read`, `write`, `json_to_record`, `marc_to_bibframe`, the leader-validation helpers, etc., which together run from roughly line 1794 to the end).

`StaleFieldError` is still defined inline in `__init__.py` (line 91, added by #247) even though the rest of the exception hierarchy moved to `mrrc/exceptions.py`; a clean extraction should relocate it to `exceptions.py` alongside its siblings.

Each class is a candidate for extraction to its own module under `mrrc/`, with re-exports from `mrrc/__init__.py` preserving the public API (`mrrc.Record`, `mrrc.MARCReader`, etc.). Aligns with pymarc's structure: `pymarc/record.py`, `pymarc/field.py`, `pymarc/leader.py`, `pymarc/reader.py`, `pymarc/writer.py`.

## Done when

- Each class lives in its own module under `mrrc/`.
- `StaleFieldError` moved to `mrrc/exceptions.py`.
- `mrrc/__init__.py` reduced to imports + module-level constants + version metadata + the thin top-level IO/format functions; well under the current 2234 lines.
- Public API unchanged (`mrrc.Record`, `mrrc.MARCReader`, etc. all still resolve).
- All tests pass unmodified.
- mypy/pyright still satisfied.

## Out of scope

- Renaming any classes (preserve pymarc-compatible names).
- Changing wrapper behavior or method signatures.
- Touching the Rust extension `_mrrc.pyi` stubs.

## Notes

Standalone task; not part of the bd-c7ok docs-drift epic (that epic generates and verifies API reference docs and is orthogonal to this source reorganization). Description facts refreshed 2026-06-07 against the current 2234-line file; the earlier baseline (~1900 lines, and a citation to bd-qzew for the exceptions extraction) was stale and has been corrected.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Sequenced with bd-d7aq.4 (field-handle ownership decision): isolate the _generation/_refresh handle machinery into its own module during the split regardless of the ownership outcome.

title: ci: one-shot mypy + pyright audit on Python wrapper before v0.8 tag
description:
## Problem

`mrrc/__init__.py` and the rest of the Python wrapper layer accumulated
significant new code in the v0.8 cycle: `recovery_mode` / `permissive`
plumbing on `MARCReader`, the typed exception subclasses
(`InvalidIndicator`, `BadSubfieldCode`, `TruncatedRecord`, etc.),
`from_path()` / `with_source()` builder methods, and structured-error
serialization (`to_dict` / `to_json` with `_hex` keys).

Neither mypy nor pyright is currently run in any pre-tag CI step.
`python-release.yml` runs them on tag with `|| true`, so findings are
non-blocking and not seen during the v0.8 development cycle.

## What to do

One-shot audit before tagging v0.8:

1. Run `uv run mypy mrrc/ tests/python/ --strict`.
2. Run `uv run pyright mrrc/ tests/python/`.
3. Categorize findings:
   - real bugs in v0.8-added code → fix before tag.
   - tooling noise (third-party stub gaps, abi3 quirks) → suppress with
     directives or `# type: ignore[code]` annotations and document why.
   - acceptable type-system limitations → file as follow-up beads.
4. Document the result in this bead's close reason: total findings,
   how many fixed, how many filed.

## Done when

- One run of each tool completed against current main.
- All real bugs either fixed or filed as separate beads.
- Findings summary recorded.

## Out of scope

- Permanent inclusion in `check.sh`. That's a separate decision after
  this audit informs whether the noise/value tradeoff justifies a
  routine gate. File a follow-up bead if "yes."
- Refactor work for bd-mcei (extracting Field/Record/Leader/etc. to
  dedicated modules) — that bead is separate and will rerun typing
  after the refactor.
close_reason: Pre-tag mypy/pyright audit completed across PRs #139, #140, #141, #142. mypy mrrc/ default-mode and pyright mrrc/ both at zero errors and gated in check.sh full mode. mypy --strict cleanup deferred and tracked in bd-bn65 (61 errors remaining, mostly annotation gaps). Audit deltas: mypy default 27→0, pyright 16→0, mypy --strict 84→61.

title: Implement io_error / recovery_cap / writer trigger mechanisms in error_coverage harness
description:
## Context

bd-0x73.3 closed the {strict, fatal, strict-only, recoverable} recovery-mode dimension for every error code that already had an end-to-end fixture. Three codes from the manifest still skip because their `trigger_kind` mechanism isn't implemented in the harness:

- **E007 IoError** — `trigger_kind = "io_error"`. Needs a `Read` impl that returns `std::io::Error` on demand to drive the parser through the underlying-stream-failure path.
- **E099 FatalReaderError** — `trigger_kind = "recovery_cap"`. Needs a fixture-runner that drives a stream of malformed records past `MarcReader::with_max_errors(N)` so the per-stream cap fires.
- **E404 WriterError** — `trigger_kind = "writer"`. Needs a `Write` impl that errors mid-record so `MarcWriter` surfaces a typed write failure with the right context fields.

Each is independent. Filing as a single bead because the harness plumbing (extending `fire_trigger` and adding a parallel `exercise_writer`-style branch) is the same shape three times.

## Done when

- Each of the three trigger_kinds has a real harness branch, not a `TriggerOutcome::UnsupportedKind`.
- The corresponding manifest cases (`e007_io_failure`, `e099_recovery_cap_exceeded`, future E404 case) drop their skip notes and assert the wired variant fires with the documented context.
- Same coverage on the Python side (`tests/python/test_error_coverage.py`).

## See also

- bd-0x73.3 — sibling, closed by the PR that filed this.
- `tests/error_coverage.toml` — manifest with the three skipped cases.
- `tests/error_coverage.rs:fire_trigger` — extension point.
close_reason: Landed in PR #193 (commits 40ccff7..7cc0e4d)

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Scope decision (2026-05-10): IN SCOPE for 0.8.1

The umbrella bd-0x73's done-when says documented codes must "fire from
the actual parse path... exercised by tests reading malformed records
(not by `raise`-ing in test code)." The current
`TriggerOutcome::UnsupportedKind` skips for `io_error`, `recovery_cap`,
and `writer` trigger_kinds violate that — the Rust core wiring is done
but the harness can't drive it.

bd-0x73.12 step 2 explicitly asserts *"all cases pass (no skips remain
except those documented as intentional)"*; these three skips don't
qualify as intentional, so the harness mechanism needs to land before
the release-readiness gate runs.

Previously framed as "harness completeness" (lower urgency); honest
reading is "harness fails to assert wired behavior the umbrella's
done-when promises."

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/187

title: Add nightly fuzz status badge to README
description:
## Context

The nightly fuzz workflow (`.github/workflows/fuzz.yml`, added in bd-dfn2) currently notifies on failure via GitHub's default scheduled-workflow email plus the red mark on the Actions tab. Both work but rely on remembering to check.

A status badge in README.md gives a passive, always-visible signal: whenever the user looks at the repo root on github.com, the badge reflects the latest nightly fuzz result.

## Why low priority

This is a convenience, not a correctness fix. Failure notifications already reach the user via email. The badge just adds one more surface.

## What to do

1. Add the standard GitHub Actions status badge for the `Fuzz (nightly)` workflow to `README.md`, near the existing CI/build badges if any. Form:
   ```markdown
   [![Fuzz (nightly)](https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/actions/workflows/fuzz.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/actions/workflows/fuzz.yml)
   ```
2. Verify it renders correctly on the GitHub-rendered README after merge.

## Out of scope

- Auto-filing issues on crash (considered and rejected in bd-dfn2 DD 4).
- Dashboard / Grafana / Slack integrations.
close_reason: Won't-fix: nightly fuzz is not a PR gate (bd-dfn2 DD 4), so a red badge would misrepresent project health. README already carries 5 PR-gating/quality badges; adding a 6th for a non-gating informational signal adds visual noise without useful info to visitors. The triage flow already promotes findings into tests/data/fuzz-regressions/ where they show up under the existing Tests badge. Notification path (email + Actions dashboard red mark) per bd-dfn2 is the right mechanism.

title: Fix stale Rust API references in docs markdown examples
description:
Originates from direct user feedback (not a GitHub issue).

## Context

User feedback, verbatim:

> "I noticed that most of the Rust examples have confabulations / stale references to old methods ... Some of that was stuff like record.get_title() needing to use record.get_titles() and the consequent iteration but there were also some basic Rust loop structure oddities on the query page."

The Rust code examples in the mkdocs documentation reference methods and call shapes that do not match the real `src/` API. The compiled `examples/*.rs` files use the correct API, but the Markdown fenced code blocks have drifted. Root cause: rustdoc doc-tests run in CI, but Markdown code fences in the mkdocs docs are never compiled or tested, so the drift went unnoticed.

## Concrete findings (grep of real API in `src/` vs. docs)

Nonexistent methods called in docs:

- `FieldQuery::tag("245")` and `FieldQuery::tag_range("600", "699")` are called as static constructors, but in `src/field_query.rs` `tag` (line 94) and `tag_range` (line 148) are builder methods on an existing query. The real shape is `FieldQuery::new().tag("245")` / `FieldQuery::new().tag_range("600", "699")`. Affected: `docs/tutorials/rust/querying-fields.md:33,49`; `docs/reference/rust-api.md:220,224,228`.
- `query.find_all(&record)` is called repeatedly, but no `find_all` method exists anywhere in `src/`. Field lookup goes through `Record` methods such as `record.fields_matching(&query)`, `record.fields_matching_range(&query)`, `record.fields_in_range(...)` (`src/record.rs:377,403,318`). Affected: `docs/tutorials/rust/querying-fields.md:34,50`; `docs/reference/rust-api.md:221,225,230`.
- `FieldQuery::...with_subfield("a", "Smith")` is called, but no `with_subfield` method exists. Presence is `has_subfield(char)` (`src/field_query.rs:121`); value matching lives in `SubfieldValueQuery`. Affected: `docs/reference/rust-api.md:229`.

Wrong call shape on `Field` (these are public fields / differently-named or differently-typed methods, confirmed against the compiled ground-truth example `examples/reading_and_querying.rs`):

- `field.subfield("a")` — wrong name and wrong argument type. The accessor is `get_subfield(code: char)` returning `Option<&str>` (`src/record.rs:1063`); it takes a `char`, not a `&str`. (Note `Field::subfield(self, code: char, value: String)` at `src/record.rs:1335` is an unrelated builder method.) Affected: `docs/tutorials/rust/querying-fields.md:18,37,53,68,86,101,110,131,132,163`; `docs/tutorials/rust/reading-records.md:50,57,119,147`; `docs/getting-started/quickstart-rust.md:39,46`; `docs/reference/marc-primer.md:145,146`; `docs/reference/rust-api.md:31,87`.
- `field.tag()`, `field.indicator1()`, `field.indicator2()` called as methods, but `tag`, `indicator1`, `indicator2` are public struct fields accessed without parentheses (`src/record.rs:89,91,93`). Affected: `docs/tutorials/rust/querying-fields.md:53,64,126,159`; `docs/tutorials/rust/reading-records.md:64,141,142,145`; `docs/reference/rust-api.md:85,86`; `docs/reference/marc-primer.md:147`.

Missing trait import for `record.title()`:

- `record.title()` is used throughout the Rust examples, but `title()` is a method on the `RecordHelpers` trait (`src/record_helpers.rs:62`, exported at `src/lib.rs:180`). None of the Rust doc pages add `use mrrc::RecordHelpers;`, so every `record.title()` call as written fails to compile. The user recalled this as `record.get_title()` / `record.get_titles()`; the actual nearby API is `RecordHelpers::title() -> Option<&str>` plus the `BibliographicQueries::get_titles() -> Vec<&Field>` trait (`src/format_queries.rs:36`). Affected pages calling `record.title()` without the import include `docs/getting-started/quickstart-rust.md`, `docs/tutorials/rust/reading-records.md`, `docs/tutorials/rust/querying-fields.md`, `docs/tutorials/rust/concurrency.md`, `docs/tutorials/rust/writing-records.md`, `docs/reference/rust-api.md`, `docs/reference/marc-primer.md`, `docs/reference/encoding.md`, `docs/reference/formats.md`, `docs/index.md`.

Type mismatch in a contributing doc:

- `docs/contributing/testing.md:114` asserts `record.title() == Some("Test Title".to_string())`, but `title()` returns `Option<&str>`, so this would not compile as written.

"Basic Rust loop structure oddities on the query page" (`docs/tutorials/rust/querying-fields.md`): the page mixes the (nonexistent) query-object `find_all` flow with hand-rolled `for field in record.fields_by_tag(...)` loops that use the wrong `Field` accessors, so the query-DSL sections do not actually demonstrate the DSL and the manual loops do not compile. The "Combining Queries" and "Complete Example" blocks (lines 120-185) combine all of the above issues at once.

## Proposed approach

Rewrite the Rust examples on the affected pages to match the real API: `FieldQuery::new().tag(...)`, `record.fields_matching(&query)` / `fields_matching_range` / `fields_in_range`, `field.get_subfield(char)`, public-field access for `field.tag` / `field.indicator1` / `field.indicator2`, and add `use mrrc::RecordHelpers;` wherever `record.title()` is used. Cross-check each rewritten block against the compiled `examples/reading_and_querying.rs`, which already uses the correct API. Fix the `testing.md` assertion to compare against `Some("Test Title")`.

## Done when

- All Rust code fences on the affected pages use methods and call shapes that exist in `src/` (verified against the real API and the compiled `examples/`).
- `find_all`, `with_subfield`, `FieldQuery::tag`/`tag_range` static-constructor usage, and `field.subfield(...)`/`field.tag()`/`field.indicator{1,2}()` method-call shapes are gone from active docs (excluding `docs/history/`).
- Every example using `record.title()` imports `RecordHelpers`.
- `docs/contributing/testing.md` title assertion compiles against `Option<&str>`.
close_reason: Fixed stale Rust API references across 11 doc files; landed in PR #238 (merged, closes GH #233). All Rust code-fence examples corrected against src/ + compiled examples/ and verified by compiling a throwaway exercising every shape. Credited @acdha. Follow-up bd-du5n filed for the rust-api.md reference tables + two Python-fence refs.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #233 (v0.8.1 milestone). Credits @acdha for the report.

title: CI hygiene: workflow consolidation, matrix discipline, and check.sh polish
description:
Umbrella for the 10-bead CI/workflow cleanup work surfaced across the v0.8 cycle. None of these are user-facing; they reduce friction in PR review (faster feedback, fewer redundant matrix cells, cleaner failure signals) and lock in conventions before they bit-rot.

## Order of operations - any order within tier

### Tier A - Workflow consolidation (p2)

- bd-qbne - drop or shrink build.yml (overlap with python-build.yml's maturin matrix)
- bd-uajr - slim PR python-build matrix; run full 3x5 only on push to main
- bd-ymcl - switch check.sh maturin invocation from develop to --release

### Tier B - Lint + check coverage (p3)

- bd-9wk8 - add compile-only checks to check.sh (examples + bench compile)
- bd-dz88 - add CHANGELOG lint to lint.yml
- bd-p97l - add process-label lint to check.sh (bead IDs, PR/version refs in source/docs); sibling of bd-dz88, shares check.sh / lint.yml scaffolding
- bd-u2or - reduce benchmark-python matrix or drop in favor of CodSpeed
- bd-tk17 - investigate transient 'No space left on device' on Benchmarks: Rust

### Tier C - Cosmetic / polish (p4)

- bd-bez7 - misc check-stack cosmetic fixes (PYO3_ABI3_* env, stale pre-push comment)
- bd-bn65 - clean up mypy --strict in mrrc/ for permanent strict-mode gating

## Done when

Every child is closed or explicitly deferred with a rationale. Not gated on a specific release.
close_reason: CI-hygiene umbrella complete. All children closed except bd-bn65 (mypy --strict cleanup, P4), which is detached to stand alone and explicitly deferred per this umbrella's done-when ('every child closed or explicitly deferred with a rationale'). Workflow consolidation, matrix slimming, CHANGELOG lint, process-label lint, and check.sh polish all landed across the v0.8 cycle.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Scope assessment 2026-06-04 (read-only review; owner deferring changes pending decision on CodSpeed usefulness). Cost profile from real run data: ~70 runner-min / ~46 jobs per PR; wheel build+test matrix (30 jobs, ~36 min) and benchmarks (6 jobs, ~26 min; CodSpeed alone 11.6 min) dominate. Per-child verdicts: bd-uajr right-sized, highest ROI (~25 min/PR; dropped cells still caught on push-to-main + release tag). bd-u2or right-sized (~14 min/PR; the pytest-benchmark matrix has no baseline comparison, so no regression detection is lost — CodSpeed is the actual gate). bd-qbne right-sized (~4 min; sequence after bd-9wk8 so examples-compile coverage moves to check.sh first). bd-ymcl/bd-9wk8/bd-dz88/bd-bez7/bd-bn65 right-sized (bd-dz88 value is CI-side enforcement for hook-bypassing PRs such as dependabot, not a missing check). bd-ytyf is deadline-bound (windows runner redirect effective 2026-06-15) and gates python-release.yml — pull forward from P3; it is also missing from the epic body's order-of-operations list (11 children, body lists 10). bd-p97l partially obsolete: its motivating mrrc-vxmp example in python-build.yml was already scrubbed (leaving garbled text), and patterns are false-positive-prone — re-spec or defer. Gaps with no bead, all zero-coverage-loss: (1) no concurrency-cancel groups on any PR workflow (force-push leaves ~46 stale jobs running; biggest cheap win); (2) dependabot PRs trigger the full matrix incl. 30 wheels + CodSpeed (gate heavy workflows on github.actor != 'dependabot[bot]'); (3) no cargo cache sharing — Build/Tests/Lint/maturin each compile mrrc from scratch under distinct cache keys; (4) no timeout-minutes on PR workflows (historic 45-48 min hangs; GitHub default 6 h). Suggested ROI order: bd-ytyf (deadline) -> bd-uajr -> dependabot gate (new) -> concurrency-cancel (new) -> bd-u2or -> bd-qbne/bd-9wk8. CodSpeed walltime-on-hosted-runners inconsistency (see PR #249 false regression report, 57 benches uniformly -20..-28% across different runners) is direct evidence for bd-u2or and for weighing whether CodSpeed walltime mode is gate-worthy at all.

title: ci: add .beads/** to paths-ignore so bead-bookkeeping commits skip heavy workflows
description:
## Problem

The heavy CI workflows (test.yml, benchmark-python.yml, benchmark-rust.yml, python-build.yml) carry `paths-ignore` lists covering `**.md`, `docs/**`, `mkdocs.yml`, `LICENSE`, `.gitignore` — but not `.beads/**`. GitHub skips a workflow only when EVERY changed file in the event matches an ignore pattern, so any commit that also touches `.beads/issues.jsonl` re-arms the full test + benchmark + wheel matrix. Two common cases hit this: a docs/code PR that also carries a bead-status commit (observed on #256, where a `.beads/issues.jsonl` change made an otherwise docs-only PR run the whole matrix), and pure `chore(beads)` commits pushed to main after a merge — those fire the full 30-job push-to-main matrix plus benchmarks for a JSONL-only change.

## What to do

Add `.beads/**` to the `paths-ignore` list in BOTH the `pull_request` and `push` sections of test.yml, benchmark-python.yml, benchmark-rust.yml, and python-build.yml. While there, check whether coverage.yml, memory-safety.yml, and miri.yml carry the same heavy cost and the same omission, and add `.beads/**` there too if so. Leave lint.yml / docs.yml / semver.yml decisions to their existing filters.

## Done when

- A commit changing only `.beads/**` (alone or alongside docs/markdown) triggers no test/benchmark/wheel jobs on PRs or on push to main.
- Heavy workflows still run normally for real source changes.
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes.

## Notes

Part of the CI Tier B track under bd-no9e. Pairs naturally with the other lint/check.sh hygiene beads since they share the workflow files.
close_reason: Done in #257: added .beads/** to paths-ignore on test.yml, benchmark-python.yml, benchmark-rust.yml, python-build.yml. Bead-only commits no longer trigger the test/benchmark/wheel matrix.

title: CI and release hardening
description: Epic. Close the operational gaps: gates that do not gate, releases that are not idempotent, failures that notify no one. Children cover OPS-1 through OPS-10 (minus resolved OPS-7 parts), TEST-3, and TEST-4 in Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md.

title: Require status checks on main; least-privilege workflow permissions; pin release-path actions
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, OPS-1, OPS-8, OPS-9.

The main ruleset has only deletion and non-fast-forward rules — the entire PR gate is advisory for direct pushes, which land untested and immediately deploy docs. 10 of 15 workflows inherit the default GITHUB_TOKEN scope. pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish pins a moving branch (release/v1) in the OIDC publish job — the highest-value pinning target; other third-party actions are tag refs.

What to do: required checks (Tests, Lint, wheel build) on the ruleset, keeping fast-forward push allowed; permissions: contents: read at top level everywhere with per-job elevation; SHA-pin release/publish-path actions (Dependabot keeps them current); verify the repo default token setting is read-only.

title: Make the release pipeline idempotent and rehearsable
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, OPS-5, OPS-6, and the OPS-7 remainder.

All 25 wheels publish in one invocation without skip-existing, and the GitHub Release step shares the job — a partial failure makes re-runs die on "file already exists", and the documented remedy (re-tag) collides identically. Cross-compiled wheels (10 of 25) are validated by filename grep only and never imported before publish. No TestPyPI rehearsal exists, so first contact with PyPI validation is the production publish.

What to do: skip-existing: true on the publish step; split GH Release creation into its own job; QEMU import-smoke for aarch64 and container-based i686 smoke; a workflow_dispatch TestPyPI input; fix the residual "(15 wheels present)" at docs/contributing/release-procedure.md:1324.
close_reason: Merged in PR #300

title: Run the wheel build/test gate on Dependabot PRs
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, OPS-4.

python-build.yml:34,73 skip both PR jobs when the actor is dependabot, so a pyo3/maturin/cargo bump that breaks the extension merges green and fails for the first time in the 30-job post-merge matrix. Demonstrated live on PR #221: every wheel build/test job SKIPPED while the bare version bump failed to compile.

What to do: one ungated slim build+test job (1 OS x 1 Python) that also runs for dependabot; keep the CodSpeed skips.
close_reason: Merged in PR #295

title: Notify on nightly-suite failures; persist the fuzz corpus; schedule cargo-audit
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, OPS-2, OPS-3, OPS-10c.

fuzz/miri/memory-safety nightlies fail silently for a solo dev (GitHub also auto-disables cron workflows after 60 days of repo inactivity). The fuzz corpus regrows from the 26 committed seeds every night and is discarded, so coverage depth never compounds. cargo-audit runs only on push/PR, so an advisory landing during a quiet stretch surfaces only at the next push.

What to do: an if: failure() step that files/updates a GitHub issue on all three nightlies; actions/cache on fuzz/corpus per target with restore-keys plus periodic cargo fuzz cmin re-commit; a schedule trigger on the audit job.
close_reason: Merged in PR #302

title: Add an OS matrix to the Rust test job
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, TEST-4.

test.yml runs on Linux only; cross-platform signal comes entirely from the Python wheel suite, so pure-Rust consumers' platform-sensitive paths (from_path, file I/O, the rayon pipeline) are never tested on macOS or Windows as Rust.

What to do: OS matrix on the cargo test job, or a slim-PR/full-main split like python-build.yml already uses.

title: Measure code coverage on a schedule for Rust and Python
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, TEST-3.

coverage.yml is workflow_dispatch-only, Rust-only (tarpaulin), and fail_ci_if_error: false; there is no pytest-cov anywhere. Coverage is effectively unmeasured and ungated.

What to do: scheduled run with codecov visibility; consider cargo llvm-cov over tarpaulin; add a Python coverage lane. Visibility first; gating later if ever.

title: CI consistency grab bag: release env parity, caches, retries, type-check gating, timeouts, comment hygiene
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, OPS-10 a/b/d/e/g/h and S-L3.

(a) PYO3_USE_ABI3_FORWARD_COMPATIBILITY is set in PR CI and check.sh but absent from python-release.yml — a new CPython fails first at release time. (b) Three inconsistent Rust cache strategies; raw actions/cache in coverage/miri/memory-safety keyed only on Cargo.lock; maturin wheel builds compile cold (sccache would shave the job that bounds the PR gate). (d) The 18-line macOS whole-suite retry is copy-pasted three times — pytest-rerunfailures gives per-test retries with per-test signal. (e) mypy/pyright block locally in check.sh but never block in CI on mrrc/ — gate the current mode in lint.yml (bd-bn65 remains the --strict follow-up). (g) Missing timeout-minutes on miri/coverage/docs/release jobs (360-minute default). (h) unsafe-block comments cite a nonexistent GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md (src-python/src/readers.rs:295) — replace with the actual invariant.

title: Stop main-push benchmark runs cancelling each other; clean up the stale CodSpeed registry
description:
Source: CodSpeed investigation 2026-06-11 (follow-on to docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md's CI theme). Scope narrowed 2026-06-12: the CodSpeed registry cleanup is DONE (stale walltime-era entries archived via UI; 37 live / 68 archived — see project memory). Remaining work is the cancel-in-progress fix only.

Both benchmark workflows use cancel-in-progress: true keyed on workflow+ref (benchmark-python.yml:23-25, benchmark-rust.yml:28-30), so back-to-back merges to main cancel the previous merge commit's benchmark run — this killed the Python baseline run at commit 9b96f2c and is why PR comparisons fall back to older base commits. Fix: cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }} so main-push baseline runs always complete.

Optional follow-up to evaluate (separate decision, not required here): merging the Rust and Python benchmark jobs into one workflow so both uploads share a CI run and CodSpeed produces one merged report; verify CodSpeed's run-grouping behavior first.

Done when: a pair of consecutive main merges both complete their benchmark runs and PR reports compare against the immediately preceding main commit.
close_reason: Merged in PR #297

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: The manual half is done (2026-06-11): all 68 stale registry entries archived via the CodSpeed UI (60 retired walltime-era entries + 8 benches/parallel_benchmarks.rs simulation entries that CI's allowlist excludes), verified 37 live + 68 archived with live entries spot-checked unarchived. Remaining in this bead: the cancel-in-progress workflow fix so main-push baseline runs stop cancelling each other, and the optional single-workflow merge evaluation.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Second observed instance (2026-06-12): PRs #292 and #293 both fell back to the same stale baseline (427a7f8, 'no successful run was found on main' footnote) because quick-succession merges cancelled main's benchmark runs. Result: identical per-benchmark pseudo-gains on both PRs — including #293, a check.sh-only diff that cannot alter performance. Shared-baseline artifacts now actively mislead per-benchmark readings; the cancel-in-progress fix is the remaining scope here and is getting urgent as merge cadence increases.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Diagnosis escalation (2026-06-12, third instance): CodSpeed's effective baseline is FROZEN at a pre-#285 snapshot regardless of the comparison commit its footnotes cite. Proof: serialize_1k_to_json BASE=27.3ms identical in #290 and #294 reports despite the LTO merge between them; Rust from_path benches (on main since #285) show as NEW in #294; successful main runs at 427a7f8/13af2ce contributed no fresh baseline data. Every PR report therefore shows the same cumulative-drift rows (~12-31%, dominated by #291 fat-LTO instruction gains) and per-PR CodSpeed verdicts are currently unreliable. Both benchmark workflows succeed GitHub-side, so the ingestion failure is CodSpeed-side — investigate on the dashboard (Playwright + user login per the dashboard-management procedure): check whether main runs since #285 appear at all, and whether the registry archive/cleanup affected baseline selection. Scope of this bead now: (1) cancel-in-progress fix for quick-succession merges, (2) restore working baseline ingestion on main. Until fixed, read per-PR reports as cumulative-vs-frozen, not per-change.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Correction/refinement from user: the registry cleanup (37 live / 68 archived, commit 4ab6763) is DONE — done via Playwright before this session. Timeline correlation: 4ab6763 immediately precedes the baseline freeze window (frozen snapshot is pre-#285, same day). Prime suspect: the bulk archive caught canonical live series, or post-cleanup uploads land in registry entries the PR-comparison logic doesn't use as baselines. Next-session dashboard audit: (1) verify the 37 live entries are the ones receiving current main-run uploads, (2) check whether any of the 68 archived entries were live series and restore them, (3) confirm the PR-report baseline unfreezes afterward. Then the cancel-in-progress fix as originally scoped.

title: ci: add mkdocs build to check.sh full mode
description:
## Problem

v0.8 ships new docs pages (`docs/reference/error-handling.md`,
`docs/reference/error-codes.md`, expanded `docs/reference/python-api.md`)
with cross-references between them. CI's `docs.yml` workflow only
builds mkdocs on push to main with `paths: docs/**` — broken
cross-links in a PR aren't surfaced until merge.

`.cargo/check.sh` doesn't run `mkdocs build` at all.

## What to do

Add to `.cargo/check.sh` full mode (skipped under `--quick`):

```bash
echo ""
echo "=== Documentation site build (mkdocs) ==="
uv run mkdocs build
```

Match `docs.yml` exactly — no `--strict` flag for now (the existing
comment in `docs.yml` line 41 notes `--strict` is gated on the
cross-linking fix work tracked separately as mrrc-s3ug).

## Done when

- `mkdocs build` step added to `check.sh` full mode.
- Local pre-push run completes the new step cleanly on current main.
- CHANGELOG entry under `[Unreleased]` `### Changed` (terse — this
  is a contributor-experience change, not a user-facing one).

## Out of scope

- Adding `--strict`; that's mrrc-s3ug.
- Adding mkdocs to `--quick` mode.
- A CI duplicate of this — `docs.yml` already covers post-merge.

title: Exclude the Rust parallel criterion bench from simulation-mode CodSpeed
description:
bd-ipu0 excludes the Python parallel-throughput files from CodSpeed because Valgrind serializes threads under simulation, making rayon/thread speedup meaningless. benches/parallel_benchmarks.rs (Rust criterion) is still collected by 'cargo codspeed' in benchmark-rust.yml under simulation mode, so it reports instruction counts for serialized 'parallel' runs that measure nothing useful and just add noise and runtime.

What to do: stop running parallel_benchmarks.rs under simulation CodSpeed, consistent with the Python exclusion. Options: drop the [[bench]] from the codspeed-built set, gate it behind a feature/cfg so 'cargo codspeed build' skips it, or move it to the parallel-throughput walltime procedure (sibling child of this epic). Whichever is chosen, the sequential-baseline functions in that file may still be worth keeping as a single-thread signal; decide explicitly.

Done when: no parallel-throughput bench runs under simulation CodSpeed (Rust matches Python); benchmark-rust.yml and Cargo.toml reflect the decision; '.cargo/check.sh' passes.
close_reason: Completed: PR #252 merged. cargo codspeed build now allowlists marc_benchmarks and error_handling_benchmarks; parallel_benchmarks stays for local criterion walltime runs.

title: Enable proptest with round-trip and structural invariant properties
description:
## State

`proptest` is enabled and `tests/properties.rs` has 2 property tests landed in #42/#46 (`binary_roundtrip`, `serialization_never_panics`) with Strategies for Leader/Field/Subfield/Record. Remaining gaps: more properties covering structural invariants; MARCXML/MARCJSON roundtrips; Cargo.toml cleanup; regression-seed discipline.

## What to do

1. Remove the stray commented `#proptest = "1.0"` at `Cargo.toml:50`.
2. Add these properties to `tests/properties.rs`:
   - `leader_length_matches_emitted_bytes` — `leader.record_length == buffer.len()` after write.
   - `directory_entries_tile_data_area` — each directory entry's `(start, length)` points into the data area; entries are non-overlapping; sum of lengths + terminators matches the data-area size.
   - `indicator_bytes_in_valid_set` — every emitted indicator byte in the serialized bytes is a digit or space.
   - `subfield_codes_are_lower_alnum` — every emitted subfield code matches `[a-z0-9]`.
   - `marcxml_roundtrip` — `parse(emit(record, MARCXML)) == record`. Strategy must include XML-metachar subfield values (`<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, `'`) so escaping edge cases are exercised.
   - `marcjson_roundtrip` — `parse(emit(record, MARCJSON)) == record`. Strategy must include JSON-problematic chars (control chars, `\`, `"`).
3. Broaden `arb_record` to cover:
   - Zero data fields (only control fields) — currently hardcoded to 1–10.
   - Zero control fields, multiple data fields — ensure the 001-required-for-control-number path isn't accidentally depended on.
4. Add a `ProptestConfig { cases: 64, ..Default::default() }` override (or document the `PROPTEST_CASES` env var) so CI runtime stays predictable once ~8 properties run. Target total runtime <10s locally; document actual in `docs/contributing/` or a test-file comment.
5. Regression-seed discipline: commit `tests/proptest-regressions/` contents when a property fails and the failing seed is saved (these are permanent regression guards). Add `proptest-regressions/*.pending` to `.gitignore` so only accepted seeds are tracked.
6. For any bug surfaced by a property: fix in a separate commit, keep the failing seed in `proptest-regressions/` as a permanent guard, file a follow-up bead if scope warrants.

## Edge cases the strategies must exercise

- Subfield values containing `<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, `'` (MARCXML escaping).
- Subfield values containing control chars, `\`, `"` (MARCJSON escaping).
- Records with zero data fields (only control fields).
- Records with zero control fields (no 001, no record_control_number).
- Non-ASCII via UTF-8 `character_coding = 'a'` — current `arb_subfield_value` is ASCII-only. Either broaden it or file a follow-up bead noting UTF-8 subfield content isn't proptest-covered yet.

## Done when

- `Cargo.toml:50` commented line removed.
- At least 7 property tests total (existing 2 + 5–6 new), all passing.
- MARCXML and MARCJSON roundtrip properties exercise their respective metachar escaping.
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes; property-test runtime stays under ~10s locally.
- `proptest-regressions/` handling decided and documented in a test-file comment.
- CHANGELOG entry under next version.
- Any bugs surfaced get their own commits + permanent seed guards; beads for scope that warrants follow-up work.

## Out of scope

- Shrinking tuning — proptest defaults are adequate.
- Property tests on BIBFRAME / MODS / Dublin Core serializers — those are one-way / lossy; round-trip isn't meaningful.
- Coverage-guided fuzzing (covered separately by bd-dfn2 cargo-fuzz).
- UTF-8 subfield content in proptest strategies (may become a follow-up bead if the scope grows).
close_reason: Delivered in #103 (merged). Property test suite expanded to 8 properties with MARCXML/JSON roundtrips and structural invariants.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/91

title: ci: add process-label lint to check.sh (bead IDs, PR refs, version tags in source/docs)
description:
## Context

Project memory `feedback_no_process_labels_in_persistent_artifacts` forbids embedding implementation-process labels in persistent artifacts — source comments, docstrings, test bodies, user-facing docs. The list includes bead IDs (`bd-xxxx`), GitHub references (`#NNN`, `PR #117`), phase letters, time-state wording ("currently", "not yet"), and version-tagged claims ("flipped in 0.8.1", "since 0.8.1").

These references rot: bead IDs get renumbered, PR numbers stop being meaningful once squashed, version tags become confusing noise once readers are well past that version. `git log` and `git blame` are the right place for provenance; the CHANGELOG is the right place for version-context; source/tests/docs should describe the system in timeless terms.

The rule is documented and has a pre-commit self-check pattern list, but enforcement is by-convention: contributors (especially AI assistants, who have produced repeated violations during the v0.8 cycle) have to remember to grep their diff. A lint catches what convention misses.

## This lint is preventive, not corrective

A 2026-05-28 audit found the tree is already clean of true violations: the `bd-[a-z0-9]{4}` bead-ID pattern has zero hits under `src/ src-python/src/ mrrc/ tests/ README.md docs/` (excluding archival), and the only version-triple hits are genuine semantic references (see allow-list below). So the "clean sweep before turning it on" precondition is essentially already met — the lint's value is catching *future* drift, not clearing a backlog.

## What needs to change

Add a lint stage to `.cargo/check.sh` (or to `lint.yml` if the CHANGELOG-lint sibling `bd-dz88` is being added at the same time and they should share scaffolding).

Grep the tracked tree under `src/`, `src-python/src/`, `mrrc/`, `tests/`, `docs/` (excluding `docs/history/` and `docs/design/profiling/`, which are archival), `.github/`, and `README.md` for these patterns:

- `bd-[a-z0-9]{4}` — beads bead IDs
- `(?i)\bPR\s*#?[0-9]+\b` — PR identifiers
- `#[0-9]+` in contexts that aren't anchor links or doc cross-refs
- `(?i)\bphase\s+[A-Z0-9]\b` — phase markers
- `(?i)\b(currently|not yet|deferred|will be|planned)\b` paired with a project-state noun (harder to grep cleanly; may need an allow-list)
- `\b[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\b` — version triples
- `(?i)since\s+(version\s+)?[0-9]+\.[0-9]+` — "since 0.8.1" / "since version 0.8"
- `(?i)flipped in [0-9]+\.[0-9]+` — release-context cue used in violation
- `(?i)as of [0-9]+\.[0-9]+` — release-context cue

Do NOT lint for a `mrrc-[a-z0-9]{4}` pattern: the legacy `mrrc-XXXX` bead-ID convention is no longer in use, and the pattern is a false-positive magnet — it matches legitimate words like `mrrc-specific`, `mrrc-python`, `mrrc-native`, `mrrc-aware`, `mrrc-testbed`, `mrrc-equivalent` (30+ hits across exceptions.py, docs, and tests). Dropped from the list.

Fail check.sh on any hit. The CHANGELOG (top-level + `[Unreleased]`) is the load-bearing exception — exclude it from the sweep. Commit messages and PR descriptions are also allowed (not in tree).

Provide an opt-out comment marker (`# noqa: process-label` or similar) for the rare legitimate version-triple references that are genuine semantic content, not process labels. Known current cases to allow-list:

- `crossbeam-epoch 0.9.18` in `src/rayon_parser_pool.rs`
- `pymarc 5.3.1` in `src/recovery.rs`

Note: extending the sweep to `.github/` would catch the lone `# See: mrrc-vxmp` comment in `python-build.yml` (the only process-label-ish reference found outside the original target dirs).

## Done when

- `.cargo/check.sh` fails when a staged diff introduces any of the forbidden patterns in source / tests / non-CHANGELOG docs / workflows.
- Existing tree passes the new lint (already clean apart from the two allow-listed version refs).
- Memory `feedback_no_process_labels_in_persistent_artifacts` is updated to point at the lint as the enforcement mechanism (manual self-check stays as backup).

## Why P3

Hygiene improvement, not a behavior fix. The class of issue has been repeatedly surfaced during AI-assisted development; lint > memory for hard rules. Filed under CI hygiene epic `bd-no9e` as a sibling to the CHANGELOG lint bead `bd-dz88` (both are check.sh / lint.yml additions).

## References

- Memory: `feedback_no_process_labels_in_persistent_artifacts` for the rule + the pre-commit self-check pattern list.
- bd-dz88 — sibling: add CHANGELOG lint to lint.yml. Likely shares scaffolding.
close_reason: process-label lint added (scripts/lint_process_labels.py) in check.sh + process-labels.yml CI workflow; surfaced violations cleaned; memory updated. Merged in PR #267.

title: Cross-link the query DSL guide from the performance, migration, and large-files guides
description:
GitHub issue #225 (https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/225) notes that the query DSL is one of the best ways to avoid crossing the Rust/Python boundary and churning objects you do not need, yet none of the performance-tuning, migration-from-pymarc, or working-with-large-files guides mention or link to it.

Confirmed: a search for "query" in all three guides returns zero matches.

- `docs/guides/performance-tuning.md`
- `docs/guides/migration-from-pymarc.md`
- `docs/guides/working-with-large-files.md`

The query DSL guide already exists at `docs/guides/query-dsl.md` and is in the mkdocs nav.

## Proposed approach

Add a short, contextual pointer to `docs/guides/query-dsl.md` from each of the three guides where it is most relevant:

- performance-tuning: in the section about avoiding unnecessary Python object creation / staying in Rust, point to the query DSL as the idiomatic way to filter fields without materializing every field as a Python object.
- migration-from-pymarc: where the guide covers `get_fields`, note that the query DSL goes beyond pymarc for indicator/range/pattern filtering.
- working-with-large-files: where streaming and per-record work is discussed, point to the query DSL for filtering fields efficiently during a streaming pass.

Keep each pointer brief and place it where a reader is already thinking about performance or field access.

## Done when

- Each of the three guides links to `docs/guides/query-dsl.md` with a sentence of context explaining why the query DSL helps in that scenario.
- `uv run mkdocs build` succeeds with no broken-link warnings for the new links.
close_reason: Cross-linked the Query DSL guide from the performance-tuning, migration-from-pymarc, and working-with-large-files guides; landed in PR #236 (merged, closes GH #234). Credited @acdha in CHANGELOG [Unreleased].

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Tracks GitHub issue #225 (https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/225) — the documentation cross-linking point.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #234 (v0.8.1 milestone). Credits @acdha (original report #225).

title: Test integrity and pymarc oracle coverage
description: Epic. Make the test suite tell the truth: activate the dormant pymarc oracle, delete tests that assert nothing, give authority/holdings the bibliographic path's rigor, and extend fuzzing to unfuzzed surfaces. Children cover PAR-2..PAR-4, TEST-1, TEST-2, TEST-5, TEST-6 in Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md.

title: Run the pymarc-oracle tests in CI and extend them to value-level comparisons
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PAR-2.

tests/python/test_pymarc_parity.py and test_repeated_control_fields.py are skip-guarded on pymarc, which is in no extra and installed by no CI job — both silently skip in every CI and local run, so the project's primary compatibility target has zero executing oracle coverage.

What to do: add pymarc as a managed test dependency in its own extra (e.g. [project.optional-dependencies] oracle = ["pymarc>=5.3"]) so the version is locked in uv.lock and Dependabot bumps it on the normal weekly cadence — do NOT hardcode a version in the CI command. Add a CI step (and a check.sh equivalent or documented opt-in) that installs the extra and runs the oracle tests. Extend the oracle beyond iteration shape to value-level comparisons (format_field(), value(), title(), as_marc() byte-equality on a valid corpus) — exactly the shape that would have caught the format_field divergence (PAR-1).

Versioning policy (the point of the extra): when a new pymarc release changes behavior, the oracle fails on Dependabot's bump PR, making the divergence a reviewable decision — adopt the new pymarc behavior (pymarc is the design north star) or document the deliberate divergence in the migration guide and adjust the oracle's expectation with a comment. Either way the adjudication is recorded in the bump PR, not discovered by users.

Complements bd-tfre (real-corpus testing): a real corpus is the natural input for value-level comparisons once both exist.
close_reason: Merged in PR #286: oracle extra, CI/check.sh enforcement, value-level parity over the 1k corpus

title: Delete vacuous test files; make the ASAN workflow run integration tests
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, TEST-1.

tests/concurrent_gil_tests.rs has 10 tests asserting nothing about mrrc (local-variable simulations, stdlib semantics, references to Python test files that do not exist). tests/memory_safety_asan.rs is stdlib-only AND memory-safety.yml runs without --tests, so the file never executes under ASAN. Two rayon_parser_pool unit tests are assert!(result.is_ok() || result.is_err()) tautologies; no successful pipeline run is unit-tested in Rust.

What to do: delete concurrent_gil_tests.rs (the real GIL proof is test_gil_release_verification.py); delete or rebuild memory_safety_asan.rs around actual mrrc parsing and add --tests to the workflow; replace the tautologies with exact assertions.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: PR #310 delivered the test deletions/rebuilds but not the workflow half (memory-safety.yml --tests), which Track A could not touch. That half is absorbed into bd-eoyi alongside the fuzz.yml matrix follow-up. Close this bead on PR #310 merge.

title: Add authority/holdings roundtrip property tests and PartialEq derives

Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, TEST-2 (plus the PartialEq half of PKG-10b).

Holdings writer output is never parsed back at any layer; authority has one shallow integration roundtrip; tests/properties.rs generates bibliographic records only — the writers flagged for consolidation have no generative verification.

What to do: arb_authority_record()/arb_holdings_record() strategies and roundtrip properties (the leader/field strategies are reusable); derive PartialEq on Record/AuthorityRecord/HoldingsRecord (one-line derives; Field/Subfield/Leader already have it).

That remaining half is absorbed into bd-d7aq.1, the writer-consolidation bead it belongs with; see the comment there. Close this bead on PR #306 merge without the PartialEq work.

title: Add Python happy-path tests for authority and holdings readers
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, PAR-3.

AuthorityMARCReader and HoldingsMARCReader appear in tests/python/ only in error-path tests; no Python test ever successfully reads either record type or touches AuthorityRecord/HoldingsRecord accessors.

What to do: test_authority_holdings_reading.py against the existing tests/data/simple_authority.mrc and simple_holdings.mrc fixtures, mirroring the Rust happy-path tests.

tests/python/test_field_handle_writeback.py (14 tests) misses: mutating the record while iterating get_fields(); handle behavior across copy/deepcopy; same-tag remove plus re-add occurrence aliasing (StaleFieldError vs silently-wrong field); handles from reader-produced vs constructed records.
