title: Add fuzz targets for authority, holdings, BIBFRAME, and lenient-mode parsing
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, TEST-5.

Nine fuzz targets cover the bibliographic reader and the text formats. Not fuzzed: the authority reader, the holdings reader (their type-specific hooks diverge from the bibliographic builder), the BIBFRAME/RDF read path (the largest external-parser dependency surface), and the recovery salvage path gets only incidental lenient-mode coverage.

What to do: parse_authority, parse_holdings, and parse_bibframe targets plus one binary target running with_recovery_mode(Lenient). Pairs with the fuzz-corpus persistence work in the CI epic — persistence multiplies the value of new targets.

title: Bound lenient-mode allocation by available input bytes
description:
Source: docs/design/code-review-findings-v0.8.2.md, TEST-6.

iso2709_skeleton.rs:269-279: in non-strict recovery, a truncated record claiming record_length=99999 yields a zero-padded buffer of the claimed length — roughly 4000x allocation amplification per 25-byte stub record (DoS-class only; bounded per record by the ISO ceiling).

What to do: size the recovery buffer to min(claimed, available); add a fuzz or property assertion bounding allocation by input size on the lenient path. Coordinate with bd-qhll — the recovery-merge work touches the same code.

title: Validate mkdocs build on pull requests, not only on push to main
description:
`docs.yml` runs only on `push` to `main` (it deploys the site to Pages). `lint.yml`, `python-build.yml`, and `test.yml` all list `docs/**`, `mkdocs.yml`, and `**.md` in `paths-ignore`. The net effect: a pull request that only changes docs runs no `mkdocs build` — a broken docs build merges green and only fails afterward on the main-branch deploy job, briefly breaking docs on main.

Surfaced on PR #263 (the bd-kxkq mkdocstrings work): the only PR check that ran was CHANGELOG lint; the docs build was validated only locally via `.cargo/check.sh`.

Fix: add a lightweight PR job (e.g. in a docs workflow or a new `docs-check.yml`) that runs `uv sync --extra docs --no-install-project` + `uv run mkdocs build` on pull_request when `docs/**` or `mkdocs.yml` change, so doc builds are gated before merge. Consider `--strict` only after the pre-existing cross-link warnings under docs/design/profiling are resolved; until then a non-strict build that fails on errors is enough.

Improvement opportunity, not a regression from any single PR.
close_reason: docs-check.yml gates mkdocs build --strict on PRs; --strict enabled everywhere (PR/deploy/check.sh). Self-tested green on its own PR. Merged in #265.

title: Normalize wrapper docstring example blocks so doctest comments don't render as headings
description:
Many wrapper method docstrings in `mrrc/__init__.py` (e.g. `Record.fields_in_range`, `fields_by_indicator`, `fields_matching*`) use Google-style `Example:` sections containing doctest blocks like:

    Example:
        >>> # Find all subject fields (600-699)
        >>> subjects = record.fields_in_range("600", "699")

When rendered by mkdocstrings (bd-kxkq), the leading `>>> # ...` comment line is parsed as a Markdown heading, so each such example injects a spurious heading (e.g. an h5 'Find all subject fields (600-699)') into the page and its table-of-contents nav. The example content is otherwise correct; only the comment-as-heading leakage is wrong.

Fix options: convert the doctest examples to fenced ```python code blocks, or move the descriptive text out of the `>>>` line so the comment no longer starts a line at heading position, or configure the docstring example rendering so doctest blocks always render as code. Whichever keeps the examples rendering as code with no leaked headings. Sweep all wrapper docstrings (and the `.pyi` if any have the same shape).

Surfaced while wiring up bd-kxkq; that page limits page TOC depth as a stopgap, but the spurious in-content headings remain until the docstrings are normalized.
close_reason: 21 wrapper docstring Example blocks wrapped in fenced code (pycon/python); leaked headings and the autorefs warning gone; toc_depth stopgap removed. Merged in PR #264.

title: Reconcile reader-constructor keyword-only signatures with PyO3 positional reality
description:
Surfaced by the v0.8.1 milestone-wide quality review (9lui) reviewing PR #229.

The reader constructors document `recovery_mode` / `validation_level` as keyword-only: `mrrc/_mrrc.pyi` declares them after a bare `*` for `AuthorityMARCReader` / `HoldingsMARCReader` (and `MARCReader` has the same shape), and `docs/reference/python-api.md` repeats this with a `*` in the signature and a "Keyword Arguments" heading. But at runtime PyO3 accepts them positionally — the `#[pyo3(signature = (source, recovery_mode = ..., validation_level = ...))]` has no `*` separator, so the params are positional-or-keyword. Verified: `AuthorityMARCReader('f.mrc', 'lenient')` succeeds.

Decision to make (pick one and apply consistently across `MARCReader`, `AuthorityMARCReader`, `HoldingsMARCReader`):
- Drop the `*` from the stubs and docs so they match the positional-or-keyword reality, OR
- Add a `*` to the PyO3 `signature`s to make the params actually keyword-only as documented (a small behavior change — positional calls would then raise — but likely the intended ergonomic API for reader options).

Done when: the stubs, docs, and the runtime signature agree for all three readers.

This is the stub/doc-vs-reality drift class that bd-9zoo (mypy.stubtest) would catch mechanically — filing under the bd-c7ok docs epic for that reason. Not v0.8.1-blocking; unmilestoned.
close_reason: Reader option args (recovery_mode/validation_level/max_errors) made keyword-only via PyO3 '*' on all three readers; stub+docs+runtime now agree (source stays positional). Merged in PR #262.

title: ci: drop or shrink build.yml — overlaps with python-build.yml maturin matrix
description:
## Problem

`.github/workflows/build.yml` runs `cargo build --package mrrc` +
`cargo build --examples` on a 3-OS matrix (ubuntu, macos, windows) on
every PR — 3 jobs. `python-build.yml`'s build-wheels job already runs
`maturin --release` on the same 3 OSes, which compiles the entire `mrrc`
Rust crate (since `mrrc-python` depends on it). The only thing `build.yml`
adds beyond that coverage is `cargo build --examples`.

## What to do

Two options:

- **Option A (cleanest):** delete `build.yml` entirely. `python-build.yml`'s
  maturin step verifies cross-OS compile of mrrc core. Move
  `cargo build --examples` to a single ubuntu step in `test.yml` (or skip
  it — examples/ rarely changes and any breakage shows up as a doc-test
  failure or a contributor build error).
- **Option B (conservative):** reduce `build.yml` to one ubuntu job that
  runs `cargo build --examples` only. Removes 2 OS matrix jobs per PR.

Recommended: A unless there's a documented case where `mrrc` core compiled
standalone but `mrrc-python` failed (worth a quick git log search).

## Done when

- `build.yml` deleted or trimmed to single ubuntu examples build.
- PR job count drops by 2-3 (build.yml is 3 jobs).
- CHANGELOG entry under `[Unreleased]` `### Changed`.
- No regression in cross-OS compile coverage.
- `.cargo/check.sh` passes.

## Out of scope

- Slimming `python-build.yml` matrix (separate bead bd-uajr).
- Reducing benchmark cadence (separate bead).
close_reason: Done in #254 via shrink (Option B), not delete: build.yml reduced to a single ubuntu examples-compile job; cross-OS core compile already covered by python-build.yml maturin matrix. Full deletion deferred to bd-9wk8, which must first move examples-compile coverage into check.sh.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Sequencing with bd-9wk8 (2026-05-28): build.yml's only unique CI coverage is `cargo build --examples`. If this bead deletes build.yml (Option A), do bd-9wk8 first or together — it moves examples-compile coverage into check.sh — so that coverage isn't lost in the gap.

title: Merge recovery::try_recover_record salvage path into iso2709_skeleton
description:
## Context

v0.8 quality review (bd-z8oc) identified that `recovery::try_recover_record` (`src/recovery.rs:172-284`) duplicates the same directory-walk + per-field-recovery + clamped-buffer-salvage logic that `iso2709_skeleton::parse_iso2709_record` (`src/iso2709_skeleton.rs:277-412`) implements. The skeleton (added in bd-lkcy / #125) already handles short-read clamping via `directory_end` / `data_start` `min` calls. The bibliographic reader's `try_recover_truncated` hook calls `try_recover_record`, which then re-walks the directory in roughly the same shape as the skeleton.

The two were not merged in bd-lkcy because the salvage path has subtle test coverage that wasn't merged into the skeleton — specifically, the `try_parse_field` helper in `recovery.rs` (also a duplicate of the parsing path).

## Why a focused PR

Re-verifying every salvage edge case requires careful test work. Risk of breaking parsing on partial buffers if the merge is rushed. Belongs in a focused PR with attentive review.

## What to do

1. Audit the salvage-specific test cases in `src/reader.rs` and `src/recovery.rs` that exercise `try_recover_truncated` → `try_recover_record`. Catalog the exact behavioral contracts.
2. Identify which contracts are already met by the skeleton's clamped-buffer path (`iso2709_skeleton.rs:260-271`).
3. Identify which contracts require additional behavior in the skeleton.
4. Extend the skeleton minimally to cover the gaps (likely a hook or a couple of additional clamping branches).
5. Replace `MarcReader`'s `try_recover_truncated` adapter to dispatch to the skeleton's path instead of `recovery::try_recover_record`.
6. Delete `recovery::try_recover_record` and its `try_parse_field` helper (~200 lines).

## Done when

- All previously passing salvage tests still pass.
- `recovery::try_recover_record` and `try_parse_field` are removed (or one of them, if only partial overlap).
- The skeleton's clamped-buffer logic is the single source of truth for short-read salvage.
- CHANGELOG entry under v0.9.

## Scope discipline

Do **not** change error-message wording or per-field error variant choices. Only consolidate the directory-walk + salvage logic.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: bd-wuo1 (non-ASCII tag-byte handling) is sequenced after or with this bead — the salvage/skeleton merge consolidates two of its three divergent decode sites.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Coordinate with bd-pk26.7 (bound lenient-mode allocation): both touch the recovery/salvage path in iso2709_skeleton; sequence or combine to avoid churn.

title: Pymarc API compatibility: comprehensive gap analysis and implementation plan
close_reason: Replaced by bd-14jh — clean consolidated version

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Pymarc API compatibility gaps identified from rmarc bench_deathmatch.py

Analysis of https://github.com/matthewdeanmartin/rmarc/blob/main/bench/bench_deathmatch.py reveals 4 API differences where mrrc diverges from pymarc's public API.

### Gap 1: Record convenience accessors are methods, pymarc uses properties

In pymarc, `title`, `author`, `isbn`, `issn`, `subjects`, `notes`, and `location` are all `@property` decorated — accessed as `record.title`, not `record.title()`.

In mrrc, these are all plain methods requiring parentheses: `record.title()`.

The benchmark script has a special mrrc branch: `rec.title()` vs `rec.title` for pymarc/rmarc.

**Fix:** Convert these to `@property` in `mrrc/__init__.py`. The underlying Rust `_inner` methods stay as-is; only the Python wrapper changes. This is a breaking change for existing mrrc users calling `record.title()` — could add a deprecation shim that detects callable usage.

### Gap 2: Missing `Field.value()` method

pymarc's `Field.value()` returns all subfield values space-joined (or `data` for control fields). mrrc's Field wrapper has no `value()` method. The benchmark uses `str(field)` for mrrc vs `field.value()` for pymarc.

**Fix:** Add `def value(self) -> str` to both `Field` and `ControlField` in the Python wrapper. For `Field`, return space-joined subfield values (matching pymarc). For `ControlField`, return `self.value` (already an attribute, but the method form is what pymarc uses).

### Gap 3: Missing `Record.as_json()` method

pymarc uses `record.as_json(**kwargs)` which serializes via `json.dumps(record.as_dict(), **kwargs)`. mrrc has `record.to_json()` instead.

**Fix:** Add `as_json()` as an alias or new method on `Record`. Ideally also add `as_dict()` for full compat. Keep `to_json()` as well since it's already in our public API.

### Gap 4: Missing `parse_xml_to_array()` top-level function

pymarc exports `parse_xml_to_array(xml_file, strict=False, normalize_form=None)` as a top-level convenience function. mrrc uses `xml_to_records()` instead, with different argument conventions (takes a string, not a file path/handle).

**Fix:** Add `parse_xml_to_array()` as a top-level function in `mrrc/__init__.py` that wraps `xml_to_records()`. Accept file paths and file-like objects for full pymarc compat.

---

Elevated to GitHub issue #65: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/65

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Additional pymarc API compatibility gaps (deep comparison)

Full API surface comparison reveals more gaps beyond the 4 from the benchmark.

### Gap 5: Record.as_marc() / as_marc21() — serialize to ISO 2709 bytes
pymarc has `record.as_marc()` and `record.as_marc21()` returning `bytes`. mrrc has no equivalent for serializing a single Record back to raw MARC binary. This is critical for round-trip workflows (read, modify, write back).

### Gap 6: Record.as_dict()
pymarc has `record.as_dict()` returning a plain dict. `as_json()` is built on top of it. mrrc has neither.

### Gap 7: Record.add_field() signature mismatch
pymarc: `add_field(self, *fields)` — accepts multiple fields. mrrc: `add_field(self, field)` — accepts exactly one.

### Gap 8: Record.remove_field() signature mismatch + missing remove_fields()
pymarc: `remove_field(self, *fields)` — accepts multiple. mrrc: one at a time. pymarc also has `remove_fields(self, *tags)` for bulk removal by tag string — mrrc has no equivalent.

### Gap 9: Record.add_ordered_field() / add_grouped_field() missing
pymarc has both for inserting fields in tag-sorted or tag-grouped positions.

### Gap 10: Naming mismatches (no underscore in pymarc)
- pymarc: `record.physicaldescription` → mrrc: `record.physical_description()`
- pymarc: `record.uniformtitle` → mrrc: `record.uniform_title()`
- pymarc: `record.addedentries` → mrrc: missing entirely

### Gap 11: Record.pubyear return type
pymarc returns `Optional[str]` (e.g. "2024"). mrrc returns `Optional[int]`. This breaks code doing string operations on pubyear.

### Gap 12: Field.value() (already noted in Gap 2 above)

### Gap 13: Field.format_field() missing
pymarc `field.format_field()` returns a human-readable string. Used internally by Record property helpers like `title`. Different from `value()`.

### Gap 14: Field.add_subfield() missing pos parameter
pymarc: `add_subfield(self, code, value, pos=None)`. mrrc only supports append.

### Gap 15: Record.decode_marc() missing
pymarc has `decode_marc(self, marc, to_unicode=True, ...)` to populate a Record from raw bytes. Used by MARCReader internally but also called directly by users.

### Gap 16: Field.as_marc() / as_marc21() missing
pymarc can serialize individual fields to ISO 2709 bytes.

---

### GIL considerations for as_json() and parse_xml_to_array()

Neither needs GIL release. Both operate on data already in Python memory:
- as_json() serializes a single already-parsed Record — too fast to justify GIL overhead
- parse_xml_to_array() wraps xml_to_records() which takes a &str — no Python I/O during Rust parsing

The GIL 3-phase model is only needed for MARCReader-style iterators that interleave Python file I/O with Rust parsing across many iterations.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Correction: parse_xml_to_array() SHOULD follow MARCReader's input dispatch pattern

pymarc's parse_xml_to_array() accepts file paths, open file handles, and StringIO objects. If we implement it to match, it should follow the same input-dispatch pattern MARCReader already uses:

- **String path / pathlib.Path** → read file in Rust (RustFile backend, no GIL)
- **File-like object** → read content via Python (GIL held), then parse in Rust
- **String content** → parse directly in Rust (no GIL needed)

The XML *parsing* itself is always GIL-free (pure Rust on &str). The GIL concern is only about the file-reading step. This is exactly the MARCReader pattern from readers.rs — detect input type, use native I/O when possible, fall back to Python I/O with proper GIL management when given a Python file object.

This means parse_xml_to_array() needs a Rust-side implementation (or at minimum a Python wrapper that handles the dispatch), not just a thin alias over xml_to_records().

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Clarification: GIL avoidance is the priority

The goal is to avoid holding the GIL as much as possible, not to replicate the MARCReader pattern specifically. For parse_xml_to_array():

- **String path / pathlib.Path** → read + parse entirely in Rust, zero GIL
- **String content** → parse entirely in Rust, zero GIL
- **Python file object** → only case where GIL is unavoidable (need it to call .read())

The MARCReader's 3-phase model is a solution to a problem we may not have here. If we can handle the common cases (file paths, string content) entirely GIL-free, that's the right approach. Only add GIL management for the Python file object fallback, and even then minimize the window.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Documentation: show preferred GIL-free usage with explicit examples

When implementing parse_xml_to_array(), update docs (intro guide, API reference, examples) to show the preferred path-based approach with clear comments about why, the same way we document passing paths to MARCReader() as preferred. E.g.:

```python
# Preferred: file path lets mrrc read + parse entirely in Rust (no GIL)
records = mrrc.parse_xml_to_array("collection.xml")

# Also good: string content parsed entirely in Rust (no GIL)
records = mrrc.parse_xml_to_array(xml_string)

# Works but slower: Python file object requires GIL for .read()
with open("collection.xml") as f:
    records = mrrc.parse_xml_to_array(f)
```

This pattern should be consistent with how MARCReader path-based usage is already documented across the docs.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
## Consolidated revision: complete gap inventory (supersedes earlier comments)

Review of earlier comments found errors, duplicates, and missing gaps. This comment is the authoritative reference.

---

### Tier 1: Structural / semantic divergences (highest impact)

**A. Separate ControlField class (NEW)**
pymarc has NO ControlField class — both control and data fields are `pymarc.Field`, distinguished by `is_control_field()`. mrrc exposes a separate `ControlField` class. This breaks `isinstance(f, Field)` checks and means `get_fields()` returns mixed types in mrrc vs uniform Field in pymarc.

**B. ControlField uses `.value` attribute; pymarc uses `.data` (NEW)**
pymarc control fields store content in `.data`. mrrc's ControlField uses `.value`. Code doing `field.data` on a control field will fail.

**C. Record convenience accessors are methods, pymarc uses @property (Gap 1)**
`title`, `author`, `isbn`, `issn`, `issn_title`, `issnl`, `subjects`, `notes`, `location`, `series`, `sudoc`, `publisher`, `pubyear`, `physicaldescription`, `uniformtitle`, `addedentries` are all `@property` in pymarc. mrrc has them as methods (and some are missing — see Tier 2).

**D. Record.__getitem__ returns None for missing tags; pymarc raises KeyError (NEW)**
pymarc `record['999']` raises `KeyError`. mrrc returns `None`. This silently changes control flow for code using try/except KeyError.

**E. Field.__str__ returns useless default repr (NEW)**
pymarc `str(field)` returns MARC display format: `=245  10$aThe Great Gatsby$cF. Scott Fitzgerald`. mrrc returns `<mrrc.Field object at 0x...>` because the Python wrapper has no `__str__` and `__getattr__` doesn't intercept dunder methods. The Rust `_inner.__str__` returns `Field(245)` which is also not pymarc-compatible. Note: the rmarc benchmark uses `str(field)` for mrrc as a workaround for missing `value()`, but it doesn't actually produce useful output.

**F. Record.as_marc() / as_marc21() missing (Gap 5)**
No way to serialize a Record to ISO 2709 bytes. MARCWriter can write to files, but there's no method to get bytes from a single Record. Critical for round-trip workflows.

---

### Tier 2: Missing methods and signature mismatches

**G. Field.value() missing (Gap 2)**
pymarc `field.value()` returns space-joined subfield values (or `.data` for control fields). mrrc has nothing equivalent. NOTE: For ControlField, we can't just add a `value()` method because `.value` is already an attribute — resolve by renaming attribute to `.data` (matching pymarc) per Gap B above.

**H. Field.format_field() missing (Gap 13)**
pymarc `field.format_field()` returns human-readable text (no indicators or subfield codes). Different from `value()` only in edge cases but used internally by Record property helpers like `title`.

**I. Record.as_json(**kwargs) missing (Gap 3)**
pymarc `record.as_json(**kwargs)` wraps `json.dumps(record.as_dict(), **kwargs)`. mrrc only has `to_json()` with no kwargs support. Keep `to_json()` in our public API.

**J. Record.as_dict() missing (Gap 6)**
pymarc builds `as_json()` on `as_dict()`. Should implement both.

**K. parse_xml_to_array() missing (Gap 4)**
pymarc top-level function accepting file paths, file handles, StringIO. mrrc has `xml_to_records()` (string-only). Implementation should avoid holding the GIL: file paths and string content → entirely Rust, zero GIL. Python file objects → GIL only for `.read()`, then Rust parsing. Document preferred path-based usage with examples, consistent with MARCReader docs.

**L. add_field(*fields) signature mismatch (Gap 7)**
pymarc accepts `*fields`. mrrc accepts exactly one.

**M. remove_field(*fields) + remove_fields(*tags) (Gap 8)**
pymarc `remove_field` accepts multiple. mrrc accepts one (Field or str). pymarc also has `remove_fields(*tags)` for bulk removal by tag — mrrc has no equivalent.

**N. add_ordered_field() / add_grouped_field() missing (Gap 9)**
pymarc has both for inserting fields in tag-sorted or tag-grouped positions.

**O. Field.add_subfield() missing pos parameter (Gap 14)**
pymarc: `add_subfield(self, code, value, pos=None)`. mrrc only supports append.

**P. Field.as_marc() / as_marc21() missing (Gap 16)**
pymarc can serialize individual fields to ISO 2709 bytes.

---

### Tier 3: Naming and return type mismatches

**Q. Property name mismatches (Gap 10)**
- pymarc `physicaldescription` → mrrc `physical_description` (underscore)
- pymarc `uniformtitle` → mrrc `uniform_title` (underscore)
- pymarc `addedentries` → mrrc: missing entirely (returns 700/710/711/730 fields)

**R. pubyear return type (Gap 11)**
pymarc returns `Optional[str]` (e.g. `"2020"`). mrrc returns `Optional[int]` (`2020`). Confirmed: mrrc returns int.

---

### Tier 4: Lower-priority gaps

**S. decode_marc() missing (Gap 15)** — semi-internal, used by MARCReader. Uncommon in user code.

**T. Missing reader/writer variants** — JSONReader, JSONWriter, MARCMakerReader, XMLWriter, TextWriter.

**U. No exception hierarchy** — pymarc has ~15 specific exception classes. mrrc exposes generic errors.

**V. No MARC8 character encoding support.**

**W. Missing convenience functions** — `map_records()`, `map_xml()`, `parse_json_to_array()`, `record_to_xml_node()`.

**X. Missing constants** — `LEADER_LEN`, `DIRECTORY_ENTRY_LEN`, `END_OF_FIELD`, `END_OF_RECORD`, `SUBFIELD_INDICATOR`, etc.

---

### GIL policy (final, supersedes earlier comments)

Avoid holding the GIL whenever possible.

- `as_json()`: no GIL concern — single record, in-memory, fast.
- `parse_xml_to_array()`: file paths and string content → entirely Rust, zero GIL. Python file objects → GIL only for `.read()`, minimize window.
- `as_marc()` / `as_marc21()`: pure serialization, no GIL needed.

---

### Removed from earlier comments
- Gap 12 was a duplicate of Gap 2 — removed.

Elevated to GitHub issue #65: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/65

title: Enrich MarcError with byte-offset and record-index context
description:
## Problem

Currently `MarcError` variants in `src/error.rs` carry only a `String` message. For a MARC parser that will encounter dirty real-world data, the single biggest diagnostic weakness is that errors do not indicate *where* in the byte stream the problem occurred, *which* record it came from, or *which* field within that record.

Today:
```
InvalidRecord: directory entry length non-digit
```

Target:
```
[record 847 · 001 'ocm01234567' · field 245 · ind1] invalid ':' — expected digit or space  (byte 0x1C31 / 7217)
```

## How pymarc and MARCEdit compare

- **pymarc**: exception classes are bare markers with zero positional attributes. The `MARCReader` exposes `current_exception` and `current_chunk` as reader attributes — but no byte offset, no record index, no field info on the exception itself.
- **MARCEdit MARCValidator**: reports are organized by record number + offending field tag, typically showing the 245\$a title for human orientation. No byte offsets.

Target granularity (record + field + byte offset, plus 001 control number and source filename) goes further than either reference implementation — near-free to capture and invaluable for dev/test loops and hex-editor debugging.

## PR sequencing

This work splits across two sequential PRs:

**PR1 — Extract shared ISO 2709 primitives** (mechanical refactor, no semantic or error-API change)
- Branch: `feat/bd-qzew-extract-iso2709` (off main).
- New module \`src/iso2709.rs\` housing: \`read_leader_bytes\`, \`parse_directory_entries\`, \`extract_field_data\`, \`parse_data_field\`, \`parse_subfields\`, \`parse_4digits\`, \`parse_5digits\`. Each takes \`&[u8]\` slices and returns current \`Result<T, MarcError>\` shapes verbatim.
- Rewrite \`src/reader.rs\`, \`src/authority_reader.rs\`, \`src/holdings_reader.rs\` to call the shared primitives. Each reader's \`read_record\` retains only record-type-specific dispatch (which \`Record\`/\`AuthorityRecord\`/\`HoldingsRecord\` to instantiate, how to route fields by tag).
- \`MarcError\` enum unchanged. All existing tests pass unmodified.
- CI green = PR1 done. Merge to main.

**PR2 — Enrich errors via ParseContext** (the substantive work)
- Branch: \`feat/bd-qzew-error-enrichment\` off main, after PR1 merges.
- Everything else in this bead.
- CHANGELOG entry under \`## [Unreleased]\` heading; no version bump in this PR (project stays on 0.7.x while related error beads land).

**Note on prior assumption:** the original bead text referenced \`src/reader_helpers.rs\` from a #82 extraction. That file does not exist — there is no shared parsing layer today; each reader has its own copy of ISO 2709 parsing. PR1 creates that shared layer; PR2 enriches it.

## Design direction

**Capture everything structurally, format the default Display to lead with actionable info and park the byte offset at the end — visually subordinate, always present, no flag needed.**

Every structural error variant carries (as applicable):

- \`record_index: Option<usize>\` — 1-based record number in the stream
- \`record_control_number: Option<String>\` — 001 field value, extracted opportunistically during parse
- \`field_tag: Option<String>\` — e.g., "245", "100"
- \`indicator_position: Option<u8>\` — 0 or 1, when relevant
- \`subfield_code: Option<u8>\` — when relevant
- \`found: Option<Vec<u8>>\` — the bad bytes, **truncated to the first 32 bytes** with an ellipsis marker appended if the source was longer. Prevents errors from holding arbitrarily large payloads.
- \`expected: Option<String>\` — human-readable description of what was expected
- \`byte_offset: Option<usize>\` — absolute offset from start of stream
- \`record_byte_offset: Option<usize>\` — offset within the current record
- \`source: Option<String>\` — filename or stream identifier if available

### Position semantics per format

- **ISO 2709**: \`byte_offset\` is the absolute byte position in the stream; \`record_byte_offset\` is relative to the start of the current record. Primary case.
- **MARCXML**: \`byte_offset\` reflects the underlying \`quick_xml\` parser's position where it exposes one; \`None\` otherwise. Record-level offsets may not be meaningful for XML-structured input.
- **MARCJSON**: text byte offset in the JSON document. Record-level offsets apply per record object.

Where a format's underlying parser doesn't expose a usable position, the field stays \`None\` — don't fabricate.

### Recovery-mode interaction

Positional metadata is populated in **all** recovery modes (\`Strict\`, \`Lenient\`, \`Permissive\`), not just Strict. The information is always available during parse; modes differ only in whether the error is raised, stored as \`current_exception\`, or used to skip/salvage the record.

### 001 availability

\`record_control_number\` is \`None\` if the error occurs before 001 is parsed (invalid leader, invalid directory, pre-001 truncation). Otherwise it's populated from the decoded 001 field value. Extraction happens opportunistically — never as a separate pass.

### Error cause chains

Variants that wrap an underlying error (I/O, XML parse, JSON parse) carry it via \`thiserror\`'s \`#[source]\` attribute so \`std::error::Error::source()\` walks the chain. Standard Rust idiom.

### Thread safety

\`MarcError\` must remain \`Send + Sync\` for the existing rayon parallel parsing paths. All new fields (\`Option<usize>\`, \`Option<String>\`, \`Option<Vec<u8>>\`, \`Option<u8>\`) are already \`Send + Sync\` so this is a non-regression requirement, not new work.

## ParseContext

Lives in \`src/iso2709.rs\` alongside the primitives it powers.

\`\`\`rust
pub struct ParseContext {
    pub source: Option<String>,                   // filename / stream id
    pub record_index: usize,                      // 1-based, incremented per record
    pub stream_byte_offset: usize,                // absolute position in stream
    pub record_start_offset: usize,               // where current record began
    pub record_control_number: Option<String>,    // 001 value, set after parsed
    pub current_field_tag: Option<String>,
    pub current_subfield_code: Option<u8>,
    pub current_indicator_position: Option<u8>,
}

impl ParseContext {
    pub fn record_byte_offset(&self) -> usize {
        self.stream_byte_offset.saturating_sub(self.record_start_offset)
    }
    pub fn error_here(&self, kind: ErrorKind) -> MarcError {
        // stamps relevant context fields onto the constructed variant
    }
}
\`\`\`

In PR2, the shared primitives' signatures change from \`fn parse_4digits(&[u8]) -> Result<usize>\` to \`fn parse_4digits(&[u8], &ParseContext) -> Result<usize>\`. Error-construction sites at every call site reduce to one \`ctx.error_here(ErrorKind::...)\` invocation rather than 7-field manual construction.

## Filename plumbing

Readers currently take generic \`R: Read\` and don't know paths. To populate \`source: Option<String>\` we add both:

\`\`\`rust
impl<R: Read> MarcReader<R> {
    pub fn new(reader: R) -> Self { /* source: None */ }
    pub fn with_source(mut self, name: impl Into<String>) -> Self { /* fluent setter */ }
}

impl MarcReader<File> {
    pub fn from_path(path: impl AsRef<Path>) -> io::Result<Self> {
        let path = path.as_ref();
        Ok(Self::new(File::open(path)?).with_source(path.display().to_string()))
    }
}
\`\`\`

Mirrored on \`AuthorityMarcReader\` and \`HoldingsMarcReader\`. PyO3 layer calls \`with_source(path)\` when it has a path; \`None\` for raw bytes/file-objects.

## Rust

### MarcError variant list (PR2)

\`\`\`rust
pub enum MarcError {
    InvalidLeader { record_index, byte_offset, record_byte_offset, source, found, expected, cause: Option<String> },
    RecordLengthInvalid { record_index, byte_offset, source, found, expected },
    BaseAddressInvalid { record_index, byte_offset, source, record_control_number, found, expected },
    BaseAddressNotFound { record_index, byte_offset, source, record_control_number },
    DirectoryInvalid { record_index, byte_offset, record_byte_offset, source, record_control_number, field_tag, found, expected },
    TruncatedRecord { record_index, byte_offset, record_byte_offset, source, record_control_number, expected_length, actual_length },
    EndOfRecordNotFound { record_index, byte_offset, record_byte_offset, source, record_control_number },
    InvalidIndicator { record_index, byte_offset, record_byte_offset, source, record_control_number, field_tag, indicator_position, found, expected },
    BadSubfieldCode { record_index, byte_offset, record_byte_offset, source, record_control_number, field_tag, subfield_code: u8 },
    InvalidField { record_index, byte_offset, record_byte_offset, source, record_control_number, field_tag, message: String },
    EncodingError { record_index, byte_offset, source, record_control_number, field_tag, message: String },
    FieldNotFound { record_index, record_control_number, field_tag },  // accessor error, no offsets
    IoError { #[source] cause: io::Error, record_index, byte_offset, source },
    XmlError { #[source] cause: quick_xml::Error, record_index, byte_offset, source },
    JsonError { #[source] cause: serde_json::Error, record_index, byte_offset, source },
    WriterError { record_index, record_control_number, message: String },
}
\`\`\`

\`Display\` is hand-written (not derived via \`thiserror\`'s \`#[error(\"...\")]\` attribute) to produce the actionable-first one-liner; \`detailed()\` is a separate method on \`MarcError\` producing multi-line output.

### Rendered — default \`Display\` (actionable one-liner, byte offset at end)

\`\`\`
[record 847 · 001 'ocm01234567' · field 245 · ind1] invalid ':' — expected digit or space  (byte 0x1C31 / 7217)
\`\`\`

### Rendered — alternate detailed format (via \`{:#}\` or \`.detailed()\`)

\`\`\`
InvalidIndicator at record 847, field 245
  source:   harvest.mrc
  001:      ocm01234567
  indicator 1: found ':', expected digit or space
  byte offset: 0x1C31 (7217) in stream
  record-relative: byte 42
\`\`\`

## Per-variant field requirements

Which positional fields each variant should always populate vs may populate:

| Variant | Always | May have | Never |
|---|---|---|---|
| \`RecordLengthInvalid\` | \`record_index\`, \`byte_offset\` | \`source\` | \`field_tag\`, \`subfield_code\`, \`indicator_position\` |
| \`InvalidLeader\` | \`record_index\`, \`byte_offset\`, \`record_byte_offset\` (=0) | \`source\` | \`field_tag\`, \`subfield_code\`, \`indicator_position\` |
| \`BaseAddressInvalid\` / \`BaseAddressNotFound\` | \`record_index\`, \`byte_offset\` | \`record_control_number\`, \`source\` | \`field_tag\`, \`subfield_code\`, \`indicator_position\` |
| \`DirectoryInvalid\` | \`record_index\`, \`byte_offset\`, \`record_byte_offset\` | \`field_tag\` (entry being parsed), \`record_control_number\`, \`source\` | \`subfield_code\`, \`indicator_position\` |
| \`TruncatedRecord\` / \`EndOfRecordNotFound\` | \`record_index\`, \`byte_offset\`, \`record_byte_offset\` | \`record_control_number\`, \`source\` | \`field_tag\`, \`indicator_position\`, \`subfield_code\` |
| \`InvalidIndicator\` | \`record_index\`, \`field_tag\`, \`indicator_position\`, \`found\`, \`expected\`, \`byte_offset\`, \`record_byte_offset\` | \`record_control_number\`, \`source\` | \`subfield_code\` |
| \`BadSubfieldCode\` | \`record_index\`, \`field_tag\`, \`subfield_code\`, \`byte_offset\`, \`record_byte_offset\` | \`record_control_number\`, \`source\` | \`indicator_position\` |
| \`InvalidField\` | \`record_index\`, \`field_tag\`, \`byte_offset\`, \`record_byte_offset\` | \`record_control_number\`, \`source\` | \`indicator_position\`, \`subfield_code\` |
| \`EncodingError\` | \`record_index\` | \`field_tag\`, \`byte_offset\`, \`source\`, \`record_control_number\` | \`indicator_position\`, \`subfield_code\` |
| \`FieldNotFound\` (accessor) | \`field_tag\` | \`record_control_number\`, \`record_index\` | \`byte_offset\` (not a parse error) |
| \`IoError\` | \`#[source]\` cause | \`byte_offset\`, \`record_index\`, \`source\` | others, depending on when I/O failed |
| \`XmlError\` / \`JsonError\` | \`#[source]\` cause, \`byte_offset\` | \`record_index\`, \`source\` | \`field_tag\`, \`subfield_code\`, \`indicator_position\` |
| \`WriterError\` | (writer context) | \`record_index\`, \`record_control_number\` | parse-only fields |

Accessor errors like \`FieldNotFound\` are structurally different from parse errors and don't carry byte offsets — expected.

## Python

### Exception hierarchy

The Python exception class set is a strict superset of pymarc: every pymarc class name exists with the same parent (\`MrrcException\` plays the role of \`PymarcException\`); new mrrc-specific classes are subclasses of the closest pymarc parent so existing pymarc-style \`except\` clauses keep catching the same conditions.

\`\`\`
Exception
├── MrrcException                               (base, pymarc.PymarcException analog)
│   ├── RecordLengthInvalid                     ← MarcError::RecordLengthInvalid
│   ├── RecordLeaderInvalid                     ← MarcError::InvalidLeader
│   ├── BaseAddressInvalid                      ← MarcError::BaseAddressInvalid
│   ├── BaseAddressNotFound                     ← MarcError::BaseAddressNotFound
│   ├── RecordDirectoryInvalid                  ← MarcError::DirectoryInvalid
│   │   ├── InvalidIndicator      (NEW)         ← MarcError::InvalidIndicator
│   │   ├── BadSubfieldCode       (NEW)         ← MarcError::BadSubfieldCode
│   │   └── InvalidField          (NEW)         ← MarcError::InvalidField
│   ├── EndOfRecordNotFound                     ← MarcError::EndOfRecordNotFound
│   │   └── TruncatedRecord       (NEW)         ← MarcError::TruncatedRecord
│   ├── FieldNotFound                           ← MarcError::FieldNotFound
│   ├── FatalReaderError                        (no direct Rust variant; reserved)
│   ├── EncodingError             (NEW)         ← MarcError::EncodingError
│   ├── XmlError                  (NEW)         ← MarcError::XmlError
│   ├── JsonError                 (NEW)         ← MarcError::JsonError
│   └── WriterError               (NEW)         ← MarcError::WriterError
└── OSError
    └── PyIOError                               ← MarcError::IoError (Python built-in, unchanged)

class BadSubfieldCodeWarning(UserWarning)        (existing pymarc warning, unchanged)
\`\`\`

Properties:
- pymarc's exception class set is fully present and unchanged in catch semantics
- \`except RecordDirectoryInvalid:\` catches \`InvalidIndicator | BadSubfieldCode | InvalidField | RecordDirectoryInvalid\`
- \`except EndOfRecordNotFound:\` catches \`TruncatedRecord | EndOfRecordNotFound\`
- \`except MrrcException:\` catches everything mrrc-specific
- \`except InvalidIndicator:\` works only when caller opts into mrrc-specific granularity

### Python typed-exception wiring (finding from exploration)

Currently \`src-python/src/error.rs::marc_error_to_py_err\` raises bare \`PyValueError\`/\`PyIOError\` regardless of variant. The typed Python classes in \`mrrc/__init__.py\` exist but are not raised on the read path. PR2 rewires this to construct the typed Python exception class with positional fields passed as kwargs:

\`\`\`rust
pub fn marc_error_to_py_err(py: Python, err: MarcError) -> PyErr {
    let mrrc = py.import("mrrc").ok();
    match err {
        MarcError::InvalidLeader { record_index, byte_offset, source, found, expected, .. } => {
            // Construct mrrc.RecordLeaderInvalid(record_index=..., byte_offset=..., ...)
            // Fall through to PyValueError::new_err(err.to_string()) if construction fails (safety net)
        },
        // ... per variant
    }
}
\`\`\`

Python exception classes in \`mrrc/__init__.py\` updated per the bead's \`InvalidIndicator\` sketch — kwarg-only positional attrs, \`*args\` passthrough so \`raise RecordLeaderInvalid()\` still works (pymarc compat). \`mrrc/_mrrc.pyi\` updated with the typed exception attributes.

### Sketch (shared structure across all exception classes)

\`\`\`python
class InvalidIndicator(RecordDirectoryInvalid):
    def __init__(
        self,
        *args,
        record_index=None,
        record_control_number=None,
        field_tag=None,
        indicator_position=None,
        found=None,
        expected=None,
        byte_offset=None,
        record_byte_offset=None,
        source=None,
    ) -> None:
        self.record_index = record_index
        self.record_control_number = record_control_number
        self.field_tag = field_tag
        self.indicator_position = indicator_position
        self.found = found
        self.expected = expected
        self.byte_offset = byte_offset
        self.record_byte_offset = record_byte_offset
        self.source = source
        super().__init__(*(args or (self._format(),)))
\`\`\`

All kwargs optional → \`except InvalidIndicator: pass\` and \`raise InvalidIndicator()\` still work.

### Rendered — default \`print(err)\` / \`str(err)\`

\`\`\`
[record 847 · 001 'ocm01234567' · field 245 · ind1] invalid b':' — expected digit or space  (byte 0x1C31 / 7217)
\`\`\`

### Rendered — \`repr(err)\`

\`\`\`
InvalidIndicator(record_index=847, record_control_number='ocm01234567', field_tag='245', indicator_position=0, found=b':', expected='digit or space', byte_offset=7217, record_byte_offset=42, source='harvest.mrc')
\`\`\`

### Rendered — \`err.detailed()\`

\`\`\`
InvalidIndicator at record 847, field 245
  source:   harvest.mrc
  001:      ocm01234567
  indicator 1: found b':', expected digit or space
  byte offset: 0x1C31 (7217) in stream
  record-relative: byte 42
\`\`\`

## ParseError unification

\`src-python/src/parse_error.rs\` currently defines a separate \`ParseError\` enum (3 string-message variants) used in GIL-released boundary scanner / buffered reader paths, with its own \`to_py_err\`. PR2 refactors:

- Replace \`ParseError\` with a thin wrapper that holds a \`MarcError\` directly. \`MarcError\` is already \`Send + Sync\` (no Py refs) so it's safe in \`allow_threads\`.
- Delete \`ParseError\`'s string-message variants.
- \`to_py_err\` becomes \`marc_error_to_py_err(py, self.0)\` — same typed-exception path as the main reader.
- Boundary scanner and buffered reader call sites updated to construct full \`MarcError\` variants instead of string-message \`ParseError\` variants. They populate what they have at scan time: \`record_index\`, \`byte_offset\`, \`source\`. Field-level metadata not available at this layer; stays \`None\`.

Net effect: one error type across the whole reader stack, typed Python exceptions reach all Python callers (including the boundary-scanner / buffered-reader paths).

## Avoiding repetition across record types

The shared parsing layer does **not** exist today. PR1 creates it (extraction phase). PR2 then evolves the shared primitives to take \`&mut ParseContext\` so error construction in the shared layer automatically inherits positional metadata. This means PR2's enrichment work happens in one place, not three.

The three readers (\`src/reader.rs\`, \`src/authority_reader.rs\`, \`src/holdings_reader.rs\`) differ only in which \`Record\`/\`AuthorityRecord\`/\`HoldingsRecord\` type they instantiate and how they dispatch fields by tag. After PR1, parsing primitives and error-construction code are shared. After PR2, all three emit errors with identical positional shape.

## What's in scope

PR2 in scope:
- \`MarcError\` restructure per the variant list above
- \`ParseContext\` introduction in \`src/iso2709.rs\`
- All three ISO 2709 readers (bib + authority + holdings) threading positional metadata
- MARCXML parse path enrichment (position from \`quick_xml\` where exposed; \`None\` otherwise)
- MARCJSON parse path enrichment (text byte offset from JSON parser)
- Writer-path error enrichment in \`src/writer.rs\` (3 sites — record validation, length overflow, base-address overflow)
- Python typed-exception raising in \`src-python/src/error.rs\` (replacing bare \`PyValueError\` mapping)
- Python exception class hierarchy update (subclass new types from pymarc parents)
- \`mrrc/_mrrc.pyi\` stub update
- \`src-python/src/parse_error.rs\` unification with \`MarcError\`
- 32-byte truncation helper for \`found\`
- \`#[source]\` chains for I/O / XML / JSON wrapping variants
- Snapshot-tested formatting (Rust: \`insta\`; Python: \`syrupy\`)
- Recovery-mode coverage for all variants (Strict/Lenient/Permissive)
- Documentation: new \`docs/reference/error-handling.md\` page (see Documentation section below)
- CHANGELOG entry under \`## [Unreleased]\` heading; **no version bump** (project remains on 0.7.x)

## What's out of scope (follow-up beads)

- **Stable error codes with help URLs and reference docs page** — bd-44wb (GH #95), depends on this bead.
- **Structured serialization (\`to_dict()\` / \`to_json()\`) and hex-dump diagnostics** — bd-kodg, depends on this bead.
- MARCXML / MARCJSON / MODS / BIBFRAME *writer-side* emit-path enrichment beyond the natural mappings already covered — file separately if needed.

## Testing strategy (PR2)

**Snapshot test deps (new):**
- \`insta = \"1\"\` in Rust \`[dev-dependencies]\`
- \`syrupy\` in Python dev deps via \`pyproject.toml\`
- \`.cargo/check.sh\` runs both as part of normal test invocation; CI fails on snapshot drift

**Rust tests (\`src/iso2709.rs\` + \`src/error.rs\`):**
- One test per \`MarcError\` variant verifying populated-field set matches the per-variant requirements table
- Snapshot tests for \`Display\` (one-liner) and \`detailed()\` (multi-line) for representative variants
- \`found\` byte-truncation test (input >32 bytes → truncated + ellipsis marker)
- \`error.source()\` walks the chain on \`IoError\`/\`XmlError\`/\`JsonError\`

**Per-reader integration tests** (parameterized across \`MarcReader\`, \`AuthorityMarcReader\`, \`HoldingsMarcReader\` to prove the shared abstraction):
- Truncated record → \`TruncatedRecord\` with correct \`byte_offset\` + \`record_index\`
- Invalid directory entry → \`DirectoryInvalid\` with stream offset, record-relative offset, record_index
- Invalid leader → \`InvalidLeader\` with offset 0 within record, correct record_index, \`record_control_number: None\`
- Invalid indicator → \`InvalidIndicator\` with all positional fields including 001 when available
- \`from_path\` populates \`source\`; \`new(bytes)\` leaves it \`None\`
- Errors carry metadata in all three recovery modes (Strict/Lenient/Permissive)

**Python tests (\`tests/python/test_errors.py\`):**
- FFI integration: each \`MarcError\` variant → corresponding Python typed exception with all expected attributes populated (no silent drops in PyO3 conversion)
- Pickle round-trip: \`pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(err))\` preserves all positional attributes
- Hierarchy assertions: \`assert issubclass(InvalidIndicator, RecordDirectoryInvalid)\`, etc., for every NEW class
- Backward-compat: \`except RecordDirectoryInvalid:\` catches the new subclasses
- Bare-constructor compat: \`RecordLeaderInvalid()\` instantiates; \`pickle\` round-trips a bare instance
- Snapshot tests for \`str(err)\`, \`repr(err)\`, \`err.detailed()\` matching Rust equivalents

## Documentation

**New page: \`docs/reference/error-handling.md\`** (added to \`mkdocs.yml\` nav under Reference). Sections in this order:

1. **Exception hierarchy** — the diagram from the Python section, with one-line "use this when" for each class.
2. **Choosing what to catch** — practical guide:
   - "I want pymarc-style catches" → catch the pymarc-named classes; everything new is a subclass.
   - "I want indicator-level granularity" → catch \`InvalidIndicator\` directly.
   - "I want everything mrrc raises" → \`except MrrcException:\`.
3. **Pymarc exception compatibility** — *scoped tightly to exception classes only*:
   - "What this page covers" — exception class names, hierarchy, and catch behavior match pymarc's. New positional attributes are additive. **Other compatibility surfaces — record APIs, reader APIs, format coverage — are out of scope for this page.**
   - Exception name mapping table (pymarc → mrrc).
   - Optional aliases snippet for symbol-level compat (with explicit note that code outside the exception layer may still need changes).
   - Three before/after examples scoped to catch sites: same-catch-more-context, opt-in-granularity, diagnostic dump.
   - "Out of scope for this guide" subsection enumerating what the page does NOT claim compat for (reader/writer constructors, \`Record\`/\`Field\`/\`Subfield\` APIs, format edge cases, perf/memory).
   - Subclass behavior reference table.
   - \`MARCReader.current_exception\` / \`current_chunk\` — document mrrc's current state honestly; if not implemented, mark "not currently exposed; track in [follow-up bead]".
4. **Per-variant field reference** — the per-variant requirements table from the Rust section, rendered clean. For each variant: which positional fields are always present, may be present, never present, and a worked example with \`Display\` + \`detailed()\` output.
5. **Position semantics by format** — ISO 2709 / MARCXML / MARCJSON, what \`byte_offset\` and \`record_byte_offset\` mean (or why they're \`None\`).
6. **Source filename plumbing** — \`from_path\` vs \`new\` + \`with_source\`, when \`source\` is populated.
7. **Recovery modes × errors** — clarifying that positional metadata is populated in all three modes.

**Inline doc updates:**
- \`MarcError\` rustdoc gets a section on the field-requirements table with a link to the docs page.
- Each Python exception class docstring gets parent-class info + which Rust variant it corresponds to.

## What to do

**PR1:**

1. Create \`src/iso2709.rs\` with shared parsing primitives (\`read_leader_bytes\`, \`parse_directory_entries\`, \`extract_field_data\`, \`parse_data_field\`, \`parse_subfields\`, \`parse_4digits\`, \`parse_5digits\`).
2. Rewrite \`src/reader.rs\`, \`src/authority_reader.rs\`, \`src/holdings_reader.rs\` to call shared primitives. Keep record-type-specific dispatch local to each reader.
3. Verify all existing tests pass; \`MarcError\` enum and signatures unchanged.
4. \`.cargo/check.sh\` clean; push; verify CI; merge.

**PR2:**

1. Restructure \`MarcError\` variants per the variant list and per-variant requirements table.
2. Introduce \`ParseContext\` in \`src/iso2709.rs\`. Provide \`ctx.error_here(...)\` helper.
3. Update shared primitives to take \`&mut ParseContext\`.
4. Wire \`ParseContext\` into all three readers — errors emerge with identical positional shape.
5. Extend position-tracking into \`src/marcxml.rs\`, \`src/marcjson.rs\` where the underlying parser exposes useful position data; \`None\` where it doesn't.
6. Extract 001 control field value opportunistically during parse; attach to errors raised after 001 is available.
7. Add \`with_source\` and \`from_path\` constructors to all three readers; thread filename via \`ParseContext.source\`.
8. Bound \`found: Vec<u8>\` to 32 bytes at construction via a single \`truncate_bytes(input: &[u8]) -> (Vec<u8>, bool)\` helper (append ellipsis marker if truncated).
9. Preserve underlying causes via \`#[source]\` on wrapping variants (I/O, XML, JSON).
10. Hand-write \`Display\` (actionable-first one-liner) and \`detailed()\` (multi-line) impls — not derived.
11. Update \`mrrc/__init__.py\` exception classes with positional kwargs and the subclass hierarchy; preserve bare-constructor compatibility. Implement matching Python \`_format()\` + \`detailed()\`.
12. Rewrite \`src-python/src/error.rs::marc_error_to_py_err\` to construct typed Python exception classes with positional kwargs (replacing bare \`PyValueError\`/\`PyIOError\` mapping).
13. Refactor \`src-python/src/parse_error.rs\` to wrap \`MarcError\`; update boundary-scanner / buffered-reader call sites.
14. Update \`mrrc/_mrrc.pyi\` type stubs.
15. Ensure enrichment applies in all recovery modes (Strict/Lenient/Permissive).
16. Enrich writer-path errors in \`src/writer.rs\` (3 sites).
17. Add \`insta\` (Rust) and \`syrupy\` (Python) dev deps; write snapshot tests.
18. Write \`docs/reference/error-handling.md\` per the Documentation section above; add to \`mkdocs.yml\`.
19. CHANGELOG entry under \`## [Unreleased]\` heading. **No version bump.**

## Done when

**PR1:**
- New module \`src/iso2709.rs\` exists with the listed primitives.
- All three readers call the shared primitives; record-type-specific code remains local.
- All existing tests pass unmodified; \`MarcError\` enum unchanged.
- \`.cargo/check.sh\` passes on all platforms.
- Merged to main.

**PR2:**
- \`MarcError\` variants carry the positional metadata per the field-requirements table.
- \`ParseContext\` exists in \`src/iso2709.rs\` and is used by all three readers and the MARCXML/MARCJSON parse paths.
- Default \`Display\` produces the actionable one-liner with byte offset visually subordinate; \`detailed()\` produces the multi-line diagnostic.
- \`#[source]\` chains present on wrapping variants; \`err.source()\` walks correctly.
- \`found\` byte-length truncation (32 bytes + ellipsis) enforced at construction.
- Snapshot-tested Rust formatting using \`insta\`; snapshot-tested Python formatting using \`syrupy\`.
- Unit tests verify the conditions enumerated in the Testing strategy section above.
- Authority reader + Holdings reader errors carry the same positional metadata as Bibliographic (parameterized tests prove the shared abstraction).
- Errors carry metadata in all three recovery modes.
- Python exceptions expose all positional fields, raised as typed classes (not bare \`PyValueError\`).
- FFI integration test: parse error on Rust side propagates to Python typed exception with all attributes populated.
- Pickle round-trip preserves all positional attributes.
- Hierarchy assertions pass: \`issubclass(InvalidIndicator, RecordDirectoryInvalid)\`, etc.
- Existing pymarc-shaped \`except\` clauses still catch what they used to (verified by tests).
- Bare-constructor compatibility preserved: \`RecordLeaderInvalid()\` instantiates cleanly.
- \`mrrc/_mrrc.pyi\` stubs updated.
- \`src-python/src/parse_error.rs\` unified with \`MarcError\`; boundary-scanner / buffered-reader paths raise typed Python exceptions.
- CHANGELOG entry under \`## [Unreleased]\` (no version bump).
- \`docs/reference/error-handling.md\` written per the Documentation section, added to \`mkdocs.yml\`.
- \`.cargo/check.sh\` passes on all platforms.
notes:
## Self-review addenda (2026-04-19)

Refinements surfaced during spec self-review and post-PR1 planning. Apply during PR2 implementation.

### 1. Pickle round-trip requires explicit `__reduce__`

The Python exception sketch in the main description uses kwarg-only `__init__` and stashes positional attributes via `self.foo = bar`. By default, `pickle` for `Exception` subclasses round-trips only the `args` tuple — instance `__dict__` attributes are lost. Without an explicit reducer, `pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(InvalidIndicator(record_index=847, ...)))` would return an exception with all kwargs reset to `None`, breaking the pickle round-trip test listed in the testing section.

Each exception class needs `__reduce__` and `__setstate__` (or equivalent). Pattern:

\`\`\`python
def __reduce__(self):
    return (self.__class__, (), self._pickle_state())

def __setstate__(self, state):
    for k, v in state.items():
        setattr(self, k, v)

def _pickle_state(self):
    return {
        "record_index": self.record_index,
        "record_control_number": self.record_control_number,
        "field_tag": self.field_tag,
        "indicator_position": self.indicator_position,
        "found": self.found,
        "expected": self.expected,
        "byte_offset": self.byte_offset,
        "record_byte_offset": self.record_byte_offset,
        "source": self.source,
    }
\`\`\`

Factor into a shared mixin or base class so each subclass doesn't repeat the boilerplate. Field set per subclass should match the kwargs that subclass actually accepts (e.g., \`FieldNotFound\` doesn't have \`byte_offset\`).

### 2. \`_format()\` definition

The Python sketch references \`self._format()\` in the \`super().__init__\` fallback but doesn't define it. \`_format()\` is the Python equivalent of Rust's \`Display\` one-liner — produces the actionable-first format shown in the "Rendered — default \`print(err)\` / \`str(err)\`" example:

\`\`\`
[record 847 · 001 'ocm01234567' · field 245 · ind1] invalid b':' — expected digit or space  (byte 0x1C31 / 7217)
\`\`\`

Implement once on \`MrrcException\` base (consulting \`self.__dict__\` for whichever positional attrs are present), let subclasses override only if their format differs. The Python \`_format()\` and \`detailed()\` outputs must match Rust's \`Display\` and \`detailed()\` byte-for-byte (verified by snapshot tests).

### 3. PR1 deliverable scoping

PR1 (mechanical extraction) deliberately deferred two of the bead's listed primitives — \`parse_data_field\` and \`parse_subfields\` — to PR2. The three readers have subtly different subfield-parsing semantics (lossy vs strict UTF-8, error vs skip on unrecognized bytes) that cannot be unified under PR1's "no semantic change" constraint. PR2's \`ParseContext\` introduces a per-format parameterization that lets these primitives finally be extracted cleanly.

PR2 implementation plan now lives in section 4 below; no separate plan document is written.

### 4. PR2 implementation ordering and quality gates

Eight commits in dependency order. Each commit fully passes \`.cargo/check.sh\` before the next begins. Four explicit quality gates (G1–G4) anchor architectural and security review.

The "G" labels and "Commit N" numbering are project-tracking shorthand for this bead — they belong here, not in commit messages, source comments, tests, or user-facing docs. Commit messages use the feature-descriptive titles given below.

---

**Commit 1** — \`refactor(error): restructure MarcError variants with positional context fields\`

- Restructure \`MarcError\` variants per the per-variant requirements table
- Hand-write \`Display\` (one-liner) and \`detailed()\` (multi-line)
- Add \`truncate_bytes\` helper for the 32-byte \`found\` cap
- \`#[source]\` chains on wrapping variants (I/O, XML, JSON)
- Update all existing call sites to construct the new variant shape — most pass \`None\` for new positional fields at this stage
- All existing tests pass

**>>> Gate G1 — Architect review of error type design**

Run \`comprehensive-review:architect-review\` on the new variant shape and \`Display\`/\`detailed()\` impls before any reader depends on the design. Highest-leverage gate: variant design is the most expensive thing to change later, and reviewing it before downstream commits depend on it prevents costly rework.

---

**Commit 2** — \`feat(iso2709): introduce ParseContext and per-context error helper\`

- \`ParseContext\` struct + \`ctx.error_here(...)\` helper in \`src/iso2709.rs\`
- Update existing \`iso2709\` primitives to take \`&mut ParseContext\`
- Add the deferred primitives \`parse_data_field\` and \`parse_subfields\` (now safe because \`ParseContext\` parameterizes the per-format decoding policy)

---

**Commit 3** — \`feat(readers): wire ParseContext through bibliographic/authority/holdings readers\`

- Wire \`ParseContext\` into all three ISO 2709 readers; errors emerge with identical positional shape
- Opportunistic 001 extraction (attached to \`ctx.record_control_number\`)
- Add \`with_source\` and \`from_path\` constructors to all three readers
- Verify enrichment in all three recovery modes (Strict / Lenient / Permissive)

---

**Commit 4** — \`feat(formats): enrich MARCXML and MARCJSON parse-path errors\`

- MARCXML parse-path enrichment: position from \`quick_xml\` where exposed; \`None\` otherwise
- MARCJSON parse-path enrichment: \`serde_json::Error::line()\`/\`column()\` translated to byte offset

---

**Commit 5** — \`feat(writer): enrich writer-path errors with record context\`

- \`src/writer.rs\` 3 sites: record validation, length overflow, base-address overflow
- Populate \`record_index\` + \`record_control_number\` where natural

---

**Commit 6** — \`feat(python): typed exception hierarchy with positional attributes\`

- \`mrrc/__init__.py\` exception classes — kwargs, subclass hierarchy, \`__reduce__\`/\`__setstate__\`/\`_format()\` per addenda 1 + 2 above
- Rewrite \`src-python/src/error.rs::marc_error_to_py_err\` to construct typed exceptions with kwargs (replacing bare \`PyValueError\`/\`PyIOError\`)
- \`mrrc/_mrrc.pyi\` stub update
- Refactor \`src-python/src/parse_error.rs\` to wrap \`MarcError\`; boundary-scanner / buffered-reader call sites raise typed exceptions

**>>> Gate G2 — Security audit of Python wiring**

Run \`comprehensive-review:security-auditor\` focused on the PyO3 typed-exception construction + ParseError unification. These paths construct Python exception classes with attacker-influenceable bytes (the \`found\` field carries raw bytes from untrusted parse input), and the boundary-scanner runs in GIL-released mode with subtle thread-safety implications.

---

**Commit 7** — \`test: snapshot-test error formatting (Rust + Python)\`

- Add \`insta\` (Rust dev-deps) + \`syrupy\` (Python dev-deps)
- Write Rust snapshot tests in \`src/iso2709.rs\` and \`src/error.rs\`
- Write Python snapshot tests in \`tests/python/test_errors.py\`
- Capture baselines via \`cargo insta accept\` / \`pytest --snapshot-update\`; commit baselines

**>>> Gate G3 — Snapshot baseline review**

Personally eyeball every Rust + Python snapshot baseline before accepting. These are externally-visible error formats and worth a careful pass. \`cargo insta review\` provides the interactive workflow for Rust; \`.ambr\` files (or syrupy's review CLI) for Python.

---

**Commit 8** — \`docs: error handling reference page; CHANGELOG entry\`

- Write \`docs/reference/error-handling.md\` per the Documentation section in the main description
- Add to \`mkdocs.yml\` nav
- CHANGELOG entry under \`## [Unreleased]\` heading (no version bump)

**>>> Gate G4 — Pre-merge comprehensive review**

Before opening the PR2 PR, run \`comprehensive-review:full-review\` which orchestrates architecture + security + performance + testing + best-practices reviews in parallel. One-shot final check.

---

Parallelism notes: commits 4 and 5 only depend on commit 1 (the new \`MarcError\` shape) and could in principle run parallel to commit 3, but the canonical pattern is best established in the binary readers first. Commit 6 only depends on commit 1 for the variant shape and could run parallel to 3/4/5; it's placed after them to keep the Rust side fully consistent before the Python wiring lands.
close_reason: Delivered in PR #100 (merged 2026-04-19). 19 commits enriching MarcError with structured positional metadata, ParseContext threading, MARCXML/MARCJSON/writer enrichment, typed Python exception hierarchy with pickle support, snapshot tests (Rust insta + Python syrupy), and a docs/reference/error-handling.md page. Followups bd-44wb (P1, error codes + URLs), bd-kodg (P1, structured serialization + hex dump), and bd-uj7c (P2, per-stream error cap) target the same 0.8.0 release window.

title: Fix mrrc.__version__ — hardcoded at 0.1.0, not updated during releases
description:
mrrc.__version__ reports '0.1.0' regardless of actual installed version. The value is hardcoded in two places:

- mrrc/__init__.py:54 — __version__ = "0.1.0"
- src-python/src/lib.rs:89 — m.add("__version__", "0.1.0")?;

Neither is updated during the release procedure (docs/contributing/release-procedure.md).

Rather than adding two more manual version-bump locations, consider deriving the version automatically:
- Rust side: use env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION") in lib.rs to read from Cargo.toml at compile time
- Python side: read from _mrrc.__version__ (set by Rust) instead of hardcoding

This would eliminate the need to manually update __version__ during releases. If we go this route, the release procedure doc doesn't need a new step. If we keep it manual, add it to section 3 (Update Configuration Files) of release-procedure.md.

Reported in https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/57 comment.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #60, merged

title: Expand E404 manifest coverage to non-size WriterError triggers
description:
## Context

`tests/error_coverage.toml` follows a per-trigger coverage convention: codes whose production paths fire from multiple distinct conditions have a manifest case per condition. E101 has three cases (`no_field_terminator`, `non_digit_length`, `non_ascii_tag`) for its three trigger paths; E106 has multiple; the leader split work (bd-0x73.2) gave E001/E003/E004 each their own case.

**E404 `WriterError` is the standout exception.** It has one manifest case (`e404_record_too_large_for_iso2709`) but three production trigger paths:

1. `crate::iso2709::check_iso2709_size` — record length or base address > 99999. **Covered** by the existing manifest case.
2. `crate::iso2709::validate_directory_tag` — field tag is not 3 ASCII bytes. **Not in the manifest.** Fires from all three writers (bibliographic, authority, holdings) on `record.add_field(Field { tag: "12".to_string(), ... })`-style records, or on records whose 001 control-field tag was mutated to a 4-byte string after parse.
3. `MarcWriter::write_record` line 139 — writing to a writer whose `finish()` already ran. **Not in the manifest.** Fires when downstream code re-invokes a finished writer.

Surfaced reviewing PR #193 (bd-mk6z) — the harness now wires `writer` as a `trigger_kind`, but the harness's writer branch only exercises the size-cap path. The other two E404 trigger conditions are real production paths with no manifest assertion.

## Audit suggestion

While here, sweep every `MarcError` variant for per-trigger coverage parity:

- `grep -n "MarcError::<Variant> {" src/` for each variant.
- Cross-reference against case count for the matching code in `tests/error_coverage.toml`.
- File-as-skipped (`wired = false`) any trigger condition without a manifest case, so the harness reports the gap on every run instead of leaving it silent.

Spot-check candidates: any other variant constructed from multiple files or multiple line ranges.

This sweep's output is what bd-0x73.12's release-readiness gate re-asserts in step 2 (Coverage harness assertion). Construction lives here; re-assertion lives there.

## What needs to change for E404 specifically

1. **Add a case for non-3-ASCII tag from the writer path.** Description: "Construct a record with a field whose tag is not 3 ASCII bytes and attempt to serialize it." `trigger_kind = "writer"` with a per-case branch in `tests/error_coverage.rs`'s writer dispatch (or the dispatch grows a small enum mirroring the accessor case-id branch pattern).
2. **Add a case for finished-writer reuse.** Description: "Call `MarcWriter::finish()`, then attempt another `write_record`." Same dispatch shape.
3. **Mirror on the Python side** if/when applicable — non-3-ASCII tag is reachable from Python; finished-writer reuse may or may not be depending on `mrrc.MARCWriter` lifecycle exposure.

## Done when

- Each distinct E404-firing condition in the production code has a manifest case.
- The harness's writer dispatch handles the additional cases without losing the existing size-cap assertion.
- Audit notes captured in a comment in the manifest (or in `docs/contributing/`) so the per-trigger convention is explicit for future contributors.

## Why P3

The first trigger (size cap) is the one users hit in practice — record-size overflow is the canonical E404 scenario named in `docs/reference/error-codes.md#E404`. The other two are less-traveled paths but still real production code; closing the verification gap is hygiene, not a behavior fix.

## References

- `tests/error_coverage.toml` — current manifest, see `e404_record_too_large_for_iso2709`.
- `src/iso2709.rs::validate_directory_tag` — non-3-ASCII tag fires E404 WriterError.
- `src/iso2709.rs::check_iso2709_size` — size cap fires E404 WriterError.
- `src/writer.rs:138-144` — finished-writer reuse fires E404 WriterError.
- `docs/reference/error-codes.md#E404` — current docs describe E404 in general but cite only the size scenario as the example.
- bd-0x73.12 — release-readiness gate that re-asserts this audit's output at tagging time.

title: Guard the error_classification fuzz target's code list against manifest drift
description:
Surfaced by the v0.8.1 milestone-wide quality review (9lui) reviewing PR #178.

`fuzz/fuzz_targets/error_classification.rs:47` defines `DOCUMENTED_CODES` — a hand-copied duplicate of the distinct error codes in `tests/error_coverage.toml`. It matches today (all 17), but nothing guards it: a new code added to the manifest will not update this list, silently weakening the fuzz target's classification assertion. The fuzz crate is an isolated workspace (nightly toolchain) so it cannot trivially read the manifest TOML at build time.

Approach: add a small unit test in the root crate (which CAN read `tests/error_coverage.toml`) asserting that every distinct `code` in the manifest appears in the fuzz target's `DOCUMENTED_CODES` list (and optionally vice-versa). The test fails when the two drift, pointing the contributor at the fuzz file to update. Alternatively, generate the list from the manifest at build time if the workspace isolation allows.

Done when: a CI-run check fails if the manifest's distinct code set and the fuzz target's `DOCUMENTED_CODES` diverge.

Not v0.8.1-blocking; unmilestoned backlog. Related to the error-coverage manifest-as-spec discipline.
close_reason: Done in #255: root-crate test fuzz_documented_codes_match_manifest in tests/error_coverage.rs cross-checks error_coverage.toml against the fuzz target source, failing on divergence either direction.

title: Fuzzing coverage: variant-targeted decision + parser fuzz target backfill
description:
Umbrella for fuzz-target additions across the parser surface. The fuzz workspace lives in fuzz/ (nightly toolchain, isolated from root); each new target is one small file plus a corpus directory. bd-bfrh is the gating decision that umbrellas the rest - if we decide 'yes' on variant-targeted fuzzing, the four implementation beads below execute against that contract.

## Order of operations

### Tier A - Decide first

- bd-bfrh (p2) - decide whether variant-targeted fuzzing is needed. Outcome shapes the corpus seeding and assertion strategy for the four target beads below; close as moot or proceed.

### Tier B - Implementation (any order, after bd-bfrh)

- bd-gbgx (p3) - parse_leader fuzz target
- bd-uss1 (p3) - parse_json fuzz target
- bd-2dia (p3) - decode_marc8 fuzz target
- bd-3t62 (p3) - parse_marcxml fuzz target

## Done when

All four parser fuzz targets land and run nightly without crashes for 24h+. Targets discovered to be redundant (e.g., already-equivalent coverage from the existing roundtrip target) close as moot.
close_reason: All format-surface fuzz targets landed (parse_json, parse_marcjson, parse_marcxml, parse_mods, decode_marc8) and all child beads closed (bd-bfrh/bd-gbgx/bd-uss1/bd-2dia/bd-3t62/bd-2wgo/bd-rk8f). Done-when met: nightly Fuzz workflow green for 12 consecutive runs (2026-05-28..06-08), each running all 9 targets via matrix; latest run's five format-target jobs all succeeded. Soak well past the 24h+ no-crash bar.

Decision outcomes for this epic: Tier A — bd-bfrh decided, variant-targeted fuzzing (Option C) ruled moot. Tier B — bd-gbgx (parse_leader) closed moot (redundant with parse_record); the blocks-edges on the remaining three were detached (they are Option B format coverage, independent of the Option C decision). The epic therefore reduces to three format-surface targets: bd-uss1 (parse_json), bd-2dia (decode_marc8), bd-3t62 (parse_marcxml). Out of scope for this epic: parser fuzz targets for CSV / Dublin Core / MODS / BIBFRAME read paths; the CSV/Dublin Core read-path question is tracked separately on bd-i7j6.

title: Document formal methods (property tests, fuzzing) in public docs
description:
## Goal

Add a docs section explaining the formal-methods / property-based testing approach mrrc uses, pitched at readers new to the concept. The reader should leave with:

1. A clear mental model of what property-based testing is and why it complements unit tests.
2. An understanding of which invariants mrrc actually asserts (binary / MARCXML / MARCJSON round-trip, directory tiling, indicator/subfield byte sets, etc.) and where to find them (`tests/properties.rs`).
3. A brief note on coverage-guided fuzzing (see bd-dfn2 cargo-fuzz work) and how it differs from proptest.
4. Further-reading links so they can go deeper.

## Where

A new page under `docs/` (likely `docs/contributing/testing/property-tests.md` or `docs/reference/formal-methods.md` — decide when starting). Link it from the top-level docs nav.

## Scope

- Explain property-based testing in plain language (hypothesis-style framing, shrinking, regression seeds) — not a full textbook, but enough that someone who has only written assert-style tests can follow.
- Enumerate the properties currently enforced by `tests/properties.rs`, with one-line explanations of each.
- Describe the proptest-regressions policy (accepted seeds are committed as permanent guards).
- Mention fuzzing briefly with a pointer to whatever cargo-fuzz infra exists (bd-dfn2).
- Provide at least 3 canonical references, e.g.:
  - QuickCheck (Claessen & Hughes, 2000) original paper
  - proptest book: https://proptest-rs.github.io/proptest/
  - Hypothesis docs (Python analogue, widely read): https://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/
  - Property-based testing primer from fast.ai / John Hughes talk / MacIver posts — pick the most approachable.

## Out of scope

- Teaching users to write new properties from scratch (link out instead).
- Formal verification (TLA+, Coq) — we are not doing that here, and conflating "formal methods" too broadly will mislead the reader. The page should clarify that we mean property-based testing + fuzzing specifically, as a lightweight point on the formal-methods spectrum.

## Done when

- Page exists, is linked from the docs nav, renders cleanly under mkdocs.
- A reader unfamiliar with property testing can read it in <10 min and know what mrrc does and why.
- At least 3 reputable references cited.
- `bd-ouji` is referenced from the page (the PR that established the current suite) so readers can trace the history.
close_reason: Delivered in PR #130 (formal-methods primer + docs extra). Docs-deploy regression introduced by the docs.yml change in #130 was fixed in PR #131 and verified green on manual workflow run 24963722131 (build: success, deploy: success). v0.8 quality-review cleanup (PR #132, in flight) further trims the page per simplifier-review findings.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #111: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/111

title: Pymarc API compatibility: ordered implementation plan
close_reason: Completed — merged in PR #72

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
Ordered implementation plan for pymarc API compatibility. Replaces bd-wifu.

Every gap below was verified via live introspection of pymarc and mrrc. The goal is to make the Python wrapper behave like pymarc — Rust internals can stay as-is; only the Python wrapper layer needs to match.

Breaking changes are acceptable — we don't have many users yet.

---

## Phase 1: Type unification (Foundation — unblocks Phases 2-3)

These must be done first because nearly everything else depends on Field behaving like pymarc's Field.

### 1A. Unify ControlField into Field

pymarc has NO separate ControlField class. Both control and data fields are `pymarc.Field`, distinguished by `is_control_field()`. mrrc's separate `ControlField` class breaks `isinstance(f, Field)` checks and means `get_fields()` returns mixed types.

**Fix:** Make `ControlField` a subclass of `Field` in the wrapper. Control fields should be constructable as `Field('001', data='12345')` (matching pymarc). `is_control_field()` returns True when tag < '010'. `get_fields()` and `Record.__getitem__` return `Field` instances for both types.

### 1B. Control field content: `.data` attribute (not `.value`)

pymarc control fields store content in `.data`. mrrc uses `.value`. This also conflicts with the `Field.value()` method needed in Phase 2.

**Fix:** Use `.data` on control fields. This is part of the unified Field class — control fields have `.data`, data fields have `.value()` method. Keep `.value` as a deprecated alias during transition if desired, but `.data` is the pymarc-compatible name.

### 1C. `Field.__str__` and `__repr__`

pymarc `str(field)` returns `'=245  10$aThe Great Gatsby$cF. Scott Fitzgerald'`. mrrc returns the default Python repr. `__getattr__` delegation doesn't intercept dunder methods.

**Fix:** Add `__str__` and `__repr__` to the wrapper Field class. Format: `=TAG  IND1IND2$aCONTENT$bCONTENT` for data fields, `=TAG  CONTENT` for control fields.

### 1D. `Record.__getitem__` raises KeyError for missing tags

pymarc `record['999']` raises `KeyError`. mrrc returns `None`. Existing mrrc tests assert `None` — those tests should be updated to match pymarc.

**Fix:** Raise `KeyError` in `__getitem__` for missing tags. `record.get(tag)` remains the safe accessor that returns None/default.

---

## Phase 2: Record-level methods (Core API surface)

### 2A. Record accessors as `@property`

All 17 pymarc Record accessors are `@property`: `title`, `author`, `isbn`, `issn`, `issn_title`, `issnl`, `subjects`, `notes`, `location`, `series`, `sudoc`, `publisher`, `pubyear`, `physical_description`, `uniform_title`. mrrc has them as methods.

**Fix:** Convert to `@property` in the wrapper. Add pymarc-compatible names as aliases: `physicaldescription` → `physical_description`, `uniformtitle` → `uniform_title`. Add missing `addedentries` (returns 700/710/711/730 fields).

### 2B. `pubyear` return type

pymarc returns `Optional[str]` (e.g. `"2020"`). mrrc returns `Optional[int]`.

**Fix:** Return `str` from the property wrapper (wrap the Rust int with `str()`).

### 2C. `add_field(*fields)` — accept multiple

pymarc `add_field(self, *fields)` accepts multiple fields. mrrc accepts one.

**Fix:** Change signature to `add_field(self, *fields)` with a loop.

### 2D. `remove_field(*fields)` + `remove_fields(*tags)`

pymarc `remove_field(self, *fields)` accepts multiple Field objects. pymarc also has `remove_fields(self, *tags)` for bulk removal by tag string. mrrc accepts one field/tag and returns removed fields (pymarc returns None).

**Fix:** Change `remove_field` to accept `*fields`. Add `remove_fields(*tags)`. Match pymarc return type (None).

### 2E. `add_ordered_field()` / `add_grouped_field()`

Both exist in pymarc, neither in mrrc. `add_ordered_field` inserts maintaining tag sort order. `add_grouped_field` inserts after the last field with the same tag.

**Fix:** Implement in wrapper.

---

## Phase 3: Field-level methods

### 3A. `Field.value()`

pymarc `field.value()` returns space-joined subfield values (or `.data` for control fields). Depends on Phase 1B (`.data` attribute).

**Fix:** Add `def value(self) -> str` to the unified Field class.

### 3B. `Field.format_field()`
