- **Option B:** add a separate `--release` flag to `check.sh` that
  switches just the maturin step; default stays `develop` for fast
  iteration; flag explicitly enables release-mode build for one-shot
  pre-release verification or perf debugging.

Recommended: **B**. Default ergonomics preserved; release-mode is
opt-in but always one flag away. Document the flag in `CLAUDE.md`
alongside the existing `--quick` and `--memory-checks` flags.

## Done when

- Decision made (A or B).
- Implemented in `check.sh`.
- `CLAUDE.md` updated to match.
- A pre-release run with release-mode build confirms no perf
  regressions before next release.
- CHANGELOG entry under `[Unreleased]` `### Changed` (contributor-
  experience).

## Out of scope

- Switching `--quick` mode to release (defeats the purpose of `--quick`).
- Reworking the broader maturin invocation (e.g., `--profile` flags,
  features) — separate bead if needed.
close_reason: Done in #254: check.sh gained a --release flag switching the maturin step to --release (default stays develop); documented in CLAUDE.md. Verified end-to-end via 'bash .cargo/check.sh --release'.

title: Pin the Windows CI runner image before windows-latest redirects to windows-2025
description:
GitHub Actions annotations on the v0.8.1 release run (and other recent runs) carry the notice: "windows-latest requests are being redirected to windows-2025-vs2026 by June 15, 2026." GitHub is migrating the `windows-latest` label to a new image; pin the Windows runner explicitly so the migration is deliberate rather than surprising a build.

## What to do

Replace `windows-latest` with an explicit image (e.g. `windows-2025`) in the workflow matrices that use it, after a quick check that the toolchain (Rust, Python, maturin) still builds/tests cleanly on the pinned image. Workflows referencing `windows-latest` include the build, python-build, python-release, and test workflows (grep `.github/workflows/` for the current list).

## Done when

- The Windows runner is pinned to an explicit image across the workflows, verified green on a push.

Not urgent — the redirect is effective ~2026-06-15 and is currently a no-op notice. CI hygiene; surfaced from the v0.8.1 release-run annotations.
close_reason: PR #250 merged; Windows runner pinned to windows-2025, verified green

title: v0.8 release quality review gate
description:
## Purpose

Final quality review gate before cutting v0.8. Hold this last so it can assess the **full landed scope** of the release — every PR, every API addition, every behavior change — as one coherent whole rather than as individual increments.

## Why a dedicated gate

The v0.8 cycle has been substantial:

- Structured `MarcError` variants with positional context (bd-qzew, etc.)
- Stable error codes + slugs (`E001`-`E099`, including the new `E099` `FatalReaderError`)
- Per-stream recovered-error cap with `with_max_errors` (bd-uj7c, #110)
- Authority + holdings recovery parity with bib (bd-lbsi #121, bd-jmrt #122)
- ISO 2709 directory-walk skeleton consolidation + shared `RecoveryCap` (bd-lkcy #125)
- Read-path optimization fixes (bd-tcym, bd-9kdt)
- Coverage-guided fuzzing infrastructure + nightly CI (bd-dfn2)
- ...plus follow-on cleanup PRs

Each landed individually with focused review, but no one has yet looked at the whole as a single release. Subtle inconsistencies — naming drift, asymmetric API shape across the three readers, partial-coverage docs, CHANGELOG line items that need merging or rephrasing — only surface when reading the full delta end-to-end.

## What to review

A non-exhaustive checklist; expand as we encounter things:

### Public API surface
- [ ] `mrrc::*` re-exports complete and consistent (no orphaned types, no accidentally-private things that should be public).
- [ ] Builder method naming consistent across the three readers (`with_recovery_mode`, `with_max_errors`, `with_source`, `from_path`).
- [ ] Type-stub (`mrrc/_mrrc.pyi`) up-to-date with every Rust addition that has Python bindings.
- [ ] PyO3 wrappers expose every Rust addition that should reach Python (or the gap is documented).
- [ ] No `pub` types that should be `pub(crate)`; no `pub(crate)` that should be `pub`.

### Error handling
- [ ] Every `MarcError` variant has a stable code, slug, body text, JSON shape, and Python class wired through.
- [ ] `docs/reference/error-codes.md` documents every code with consistent shape (Context / Applies to / Populates / Common causes / How to recover / Python class).
- [ ] `docs/reference/error-handling.md` accurately reflects the current variant set + class hierarchy.
- [ ] No `unwrap()` / `expect()` on user-input paths (parse, deserialize, etc.) — only on framework guarantees.

### Recovery + cap
- [ ] All three readers behave consistently in `RecoveryMode::Strict` / `Lenient` / `Permissive` (per the bd-lbsi + bd-jmrt + bd-lkcy work).
- [ ] `RecoveryCap` has one home (`recovery::RecoveryCap`), not duplicated.
- [ ] Cap default + `with_max_errors(0)` semantics documented in one place and consistent across readers.

### Performance
- [ ] No regression on bib `read_*` benches vs the pre-bd-lkcy baseline (recorded as a comment on bd-lkcy).
- [ ] CodSpeed history clean across the v0.8 cycle.
- [ ] `bench_*` suite still runs in CI on every push.

### CHANGELOG
- [ ] `[Unreleased]` section reads as a coherent release narrative (not a stream-of-consciousness PR-by-PR log).
- [ ] Consolidate near-duplicate entries (e.g., the per-reader recovery-parity entries probably want to merge into one "Per-field lenient recovery parity across all three ISO 2709 readers" section).
- [ ] Breaking-change entries call out migration paths.
- [ ] Link out to GitHub issues for every entry.

### Docs
- [ ] mkdocs nav reflects new pages (formal-methods primer if bd-seja landed, etc.).
- [ ] `docs/contributing/fuzzing.md` reflects current target list.
- [ ] README feature table accurate.
- [ ] No `[Unreleased]` text in user-facing docs.

### Tests
- [ ] No commented-out tests left behind.
- [ ] No `#[ignore]` tests without a tracking bead/issue.
- [ ] `tests/python/` covers every Python wrapper addition.
- [ ] Property + fuzz coverage statements in docs match what's actually run.

### Beads / issues hygiene
- [ ] Every elevated bead has its closing comment + GitHub issue closed.
- [ ] No `in_progress` beads from earlier in the cycle that were silently abandoned.
- [ ] `MEMORY.md` references still point at extant docs.

## Deliverable

A v0.8 release-readiness report — could be a comment on this bead, a draft of the v0.8 release notes, or a checklist of last-mile fixes broken out into individual PRs. Whichever shape best surfaces what's left.

## Done when

- All checklist items above (and any added during review) are either ✓ or have a tracking bead/PR for v0.8 follow-up.
- `[Unreleased]` is rewritten as the v0.8 release-notes draft (or close to it).
- A go/no-go call is made on cutting v0.8.

## Out of scope

- Net-new features. This is a polish + audit gate, not a "while we're here" expansion.
- Items already explicitly deferred to post-v0.8 (e.g., bd-bov7 UTF-8 unification, the broader bd-lkcy follow-ups if any).
close_reason: Delivered in PR #132 (merge commit e6a494e); all CI green including CodSpeed regression detection (no-op vs main after the inline-restore fix-up). Three v0.9 follow-up beads filed for larger refactors: bd-hgv6, bd-qhll, bd-fe3l.

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: Elevated to GitHub issue #126: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/126

comments.author: dchud
comments.text:
**CHANGELOG cross-cycle consolidation note**

When reviewing the `[Unreleased]` section, look at the recent refactor entries (bd-uj7c, bd-lbsi, bd-jmrt, bd-lkcy) alongside the *original* development entries for the same code areas (e.g., the structured-error work, the shared ISO 2709 parsing primitives that were already present before the cap/parity/skeleton iterations).

Likely consolidation opportunities:

- The four "Added — per-stream cap / authority parity / holdings parity / skeleton consolidation" sections all touch the same three readers in increments. From a release-notes perspective this is one story: *"the three ISO 2709 readers now share a unified parse skeleton with per-stream cap, full per-field lenient/permissive recovery parity, and pluggable per-type field dispatch"* — collapse the four into one tight section.
- The skeleton consolidation also subsumes the cap-state extraction; mention `RecoveryCap` as a structural detail under the skeleton section, not a separate entry.
- The "shared ISO 2709 parsing primitives" entry from the earlier development cycle (already in `[Unreleased]`) sits in the same conceptual space — readers will naturally read those together. Worth deciding whether they merge into one Reader Architecture section or stay as before/after entries.
- The behavior-change subbullets (authority's silent skip → cap-counted skip, holdings' field-exceeds-data salvage attempt, holdings' message wording) are good candidates for a single "Changed (lenient/permissive recovery)" entry rather than nested under each per-reader Added section.

Goal: a reader who has never touched the code should be able to read `[Unreleased]` once and know what shipped. Stream-of-consciousness PR-by-PR ordering makes that hard.

title: Upgrade pyo3 to 0.29 (RUSTSEC-2026-0176)
description: RUSTSEC-2026-0176 (published 2026-06-11): out-of-bounds read in nth/nth_back for PyList and PyTuple iterators in pyo3 <=0.28.x; fix is pyo3 >=0.29.0. cargo audit now fails, which fails .cargo/check.sh and therefore blocks every push via the pre-push hook. src-python/Cargo.toml pins pyo3 = 0.28. No Dependabot PR open as of filing. Done when: pyo3 bumped to 0.29, any API migration compiles clean, check.sh green including audit, CI green.
close_reason: pyo3 0.29 merged in PR #284; audit green, pushes unblocked

title: MARCXML whitespace-only text content gets stripped on round-trip
description:
## Problem

The MARCXML serializer + deserializer do not round-trip whitespace-only control-field or subfield values. Surfaced by bd-ouji proptest expansion with minimal failing input:

```
control_fields: {"001": [" "]}   // single space
```

Round-trip produces:
`XmlError { cause: Custom("missing field `$value`"), ... }`

Root cause (suspected): `quick-xml` + `serde(rename = "$value")` with `value: String` treats whitespace-only text content as absent, so the deserializer returns an error. The serializer likely also has trouble round-tripping — needs confirmation.

## Reproduction

Temporarily revert the whitespace exclusion in `tests/properties.rs::arb_control_value_xml` and `arb_subfield_value_xml` and run `cargo test --test properties marcxml_roundtrip`. Saved failing seed:

```
cc c55bf86fe34e98890f3851b08dc2838087458f63372964533b0190c7d491c89e
```

(Not committed to `tests/proptest-regressions/` because the current strategy avoids the input — re-committing the seed is part of this bead.)

## Why it matters

MARC spec allows whitespace in control fields (e.g., padded dates in 008). Real-world records sometimes contain `<controlfield tag="008">      </controlfield>` or subfields whose content is whitespace only. Silently dropping or failing to parse these is a correctness bug.

## What to do

1. Investigate whether switching `MarcxmlControlField::value` / `MarcxmlSubfield::value` from `String` to `Option<String>` (with `#[serde(default)]`) round-trips whitespace-only content. If so, adapt the conversion path to materialise `None` as `""` (or a single space — needs decision). Quick-xml 0.39 also has whitespace-preservation options worth investigating (`trim_text(false)` etc.).
2. Re-broaden `arb_subfield_value_xml` and `arb_control_value_xml` in `tests/properties.rs` to allow whitespace-only and whitespace-edge values; commit the saved seed (`cc c55bf86f...`) to `tests/proptest-regressions/properties.txt`.
3. Add a targeted unit test in `src/marcxml.rs` that round-trips a `<controlfield tag="008">` with whitespace-padded content.
4. Confirm `.cargo/check.sh` still passes.

## Out of scope

- MARCJSON whitespace handling (confirmed working in bd-ouji).
- Binary ISO 2709 whitespace handling (confirmed working in bd-ouji).
close_reason: Delivered in #109 (merged to main)

title: Phase 3: Linked field navigation and specialized queries
description:
Phase 3 of Advanced Field Query DSL (see design/FIELD_QUERY_DSL.md).

Implement specialized query patterns for linked fields, authority control, and format-specific queries.

Tasks:
1. Linked field navigation (880 field linkage)
   - Implement subfield 6 parsing for linkage occurrence numbers
   - Methods:
     * field.get_linked_field(record) -> Option<Field> (880 partner)
     * record.get_all_linked_fields(field) -> Vec<Field>
     * record.get_field_pairs(tag) -> Vec<(Field, Option<Field>)> (original + 880)
   - Support for bidirectional lookups

2. Authority control helpers (for authority records)
   - Methods for finding related authority headings
   - Navigate 4XX (see from) and 5XX (see also) tracings
   - Helper: find_related_headings(heading: &Field) -> Vec<Field>
   - Extract authority relationship information from 7XX fields

3. Format-specific query traits
   - BibliographicQueries trait (for Record)
   - AuthorityQueries trait (for AuthorityRecord)
   - HoldingsQueries trait (for HoldingsRecord)
   - Each provides domain-specific helper methods

4. Comprehensive testing
   - Test 880 field linkage with real MARC-8 encoded data
   - Test authority record navigation patterns
   - Test complex record type-specific queries
   
Dependencies: Requires Phase 1 (mrrc-9n8) and Phase 2 (mrrc-131) completed
notes: Phase 3 Status: Linked field navigation COMPLETED (28 tests). Authority control helpers TODO. Format-specific traits TODO.

title: Remove duplicate inherent methods on Record that shadow RecordHelpers trait
description:
22 methods on Record (in src/record.rs lines ~795-1022) are defined as inherent methods that duplicate identical implementations on the RecordHelpers trait (in src/record_helpers.rs). Since Record implements MarcRecord, it gets RecordHelpers automatically. The inherent methods shadow the trait methods, creating maintenance burden and confusion.

Duplicated methods: title, title_with_responsibility, author, authors, corporate_author, publisher, publication_date, isbn, isbns, issn, language, control_number, lccn, physical_description, series, is_book, is_serial, is_music, is_audiovisual, publication_info, publication_year, place_of_publication.

Note: subjects() was already cleaned up in PR #25.

The trait also defines 7 methods NOT duplicated on Record (location, notes, uniform_title, sudoc, issn_title, issnl, pubyear) — these are fine.

Fix: remove all 22 inherent methods from Record and add 'use RecordHelpers' where needed in callers (examples, tests, PyO3 wrappers).
close_reason: Merged in PR #28

title: Avoid Python file I/O in examples
close_reason: Merged in PR #54

title: Phase 2: Subfield pattern matching with regex and convenience methods
description:
Phase 2 of Advanced Field Query DSL (see design/FIELD_QUERY_DSL.md).

Implement subfield pattern matching and value-based filtering to extend Phase 1 query capabilities.

Tasks:
1. Add regex pattern matching for subfield values
   - Method: record.fields_where_subfield_matches(tag, code, regex_pattern)
   - Support for common MARC patterns (e.g., ISBNs, dates)
   
2. Implement value-based filtering helpers
   - Methods like: record.fields_where_subfield_value(tag, code, value)
   - Support wildcards and partial matching
   
3. Add convenience methods for common domain-specific searches
   - subjects_with_subdivision(code, value) - e.g., subjects_with_subdivision('x', 'History')
   - isbns_matching(pattern) - find ISBNs matching pattern
   - names_in_range(start_tag, end_tag) - get all name fields in range
   - authors_with_dates() - extract birth/death dates from 100/700 fields

4. Comprehensive testing
   - Unit tests for regex pattern matching
   - Integration tests with realistic MARC records
   - Performance tests with large record sets
   
Dependencies: Requires Phase 1 (mrrc-9n8) completed
notes:
Phase 2 of field query DSL (see design/FIELD_QUERY_DSL.md):

Implement:
1. Subfield pattern matching with regex (e.g., matches_pattern() method)
2. Value-based filtering helpers (e.g., fields_where_subfield_matches())
3. Convenience methods for common searches:
   - subjects_with_subdivision(code, value)
   - names_in_range(start, end)
   - isbns_matching(pattern)

Tests: Unit and integration tests covering regex patterns and value-based filtering
close_reason: Completed - Phase 2 of field query DSL fully implemented with 39 comprehensive tests covering pattern matching, value filtering, and convenience methods. All quality gates passing.

title: Improve documentation for agent discoverability and usability
description:
Epic to improve how agents interact with the mrrc codebase, addressing two distinct audiences:

**Audience 1: Agents developing mrrc** (e.g. Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot working on the repo)
- Create CLAUDE.md with current workflow (check.sh, beads via br, session protocol)
- Update AGENTS.md to follow the cross-tool convention (build/test/conventions, not inception story)
- Cross-reference CLAUDE.md <-> AGENTS.md for easy discovery

**Audience 2: Agents using mrrc as a library** (e.g. coding agents whose users depend on mrrc)
- Surface mrrc/_mrrc.pyi prominently — it's the ground-truth Python API
- Add context7.json so Context7 indexes our docs and code examples
- Fix example links to use relative paths agents can follow locally
- Collapse docs/history/ in nav to avoid wasting agent context on 94 archival files

Subtasks (revised):
1. Create CLAUDE.md + update AGENTS.md (mrrc-8h4o) — P1
2. Surface _mrrc.pyi + fix docs navigability (mrrc-sb4b) — P1
3. Add context7.json for Context7 indexing (mrrc-vhk5, repurposed from llms.txt) — P2

title: Record and Field constructor ease of use (pymarc parity)
description:
For parity with pymarc, Record's constructor should accept a fields parameter and Field's constructor should accept a subfields parameter, enabling inline construction:

```python
from mrrc import Record, Field, Subfield

r = Record(
    fields=[
        Field(
            '245',
            indicators=['0', '1'],
            subfields=[
                Subfield('a', 'Pragmatic Programmer')
            ]
        )
    ]
)
```

Currently mrrc requires temporary variables and separate add_field()/add_subfield() calls.

The underlying Rust code already supports this pattern (Record::new with fields, DataField with subfields), so this should be a matter of updating the PyO3 bindings.

Ping @edsu on the PR when implemented.

Original issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc-experiments/issues/15
close_reason: Implemented Field(subfields=, indicators=) and Record(fields=) kwargs in both mrrc/__init__.py and src-python/__init__.py. Updated type stubs, added 8 tests, updated 5 doc files and examples.

title: Remove stale src-python/python/mrrc/ directory
description:
## Background

During Python wrapper development, the project underwent a layout migration in Phase 5 (mrrc-experiments d2bc400):

- **Phase 1–4**: src-python/python/mrrc/ was the canonical Python package, using maturin's `python-source = "python"` layout with src-python/pyproject.toml.
- **Phase 5**: Migrated to maturin's default mixed-project layout — root pyproject.toml with `python-packages = ["mrrc"]` pointing at root mrrc/. Created root mrrc/__init__.py with expanded content.
- **Audit (2974f82)**: Deleted redundant src-python/pyproject.toml but missed the stale src-python/python/mrrc/ directory.
- **Post-Phase 5**: All new Python code (formats/, rayon_parser_pool.py, query DSL, BIBFRAME, etc.) was added exclusively to root mrrc/. src-python/python/mrrc/ was never updated.

Result: src-python/python/mrrc/ is the abandoned original (565 lines), root mrrc/ is the actively maintained successor (1,466 lines). Both are tracked in git, creating confusion about which to edit.

## Current state of src-python/python/mrrc/

- __init__.py (565 lines) — stale, uses absolute imports (`from mrrc._mrrc`), missing Query DSL, BIBFRAME, batch parsing, format modules, and many features added since Phase 5
- __init__.pyi (669 lines) — type stubs for the Python wrapper classes. Root mrrc/ has _mrrc.pyi (extension stubs) but NO __init__.pyi. This file has value but is likely out of date.
- py.typed (PEP 561 marker) — root mrrc/ is missing this. Needed for downstream type checking.

## Action plan

1. Move src-python/python/mrrc/py.typed → mrrc/py.typed
2. Move src-python/python/mrrc/__init__.pyi → mrrc/__init__.pyi (will need updating — see mrrc-is9)
3. Delete src-python/python/mrrc/ entirely (including __init__.py)
4. Revert the MODS imports we mistakenly added to src-python/python/mrrc/__init__.py (commit 302fd37) since the file is being deleted anyway
5. Verify: `uv run maturin develop --release`, `uv run pytest tests/python/`, `cargo test`
6. Confirm maturin layout matches best practice (root mrrc/ + pyproject.toml with python-packages)

## Maturin best practice reference

From maturin docs, the default mixed-project layout is:
```
project/
├── Cargo.toml
├── my_project/        # Python package at root
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── ...
├── pyproject.toml
└── src/
    └── lib.rs
```

Our structure (with workspace) correctly follows this, except for the stale src-python/python/mrrc/ leftover.
close_reason: Cleaned up dual Python package directories: moved py.typed to root mrrc/, deleted stale src-python/python/, merged type stubs into _mrrc.pyi with MODS functions and rich docstrings

title: Fix: Remove Python::with_gil() wrapper from __next__() - re-acquiring GIL defeats allow_threads() release
description:
CRITICAL FINDING: GIL is still NOT being released even after removing Python::with_gil() wrapper.

Root cause identified: The allow_threads() method on a bound Python<'py> handle (obtained via PyRefMut::py()) does not actually release the GIL in PyO3 0.27.

Evidence:
- Sequential read of 100k records: 0.85s
- Parallel read of 2x 100k records (2 threads): 1.95s each (~0.44x speedup)
- Expected speedup with GIL release: 1.8x
- Both threads take same time (~1.95s), indicating serial execution, not parallel

The threading.Event test shows that SOME other thread activity can occur (suggesting the GIL might be released momentarily), but not enough for parallelism.

PyO3 0.27 API changes:
- allow_threads() is deprecated
- Should use Python::detach().allow_threads() or similar pattern
- Bound Python<'py> might not support GIL release in same way as unbound Python<'unbound>

Next: Investigate correct PyO3 0.27 pattern for actual GIL release on a bound Python handle.
notes: RESOLVED: Code uses py.detach() correctly. Real issue is architectural: Python file I/O requires GIL. Mitigation: Phase H RustFile backend needed for parallelism.

title: Flaky benchmark: test_backend_comparison_1k timing assertion
description:
## Problem

`test_benchmark_writing.py::TestBackendComparison::test_backend_comparison_1k` fails intermittently on CI. It asserts RustFile time < PythonFile time * 2, but a single timing outlier (10ms vs typical 4ms) causes failure.

## Recent failure

CI run 21963977156 (Benchmarks workflow, 2026-02-12):
```
FAILED tests/python/test_benchmark_writing.py::TestBackendComparison::test_backend_comparison_1k - AssertionError: RustFile performance regression
assert 0.010333120000041163 < (0.004083065000031638 * 2)
```

Same pattern as the flaky GIL release test fixed in #3 — timing-based assertions on shared CI runners.

## Suggested fix

Either widen the tolerance, add retries, or mark as `@pytest.mark.benchmark` so it doesn't block CI.

## Relevant files
- `tests/python/test_benchmark_writing.py`
close_reason: Fix verified in CI — all 5 workflows pass including the previously-failing Python Benchmarks workflow.

title: GIL Release: Implement ParseError wrapper and defer error conversion
description:
GIL Release: Implement ParseError wrapper and defer error conversion

CRITICAL FIX: Investigation revealed that Phase 1 I/O (reading from Python file) requires GIL and is inherently sequential. This prevents parallel Phase 2 parsing.

Root Cause Analysis:
- Each thread must acquire GIL for Phase 1 I/O (sequential bottleneck)
- Phase 2 can release GIL but by then threads are already blocked waiting for Phase 1
- Result: 0.85x speedup (worse than sequential)

Solution Architecture:
- Part A: Error conversion fix - use py.detach() instead of allow_threads() [DONE]
- Part B: Batch reading (Phase C) - read multiple records per Phase 1, parse batch in Phase 2 [REQUIRED]

Acceptance Criteria:
- Error conversion deferred to Phase 3 [DONE]
- py.detach() properly releases GIL [DONE]
- Performance test shows >= 1.8x speedup after both fixes [PENDING]

If speedup < 1.8x after error fix, Phase C (batch reading) becomes blocking requirement.
notes: Completed: py.detach() error handling fix applied and tested

title: marcjson_to_record() returns unwrapped Rust Field objects
description: Records created via marcjson_to_record() and json_to_record() return raw Rust builtins.Field and builtins.Leader objects from methods like get_fields() and leader(), rather than the Python mrrc.Field and mrrc.Leader wrappers. This means field subscripting (field['a']), get_subfields(), and leader indexing (leader[9]) all fail. Records created via the Python constructor work correctly. GitHub issue #16.

title: GIL Release: Unlock threading parallelism in pymrrc
description:
GIL Release: Unlock threading parallelism in pymrrc

See: @docs/design/GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md

Goal: Enable GIL release during I/O operations to achieve 2-3x threading speedup on concurrent workloads.

Architecture:
Three-phase pattern separates Python object access (requires GIL) from CPU-intensive parsing (can release GIL):
- Phase 1 (GIL held): Read bytes from Python file object
- Phase 2 (GIL released): Parse/process bytes (pure Rust, no Python refs)
- Phase 3 (GIL held): Convert result to Python object

Critical Technical Decisions:
- SmallVec<[u8; 4096]> for owned record bytes (avoids borrow checker violation)
- ParseError enum for GIL-free error creation
- Python::assume_gil_acquired() for safe GIL handle access

Roadmap: 7 phases (6 critical path A-B-D-E-F-G, Phase C optional)
- Phase A: Core Buffering Infrastructure (1 week)
- Phase B: GIL Release Integration (1-2 weeks)
- Phase C: Performance Optimizations (OPTIONAL, deferred if speedup ≥2x)
- Phase D: Writer Implementation (1 week)
- Phase E: Comprehensive Validation (1 week)
- Phase F: Benchmark Refresh (1 week)
- Phase G: Documentation Refresh (1 week)

Expected Outcome: 2-3x threading speedup with pymrrc approaching pure Rust parallelism efficiency.

Key References in GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md:
- Executive Summary (lines 11-35): Goals and expected outcomes
- Part 1 (lines 38-85): Architecture overview
- Part 5 (lines 313-504): Complete implementation roadmap for all phases
- Part 10 (lines 1350-1375): Timeline and resource estimate
close_reason: Phases A-D, H complete. Proceeding to Phase E validation.

ParseError is designed to be created inside allow_threads() closure (GIL released).
Only String fields allowed (no Py<T>). After GIL re-acquisition, ParseError converts to PyErr.

title: Create src-python/src/buffered_reader.rs with BufferedMarcReader struct
description:
Create src-python/src/buffered_reader.rs with BufferedMarcReader struct

See: @docs/design/GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md

Key Sections:
- Part 1 (lines 68-85): BufferedMarcReader architecture overview
- Part 2, Fix 1 (lines 90-145): SmallVec strategy and performance justification
- Part 5, Phase A (lines 315-379): Stream state machine specification and edge cases

File: src-python/src/buffered_reader.rs

Struct: BufferedMarcReader
- file_wrapper: PyFileWrapper (wraps Py<PyAny> file object)
- reader: BorrowMut<MarcReader> or similar Rust reader
- buffer: SmallVec<[u8; 4096]> (owns record bytes, no borrow issues)

Key Methods:

1. read_next_record_bytes(&mut self, py: Python) -> PyResult<Option<Vec<u8>>>
   - Phase 1 operation (GIL held)
   - Reads complete ISO 2709 MARC record from file
   - Returns Ok(Some(bytes)) for complete record
   - Returns Ok(None) at EOF (idempotent on subsequent calls)
   - Returns Err(PyErr) for I/O or boundary detection errors
   - Documentation with GIL requirement in design plan Part 2, Fix 2 (lines 205-235)

2. read_exact_from_file(&mut self, py: Python, n_bytes: usize) -> PyResult<Vec<u8>>
   - Reads exactly n_bytes from file object via file.read(n)
   - Returns Err(IOError) if fewer bytes available and EOF reached
   - Stream state machine spec in Part 5, Phase A (lines 322-348)

3. parse_record_length(bytes: &[u8]) -> PyResult<usize>
   - Parses ISO 2709 record length from first 5 bytes
   - Validates that bytes 0-4 are ASCII digits
   - Spec in Part 5, Phase A (behavior #4, line 335)

SmallVec Sizing Rationale (from design plan lines 124-125):
MARC records typically range 100B–5KB. A 4KB inline buffer avoids allocation for ~85–90% of records.
Automatic heap spillover for >4KB records. This balances memory footprint against allocation frequency.
close_reason: BufferedMarcReader struct with PyFileWrapper, read_next_record_bytes(), read_exact(), parse_record_length() methods fully implemented

title: Add smallvec dependency and Cargo.toml updates
description:
Add required dependencies for Phase A implementation.

Changes to Cargo.toml:
- Add: smallvec = "1.11" under [dependencies]

Rationale:
SmallVec<[u8; 4096]> provides:
- Inline storage (4KB buffer embedded in struct, no heap allocation for typical records)
- Automatic spillover (if record >4KB, SmallVec allocates Vec on heap transparently)
- Performance: avoids allocation for ~85-90% of real-world MARC records
- Memory footprint: 4KB stack overhead per BufferedMarcReader (acceptable tradeoff)

Record size distribution (Library of Congress, Worldcat, local catalogs):
- Median: ~1.5KB
- 95th percentile: ~8KB
- 4KB inline buffer captures ~85-90% without allocation

References:
- Design doc Part 2, Fix 1 (140-145) - Cargo.toml additions and sizing rationale
- Design doc Part 9 (1331-1338) - Dependencies and configuration

title: Add comprehensive unit tests for BufferedMarcReader boundary detection
description:
Implement unit tests covering all ISO 2709 boundary detection edge cases.

Test Cases (minimum 10):
1. Complete records - reads full record correctly
2. Records split across multiple reads - partial reads handled correctly
3. Corrupted length headers - non-ASCII digits detected and rejected
4. Missing terminator - record boundary violations detected
5. Variable-length records - 100B, 5KB, 100KB records all handled
6. EOF on first byte - incomplete header detected
7. EOF mid-record - incomplete body detected
8. Empty files - EOF on first read returns None
9. Multiple sequential records - reads all records without mixing data
10. Record at file boundary - last record read completely

Stream State Machine Verification (from design doc Part 5, Phase A):
Test behavior #1-7 as specified in state machine (lines 326-347):
- [ ] read_next_record_bytes() on closed file → PyIOError
- [ ] read_next_record_bytes() at EOF → Ok(None)
- [ ] read_next_record_bytes() after EOF → Ok(None) again (idempotent)
- [ ] Corrupted record length header → PyValueError with details
- [ ] Record header incomplete before EOF → PyIOError with details
- [ ] Record body incomplete before EOF → PyIOError with details
- [ ] File closed during Phase 2 → Handled by Python file.read() IOError

Testing Strategy:
- Use synthetic MARC test files in tests/fixtures/marc/
- Create corrupted variants for each edge case
- Verify error messages include diagnostic details (offset, expected bytes, actual bytes)

Success Criteria:
- All tests pass (no panics, all errors converted to PyErr)
- Coverage: 100% of boundary detection logic
- Error messages clear and actionable

References:
- Design doc Part 5, Phase A (315-379) - Stream state machine with all edge cases
- Design doc Part 2, Fix 1 (lines 106-115) - SmallVec copy pattern in context
close_reason: 13 unit tests implemented: parse_record_length validation, boundary detection, variable-length records (24B-5KB), SmallVec spillover. All tests pass.

title: Implement three-phase GIL release pattern in PyMarcReader.__next__()
description:
Implement the three-phase GIL release pattern in __next__() method.

Overview:
The __next__() method must separate Python object access from CPU-intensive parsing to allow other threads to execute during Phase 2 (parsing).

Phase Pattern (executed sequentially):

Phase 1 (GIL held):
  - Get Python handle: unsafe { Python::assume_gil_acquired() }
  - Call: record_bytes_ref = buffered_reader.read_next_record_bytes(py)?
  - If EOF (None), return Ok(None)

Phase 2 (GIL released):
  - Copy bytes to owned SmallVec: SmallVec::from_slice(record_bytes_ref)
  - Call: py.allow_threads(|| { reader.read_from_bytes(...) })
  - Returns Result<MarcRecord, ParseError>
  - ParseError can be created here (no GIL needed)

Phase 3 (GIL held):
  - Convert ParseError to PyErr (after GIL re-acquired): .map_err(PyErr::from)?
  - Convert MarcRecord to PyRecord (requires GIL): PyRecord::from_rust(record, py)
  - Return Ok(Some(PyObject))

Code Structure (pseudocode):


Implementation Notes:
- SmallVec is already owned by buffered_reader, but we create a copy for Phase 2 closure
- The ? operator in Phase 2 applies impl From<ParseError> -> PyErr
- Python::assume_gil_acquired() is safe (PyRefMut guarantees GIL)
- Add comments explaining each phase

References:
- Design doc Part 1 (52-66) - Three-phase pattern visualization
- Design doc Part 2, Fix 1 (98-115) - SmallVec copy and Phase 2 closure capture
- Design doc Part 2, Fix 3 (262-275) - ParseError conversion after GIL re-acquisition
- Design doc Part 5, Phase B (389-392) - Three-phase deliverable specification
close_reason: Completed - Three-phase pattern fully implemented in __next__: Phase 1 read (GIL held), Phase 2 parse (GIL released), Phase 3 convert (GIL re-acquired)

title: Implement PyMarcWriter with three-phase write pattern
description:
Implement write-side GIL release pattern for PyMarcWriter.

Pattern (differs from reader):
- Phase 1 (GIL held): Extract field data from Python PyRecord object into Rust MarcRecord
- Phase 2 (GIL released): Serialize Rust MarcRecord to MARC bytes (CPU-intensive)
- Phase 3 (GIL held): Write serialized bytes to Python file object via .write()

Implementation Overview:

1. Phase 1 - Data Collection (GIL held):
   - Accept PyRecord object (already bound, GIL held)
   - Extract leader, fields, subfields into Rust MarcRecord structure
   - Validate record structure before serialization

2. Phase 2 - Serialization (GIL released):
   - Use py.allow_threads(|| { record.to_bytes() })
   - Returns Result<Vec<u8>, SerializationError>
   - SerializationError (similar to ParseError) created inside allow_threads()

3. Phase 3 - File Writing (GIL held):
   - Call file.write(bytes) on Python file object
   - Handle write errors (partial writes, closed file)
   - Return number of records written

Key Design Decisions:
- Reuse ParseError or create SerializationError (GIL-free)
- Buffer writes if beneficial (optional optimization for Phase C)
- Round-trip compatibility: bytes written by writer must be readable by reader

Method Signature:
pub fn write(&mut self, record: PyRecord, py: Python) -> PyResult<()>

Or Iterator Pattern (if compatible with current API):
- write(record) enqueues for batched writing
- flush() ensures all records written

References:
- Design doc Part 5, Phase D (474-504) - Write pattern and phase specification
- Design doc Part 2, Fix 3 (268-283) - Error handling pattern (reuse ParseError or similar)
- Design doc Part 1 (52-66) - Three-phase pattern (apply to writer)

title: Implement round-trip tests: read-write-read verification
description:
Verify that records survive round-trip: read original → write to new file → read back.

Test Strategy:

1. Load original file (fixture_1k.mrc)
2. Read all records into list
3. Write list to new file
4. Read new file back into list
5. Verify byte-for-byte identical records

Implementation:
test_round_trip_read_write_read():
  - Load fixture_1k.mrc
  - reader1 = MARCReader(fixture_1k.mrc)
  - records = [r for r in reader1]
  - writer = MARCWriter(output_file)
  - for r in records: writer.write(r)
  - writer.close() or flush()
  - reader2 = MARCReader(output_file)
  - records2 = [r for r in reader2]
  - assert len(records) == len(records2)
  - assert all(r1.leader == r2.leader for r1, r2 in zip(records, records2))
  - assert all(r1 == r2 for r1, r2 in zip(records, records2))

Edge Cases:
- Empty files (0 records)

title: Concurrency testing: threading contention, EOF, file close semantics
description:
Implement comprehensive concurrency tests for edge cases.

Test Categories:

1. test_threading_contention:
   - Measure speedup degradation as thread count increases
   - 2 threads: expect ~1.8x speedup
   - 4 threads: expect ~3.2x speedup (some contention)
   - 8 threads: expect ~4.5x speedup (visible contention)
   - Verify speedup curve is monotonic (no inversions)
   - Rationale: GIL contention increases with thread count (expected)

2. test_streaming_eof_behavior:
   - Reader reaches EOF
   - First call: returns record
   - Second call: raises StopIteration
   - Third call: raises StopIteration again (consistent)
   - Verify idempotent EOF behavior

3. test_file_close_semantics:
   - Thread A: reading from file (Phase 2 parsing in progress)
   - Thread B: closes file via file.close()
   - Thread A: should raise IOError (not panic, not data corruption)
   - Verify graceful error handling

4. test_exception_propagation:
   - Corrupt record in middle of file
   - Reader encounters error
   - Exception raised to calling thread
   - Other threads continue unaffected
   - Verify no deadlocks

5. test_single_reader_multiple_threads_fails_safely:
   - DON'T do this: share reader across threads without locking
   - Expected: Error or undefined behavior (documented as unsafe)
   - If enforced: RuntimeError with message
   - Test serves as documentation of API constraint

Implementation Notes:
- Use threading.ThreadPoolExecutor for thread management
- Use concurrent.futures for result collection
- Add timeout to prevent hanging tests
- Measure execution time to verify speedup

References:
- Design doc Part 6 (1243-1266) - Concurrency test specifications
- Design doc Part 7 (1280-1293) - Risk mitigation for thread contention
close_reason: All concurrency tests pass

title: Compare pymrrc threading efficiency to pure Rust baseline (rayon)
description:
Measure pymrrc threading performance relative to pure Rust parallelism.

Benchmark Test: test_pymrrc_vs_rayon_efficiency

Purpose:
Verify that pymrrc threading speedup approaches Rust-only performance (rayon or similar).
Goal: pymrrc >= 90% efficiency of pure Rust baseline for same hardware.

Procedure:

1. Rust Baseline (rayon):
   - Write Rust bench: cargo bench parallel_read_4x_10k
   - Reads 10K records in parallel using rayon (no Python, no GIL)
   - Measures: time for 4 threads to each read 2.5K records
   - Result: baseline_4thread_time_ms

2. pymrrc Measurement:
   - test_pymrrc_threading_4thread_10k()
   - Python: 4 threads, each reads 2.5K records from separate file
   - Result: pymrrc_4thread_time_ms

3. Efficiency Calculation:
   - efficiency = baseline_time / pymrrc_time
   - Example: rayon 1000ms / pymrrc 1100ms = 0.91 = 91% efficiency
   - Target: efficiency >= 0.90 (90%)

4. Document Results:
   File: .benchmarks/pymrrc_vs_rayon_efficiency.txt
   Format:
   

Success Criteria:
- pymrrc efficiency >= 90% of Rayon baseline
- Results documented
- Analysis includes discussion of GIL overhead

If efficiency < 90%:
- Indicates GIL contention is higher than expected
- Investigate: GIL crossing cost, batch reading benefit
- May trigger Phase C optimizations

References:
- Design doc Part 5, Phase C (468) - pymrrc vs Rayon comparison requirement
- Design doc Part 8 (1302) - Rust performance parity success criterion

title: Add MARC Authority to MADS 2.1 XML export
description:
Add one-way export from MARC Authority records to MADS 2.1 XML, following the pattern established by the existing MODS conversion module.

## Reference Specifications

- MADS home: https://www.loc.gov/standards/mads/
- MARC Authority to MADS 2.1 mapping (Excel): https://www.loc.gov/standards/mads/mads-mapping-2-1.xlsx
- XSLT 1.0 stylesheet: https://www.loc.gov/standards/marcxml/xslt/MARC21slim2MADS.xsl
- XSLT 2.0 stylesheet: https://www.loc.gov/standards/marcxml/xslt/MARC21slim2MADS_XSLT2.0.xsl
- MADS 2.1 schema (XSD): https://www.loc.gov/standards/mads/mads-2-1.xsd
- MARC Authority to MADS 2.0 mapping (HTML): https://www.loc.gov/standards/mads/mads-mapping.html

## Scope

One-way conversion only: MARC Authority → MADS 2.1 XML. No reverse mapping (MADS → MARC Authority) — there is no official LOC crosswalk for that direction, and the LOC notes the forward mapping "is not intended to be a crosswalk that allows for bi-directional conversions without some loss of data."

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Rust core module: `src/mads.rs`

Primary function:

```
pub fn authority_record_to_mads_xml(record: &AuthorityRecord) -> Result<String>
```

Also support converting a generic `Record` with leader/06=z:

```
pub fn record_to_mads_xml(record: &Record) -> Result<String>
```

Follow the manual XML construction pattern used in `src/mods.rs` (string building with `std::fmt::Write`), not the serde intermediate struct pattern.

### 2. Key field mappings (MARC Authority → MADS 2.1)

**Authority headings (1XX → `<mads:authority>`):**
- 100 → `<mads:name type="personal">`
- 110 → `<mads:name type="corporate">`
- 111 → `<mads:name type="conference">`
- 130 → `<mads:titleInfo>`
- 148 → `<mads:temporal>`
- 150 → `<mads:topic>`
- 151 → `<mads:geographic>`
- 155 → `<mads:genre>`

**Variant headings (4XX → `<mads:variant>`):**
- 400/410/411/430/448/450/451/455 → same descriptor types as above within `<variant>`

**Related headings (5XX → `<mads:related>`):**
- 500/510/511/530/548/550/551/555 → same descriptor types within `<related>`
- Map $w control subfield to `type` attribute (broader/narrower/earlier/later/equivalent)

**Metadata elements:**
- 008 positions → various attributes (e.g., encoding level)
- 010 → `<mads:identifier type="lccn">`
- 024 → `<mads:identifier>` with type from $2
- 040 → `<mads:recordInfo>` (cataloging source)
- 042 → `<mads:recordInfo>` (authentication code)
- 046 → date elements
- 370 → `<mads:affiliation>` or geographic metadata
- 371 → `<mads:affiliation>` (address)
- 372 → `<mads:fieldOfActivity>`
- 373 → `<mads:affiliation>` (group)
- 374 → `<mads:occupation>` or personInfo
- 375 → gender info
- 376 → `<mads:familyInfo>` or `<mads:personInfo>`
- 377 → `<mads:language>`
- 378 → `<mads:name>` (fuller form)
- 380-385 → various descriptive elements
- 667/670/675/678 → `<mads:note>`

**Subdivision subfields** ($v, $x, $y, $z in 1XX/4XX/5XX):
- $v → `<mads:genre>` (within authority/variant/related)
- $x → `<mads:topic>`
- $y → `<mads:temporal>`
- $z → `<mads:geographic>`

title: Add explicit GIL release verification tests
description:
Current test suite verifies GIL release indirectly: concurrent tests pass without deadlocking. But no test explicitly confirms the GIL is actually released during the parsing phase. A regression could silently make everything serial without failing any test.

Proposed approach: use threading.Event coordination to prove Python code can execute in a second thread while __next__() is mid-parse. E.g.:
- Thread A iterates a large file (triggering batch read + GIL-released parse)
- Thread B runs Python code (requiring GIL) during Thread A's parse phase
- If Thread B executes promptly, GIL release is confirmed
- If Thread B blocks until Thread A finishes, GIL is being held (test fails)

This would guard the three-phase pattern (read → detach(parse) → convert) in src-python/src/readers.rs as a tested invariant, not just trusted architecture.

Relevant after pyo3 0.27→0.28 migration (PR #8) which renamed GIL-related APIs.
close_reason: Deterministic GIL release verification tests added and passing in CI

title: H.4a Bug: RecordBoundaryScanner scans for 0x1E (field terminator) instead of 0x1D (record terminator)
description: The RecordBoundaryScanner scans for 0x1E bytes (field terminators) but MARC ISO 2709 records end with 0x1D (record terminator). This causes boundaries to split records at field boundaries rather than record boundaries. This breaks H.4b (Rayon Parser Pool) which expects boundaries that represent complete, parseable records. The scanner should look for 0x1D instead.

title: Flaky GIL release test: replace timing proxy with deterministic verification
description:
## Problem

`test_pathlib_gil_release.py::test_pathlib_path_threading_equivalent_to_str` fails intermittently on macOS CI runners. The test measures threading speedup ratios as a proxy for whether pathlib.Path input releases the GIL, asserting `0.7 < speedup_ratio < 1.3`. On shared CI infrastructure, timing variance causes false negatives — e.g. ratio of 0.59x on macOS (pathlib.Path 0.29s seq / 0.49s parallel = 0.60x "speedup").

Recent failure: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/actions/runs/21933435597/job/63342489677

The same test passed on all 5 macOS jobs for the immediately preceding commit, confirming this is timing flakiness rather than a real regression.

## Root Cause

The test uses a timing-based proxy to verify GIL behavior. This is unreliable because:
- CI runners share CPU with other tenants
- macOS runners are particularly variable (thermal throttling, ARM scheduling)
- Small files (10K records) complete fast enough that thread overhead dominates
- The 3-retry mechanism in CI masks but doesn't fix the flakiness

## What We Actually Want to Test

The real concern: does passing `pathlib.Path` to `MARCReader` cause Python to hold the GIL during file reads, or does it correctly route to the Rust file backend where the GIL can be released?

Code path (verified by reading the source):
1. `MARCReader.__new__()` → `BatchedUnifiedReader::new()` → `UnifiedReader::from_python()` → `ReaderBackend::from_python()`
2. In `backend.rs:78-106`: `pathlib.Path` detected via `__fspath__()` → string extracted → `File::open()` → `ReaderBackend::RustFile(file)`
3. During reading: `read_record_bytes_from_reader(file)` — pure Rust I/O, identical to `str` path
4. GIL released during parsing via `py.detach()` in `readers.rs`

Both `str` and `pathlib.Path` inputs produce `RustFile` variant and follow identical read+parse paths. The type detection happens only at construction time.

## Suggested Fix

Replace the timing-based proxy with deterministic verification:

1. **Backend type detection test**: Expose a diagnostic method (e.g. `MARCReader.backend_type` or `_backend_type()`) that returns `"rust_file"`, `"cursor"`, or `"python_file"`. Assert that `pathlib.Path` input produces `"rust_file"` — this is the actual invariant we care about.

2. **Keep a simplified threading smoke test**: Rather than asserting exact speedup ratios, verify only that parallel execution completes without error and produces correct results. The backend-type test above proves GIL release capability; we don't also need to measure it via timing.

3. **Mark remaining timing tests as benchmarks**: If we want to keep speedup measurement for informational purposes, mark them with `@pytest.mark.benchmark` so they don't block CI.

## Relevant Files
- `tests/python/test_pathlib_gil_release.py` — flaky test
- `src-python/src/backend.rs:54-131` — type detection / backend routing
- `src-python/src/readers.rs:77-93` — MARCReader construction
- `src-python/src/readers.rs:182-250` — `__next__` with `py.detach()` GIL release
notes: GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/3
close_reason: Fix verified in CI on macOS, Ubuntu, and Windows. All 8 new deterministic tests pass on all platforms. GitHub issue #3 closed.

title: Enable GIL release in pymrrc I/O operations
description:
Enable GIL release in pymrrc I/O operations to unlock threading parallelism.

Current State:
- Python::attach() HOLDS the GIL during all file I/O
- This blocks other Python threads from running
- Expected improvement: 1.41x → 3.4x speedup for concurrent reads (currently not achieved)

Root Cause:
- PyFileWrapper holds Py<PyAny> (Python file object reference)
- Py<T> requires the GIL to be held - it's not Send without GIL
- When we try to use detach() or pass it between threads, PyO3 rejects it

Required Fix:
1. Restructure I/O layer to separate Python object management from actual I/O
   - Move Python file object interaction to wrapper layer
   - Make core reading/writing logic independent of Python references
2. Use PyO3's allow_threads or detach() to explicitly release GIL during:
   - read_record() in readers.rs - the actual MarcReader::read_record() call
   - write_record() in writers.rs - the actual MarcWriter::write_record() call  
3. Option A: Create intermediate buffer/bytes that don't hold Python refs
   - PyFileWrapper reads into Vec<u8> while holding GIL
   - Pass bytes to Rust code WITHOUT Python refs
   - Rust code processes without GIL held
4. Option B: Use separate thread pool with GIL management
   - More complex but cleaner API

Implementation Steps:
- Refactor PyFileWrapper to not hold references during I/O operations
- Add allow_threads decorator or use detach() pattern
- Update PyO3 decorators in src-python/src/readers.rs and writers.rs
- Benchmark threading after implementation
- Verify pymrrc matches Rust parallelism efficiency

Depends on: Phase 2 results showing current threading limitation
close_reason: Obsolete: Superseded by Phase H RustFile implementation which provides true parallelism via pure Rust I/O. This investigation task was for Python file object approach which has architectural GIL limitations.

title: 🔴 CRITICAL: GIL Release Not Working - 0.83x Speedup
notes: Investigation Complete (mrrc-tcb): Root cause identified - Python file I/O requires GIL. Phase C can amortize GIL overhead (100x reduction in acquire/release frequency), but parallelism impossible with Python file objects. Strategy: Proceed with Phase C as-is for GIL optimization; implement Phase H RustFile backend for actual parallelism (Phase H.2). Expected: Phase C ≥1.2x-1.4x speedup (amortization), Phase H ≥2.5x speedup (pure Rust I/O).

title: Audit src-python/python/mrrc/__init__.pyi type stubs
description:
## Context

src-python/python/mrrc/__init__.pyi has 669 lines of type stubs for the Python wrapper classes (Field, Leader, Record, MARCReader, MARCWriter, Subfield, format functions). This file was created in Phase 4 (mrrc-experiments ccb880b) when src-python/python/mrrc/ was still the canonical package location.

Root mrrc/ has _mrrc.pyi (stubs for the compiled Rust extension module) but NO __init__.pyi (stubs for the Python wrapper classes). This means downstream users and IDEs don't get full type information for the wrapper layer.

## After mrrc-3wz

Once src-python/python/mrrc/ is removed and __init__.pyi is moved to root mrrc/:

1. Audit __init__.pyi against the current root mrrc/__init__.py (1,466 lines) — the stubs were written when the package was 565 lines and are missing:
   - Query DSL classes (FieldQuery, TagRangeQuery, SubfieldPatternQuery, SubfieldValueQuery)
   - BIBFRAME functions (marc_to_bibframe, bibframe_to_marc, BibframeConfig, RdfGraph)
   - Format functions (record_to_csv, records_to_csv, mods_to_record, mods_collection_to_records, etc.)
   - Enhanced classes (Indicators, ControlField)
   - Module-level helpers (read(), write(), get_leader_valid_values(), etc.)
   - Authority/Holdings readers and records
   - All the query methods on Record (fields_by_indicator, fields_in_range, fields_matching, etc.)
2. Update __init__.pyi to cover the full current API
3. Verify pyright/mypy pass with the updated stubs
4. Ensure py.typed marker is present (moved from src-python in mrrc-3wz)
close_reason: Cleaned up dual Python package directories: moved py.typed to root mrrc/, deleted stale src-python/python/, merged type stubs into _mrrc.pyi with MODS functions and rich docstrings

title: Document project directory layout and build system in architecture.md
description:
docs/contributing/architecture.md covers runtime architecture (GIL release, parser pipeline, concurrency) but has no section on project structure or build system. After the cleanup of src-python/python/ (mrrc-3wz), add a section explaining:

1. **Directory layout** — what lives where and why:
   - `src/` — Core Rust library (mrrc crate)
   - `src-python/` — PyO3 extension crate (compiles to _mrrc.so)
   - `mrrc/` — Python package (wrapper classes + compiled extension)
   - `tests/`, `tests/python/` — Rust and Python test suites
   - `tests/data/` — Test fixtures

2. **Build flow** — how the pieces connect:
   - Cargo workspace: root `mrrc` crate + `src-python` member
   - maturin compiles `src-python/src/*.rs` → `mrrc/_mrrc.so`
   - Root `pyproject.toml` is the single source of truth (`python-packages = ["mrrc"]`, `manifest-path = "src-python/Cargo.toml"`)
   - `mrrc/__init__.py` wraps `_mrrc` extension with Pythonic API

3. **Why the split** — Rust library vs PyO3 extension vs Python wrapper are three distinct layers

This follows maturin's recommended mixed-project layout (https://maturin.rs/project_layout).
close_reason: Added docs/contributing/project-layout.md with full project structure, build system, and workflow documentation.

title: Fix Phase 2: Use Python::assume_gil_acquired() instead of bound py.allow_threads()
description:
CRITICAL FIX per GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md Part 2 Fix 2 (lines 149-235):

The issue: py.allow_threads() on a bound Python<'py> handle (from PyRefMut) does not actually release the GIL.

The solution: Use Python::assume_gil_acquired() to get an unbound Python handle that properly supports GIL release.

Required Changes:
1. In PyMARCReader::__next__(), replace:
   let py = slf.py();  // WRONG - bound handle
   
   With:
   let py = unsafe { Python::assume_gil_acquired() };  // CORRECT - unbound handle

2. Safety guarantee: PyRefMut guarantees GIL is held, so assume_gil_acquired() is safe

3. This matches the exact pattern in the implementation plan (lines 187-189)

Expected Outcome:
- GIL will actually be released during Phase 2 parsing
- Threading speedup will jump from 0.83x to 1.8x+
- Two threads will execute truly concurrently

Files to modify:
- src-python/src/readers.rs (__next__ method)
close_reason: Applied critical GIL release fix per GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md Part 2 Fix 2. Changed __next__() to use Python::assume_gil_acquired() instead of slf.py().

title: Add MODS read support
description:
Implement support for reading/parsing MODS XML records into MARC records.

The Library of Congress maintains official MODS-to-MARC crosswalk mappings and XSLT stylesheets:
- Spec: https://www.loc.gov/standards/mods/mods-conversions.html
- Use the MODS 3.8 to MARCXML mapping as the reference specification
- LOC provides XSLT 1.0 and 2.0 stylesheets for MODS 3.8 → MARCXML conversion

Implementation should:
1. Parse MODS XML input (version 3.8, with reasonable fallback for 3.4-3.7)
2. Map MODS elements to MARC fields per the LOC crosswalk
3. Return standard mrrc Record objects
4. Expose via both Rust API and Python bindings (MARCReader or dedicated reader)

The existing MODS write support can serve as a reference for the element/field mappings in the reverse direction.

Ref: README.md format table shows MODS Read as '-', Write as 'Yes'.
notes: GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/2 — close that issue when this bead is completed.
close_reason: Implemented in commit 302fd37. mods_to_record() and mods_collection_to_records() working in both Rust and Python.

Phase C testing (test_queue_state_machine_c2.py) doesn't exercise these, so they weren't adopted.

title: Phase C Deferral Gate: Analyze Phase F results and decide Phase C execution
description:
Phase C Deferral Decision Point

See: GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md Part 10 (lines 1364-1373)

After Phase F completes (mrrc-9wi.5.2), analyze the benchmark results to decide whether Phase C (Performance Optimizations) should be executed.

**Decision Criteria:**

1. Compare 2-thread speedup vs baseline (established in mrrc-9wi.2.5)
   - Baseline: Current performance before GIL release implementation
   - Target: ≥ 2.0x speedup for 2-thread concurrent reading

2. If Phase F shows speedup ≥ 2.0x:
   - Phase C is SKIPPED (optional, goals achieved)
   - Proceed directly to Phase G (Documentation Refresh)
   - Critical Path: A → B → D → E → F → Phase G

3. If Phase F shows speedup < 2.0x:
   - Phase C is REQUIRED (performance optimization needed)
   - Create mrrc-9wi.7 epic for Phase C before Phase G
   - Critical Path: A → B → D → E → F → Phase C → Phase G

**Deliverable:**
- Document decision in .benchmarks/PHASE_C_DECISION.txt
- Record final speedup metrics vs baseline
- Justify decision (≥2x achieved or optimization needed)

**Blockers for Phase G:**
- If Phase C required: All mrrc-9wi.7 tasks must complete before Phase G
- If Phase C skipped: Phase G proceeds immediately

References:
- Phase F: mrrc-9wi.5 (Benchmark Refresh)
- Phase G: mrrc-9wi.6 (Documentation Refresh)
- Baseline: mrrc-9wi.2.5 (Establish baseline benchmark)

title: Add context7.json to repo for future indexing
description:
Add context7.json to the repo root so that when we're ready to submit mrrc to Context7, the indexer will be properly guided. This is preparation, NOT submission.

context7.json controls what Context7 indexes:
- projectTitle: 'MRRC: MARC Rust Crate'
- description: concise summary of Rust MARC library with Python bindings
- folders: docs/, src/, mrrc/, examples/ (guide indexer to useful content)
- excludeFolders: docs/history/ (94 archival files), target/, .beads/, .claude/
- rules: key conventions (e.g. Python API wraps Rust via _mrrc.pyi, pymarc-compatible)

Also benefits Docfork and other services that read repo structure, since well-organized docs and clear exclusions help any indexer.

Submission to Context7 and Docfork tracked separately in mrrc-TBD (do not submit until overall quality is where we want it).

title: Add nightly Miri CI job
description:
Add a nightly scheduled CI job that runs the test suite under Miri (Rust's undefined behavior detector) to catch memory safety issues, use-after-free, uninitialized reads, and other UB.

GitHub issue: https://github.com/dchud/mrrc/issues/38

Implementation:
- Add .github/workflows/miri.yml with daily schedule (cron) and workflow_dispatch
- Use dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly with miri component
- Run unit tests only (cargo +nightly miri test --lib) — integration tests do file I/O that Miri can't handle
- Use MIRIFLAGS=-Zmiri-disable-isolation
- If any unit tests fail under Miri, triage: fix real bugs, #[cfg_attr(miri, ignore)] tests that do unsupported I/O

Acceptance criteria:
- Miri job runs nightly on schedule
- Miri job can be triggered manually via workflow_dispatch
- Unit tests pass under Miri, or failing tests are triaged and tracked
- Integration tests are excluded
